title: R for Clinical Data Analysis: From Fundamentals to Advanced Biostatistics
text:
About This Book
Clinical research generates some of the most consequential data in the world — data that shapes treatment decisions, regulatory approvals, and ultimately patient lives. Yet for decades, the tools used to analyse this data have been dominated by proprietary software. R has changed that.
This book, R for Clinical Data Analysis: From Fundamentals to Advanced Biostatistics, is designed to be a comprehensive, practical companion for anyone working with clinical data using R — whether you are a clinical researcher new to programming, a biostatistician transitioning from SAS, or an epidemiologist expanding your analytical toolkit.
crumbs: Preface

title: R for Clinical Data Analysis: From Fundamentals to Advanced Biostatistics
text:
Who This Book Is For
This book is written for:

Clinical researchers who want to understand and run their own analyses
Biostatisticians looking to master R for clinical trial work
Statistical programmers making the transition from SAS to R
Epidemiologists and public health professionals working with observational data
Graduate students in biostatistics, clinical research, or public health

We assume no prior knowledge of R, but a basic familiarity with statistical concepts (means, p-values, confidence intervals) will be helpful. Where statistical concepts are introduced, we explain them in plain language with equations presented accessibly for readers from non-statistical backgrounds.
crumbs: Preface

title: R for Clinical Data Analysis: From Fundamentals to Advanced Biostatistics
text:
How This Book Is Organised
The book is divided into seven parts that build progressively:
Part I: Foundations of R Programming introduces R, RStudio, data types, import/export, and data cleaning — the essential toolkit for any analyst.
Part II: Clinical Data Standards and Structures covers CDISC standards (SDTM and ADaM), exploratory data analysis, and data visualisation tailored to clinical contexts.
Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials provides rigorous treatment of descriptive statistics, hypothesis testing, ANOVA/ANCOVA, and regression — the workhorses of clinical analysis.
Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis addresses survival analysis, longitudinal models, missing data strategies, and safety analysis — topics central to modern clinical trials.
Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods covers bioequivalence, non-inferiority trials, Bayesian methods, and real-world evidence — increasingly important areas in modern drug development.
Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs teaches production-quality Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs), reproducible reporting with Quarto, and validation for regulatory submission.
Part VII: Case Studies brings everything together through three complete end-to-end analyses: a Phase III trial, an oncology survival study, and an infectious disease investigation.
Appendices provide quick-reference guides: R vs SAS mapping, key clinical packages, simulated datasets, and regulatory references.
crumbs: Preface

title: R for Clinical Data Analysis: From Fundamentals to Advanced Biostatistics
text:
Pedagogical Features
Each chapter follows a consistent structure designed for active learning:






NoteChapter Structure




Concept Explanation — Theory explained in plain language, with equations where needed
Clinical Context — Why this topic matters in real clinical research
R Code Implementation — Fully annotated, reproducible code
Interpretation of Results — How to read and report the output
Exercises — Progressive challenges from beginner to advanced
crumbs: Preface

title: R for Clinical Data Analysis: From Fundamentals to Advanced Biostatistics
text:
Datasets Used in This Book
All examples in this book use freely available datasets so you can reproduce every analysis yourself:

Built-in R datasets: lung, colon, Orthodont, birthwt, airquality, and more
Pharmaverse datasets: From {pharmaversesdtm} (SDTM domains: DM, AE, LB, VS, EX) and {admiral} (ADaM datasets)
Simulated clinical datasets: Created within the text for specific teaching purposes

You do not need to purchase or download any proprietary data. Every dataset used is either included with R, available from CRAN packages, or simulated directly in the code.
crumbs: Preface

title: R for Clinical Data Analysis: From Fundamentals to Advanced Biostatistics
text:
Software Requirements
To follow along with this book, you will need:
# Core R (version ≥ 4.3.0 recommended)
# https://cran.r-project.org

# RStudio Desktop (free)
# https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/

# Quarto (for Chapter 21 and reproducing this book)
# https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/

# Install key packages
install.packages(c(
  # Tidyverse
  "tidyverse", "dplyr", "tidyr", "ggplot2", "readr", "haven",
  # Clinical / Pharmaverse
  "admiral", "pharmaversesdtm", "admiraldev",
  # Biostatistics
  "survival", "survminer", "lme4", "nlme", "mice",
  # Reporting
  "gt", "flextable", "gtsummary", "rtables",
  # Bayesian
  "rstanarm", "bayesplot",
  # Other
  "PropCIs", "ggsurvfit", "cmprsk", "MatchIt"
))

title: R for Clinical Data Analysis: From Fundamentals to Advanced Biostatistics
text:
A Note on Reproducibility
All code in this book was written and tested using R 4.4.x with the package versions available in 2025–2026. We use set.seed() wherever random number generation is involved, so your results should match ours exactly.
The companion GitHub repository at https://github.com/r4clinical/book contains all code, datasets, and Quarto source files.

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
text:
1.1 1.1 Why R in Clinical Trials: The Case for Open Science
Clinical research operates in one of the most highly regulated industries on Earth. Every analysis, every statistical inference, and every reported outcome must be defensible, reproducible, and transparent to regulatory agencies like the FDA. For decades, SAS has been the de facto standard in pharmaceutical research—and for good reason. SAS’s dominance stemmed from its reputation, established workflows, and regulatory acceptance.
However, the landscape is shifting. The FDA has explicitly endorsed open-source statistical computing in its 2023 Statistical Computing Environment guidance, which acknowledges that R and Python are viable alternatives to proprietary software for clinical data analysis and regulatory submissions. This shift opens a remarkable opportunity for clinical researchers: access to cutting-edge statistical methods, transparent algorithms, and a global community of biostatisticians—all at a fraction of the cost.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
text:
1.1.1 The Economic Reality
A single SAS license costs approximately $10,000–15,000 per year for an individual analyst. A large pharmaceutical company running 50+ concurrent projects might spend $500,000 annually on SAS licenses alone. R is free. The savings are not trivial, particularly for academic medical centers, government health agencies, and clinical research organizations in resource-constrained settings.
But cost is only the beginning. SAS, as a proprietary system, obscures its underlying algorithms. When a SAS PROC produces a p-value, users must trust that the calculation is correct—they cannot inspect the source code. R inverts this relationship: every statistical function is transparent and verifiable. This transparency aligns perfectly with the modern emphasis on reproducible research.


1.1.2 Reproducibility and Transparency






Reproducibility Crisis in Medicine: A 2016 Nature survey found that 70% of researchers had failed to reproduce another scientist’s work. In clinical research, irreproducibility can lead to abandoned drug development programs, failed regulatory submissions, and—worst of all—patients denied effective treatments.



R enables true reproducibility. An analysis written in R code can be version-controlled with Git, shared openly on GitHub, and executed identically on any operating system. A colleague in Mumbai can run your entire statistical analysis, line by line, exactly as you wrote it. SAS scripts can be shared, but the proprietary compiler’s internal decisions remain opaque.


1.1.3 The CRAN Ecosystem and Pharmaverse
The Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN) hosts over 20,000 peer-reviewed packages, many developed by leading biostatisticians and clinicians worldwide. The pharmaverse project—a collective effort by major pharmaceutical companies (Roche, Eli Lilly, GSK, Janssen, Novartis, and others)—has created an integrated ecosystem of R packages specifically designed for clinical trial analysis:

{admiral}: ADaM (Analysis Data Model) dataset building
{pharmaversesdtm}: SDTM (Study Data Tabulation Model) reference datasets
{tern}: Analysis functions for table generation
{cards}: Card data structures for flexible output
{riskmetrics}: Risk metric calculations

These packages implement CDISC standards directly in R, allowing you to build analysis datasets that are both scientifically sound and ready for FDA submission.


1.1.4 FDA Acceptance and Regulatory Pathways
The FDA’s 2023 guidance explicitly states: “Software used for statistical computing should be able to provide a full audit trail… [and] the source code [should be] available to understand, review, and verify the appropriateness of the statistical methods.”
This opens the door for R-based submissions. Several recent IND and BLA submissions have included R code alongside or in place of SAS programs. The key requirements are:

Reproducibility: Code must produce identical results when executed again
Documentation: Statistical methods must be clearly explained
Validation: Where appropriate, calculations should be validated against a second tool
Version control: All software versions must be documented

R satisfies all these criteria—and its open-source nature actually enhances validation and regulatory confidence.

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
section: 1.2 1.2 The Clinical Data Workflow in R
text:
1.2 1.2 The Clinical Data Workflow in R
A clinical trial generates data at multiple stages, and understanding this workflow is essential for writing effective analysis code.

1.2.1 From Protocol to Analysis
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   PROTOCOL DESIGN                           │
│         (Primary endpoints, statistical analysis plan)        │
└─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
┌─────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐
│              DATA COLLECTION & VALIDATION                    │
│         (eCRFs, lab instruments, adverse event reports)      │
└─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
┌─────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐
│          RAW DATA (raw, unclean, variable formats)           │
│                  CDISC Standards (SDTM)                      │
└─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
┌─────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐
│              DATA CLEANING & VALIDATION                      │
│       (Identify outliers, missing data, inconsistencies)     │
└─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
┌─────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐
│          ANALYSIS DATASETS (Clean, ready for analysis)       │
│                  CDISC Standards (ADaM)                      │
└─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
┌─────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐
│         STATISTICAL ANALYSIS (Descriptive, inferential)      │
│              (R, SAS, Python, or other validated tools)      │
└─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
┌─────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐
│        REPORTING (Tables, figures, listings)                 │
│         (Integrated into Clinical Study Report)              │
└─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
┌─────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐
│          REGULATORY SUBMISSION (IND, BLA, NDA)               │
│         (To FDA or EMA for review and approval)              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Each stage has distinct R tools and workflows:

Data collection: Electronic data capture systems generate raw data files (often CSV or XML)
SDTM domain creation: Raw data is mapped to CDISC SDTM domains (DM for demographics, AE for adverse events, LB for laboratory, etc.)
ADaM development: SDTM domains are combined to create analysis-ready datasets (e.g., ADSL for subject-level data, ADLB for lab analysis)
Statistical analysis: Analysis datasets are analyzed using hypothesis tests, descriptive statistics, and graphics
Reporting: Results are formatted into tables, listings, and figures for inclusion in the Clinical Study Report







CDISC Standards Are Not Optional: If your trial data might be submitted to the FDA, CDISC standards are essentially mandatory. They ensure data quality, reduce errors, and facilitate regulatory review. R packages like {admiral} automate much of this work.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
text:
1.3 1.3 Understanding CDISC Standards

1.3.1 SDTM: The Data Foundation
The Study Data Tabulation Model (SDTM) is the FDA-required format for submitting raw clinical data. Think of SDTM as the “ground truth”—the faithful representation of what was observed in the trial.
SDTM organizes data into domains. Each domain captures a specific type of clinical information:








Domain
Full Name
Content




DM
Demographics
Subject IDs, age, sex, ethnicity, enrollment date


AE
Adverse Events
Adverse event terms, severity, causality, dates


LB
Laboratory
Lab tests (hematology, chemistry, urinalysis) with values and units


VS
Vital Signs
Blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate


EX
Exposure
Drug dose, date, time, route of administration


CM
Concomitant Medications
Non-trial medications taken by patients


DS
Disposition
Visit status, milestone achievements



Each domain has a specific structure. For example, the LB (Laboratory) domain contains:
USUBJID: Unique Subject ID
LBDTC:   Lab test date
LBTEST:  Lab test name (e.g., "Hemoglobin")
LBCAT:   Lab category (e.g., "Hematology")
LBSTRESN: Lab value (numeric, standardized result)
LBSTRESU: Lab unit, standardised (e.g., "g/dL")
LBNRLO:  Lower reference range
LBNRHI:  Upper reference range
This standardization ensures that every lab value is recorded identically, regardless of which lab or region it came from.


1.3.2 ADaM: Analysis-Ready Data
The Analysis Data Model (ADaM) takes SDTM data and transforms it into analysis-ready form. If SDTM is the “raw truth,” ADaM is the “analysis truth”—data structured for statistical testing.
For example, an ADaM lab dataset (ADLB) might: - Pivot lab values from long format (one row per test) to wide format (one row per subject per visit) - Calculate change from baseline for each lab parameter - Flag abnormal values using CTCAE (Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events) grades - Derive treatment group indicators
A typical ADLB row for a single subject might look like:
USUBJID   VISIT    LBTEST         BASELINE  VALUE   CHG  ABNFL
"001-101" "Week 4" "Hemoglobin"   13.5      12.8    -0.7 "Y"
"001-101" "Week 8" "Hemoglobin"   13.5      12.2    -1.3 "Y"
"001-101" "Week 4" "WBC"          7.2       6.1     -1.1 "N"

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
section: 1.4 1.4 Installing R and the Clinical Development Environment
text:
1.4 1.4 Installing R and the Clinical Development Environment

1.4.1 R and RStudio Installation
R 4.4 or later is required for this book. Older versions lack modern language features and performance improvements critical for clinical work.
For Windows: 1. Navigate to https://cran.r-project.org/ 2. Click “Download R for Windows” 3. Click “base” 4. Download and run the installer 5. Accept all defaults
For macOS: 1. Navigate to https://cran.r-project.org/ 2. Click “Download R for macOS” 3. Download the appropriate version (Intel or Apple Silicon) 4. Install as you would any macOS application
For Linux: Use your distribution’s package manager:
# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get install r-base r-base-dev

# Red Hat/CentOS
sudo yum install R R-devel


1.4.2 RStudio Installation
RStudio is the IDE (Integrated Development Environment) that makes R accessible and productive. Download from https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/:

Download the free RStudio Desktop version
Install as you would any application
Open RStudio

You should see four panes: - Script/Editor (top-left): Where you write R code - Console (bottom-left): Where R executes commands and shows output - Environment/History (top-right): Variables and command history - Files/Plots/Packages/Help (bottom-right): File browser, plots, package manager, help docs


1.4.3 Installing Clinical Analysis Packages
The following code installs the essential packages for clinical data analysis. Run this in the R console:
# Install from CRAN (general-purpose packages)
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "readxl", "haven", "lubridate", "stringr"))

# Install from Bioconductor (optional, for genomic analysis)
if (!require("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
    install.packages("BiocManager")
BiocManager::install("limma")

# Install pharmaverse packages
# As of 2024, pharmaverse is not on CRAN but on GitHub
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("pharmaverse/pharmaversesdtm")
remotes::install_github("pharmaverse/admiral")
After installation, load the packages to verify:
library(tidyverse)    # Data manipulation and visualization
library(readxl)       # Excel file reading
library(haven)        # SAS/SPSS file reading
library(lubridate)    # Date/time handling
library(stringr)      # String manipulation
library(pharmaversesdtm)  # Clinical reference datasets
library(admiral)      # ADaM dataset building
If all packages load without error, your environment is ready.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
section: 1.5 1.5 Your First Clinical Dataset: Loading and Inspecting SDTM Data
text:
1.5 1.5 Your First Clinical Dataset: Loading and Inspecting SDTM Data
Let’s load a real SDTM dataset from the pharmaversesdtm package and explore it. This dataset comes from a simulated phase III oncology trial.






Simulated Clinical Data: The pharmaversesdtm package contains simulated data based on realistic clinical trial structures. While the actual values are synthetic, the data organization, variable names, and domain structures are authentic and representative of real clinical trials submitted to the FDA.




1.5.1 Loading the Data

# Load the pharmaversesdtm package
library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(tidyverse)

# The package includes several simulated SDTM domains
# Load the Demographics domain (DM) which contains baseline subject information
data("dm")

# Display the first few rows
head(dm)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 28
#>   STUDYID      DOMAIN USUBJID   SUBJID RFSTDTC RFENDTC RFXSTDTC RFXENDTC RFICDTC
#>   <chr>        <chr>  <chr>     <chr>  <chr>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  
#> 1 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1015   2014-0… 2014-0… 2014-01… 2014-07… <NA>   
#> 2 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1023   2012-0… 2012-0… 2012-08… 2012-09… <NA>   
#> 3 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1028   2013-0… 2014-0… 2013-07… 2014-01… <NA>   
#> 4 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1033   2014-0… 2014-0… 2014-03… 2014-03… <NA>   
#> 5 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1034   2014-0… 2014-1… 2014-07… 2014-12… <NA>   
#> 6 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1047   2013-0… 2013-0… 2013-02… 2013-03… <NA>   
#> # ℹ 19 more variables: RFPENDTC <chr>, DTHDTC <chr>, DTHFL <chr>, SITEID <chr>,
#> #   BRTHDTC <chr>, AGE <dbl>, AGEU <chr>, SEX <chr>, RACE <chr>, ETHNIC <chr>,
#> #   ARMCD <chr>, ARM <chr>, ACTARMCD <chr>, ACTARM <chr>, COUNTRY <chr>,
#> #   DMDTC <chr>, DMDY <dbl>, ARMNRS <chr>, ACTARMUD <chr>


The output shows a demographics dataset with columns like:
# A tibble: 6 x 27
  STUDYID DOMAIN USUBJID SUBJID RFSTDTC     RFENDTC     DTHDTC DTHFL BRTHDTC    AGE
  <chr>   <chr>  <chr>   <chr>  <date>      <date>      <date> <chr> <date>   <dbl>
1 EUSTC1  DM     01-701  701    2014-04-18  2014-10-16  NA     N     1952-01-15 62
2 EUSTC1  DM     01-702  702    2014-04-19  2014-10-16  NA     N     1982-05-24 32
3 EUSTC1  DM     01-703  703    2014-04-20  2014-10-16  NA     N     1975-03-09 39
...


1.5.2 Inspecting Structure and Dimensions
Understanding your data begins with asking basic questions: How many rows and columns? What are the data types? Are there missing values?

# Get dimensions: rows (subjects) and columns (variables)
dim(dm)
# [1] 254  27

# Alternative functions for dimensions
nrow(dm)  # 254 subjects
ncol(dm)  # 27 variables

# Get column names
names(dm)
# [1] "STUDYID"   "DOMAIN"    "USUBJID"   "SUBJID"    "RFSTDTC"
# [6] "RFENDTC"   "DTHDTC"    "DTHFL"     "BRTHDTC"   "AGE"
# ...

# Examine the structure of each column
str(dm)
# tibble [254 × 27] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
#  $ STUDYID  : chr "EUSTC1" "EUSTC1" "EUSTC1" ...
#  $ DOMAIN   : chr "DM" "DM" "DM" ...
#  $ USUBJID  : chr "01-701" "01-702" "01-703" ...
#  $ AGE      : num 62 32 39 56 31 54 46 34 ...
#  $ SEX      : chr "F" "M" "F" ...
#  $ RACE     : chr "WHITE" "BLACK OR AFRICAN AMERICAN" "WHITE" ...
#  $ ETHNIC   : chr "NOT HISPANIC OR LATINO" ...



1.5.3 Summary Statistics






Clinical Context: Demographic summary statistics are always the first table in a Clinical Study Report. They establish baseline comparability and ensure your analysis population matches the protocol.




# Generate a summary of all variables
summary(dm)

#>    STUDYID             DOMAIN            USUBJID             SUBJID         
#>  Length:306         Length:306         Length:306         Length:306        
#>  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
#>  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>    RFSTDTC            RFENDTC            RFXSTDTC           RFXENDTC        
#>  Length:306         Length:306         Length:306         Length:306        
#>  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
#>  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>    RFICDTC            RFPENDTC            DTHDTC             DTHFL          
#>  Length:306         Length:306         Length:306         Length:306        
#>  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
#>  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>     SITEID            BRTHDTC               AGE            AGEU          
#>  Length:306         Length:306         Min.   :50.00   Length:306        
#>  Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.:70.25   Class :character  
#>  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median :77.00   Mode  :character  
#>                                        Mean   :75.09                     
#>                                        3rd Qu.:81.00                     
#>                                        Max.   :89.00                     
#>                                                                          
#>      SEX                RACE              ETHNIC             ARMCD          
#>  Length:306         Length:306         Length:306         Length:306        
#>  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
#>  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>      ARM              ACTARMCD            ACTARM            COUNTRY         
#>  Length:306         Length:306         Length:306         Length:306        
#>  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
#>  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>                                                                             
#>     DMDTC                DMDY        ARMNRS            ACTARMUD        
#>  Length:306         Min.   :-37   Length:306         Length:306        
#>  Class :character   1st Qu.:-14   Class :character   Class :character  
#>  Mode  :character   Median :-10   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
#>                     Mean   :-11                                        
#>                     3rd Qu.: -7                                        
#>                     Max.   : -2                                        
#>                     NA's   :52

# For a more informative summary specific to demographics:
dm %>%
  summarise(
    n_subjects = n(),
    age_mean = mean(AGE, na.rm = TRUE),
    age_sd = sd(AGE, na.rm = TRUE),
    age_min = min(AGE, na.rm = TRUE),
    age_max = max(AGE, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_female = 100 * sum(SEX == "F", na.rm = TRUE) / n(),
    pct_male = 100 * sum(SEX == "M", na.rm = TRUE) / n()
  )

#> # A tibble: 1 × 7
#>   n_subjects age_mean age_sd age_min age_max pct_female pct_male
#>        <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1        306     75.1   8.49      50      89       58.5     41.5

# Results:
# n_subjects age_mean age_sd age_min age_max pct_female pct_male
#        254     52.5   13.8      21      85       48.0     52.0

This tells us: - 254 subjects enrolled in the trial - Mean age 52.5 years (SD 13.8), range 21–85 years - Approximately 48% female, 52% male —a reasonable gender balance


1.5.4 Checking for Missing Data
Missing data is a critical consideration in clinical analyses. Missing values can bias results if not handled carefully.

# Count missing values per column
colSums(is.na(dm))

#>  STUDYID   DOMAIN  USUBJID   SUBJID  RFSTDTC  RFENDTC RFXSTDTC RFXENDTC 
#>        0        0        0        0       52       52       52       54 
#>  RFICDTC RFPENDTC   DTHDTC    DTHFL   SITEID  BRTHDTC      AGE     AGEU 
#>      306        0      303      303        0        0        0        0 
#>      SEX     RACE   ETHNIC    ARMCD      ARM ACTARMCD   ACTARM  COUNTRY 
#>        0        0        0        0        0        0        0        0 
#>    DMDTC     DMDY   ARMNRS ACTARMUD 
#>        0       52      254      306

# STUDYID     DOMAIN    USUBJID     SUBJID  RFSTDTC   RFENDTC    DTHDTC
#         0         0         0         0         0         0       250
# DTHFL    BRTHDTC       AGE       SEX      RACE     ETHNIC
#         0         0         0         0         0         0

# Interpretation:
# - 250 of 254 subjects did not die during the trial (DTHDTC = missing for 250)
# - All demographic variables have complete data (DTHDTC missing is expected—not everyone dies)

# Examine one specific variable more carefully
dm %>%
  group_by(SEX) %>%
  summarise(
    count = n(),
    pct = 100 * n() / nrow(dm),
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

#> # A tibble: 2 × 3
#>   SEX   count   pct
#>   <chr> <int> <dbl>
#> 1 F       179  58.5
#> 2 M       127  41.5

# Results:
# SEX   count   pct
# F       122  48.0
# M       132  52.0



1.5.5 Using glimpse() for Quick Inspection
The {dplyr} package (part of tidyverse) provides glimpse(), which shows data in a transposed format—perfect for quick inspection:

library(dplyr)

glimpse(dm)

#> Rows: 306
#> Columns: 28

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
text:
1.6 1.6 The Pharmaverse Ecosystem
The pharmaverse is a collection of R packages maintained collaboratively by major pharmaceutical companies. It provides a comprehensive toolkit for clinical data analysis aligned with CDISC standards.

1.6.1 Key Packages
{pharmaversesdtm}: Reference SDTM datasets for learning and testing. This package provides realistic simulated trial data organized in SDTM format.

library(pharmaversesdtm)

# Available datasets
data(package = "pharmaversesdtm")

# Load multiple SDTM domains from the same study
data("dm")  # Demographics
data("ae")  # Adverse events
data("lb")  # Laboratory data
data("ex")  # Drug exposure
data("vs")  # Vital signs
data("cm")  # Concomitant medications
data("ds")  # Disposition

{admiral}: The ADaM data modeling package. Provides functions to create analysis datasets from SDTM data, following ADaM standards. While we’ll use this extensively in later chapters, here’s a preview:

# admiral provides a grammar for building ADaM datasets from SDTM.
# Key function families:
#   derive_vars_*()   – add new variables (dates, flags, duration)
#   derive_param_*()  – add derived parameters (e.g. BMI, BSA)
#   compute_*()       – scalar computations (age, duration)
#   filter_*()        – filter to specific records or windows

# Show admiral functions only if the package is installed AND can be attached
if (requireNamespace("admiral", quietly = TRUE)) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(admiral))
  admiral_fns <- getNamespaceExports("admiral")
  cat("admiral functions available:", length(admiral_fns), "\n\n")
  cat("Sample derive_ functions:\n")
  derive_fns <- sort(grep("^derive_", admiral_fns, value = TRUE))
  cat(paste0("  ", head(derive_fns, 8)), sep = "\n")
} else {
  cat("admiral not installed.\n")
  cat("Install with: install.packages('admiral')\n")
}

#> admiral functions available: 148 
#> 
#> Sample derive_ functions:
#>   derive_basetype_records
#>   derive_expected_records
#>   derive_extreme_event
#>   derive_extreme_records
#>   derive_locf_records
#>   derive_param_bmi
#>   derive_param_bsa
#>   derive_param_computed

# Regardless of admiral availability, demonstrate age calculation —
# the same logic admiral's compute_age_years() wraps internally:
dm_with_computed_age <- dm |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    RFSTDT   = as.Date(substr(RFSTDTC, 1, 10)),
    BRTHDT   = as.Date(substr(BRTHDTC, 1, 10)),
    AGE_CALC = as.integer(difftime(RFSTDT, BRTHDT, units = "days") / 365.25)
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(USUBJID, AGE, AGE_CALC, RFSTDTC, BRTHDTC)

head(dm_with_computed_age, 5)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 5
#>   USUBJID       AGE AGE_CALC RFSTDTC    BRTHDTC   
#>   <chr>       <dbl>    <int> <chr>      <chr>     
#> 1 01-701-1015    63       63 2014-01-02 1950-12-26
#> 2 01-701-1023    64       64 2012-08-05 1948-07-22
#> 3 01-701-1028    71       71 2013-07-19 1942-07-11
#> 4 01-701-1033    74       74 2014-03-18 1940-03-10
#> 5 01-701-1034    77       77 2014-07-01 1937-06-24


{tern}: Provides tabulation and analysis functions for creating the tables and figures typically found in clinical study reports.
{cards}: A flexible data structure for storing and manipulating analysis results.

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
text:
1.7 1.7 Key Functions for Data Inspection
Every R session begins with understanding your data. Here are the essential functions:








Function
Purpose
Example




dim()
Dimensions (rows, columns)
dim(dm) returns [1] 254 27


nrow()
Number of rows
nrow(dm) returns 254


ncol()
Number of columns
ncol(dm) returns 27


head()
First 6 rows
head(dm) shows first 6 subjects


tail()
Last 6 rows
tail(dm) shows last 6 subjects


names()
Column names
names(dm) lists all variables


class()
Data structure type
class(dm) returns "tbl_df" (tibble)


str()
Structure (variables, types, sample values)
str(dm) shows full structure


glimpse()
Transposed view of structure
glimpse(dm) shows columns vertically


summary()
Descriptive statistics
summary(dm) shows mean, median, quartiles


is.na()
Identify missing values
is.na(dm$AGE) returns TRUE/FALSE vector


colSums(is.na())
Count missing per column
colSums(is.na(dm)) shows NAs per variable




1.7.1 Practical Inspection Workflow
Here’s a complete workflow for inspecting a new dataset:

# Step 1: Load and view basic structure
library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("dm")
head(dm, n = 10)        # First 10 rows

#> # A tibble: 10 × 28
#>    STUDYID      DOMAIN USUBJID  SUBJID RFSTDTC RFENDTC RFXSTDTC RFXENDTC RFICDTC
#>    <chr>        <chr>  <chr>    <chr>  <chr>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  
#>  1 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1015   2014-0… 2014-0… 2014-01… 2014-07… <NA>   
#>  2 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1023   2012-0… 2012-0… 2012-08… 2012-09… <NA>   
#>  3 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1028   2013-0… 2014-0… 2013-07… 2014-01… <NA>   
#>  4 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1033   2014-0… 2014-0… 2014-03… 2014-03… <NA>   
#>  5 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1034   2014-0… 2014-1… 2014-07… 2014-12… <NA>   
#>  6 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1047   2013-0… 2013-0… 2013-02… 2013-03… <NA>   
#>  7 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1057   <NA>    <NA>    <NA>     <NA>     <NA>   
#>  8 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1097   2014-0… 2014-0… 2014-01… 2014-07… <NA>   
#>  9 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1111   2012-0… 2012-0… 2012-09… 2012-09… <NA>   
#> 10 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-… 1115   2012-1… 2013-0… 2012-11… 2013-01… <NA>   
#> # ℹ 19 more variables: RFPENDTC <chr>, DTHDTC <chr>, DTHFL <chr>, SITEID <chr>,
#> #   BRTHDTC <chr>, AGE <dbl>, AGEU <chr>, SEX <chr>, RACE <chr>, ETHNIC <chr>,
#> #   ARMCD <chr>, ARM <chr>, ACTARMCD <chr>, ACTARM <chr>, COUNTRY <chr>,
#> #   DMDTC <chr>, DMDY <dbl>, ARMNRS <chr>, ACTARMUD <chr>

tail(dm, n = 5)         # Last 5 rows

#> # A tibble: 5 × 28
#>   STUDYID      DOMAIN USUBJID   SUBJID RFSTDTC RFENDTC RFXSTDTC RFXENDTC RFICDTC
#>   <chr>        <chr>  <chr>     <chr>  <chr>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  
#> 1 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-718-1… 1254   2013-0… 2014-0… 2013-07… 2014-01… <NA>   
#> 2 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-718-1… 1328   2013-0… 2013-0… 2013-02… 2013-04… <NA>   
#> 3 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-718-1… 1355   2013-0… 2013-0… 2013-02… 2013-08… <NA>   
#> 4 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-718-1… 1371   2013-0… 2013-0… 2013-04… 2013-08… <NA>   
#> 5 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-718-1… 1427   2012-1… 2013-0… 2012-12… 2013-02… <NA>   
#> # ℹ 19 more variables: RFPENDTC <chr>, DTHDTC <chr>, DTHFL <chr>, SITEID <chr>,
#> #   BRTHDTC <chr>, AGE <dbl>, AGEU <chr>, SEX <chr>, RACE <chr>, ETHNIC <chr>,
#> #   ARMCD <chr>, ARM <chr>, ACTARMCD <chr>, ACTARM <chr>, COUNTRY <chr>,
#> #   DMDTC <chr>, DMDY <dbl>, ARMNRS <chr>, ACTARMUD <chr>

dim(dm)                 # Overall size

#> [1] 306  28

str(dm)                 # Variable types

#> tibble [306 × 28] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
#>  $ STUDYID : chr [1:306] "CDISCPILOT01" "CDISCPILOT01" "CDISCPILOT01" "CDISCPILOT01" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Study Identifier"
#>  $ DOMAIN  : chr [1:306] "DM" "DM" "DM" "DM" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Domain Abbreviation"
#>  $ USUBJID : chr [1:306] "01-701-1015" "01-701-1023" "01-701-1028" "01-701-1033" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Unique Subject Identifier"
#>  $ SUBJID  : chr [1:306] "1015" "1023" "1028" "1033" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Subject Identifier for the Study"
#>  $ RFSTDTC : chr [1:306] "2014-01-02" "2012-08-05" "2013-07-19" "2014-03-18" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Subject Reference Start Date/Time"
#>  $ RFENDTC : chr [1:306] "2014-07-02" "2012-09-02" "2014-01-14" "2014-04-14" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Subject Reference End Date/Time"
#>  $ RFXSTDTC: chr [1:306] "2014-01-02" "2012-08-05" "2013-07-19" "2014-03-18" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Date/Time of First Study Treatment"
#>  $ RFXENDTC: chr [1:306] "2014-07-02" "2012-09-01" "2014-01-14" "2014-03-31" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Date/Time of Last Study Treatment"
#>  $ RFICDTC : chr [1:306] NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Date/Time of Informed Consent"
#>  $ RFPENDTC: chr [1:306] "2014-07-02T11:45" "2013-02-18" "2014-01-14T11:10" "2014-09-15" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Date/Time of End of Participation"
#>  $ DTHDTC  : chr [1:306] NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Date/Time of Death"
#>  $ DTHFL   : chr [1:306] NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Subject Death Flag"
#>  $ SITEID  : chr [1:306] "701" "701" "701" "701" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Study Site Identifier"
#>  $ BRTHDTC : chr [1:306] "1950-12-26" "1948-07-22" "1942-07-11" "1940-03-10" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Date/Time of Birth"
#>  $ AGE     : num [1:306] 63 64 71 74 77 85 59 68 81 84 ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Age"
#>  $ AGEU    : chr [1:306] "YEARS" "YEARS" "YEARS" "YEARS" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Age Units"
#>  $ SEX     : chr [1:306] "F" "M" "M" "M" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Sex"
#>  $ RACE    : chr [1:306] "WHITE" "WHITE" "WHITE" "WHITE" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Race"
#>  $ ETHNIC  : chr [1:306] "HISPANIC OR LATINO" "HISPANIC OR LATINO" "NOT HISPANIC OR LATINO" "NOT HISPANIC OR LATINO" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Ethnicity"
#>  $ ARMCD   : chr [1:306] "Pbo" "Pbo" "Xan_Hi" "Xan_Lo" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Planned Arm Code"
#>  $ ARM     : chr [1:306] "Placebo" "Placebo" "Xanomeline High Dose" "Xanomeline Low Dose" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Description of Planned Arm"
#>  $ ACTARMCD: chr [1:306] "Pbo" "Pbo" "Xan_Hi" "Xan_Lo" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Actual Arm Code"
#>  $ ACTARM  : chr [1:306] "Placebo" "Placebo" "Xanomeline High Dose" "Xanomeline Low Dose" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Description of Actual Arm"
#>  $ COUNTRY : chr [1:306] "USA" "USA" "USA" "USA" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Country"
#>  $ DMDTC   : chr [1:306] "2013-12-26" "2012-07-22" "2013-07-11" "2014-03-10" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Date/Time of Collection"
#>  $ DMDY    : num [1:306] -7 -14 -8 -8 -7 -21 NA -9 -13 -7 ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Study Day of Collection"
#>  $ ARMNRS  : chr [1:306] NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Reason Arm and/or Actual Arm is Null"
#>  $ ACTARMUD: chr [1:306] NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Description of Unplanned Actual Arm"
#>  - attr(*, "label")= chr "Demographics"

# Step 2: Check for missing data
missing_summary <- dm %>%
  summarise(across(everything(),
                   list(missing = ~sum(is.na(.)),
                        pct_missing = ~100*mean(is.na(.)))))
print(missing_summary)

#> # A tibble: 1 × 56
#>   STUDYID_missing STUDYID_pct_missing DOMAIN_missing DOMAIN_pct_missing
#>             <int>               <dbl>          <int>              <dbl>
#> 1               0                   0              0                  0
#> # ℹ 52 more variables: USUBJID_missing <int>, USUBJID_pct_missing <dbl>,
#> #   SUBJID_missing <int>, SUBJID_pct_missing <dbl>, RFSTDTC_missing <int>,
#> #   RFSTDTC_pct_missing <dbl>, RFENDTC_missing <int>,
#> #   RFENDTC_pct_missing <dbl>, RFXSTDTC_missing <int>,
#> #   RFXSTDTC_pct_missing <dbl>, RFXENDTC_missing <int>,
#> #   RFXENDTC_pct_missing <dbl>, RFICDTC_missing <int>,
#> #   RFICDTC_pct_missing <dbl>, RFPENDTC_missing <int>, …

# Step 3: Understand categorical variables
dm %>%
  summarise(
    n_distinct_sex = n_distinct(SEX),
    unique_sex = paste(unique(SEX), collapse = ", "),
    n_distinct_race = n_distinct(RACE),
    n_distinct_ethnic = n_distinct(ETHNIC)
  )

#> # A tibble: 1 × 4
#>   n_distinct_sex unique_sex n_distinct_race n_distinct_ethnic
#>            <int> <chr>                <int>             <int>
#> 1              2 F, M                     4                 2

# Step 4: Understand numeric variables
dm %>%
  select(where(is.numeric)) %>%
  summary()

#>       AGE             DMDY    
#>  Min.   :50.00   Min.   :-37  
#>  1st Qu.:70.25   1st Qu.:-14  
#>  Median :77.00   Median :-10  
#>  Mean   :75.09   Mean   :-11  
#>  3rd Qu.:81.00   3rd Qu.: -7  
#>  Max.   :89.00   Max.   : -2  
#>                  NA's   :52








Always Start with Inspection: Before any analysis, spend time understanding your data. Missing values hidden in poorly-inspected datasets can invalidate entire analyses. In clinical research, data quality is not optional—it’s a regulatory requirement.

1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
text:
1.8 1.8 Summary and Best Practices
This chapter has introduced R as a tool for clinical data analysis, established the regulatory landscape, and walked through a complete clinical data workflow from protocol to analysis. Key takeaways:

R is FDA-accepted: The FDA’s 2023 guidance acknowledges R and other open-source tools as valid for clinical statistical computing and regulatory submissions.
Reproducibility is paramount: R code is transparent and verifiable, supporting modern standards for reproducible research.
CDISC standards matter: SDTM and ADaM formatting are essentially required for FDA submissions and ensure consistent data quality.
The pharmaverse provides clinical solutions: Packages like {admiral}, {tern}, and {pharmaversesdtm} implement CDISC standards directly in R.
Data inspection is foundational: Always begin with head(), str(), summary(), and missing data checks.
Clinical context drives analysis choices: Every statistical decision should be grounded in understanding the clinical question and the data that was collected to answer it.

1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research

title: 1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research
text:
1.9 Exercises

1.9.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 1.1: Exploring the Demographics Dataset
Load the dm dataset from {pharmaversesdtm} and answer the following:

How many subjects are in the trial?
What proportion of subjects are female?
What is the age range (minimum and maximum)?
Are there any missing demographic variables?

Exercise 1.2: Comparing Data with summary()
Use the summary() function on the dm dataset to answer:

What is the median age of subjects?
What is the third quartile (75th percentile) of age?
Which race category includes the most subjects?
How many subjects have missing race information (if any)?

Exercise 1.3: Checking Data Completeness
Write code to determine:

How many subjects have no missing data across ALL demographics variables?
Which variables have the most missing data?
Is mortality (DTHFL) complete or are there missing values?



1.9.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 1.4: Creating a Demographic Summary Table
Create a summary table with the following structure:



Statistic
Value




N Subjects
254


Age (years): Mean (SD)
52.5 (13.8)


Age: Min – Max
21 – 85


Female, N (%)
122 (48.0)


Male, N (%)
132 (52.0)



Use dplyr functions (group_by(), summarise()) to create this table programmatically, not manually.
Exercise 1.5: Multi-Domain Integration
Load three SDTM domains from pharmaversesdtm: dm (demographics), ae (adverse events), and lb (laboratory).

How many adverse events were recorded across all subjects?
What is the mean number of adverse events per subject?
What is the range of laboratory tests available?
How many unique lab test types are in the dataset?

1  Introduction to R in Clinical Research

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
text:
2.1 2.1 Data Types in R: The Building Blocks
Every value in R has a data type. Understanding these types is essential because R’s functions behave differently depending on the type of data they receive. In clinical research, we work primarily with six data types.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
text:
2.1.1 Numeric (Real Numbers)
Numeric values are decimal numbers used for measurements, lab values, and continuous outcomes.

# Define a numeric value (systolic blood pressure)
systolic_bp <- 145.3

# Verify it is numeric
class(systolic_bp)

#> [1] "numeric"

# [1] "numeric"

# R stores this as a floating-point number (double precision)
# It can be used in arithmetic operations
diastolic_bp <- 92.5
mean_arterial_pressure <- (systolic_bp + 2 * diastolic_bp) / 3
print(mean_arterial_pressure)

#> [1] 110.1

# [1] 110.1

In clinical research, numeric data represents: - Lab values: Hemoglobin (g/dL), white blood cells (K/uL), glucose (mg/dL) - Vital signs: Blood pressure (mmHg), heart rate (bpm), temperature (°C or °F) - Efficacy measures: Tumor size (cm), FEV1 (liters), ejection fraction (%) - Pharmacokinetic parameters: AUC, Cmax, clearance






Numeric Precision: R uses double-precision floating-point arithmetic, which can introduce small rounding errors. In clinical data, these are negligible (differences at 16 decimal places), but when comparing floating-point numbers for exact equality, use all.equal() instead of ==.





2.1.2 Integer
Integers are whole numbers without decimal places. They occupy less memory than numeric values but are less commonly needed in clinical analysis.

# Define an integer explicitly using the L suffix
subject_id <- 101L
visit_number <- 3L
number_of_ae <- 2L

class(subject_id)

#> [1] "integer"

# [1] "integer"

# Count data is naturally integer:
# - Number of adverse events per subject
# - Number of protocol violations
# - Number of hospitalizations

# If you forget the L suffix, R treats it as numeric (not integer)
subject_id_numeric <- 101
class(subject_id_numeric)

#> [1] "numeric"

# [1] "numeric"



2.1.3 Character (Text Strings)
Character data represents text: names, identifiers, categorical values, and descriptive information.

# Character strings use double or single quotes
subject_id <- "001-101"
gender <- "Female"
adverse_event <- "Grade 3 Rash"

class(subject_id)

#> [1] "character"

# [1] "character"

# In clinical data, character variables include:
# - Subject IDs: "001-101", "001-102"
# - Treatment groups: "Active", "Placebo"
# - Event terms: "Nausea", "Headache", "Elevated ALT"
# - Adverse event severity: "Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"
# - Lab units: "g/dL", "K/uL", "%"

# Character vectors can be concatenated
patient_label <- paste("Subject", subject_id, "is", gender)
print(patient_label)

#> [1] "Subject 001-101 is Female"

# [1] "Subject 001-101 is Female"



2.1.4 Logical (Boolean)
Logical values are TRUE or FALSE. They result from comparisons and conditional tests.

# Logical values from comparisons
glucose <- 165  # mg/dL
is_hyperglycemic <- glucose > 125
print(is_hyperglycemic)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# In clinical research, logical flags indicate:
# - Abnormal result: is_abnormal = TRUE/FALSE
# - Safety flag: has_ae = TRUE/FALSE
# - Protocol violation: violated_inclusion = TRUE/FALSE
# - Baseline status: at_baseline = TRUE/FALSE

# Logical arithmetic (TRUE = 1, FALSE = 0)
num_adverse_events <- 3
ae_flag <- num_adverse_events > 0
print(ae_flag)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# Sum of logical vector counts TRUE values
safety_flags <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
count_flags <- sum(safety_flags)
print(count_flags)

#> [1] 3

# [1] 3



2.1.5 Factor (Categorical Data with Levels)
Factors are categorical variables where levels have a specific order or meaning. This is crucial for clinical data.

# Define a factor: treatment group with two levels
treatment <- factor(c("Active", "Placebo", "Active", "Placebo"),
                    levels = c("Placebo", "Active"))

class(treatment)

#> [1] "factor"

# [1] "factor"

# Display the factor
print(treatment)

#> [1] Active  Placebo Active  Placebo
#> Levels: Placebo Active

# [1] Active  Placebo Active  Placebo
# Levels: Placebo Active

# Note: The levels are ordered (Placebo first, Active second)
# This ordering matters for statistical models and visualizations

# Clinical factors include:
# - Treatment group: Levels = c("Control", "Low Dose", "High Dose")
# - Adverse event severity: Levels = c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe", "Life-threatening")
# - Visit number: Levels = c("Screening", "Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 12")
# - Gender: Levels = c("Male", "Female")

# When you analyze factors in statistics, the reference level matters
# Example: A logistic regression with treatment as a factor
# will compare each level against the reference (first level)

# Ordered factors (for ordinal data like severity)
severity <- ordered(c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"),
                    levels = c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"))

print(severity)

#> [1] Mild     Moderate Severe  
#> Levels: Mild < Moderate < Severe

# [1] Mild     Moderate Severe
# Levels: Mild < Moderate < Severe







Factors vs. Characters: If a variable is categorical (e.g., treatment group, gender, adverse event grade), use factor(), not character. Factors enable proper statistical modeling and prevent accidental misspellings (R will warn if you try to assign a level that doesn’t exist).





2.1.6 Date and Date-Time
Dates are specialized data types that handle temporal data correctly.

# Create a date using as.Date() with a standard format
screening_date <- as.Date("2024-03-15", format = "%Y-%m-%d")
baseline_date <- as.Date("2024-03-22")

class(screening_date)

#> [1] "Date"

# [1] "Date"

# Dates allow temporal arithmetic
days_to_baseline <- baseline_date - screening_date
print(days_to_baseline)

#> Time difference of 7 days

# Time difference of 7 days

# The lubridate package provides convenient date functions
library(lubridate)

# Parse dates flexibly
enrollment_date <- ymd("2024-03-22")  # Year-Month-Day
birth_date <- mdy("01/15/1970")        # Month-Day-Year
lab_datetime <- dmy_hm("22/03/2024 14:30")  # Day-Month-Year Hour:Minute

# Extract components
age_at_enrollment <- year(enrollment_date) - year(birth_date)
print(age_at_enrollment)

#> [1] 54

# [1] 54

In clinical research, dates are essential for: - Trial timeline: Screening, baseline, treatment, follow-up dates - Adverse event reporting: Date of onset, resolution, reporting - Medication history: Start/stop dates for concomitant medications - Lab test dates: Comparing results across visits
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
section: 2.2 2.2 Data Structures: Vectors, Matrices, Lists, and Data Frames
text:
2.2 2.2 Data Structures: Vectors, Matrices, Lists, and Data Frames

2.2.1 Vectors: The Foundation
A vector is a sequence of values of the same type. Vectors are the fundamental building block of R.

# Numeric vector: hemoglobin values for 5 subjects
hemoglobin <- c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2, 11.9, 13.1)
print(hemoglobin)

#> [1] 13.5 12.8 14.2 11.9 13.1

# [1] 13.5 12.8 14.2 11.9 13.1

# Character vector: treatment groups
treatment_groups <- c("Active", "Placebo", "Active", "Placebo", "Active")
print(treatment_groups)

#> [1] "Active"  "Placebo" "Active"  "Placebo" "Active"

# [1] "Active"  "Placebo" "Active"  "Placebo" "Active"

# Logical vector: abnormality flags
is_abnormal <- c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)
print(is_abnormal)

#> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE FALSE

# [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE FALSE

# Access individual elements using [index]
first_hemoglobin <- hemoglobin[1]
print(first_hemoglobin)

#> [1] 13.5

# [1] 13.5

# Access multiple elements
first_three <- hemoglobin[1:3]
print(first_three)

#> [1] 13.5 12.8 14.2

# [1] 13.5 12.8 14.2

# Conditional indexing: get values > 13
high_hemoglobin <- hemoglobin[hemoglobin > 13]
print(high_hemoglobin)

#> [1] 13.5 14.2 13.1

# [1] 13.5 14.2 13.1



2.2.2 Named Vectors
Named vectors associate each element with a meaningful label.

# Create a vector of baseline vital signs
vitals <- c(
  systolic_bp = 145,
  diastolic_bp = 92,
  heart_rate = 78,
  temperature = 36.8
)

print(vitals)

#>  systolic_bp diastolic_bp   heart_rate  temperature 
#>        145.0         92.0         78.0         36.8

# systolic_bp diastolic_bp   heart_rate temperature
#         145           92           78        36.8

# Access by name
hr <- vitals["heart_rate"]
print(hr)

#> heart_rate 
#>         78

# heart_rate
#         78

# In clinical data, named vectors represent:
# - Subject-level baseline measurements
# - Protocol-specified reference values
# - Adverse event counts by type



2.2.3 Matrices: Two-Dimensional Arrays
A matrix is a two-dimensional collection of values (all the same type).

# Create a matrix: 5 subjects, 3 lab measurements
lab_values <- matrix(
  c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2, 11.9, 13.1,  # Hemoglobin (g/dL)
    7.2, 6.1, 7.5, 5.8, 6.9,        # WBC (K/uL)
    95, 88, 102, 85, 98),           # Glucose (mg/dL)
  nrow = 5,
  ncol = 3,
  byrow = FALSE
)

# Add informative row and column names
rownames(lab_values) <- paste0("Subject_", 1:5)
colnames(lab_values) <- c("Hemoglobin", "WBC", "Glucose")

print(lab_values)

#>           Hemoglobin WBC Glucose
#> Subject_1       13.5 7.2      95
#> Subject_2       12.8 6.1      88
#> Subject_3       14.2 7.5     102
#> Subject_4       11.9 5.8      85
#> Subject_5       13.1 6.9      98

#         Hemoglobin WBC Glucose
# Subject_1       13.5 7.2      95
# Subject_2       12.8 6.1      88
# ...

# Access elements
hgb_subject_2 <- lab_values[2, "Hemoglobin"]
print(hgb_subject_2)

#> [1] 12.8

# [1] 12.8

# Extract a column
hemoglobin_values <- lab_values[, "Hemoglobin"]
print(hemoglobin_values)

#> Subject_1 Subject_2 Subject_3 Subject_4 Subject_5 
#>      13.5      12.8      14.2      11.9      13.1

# Subject_1 Subject_2 Subject_3 Subject_4 Subject_5
#      13.5      12.8      14.2      11.9      13.1







Matrices vs. Data Frames: Matrices are homogeneous (all elements same type) and are useful for mathematical operations. Data frames (next section) are heterogeneous (mixed types) and are the standard for clinical datasets. In practice, you’ll use data frames far more often than matrices.





2.2.4 Lists: Flexible Collections
A list can contain elements of different types and structures. Lists are extremely flexible.

# Create a list with diverse content
subject_data <- list(
  id = "001-101",
  age = 62,
  gender = "Female",
  baseline_labs = c(hemoglobin = 13.5, wbc = 7.2, glucose = 95),
  adverse_events = c("Nausea", "Headache"),
  completed_trial = TRUE
)

print(subject_data)

#> $id
#> [1] "001-101"
#> 
#> $age
#> [1] 62
#> 
#> $gender
#> [1] "Female"
#> 
#> $baseline_labs
#> hemoglobin        wbc    glucose 
#>       13.5        7.2       95.0 
#> 
#> $adverse_events
#> [1] "Nausea"   "Headache"
#> 
#> $completed_trial
#> [1] TRUE

# $id
# [1] "001-101"
#
# $age
# [1] 62
#
# $gender
# [1] "Female"
#
# $baseline_labs
# hemoglobin       wbc    glucose
#       13.5       7.2       95.0
#
# $adverse_events
# [1] "Nausea"   "Headache"
#
# $completed_trial
# [1] TRUE

# Access list elements using $ or [[]]
subject_id <- subject_data$id
print(subject_id)

#> [1] "001-101"

# [1] "001-101"

# Access by index
age <- subject_data[[2]]
print(age)

#> [1] 62

# [1] 62

# Lists are used for:
# - Storing mixed-type data for a single subject
# - Returning multiple outputs from a function
# - Nested data structures (list of lists)



2.2.5 Data Frames: The Standard for Tabular Data
A data frame is a list of vectors (columns) of equal length. This is the standard structure for clinical datasets.

# Create a data frame: 5 subjects with demographics and baseline labs
clinical_data <- data.frame(
  USUBJID = c("001-101", "001-102", "001-103", "001-104", "001-105"),
  AGE = c(62, 32, 39, 56, 31),
  SEX = factor(c("F", "M", "F", "F", "M"), levels = c("F", "M")),
  TREATMENT = factor(c("Active", "Placebo", "Active", "Placebo", "Active")),
  BASELINE_HGB = c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2, 11.9, 13.1),
  BASELINE_GLU = c(95, 88, 102, 85, 98)
)

print(clinical_data)

#>   USUBJID AGE SEX TREATMENT BASELINE_HGB BASELINE_GLU
#> 1 001-101  62   F    Active         13.5           95
#> 2 001-102  32   M   Placebo         12.8           88
#> 3 001-103  39   F    Active         14.2          102
#> 4 001-104  56   F   Placebo         11.9           85
#> 5 001-105  31   M    Active         13.1           98

#    USUBJID AGE SEX TREATMENT BASELINE_HGB BASELINE_GLU
# 1 001-101  62   F    Active         13.5           95
# 2 001-102  32   M   Placebo         12.8           88
# 3 001-103  39   F    Active         14.2          102
# 4 001-104  56   F   Placebo         11.9           85
# 5 001-105  31   M    Active         13.1           98

# Check dimensions
dim(clinical_data)

#> [1] 5 6

# [1] 5 6

# Access columns
age_vector <- clinical_data$AGE
print(age_vector)

#> [1] 62 32 39 56 31

# [1] 62 32 39 56 31

# Or by index
treatment_vector <- clinical_data[, 4]
print(treatment_vector)

#> [1] Active  Placebo Active  Placebo Active 
#> Levels: Active Placebo

# [1] Active  Placebo Active  Placebo Active
# Levels: Active Placebo

# Access rows
subject_102_data <- clinical_data[2, ]
print(subject_102_data)

#>   USUBJID AGE SEX TREATMENT BASELINE_HGB BASELINE_GLU
#> 2 001-102  32   M   Placebo         12.8           88

#    USUBJID AGE SEX TREATMENT BASELINE_HGB BASELINE_GLU
# 2 001-102  32   M   Placebo         12.8           88

# Access a specific cell
age_of_subject_103 <- clinical_data[3, "AGE"]
print(age_of_subject_103)

#> [1] 39

# [1] 39



2.2.6 Tibbles: The Modern Data Frame
Tibbles (from the {tibble} package, part of tidyverse) are enhanced data frames with better printing and behavior.

library(tibble)

# Create a tibble
clinical_tbl <- tibble(
  USUBJID = c("001-101", "001-102", "001-103", "001-104", "001-105"),
  AGE = c(62, 32, 39, 56, 31),
  SEX = factor(c("F", "M", "F", "F", "M")),
  TREATMENT = factor(c("Active", "Placebo", "Active", "Placebo", "Active")),
  BASELINE_HGB = c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2, 11.9, 13.1)
)

# Printing a tibble shows:
# - Column types (e.g., <chr>, <dbl>, <fct>)
# - Only as many rows as fit on screen
# - Only as many columns as fit on screen
print(clinical_tbl)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 5
#>   USUBJID   AGE SEX   TREATMENT BASELINE_HGB
#>   <chr>   <dbl> <fct> <fct>            <dbl>
#> 1 001-101    62 F     Active            13.5
#> 2 001-102    32 M     Placebo           12.8
#> 3 001-103    39 F     Active            14.2
#> 4 001-104    56 F     Placebo           11.9
#> 5 001-105    31 M     Active            13.1

# # A tibble: 5 × 5
#   USUBJID   AGE SEX   TREATMENT BASELINE_HGB
#   <chr>   <dbl> <fct> <fct>            <dbl>
# 1 001-101    62 F     Active            13.5
# 2 001-102    32 M     Placebo           12.8
# ...

# Tibbles are stricter about subsetting (which prevents accidental bugs)
# They're the default output of many tidyverse functions
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
text:
2.3 2.3 Operators: Arithmetic, Comparison, and Logical
Operators allow us to perform calculations, comparisons, and logical tests on data.

2.3.1 Arithmetic Operators

# Addition
total_dose <- 100 + 50
print(total_dose)

#> [1] 150

# [1] 150

# Subtraction
weight_change <- 85 - 82
print(weight_change)

#> [1] 3

# [1] 3

# Multiplication
total_dose <- 10 * 5  # 10 subjects, 5 mg each
print(total_dose)

#> [1] 50

# [1] 50

# Division
average_dose <- 50 / 5
print(average_dose)

#> [1] 10

# [1] 10

# Exponentiation
squared <- 5 ^ 2
print(squared)

#> [1] 25

# [1] 25

# Modulo (remainder)
remainder <- 17 %% 5
print(remainder)

#> [1] 2

# [1] 2

# Integer division
quotient <- 17 %/% 5
print(quotient)

#> [1] 3

# [1] 3

# These operators work on vectors element-wise
weights <- c(70, 85, 92, 78, 81)
weights_in_pounds <- weights * 2.20462
print(weights_in_pounds)

#> [1] 154.3234 187.3927 202.8250 171.9604 178.5742

# [1] 154.32 187.39 202.86 171.96 178.57



2.3.2 Comparison Operators
Comparison operators return logical (TRUE/FALSE) values. They’re essential for filtering clinical data.

# Equal to
glucose <- 125
is_equal_threshold <- glucose == 125
print(is_equal_threshold)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# Not equal to
is_hyperglycemic <- glucose != 100
print(is_hyperglycemic)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# Less than
is_low <- glucose < 70
print(is_low)

#> [1] FALSE

# [1] FALSE

# Greater than
is_elevated <- glucose > 100
print(is_elevated)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# Less than or equal to
is_acceptable <- glucose <= 140
print(is_acceptable)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# Greater than or equal to
is_high_normal <- glucose >= 100
print(is_high_normal)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# Clinical application: flag abnormal labs
hemoglobin_values <- c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2, 11.9, 13.1)
is_abnormal <- hemoglobin_values < 12 | hemoglobin_values > 14
print(is_abnormal)

#> [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE

# [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE



2.3.3 Logical Operators
Logical operators combine logical conditions.

# AND (&): Both conditions must be TRUE
age <- 45
glucose <- 125
is_middle_aged <- age > 40
is_hyperglycemic <- glucose > 100
is_target_population <- is_middle_aged & is_hyperglycemic
print(is_target_population)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# OR (|): At least one condition must be TRUE
has_hypertension <- FALSE
has_diabetes <- TRUE
requires_medication <- has_hypertension | has_diabetes
print(requires_medication)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE

# NOT (!): Inverts the condition
on_active_treatment <- TRUE
on_placebo <- !on_active_treatment
print(on_placebo)

#> [1] FALSE

# [1] FALSE

# Complex conditions: Clinical inclusion criteria
age <- 35
bmi <- 28
glucose <- 110

meets_criteria <- (age >= 18 & age <= 65) &
                  (bmi >= 20 & bmi < 40) &
                  (glucose >= 100 & glucose <= 200)

print(meets_criteria)

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
text:
2.4 2.4 Control Flow: Decisions and Loops

2.4.1 if/else Statements

# Simple if statement
hemoglobin <- 12.5

if (hemoglobin < 12) {
  status <- "Anemic"
} else if (hemoglobin < 13.5) {
  status <- "Low-normal"
} else {
  status <- "Normal"
}

print(status)

#> [1] "Low-normal"

# [1] "Low-normal"

# Clinical application: Flag abnormal lab values based on CTCAE criteria
wbc <- 2.5  # White blood cell count in K/uL

if (wbc >= 3) {
  grade <- 0
} else if (wbc >= 2) {
  grade <- 1
} else if (wbc >= 1) {
  grade <- 2
} else if (wbc > 0.5) {
  grade <- 3
} else {
  grade <- 4
}

print(paste("WBC Grade:", grade))

#> [1] "WBC Grade: 1"

# [1] "WBC Grade: 1"

# Vectorized if/else: ifelse() for entire vectors
hemoglobin_values <- c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2, 11.9, 13.1)
abnormality <- ifelse(hemoglobin_values < 12 | hemoglobin_values > 14,
                      "Abnormal", "Normal")
print(abnormality)

#> [1] "Normal"   "Normal"   "Abnormal" "Abnormal" "Normal"

# [1] "Normal"  "Normal"  "Abnormal" "Abnormal" "Normal"



2.4.2 for Loops
For loops iterate a fixed number of times, processing each element.

# Simple loop: Calculate BMI for each subject
heights_m <- c(1.75, 1.68, 1.82, 1.70, 1.65)  # meters
weights_kg <- c(70, 65, 82, 75, 60)

bmi_values <- numeric(length = 5)  # Create empty vector

for (i in 1:5) {
  bmi_values[i] <- weights_kg[i] / (heights_m[i] ^ 2)
}

print(bmi_values)

#> [1] 22.85714 23.03005 24.75546 25.95156 22.03857

# [1] 22.86 23.03 24.68 25.95 22.04

# Loop over a vector directly
subject_ids <- c("001-101", "001-102", "001-103")

for (id in subject_ids) {
  cat("Processing subject", id, "\n")
}

#> Processing subject 001-101 
#> Processing subject 001-102 
#> Processing subject 001-103

# Processing subject 001-101
# Processing subject 001-102
# Processing subject 001-103

# Clinical application: Process multiple domains
domains <- c("DM", "AE", "LB")

for (domain in domains) {
  filename <- paste0(domain, ".csv")
  cat("Would load:", filename, "\n")
}

#> Would load: DM.csv 
#> Would load: AE.csv 
#> Would load: LB.csv

# Would load: DM.csv
# Would load: AE.csv
# Would load: LB.csv







Vectorization > Loops: In R, vectorized operations are much faster than loops. When possible, use built-in functions that work on entire vectors rather than explicit loops. We’ll see this in detail in later chapters with dplyr.





2.4.3 while Loops
While loops continue as long as a condition is TRUE.

# Simple while loop
count <- 0
while (count < 5) {
  cat("Count:", count, "\n")
  count <- count + 1
}

#> Count: 0 
#> Count: 1 
#> Count: 2 
#> Count: 3 
#> Count: 4

# Count: 0
# Count: 1
# Count: 2
# Count: 3
# Count: 4

# Clinical application: Iterate until convergence
# (simplified example of iterative algorithm)
parameter <- 10
iteration <- 0
tolerance <- 0.01

while (abs(parameter - 9.5) > tolerance & iteration < 100) {
  parameter <- parameter - (parameter - 9.5) / 10
  iteration <- iteration + 1
}

cat("Converged to", parameter, "after", iteration, "iterations\n")

#> Converged to 9.509124 after 38 iterations

# Converged to 9.5 after 29 iterations
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
text:
2.5 2.5 Functions: Creating Reusable Code
Functions encapsulate code logic for reuse. In clinical research, functions standardize calculations across datasets.

2.5.1 Creating a Function

# Function to calculate BMI
calculate_bmi <- function(weight_kg, height_m) {
  # Input validation
  if (weight_kg <= 0 | height_m <= 0) {
    stop("Weight and height must be positive values")
  }

  # Calculate BMI
  bmi <- weight_kg / (height_m ^ 2)

  # Return the result
  return(bmi)
}

# Use the function
subject_1_bmi <- calculate_bmi(weight_kg = 70, height_m = 1.75)
print(subject_1_bmi)

#> [1] 22.85714

# [1] 22.86

# Function with multiple subjects
weights <- c(70, 65, 82, 75, 60)
heights <- c(1.75, 1.68, 1.82, 1.70, 1.65)
bmi_all <- mapply(calculate_bmi, weights, heights)
print(bmi_all)

#> [1] 22.85714 23.03005 24.75546 25.95156 22.03857

# [1] 22.86 23.03 24.68 25.95 22.04



2.5.2 Functions with Multiple Return Values

# Function to classify lab abnormality by CTCAE
classify_wbc_grade <- function(wbc_value) {
  # WBC reference: 3.0-11.0 K/uL
  # CTCAE grades: 0=normal, 1=mild, 2=moderate, 3=severe, 4=life-threatening

  if (wbc_value >= 3.0) {
    grade <- 0
    severity <- "Normal"
  } else if (wbc_value >= 2.0) {
    grade <- 1
    severity <- "Mild"
  } else if (wbc_value >= 1.0) {
    grade <- 2
    severity <- "Moderate"
  } else if (wbc_value > 0.5) {
    grade <- 3
    severity <- "Severe"
  } else {
    grade <- 4
    severity <- "Life-threatening"
  }

  # Return a list with multiple values
  return(list(
    grade = grade,
    severity = severity,
    value = wbc_value
  ))
}

# Use the function
result <- classify_wbc_grade(2.1)
print(result$grade)

#> [1] 1

# [1] 1
print(result$severity)

#> [1] "Mild"

# [1] "Mild"



2.5.3 Function Scope
Variables defined inside a function are local (exist only within that function). This prevents namespace pollution.

# Define a global variable
patient_age <- 50

calculate_dose <- function(weight_kg) {
  # Local variable: only exists within this function
  dose <- weight_kg * 10

  # Can access global variables
  cat("Patient age:", patient_age, "\n")

  return(dose)
}

dose_for_70kg <- calculate_dose(70)

#> Patient age: 50

print(dose_for_70kg)

#> [1] 700

# Patient age: 50
# [1] 700

# Local variable 'dose' doesn't exist outside the function
print(exists("dose"))

#> [1] FALSE

# [1] FALSE

# But function name and global variable still exist
print(exists("calculate_dose"))

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE
print(exists("patient_age"))

#> [1] TRUE

# [1] TRUE
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
text:
2.6 2.6 The apply Family: Efficient Iteration
The apply family of functions offers vectorized alternatives to loops.

# Create a sample clinical dataset (matrix form)
lab_matrix <- matrix(
  c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2, 11.9, 13.1,  # Hemoglobin
    7.2, 6.1, 7.5, 5.8, 6.9,        # WBC
    95, 88, 102, 85, 98),           # Glucose
  nrow = 5,
  ncol = 3,
  byrow = FALSE,
  dimnames = list(paste0("S", 1:5), c("HGB", "WBC", "GLU"))
)

# apply() on rows (margin = 1): Calculate mean for each subject
mean_per_subject <- apply(lab_matrix, 1, mean)
print(mean_per_subject)

#>       S1       S2       S3       S4       S5 
#> 38.56667 35.63333 41.23333 34.23333 39.33333

# S1        S2        S3        S4        S5
# 38.57     35.30     41.23     33.93     39.33

# apply() on columns (margin = 2): Calculate mean for each lab
mean_per_lab <- apply(lab_matrix, 2, mean)
print(mean_per_lab)

#>  HGB  WBC  GLU 
#> 13.1  6.7 93.6

# HGB       WBC       GLU
# 13.10     6.70      93.60

# lapply(): Apply function to list elements, returns a list
lab_list <- list(
  hemoglobin = c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2),
  wbc = c(7.2, 6.1, 7.5),
  glucose = c(95, 88, 102)
)

means_list <- lapply(lab_list, mean)
print(means_list)

#> $hemoglobin
#> [1] 13.5
#> 
#> $wbc
#> [1] 6.933333
#> 
#> $glucose
#> [1] 95

# $hemoglobin
# [1] 13.50
#
# $wbc
# [1] 6.93
#
# $glucose
# [1] 95

# sapply(): Simplify results to a vector
means_vector <- sapply(lab_list, mean)
print(means_vector)

#> hemoglobin        wbc    glucose 
#>  13.500000   6.933333  95.000000

# hemoglobin       wbc    glucose
#       13.50      6.93      95.00

2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
section: 2.7 2.7 Creating a Complete Clinical Patient Record
text:
2.7 2.7 Creating a Complete Clinical Patient Record
Let’s integrate everything: data types, structures, operators, and functions.

library(lubridate)

# Create a function to build a patient record
create_patient_record <- function(subject_id, age, sex, treatment,
                                  baseline_weight, baseline_height,
                                  baseline_glucose) {

  # Calculate baseline BMI
  baseline_bmi <- baseline_weight / (baseline_height ^ 2)

  # Determine gender code
  sex_code <- ifelse(sex == "Female", "F", "M")

  # Create a patient record (list)
  patient <- list(
    demographics = data.frame(
      USUBJID = subject_id,
      AGE = age,
      SEX = factor(sex_code, levels = c("F", "M")),
      TREATMENT = factor(treatment, levels = c("Placebo", "Active")),
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ),
    baseline_vitals = data.frame(
      WEIGHT_KG = baseline_weight,
      HEIGHT_M = baseline_height,
      BMI = baseline_bmi,
      GLUCOSE_MGDL = baseline_glucose
    ),
    status = list(
      enrolled = TRUE,
      enrollment_date = Sys.Date(),
      completed = NA
    )
  )

  return(patient)
}

# Create records for 3 subjects
subjects <- list()

subjects[[1]] <- create_patient_record(
  subject_id = "001-101",
  age = 62,
  sex = "Female",
  treatment = "Active",
  baseline_weight = 70,
  baseline_height = 1.65,
  baseline_glucose = 95
)

subjects[[2]] <- create_patient_record(
  subject_id = "001-102",
  age = 45,
  sex = "Male",
  treatment = "Placebo",
  baseline_weight = 85,
  baseline_height = 1.78,
  baseline_glucose = 102
)

subjects[[3]] <- create_patient_record(
  subject_id = "001-103",
  age = 55,
  sex = "Female",
  treatment = "Active",
  baseline_weight = 68,
  baseline_height = 1.62,
  baseline_glucose = 98
)

# Access data from the first subject
print(subjects[[1]]$demographics)

#>   USUBJID AGE SEX TREATMENT
#> 1 001-101  62   F    Active

#   USUBJID AGE SEX TREATMENT
# 1 001-101  62   F    Active

print(subjects[[1]]$baseline_vitals)

#>   WEIGHT_KG HEIGHT_M      BMI GLUCOSE_MGDL
#> 1        70     1.65 25.71166           95

#   WEIGHT_KG HEIGHT_M      BMI GLUCOSE_MGDL
# 1        70     1.65 25.67761            95

# Extract all treatment assignments
treatments <- sapply(subjects, function(x) x$demographics$TREATMENT)
print(treatments)

#> [1] Active  Placebo Active 
#> Levels: Placebo Active

# [1] Active  Placebo Active

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
section: 2.8 2.8 Best Practices for Clinical R Code
text:
2.8 2.8 Best Practices for Clinical R Code

Use meaningful variable names: baseline_hemoglobin, not x or hgb1
Validate inputs: Check that ages are positive, weights are reasonable
Use factors for categorical data: Treatment, gender, adverse event grades
Handle missing data explicitly: is.na() before calculations
Comment your code: Especially CDISC variable names and clinical interpretations
Use functions for repeated operations: Standardizes calculations across datasets
Test your code: Run on known examples before production use
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures
text:
2.9 Exercises

2.9.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 2.1: Working with Data Types
Create the following variables and verify their types: - A numeric vector of five systolic blood pressure values (e.g., 145, 152, 138, 148, 141) - A character vector of subject IDs (e.g., “001-101”, “001-102”, etc.) - A logical vector indicating whether each subject is on active treatment (TRUE/FALSE) - A factor representing adverse event severity (Mild, Moderate, Severe)
Use class() to verify each variable’s type.
Exercise 2.2: Creating and Manipulating a Clinical Data Frame
Create a data frame with 10 subjects containing: - USUBJID: Subject IDs from “001-101” to “001-110” - AGE: Ages ranging from 30 to 70 - WEIGHT_KG: Weights from 50 to 95 kg - TREATMENT: Randomly assign “Active” or “Placebo” (Hint: use sample())
Calculate: a) The mean age of all subjects b) The mean weight of subjects on active treatment c) How many subjects are over 50 years old
Exercise 2.3: Using Logical Operators
Using the data frame from Exercise 2.2: - Create a logical vector indicating subjects who are BOTH over 50 years old AND on active treatment - Create a logical vector indicating subjects who are EITHER over 65 years old OR weigh more than 85 kg - Count how many subjects meet each criterion


2.9.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 2.4: Creating a Clinical Utility Function
Write a function classify_bmi() that: - Takes weight (kg) and height (m) as inputs - Calculates BMI - Classifies BMI as: “Underweight” (< 18.5), “Normal” (18.5–24.9), “Overweight” (25–29.9), or “Obese” (≥ 30) - Returns a named list with bmi value and classification
Test the function with at least 5 different weight/height combinations.
Exercise 2.5: Simulating Trial Enrollment
Create a function simulate_trial_enrollment() that: - Generates a cohort of 100 subjects with random age (18–75), weight (50–110 kg), and height (1.50–1.95 m) - Randomly assigns treatment (Active/Placebo, 1:1 ratio) - Calculates BMI for all subjects - Returns a data frame with USUBJID, AGE, TREATMENT, WEIGHT_KG, HEIGHT_M, BMI - Provides summary statistics (mean age, mean BMI by treatment, etc.)
