2  R Fundamentals and Data Structures

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.1 3.1 Understanding Clinical Data File Formats
Clinical data arrives in multiple formats, each with specific advantages and requirements. Understanding these formats and how to work with them is essential for any clinical analyst.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.1.1 CSV: The Universal Format
CSV (Comma-Separated Values) is plain text: simple, portable, and human-readable. Most clinical data systems can export to CSV.
USUBJID,AGE,SEX,TREATMENT,BASELINE_HGB
001-101,62,F,Active,13.5
001-102,45,M,Placebo,12.8
001-103,55,F,Active,14.2
Advantages: - Platform-independent (works on Windows, Mac, Linux) - Readable by any text editor - Easy to version control with Git - Small file sizes
Disadvantages: - Loses data types (everything is text initially) - No support for multiple sheets - Comma conflicts in text fields can cause parsing issues


3.1.2 Excel: The Industry Standard
Excel files (.xlsx, .xls) are ubiquitous in clinical research. While not ideal for reproducible analysis, they’re often unavoidable.
├─ Sheet1: Demographics
│  ├─ USUBJID
│  ├─ AGE
│  ├─ SEX
│  ├─ TREATMENT
│  └─ BASELINE_HGB
├─ Sheet2: Lab Results
└─ Sheet3: Adverse Events
Advantages: - Multiple sheets in one file - Preserves some formatting - Familiar to non-programmers - Can include formulas (though importing them is problematic)
Disadvantages: - Proprietary format (XML-based, but complex) - Formula preservation issues - Formatting can create parsing problems - Less transparent than plain text


3.1.3 SAS Formats: The Regulatory Standard
SAS data files (.sas7bdat) are the pharmaceutical industry standard. If submitting to the FDA, you’ll likely encounter them.
SDTM domains are typically provided in SAS format: - dm.sas7bdat (Demographics) - ae.sas7bdat (Adverse Events) - lb.sas7bdat (Laboratory) - vs.sas7bdat (Vital Signs)


3.1.4 SAS Transport (XPT) Format
XPT files (.xpt) are the FDA-mandated format for data submissions. They’re text-based and transportable across systems, but compressed.






XPT Format in Regulatory Submissions: The FDA requires clinical trial data in XPT (SAS Transport format) v5, which has specific formatting requirements: - Variable names limited to 8 characters - No special characters - Specific metadata structure - Can be read and written by R but requires care
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.2 3.2 Reading CSV Files

3.2.1 Using Base R: read.csv()

# Read a CSV file
dm_data <- read.csv("dm.csv")

# Display the first few rows
head(dm_data)

# Check structure
str(dm_data)

# Common parameters:
# - sep: Field separator (default ",", can be "\t" for tab, "|" for pipe)
# - header: TRUE if first row contains column names (default TRUE)
# - na.strings: How missing values are represented (default "NA")
# - stringsAsFactors: Should character columns be factors? (default FALSE in modern R)

# Example: Tab-separated file with missing values as empty strings
vs_data <- read.csv("vs.txt",
                    sep = "\t",
                    na.strings = "")

# Example: Pipe-separated file with comments
cm_data <- read.csv("cm.txt",
                    sep = "|",
                    comment.char = "#")



3.2.2 Using readr: read_csv()
The {readr} package (part of tidyverse) provides faster, more robust CSV reading.

library(readr)

# Read with readr (returns a tibble)
dm_data <- read_csv("dm.csv")

# readr automatically detects column types
# and shows a message like:
# ── Column specification ────────────────────────────
# cols(
#   USUBJID = col_character(),
#   AGE = col_double(),
#   SEX = col_character(),
#   TREATMENT = col_character(),
#   BASELINE_HGB = col_double()
# )

# Explicit column specification
ae_data <- read_csv(
  "ae.csv",
  col_types = cols(
    USUBJID = col_character(),
    AETERM = col_character(),
    AESEV = col_factor(levels = c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe")),
    AEREL = col_factor(levels = c("Unrelated", "Remote", "Possible", "Probable", "Definite")),
    AESTDTC = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d")
  )
)

# Skip the first few rows if they contain metadata
lb_data <- read_csv("lb.csv", skip = 3)

# Read only specific columns
dm_subset <- read_csv("dm.csv",
                      col_select = c("USUBJID", "AGE", "SEX"))

# Handle problematic files
# Show problems encountered during parsing
problems(dm_data)



3.2.3 Practical CSV Reading Workflow

library(readr)

# Step 1: Read with automatic detection
raw_data <- read_csv("clinical_data.csv")

# Step 2: Check what was parsed
str(raw_data)

# Step 3: Review the first few rows
head(raw_data, n = 10)

# Step 4: Check for parsing problems
if (nrow(problems(raw_data)) > 0) {
  print("Parsing problems detected:")
  print(problems(raw_data))
  # Re-read with corrected col_types if needed
  raw_data <- read_csv("clinical_data.csv",
                       col_types = cols(
                         # Specify corrections here
                       ))
}

# Step 5: Verify structure matches expectations
expected_cols <- c("USUBJID", "AGE", "SEX", "TREATMENT", "BASELINE_HGB")
missing_cols <- setdiff(expected_cols, names(raw_data))
if (length(missing_cols) > 0) {
  warning(paste("Missing columns:", paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", ")))
}

3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.3 3.3 Reading Excel Files

3.3.1 Using readxl: read_excel()
The {readxl} package provides robust Excel reading without external dependencies.

library(readxl)

# Read a single sheet (first sheet by default)
dm_data <- read_excel("clinical_data.xlsx")

# Read a specific sheet by name
ae_data <- read_excel("clinical_data.xlsx", sheet = "Adverse_Events")

# Read a specific sheet by number
lb_data <- read_excel("clinical_data.xlsx", sheet = 3)

# See all sheet names
sheet_names <- excel_sheets("clinical_data.xlsx")
print(sheet_names)
# [1] "Demographics" "Adverse_Events" "Laboratory" "Vital_Signs"

# Read with specific column types
vs_data <- read_excel(
  "clinical_data.xlsx",
  sheet = "Vital_Signs",
  col_types = c("text", "numeric", "numeric", "numeric", "date")
)

# Skip rows (e.g., if there's a title row)
dm_data <- read_excel("clinical_data.xlsx",
                      skip = 2)

# Read only specific rows and columns
ae_subset <- read_excel("clinical_data.xlsx",
                        sheet = "Adverse_Events",
                        range = "A1:D100")

# Specify missing value representations
cm_data <- read_excel("clinical_data.xlsx",
                      sheet = "Concomitant_Meds",
                      na = c("", "NA", "N/A", "."))



3.3.2 Reading Multiple Sheets at Once
Often clinical Excel files contain multiple domains. Reading them all into a list is efficient:

library(readxl)
library(purrr)

# Get sheet names from Excel file
sheets <- excel_sheets("clinical_trial.xlsx")

# Read all sheets into a list
all_data <- map(sheets, ~read_excel("clinical_trial.xlsx", sheet = .x))

# Name the list elements
names(all_data) <- sheets

# Access specific domains
dm <- all_data$Demographics
ae <- all_data$`Adverse_Events`
lb <- all_data$Laboratory

# Or using the $ operator directly
dm <- all_data[["Demographics"]]

3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.4 3.4 Reading SAS Data Files

3.4.1 Using haven: read_sas()
The {haven} package reads SAS, SPSS, and Stata files. It preserves SAS metadata like variable labels and formats.

library(haven)

# Read a SAS dataset
dm_data <- read_sas("dm.sas7bdat")

# SAS labels are preserved as "label" attribute
attributes(dm_data$AGE)$label
# [1] "Age (years)"

# View labels with a helper function
label_list <- sapply(dm_data, function(x) attr(x, "label"))
print(label_list)
# USUBJID: "Unique Subject ID"
# AGE: "Age (years)"
# SEX: "Sex"
# ...

# Use multiple SAS files from same study
dm <- read_sas("sdtm/dm.sas7bdat")
ae <- read_sas("sdtm/ae.sas7bdat")
lb <- read_sas("sdtm/lb.sas7bdat")
vs <- read_sas("sdtm/vs.sas7bdat")
ex <- read_sas("sdtm/ex.sas7bdat")



3.4.2 Understanding SAS Variable Formats
SAS uses “formats” to control how values are displayed. Haven preserves these in attributes.

library(haven)

# Read SAS data
dm <- read_sas("dm.sas7bdat")

# Check format information
attr(dm$SEX, "format.sas")
# [1] "$SEX."

# SAS value labels (stored as attributes)
attr(dm$SEX, "labels")
# F       M
# Female Male

# When converting to R factor, use these labels
dm$SEX <- factor(dm$SEX,
                 labels = attr(dm$SEX, "labels"))

# The as_factor() function from haven does this automatically
dm$SEX <- as_factor(dm$SEX)

# Check the result
print(dm$SEX)
# [1] Female Male Female Male Male ...
# Levels: Female Male



3.4.3 Practical SAS Import Workflow

library(haven)
library(tidyverse)

# Function to import and standardize SDTM domains
import_sdtm <- function(domain_code, folder = "sdtm") {
  filename <- paste0(folder, "/", tolower(domain_code), ".sas7bdat")

  if (!file.exists(filename)) {
    stop(paste("File not found:", filename))
  }

  # Read the SAS file
  data <- read_sas(filename)

  # Convert SAS variable labels to a comment
  var_labels <- sapply(data, function(x) attr(x, "label"))
  attr(data, "variable_labels") <- var_labels

  # Convert factors with SAS formats
  data <- data %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.character), ~as_factor(.) %||% .))

  return(data)
}

# Load all SDTM domains
dm <- import_sdtm("DM")
ae <- import_sdtm("AE")
lb <- import_sdtm("LB")
vs <- import_sdtm("VS")
ex <- import_sdtm("EX")

# Verify structure
str(dm)

3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.5 3.5 Reading SAS Transport (XPT) Files

3.5.1 Using haven: read_xpt()
XPT (SAS Transport v5) is the FDA-mandated format for data submission.

library(haven)

# Read an XPT file
dm <- read_xpt("dm.xpt")

# XPT files follow CDISC standards strictly
# Check column names (limited to 8 characters in SDTM)
names(dm)
# [1] "STUDYID" "DOMAIN" "USUBJID" "SUBJID" "RFSTDTC" ...

# Read multiple XPT files
xpt_files <- c("dm.xpt", "ae.xpt", "lb.xpt", "vs.xpt")

domains <- lapply(xpt_files, read_xpt)
names(domains) <- c("DM", "AE", "LB", "VS")

# Access individual domains
dm <- domains$DM
ae <- domains$AE







XPT File Validation: XPT files from FDA submissions must be valid and compliant. R can read them, but when writing XPT files, use specialized tools like {xportr} from the pharmaverse for full compliance.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.6 3.6 Reading Data from Pharmaversesdtm Package
The {pharmaversesdtm} package provides simulated clinical data in SDTM format, perfect for learning and testing.

3.6.1 Loading Datasets

library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(tidyverse)

# See available datasets
data(package = "pharmaversesdtm")

# Load specific domains
data("dm")   # Demographics
data("ae")   # Adverse Events
data("lb")   # Laboratory
data("vs")   # Vital Signs
data("ex")   # Exposure
data("cm")   # Concomitant Medications
data("ds")   # Disposition

# These are immediately available
head(dm)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 28
#>   STUDYID      DOMAIN USUBJID   SUBJID RFSTDTC RFENDTC RFXSTDTC RFXENDTC RFICDTC
#>   <chr>        <chr>  <chr>     <chr>  <chr>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  
#> 1 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1015   2014-0… 2014-0… 2014-01… 2014-07… <NA>   
#> 2 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1023   2012-0… 2012-0… 2012-08… 2012-09… <NA>   
#> 3 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1028   2013-0… 2014-0… 2013-07… 2014-01… <NA>   
#> 4 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1033   2014-0… 2014-0… 2014-03… 2014-03… <NA>   
#> 5 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1034   2014-0… 2014-1… 2014-07… 2014-12… <NA>   
#> 6 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1047   2013-0… 2013-0… 2013-02… 2013-03… <NA>   
#> # ℹ 19 more variables: RFPENDTC <chr>, DTHDTC <chr>, DTHFL <chr>, SITEID <chr>,
#> #   BRTHDTC <chr>, AGE <dbl>, AGEU <chr>, SEX <chr>, RACE <chr>, ETHNIC <chr>,
#> #   ARMCD <chr>, ARM <chr>, ACTARMCD <chr>, ACTARM <chr>, COUNTRY <chr>,
#> #   DMDTC <chr>, DMDY <dbl>, ARMNRS <chr>, ACTARMUD <chr>

head(ae)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 35
#>   STUDYID  DOMAIN USUBJID AESEQ AESPID AETERM AELLT AELLTCD AEDECOD AEPTCD AEHLT
#>   <chr>    <chr>  <chr>   <dbl> <chr>  <chr>  <chr>   <dbl> <chr>    <dbl> <chr>
#> 1 CDISCPI… AE     01-701…     1 E07    APPLI… APPL…      NA APPLIC…     NA HLT_…
#> 2 CDISCPI… AE     01-701…     2 E08    APPLI… APPL…      NA APPLIC…     NA HLT_…
#> 3 CDISCPI… AE     01-701…     3 E06    DIARR… DIAR…      NA DIARRH…     NA HLT_…
#> 4 CDISCPI… AE     01-701…     3 E10    ATRIO… AV B…      NA ATRIOV…     NA HLT_…
#> 5 CDISCPI… AE     01-701…     1 E08    ERYTH… ERYT…      NA ERYTHE…     NA HLT_…
#> 6 CDISCPI… AE     01-701…     2 E09    ERYTH… LOCA…      NA ERYTHE…     NA HLT_…
#> # ℹ 24 more variables: AEHLTCD <dbl>, AEHLGT <chr>, AEHLGTCD <dbl>,
#> #   AEBODSYS <chr>, AEBDSYCD <dbl>, AESOC <chr>, AESOCCD <dbl>, AESEV <chr>,
#> #   AESER <chr>, AEACN <chr>, AEREL <chr>, AEOUT <chr>, AESCAN <chr>,
#> #   AESCONG <chr>, AESDISAB <chr>, AESDTH <chr>, AESHOSP <chr>, AESLIFE <chr>,
#> #   AESOD <chr>, AEDTC <chr>, AESTDTC <chr>, AEENDTC <chr>, AESTDY <dbl>,
#> #   AEENDY <dbl>

# Check the structure of each domain
str(lb)

#> tibble [59,580 × 23] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
#>  $ STUDYID : chr [1:59580] "CDISCPILOT01" "CDISCPILOT01" "CDISCPILOT01" "CDISCPILOT01" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Study Identifier"
#>  $ DOMAIN  : chr [1:59580] "LB" "LB" "LB" "LB" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Domain Abbreviation"
#>  $ USUBJID : chr [1:59580] "01-701-1015" "01-701-1015" "01-701-1015" "01-701-1015" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Unique Subject Identifier"
#>  $ LBSEQ   : num [1:59580] 1 39 74 104 134 164 199 229 259 294 ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Sequence Number"
#>  $ LBTESTCD: chr [1:59580] "ALB" "ALB" "ALB" "ALB" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Lab Test or Examination Short Name"
#>  $ LBTEST  : chr [1:59580] "Albumin" "Albumin" "Albumin" "Albumin" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Lab Test or Examination Name"
#>  $ LBCAT   : chr [1:59580] "CHEMISTRY" "CHEMISTRY" "CHEMISTRY" "CHEMISTRY" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Category for Lab Test"
#>  $ LBORRES : chr [1:59580] "3.8" "3.9" "3.8" "3.7" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Result or Finding in Original Units"
#>  $ LBORRESU: chr [1:59580] "g/dL" "g/dL" "g/dL" "g/dL" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Original Units"
#>  $ LBORNRLO: chr [1:59580] "3.3" "3.3" "3.3" "3.3" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Reference Range Lower Limit in Orig Unit"
#>  $ LBORNRHI: chr [1:59580] "4.9" "4.9" "4.9" "4.9" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Reference Range Upper Limit in Orig Unit"
#>  $ LBSTRESC: chr [1:59580] "38" "39" "38" "37" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Character Result/Finding in Std Format"
#>  $ LBSTRESN: num [1:59580] 38 39 38 37 38 38 37 37 38 38 ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ LBSTRESU: chr [1:59580] "g/L" "g/L" "g/L" "g/L" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Standard Units"
#>  $ LBSTNRLO: num [1:59580] 33 33 33 33 33 33 33 33 33 33 ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Reference Range Lower Limit-Std Units"

3.6.2 Understanding Pharmaversesdtm Structure

library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(dplyr)

# Load a domain
data("lb")

# Examine structure
glimpse(lb)

#> Rows: 59,580
#> Columns: 23
#> $ STUDYID  <chr> "CDISCPILOT01", "CDISCPILOT01", "CDISCPILOT01", "CDISCPILOT01…

#> $ LBTESTCD <chr> "ALB", "ALB", "ALB", "ALB", "ALB", "ALB", "ALB", "ALB", "ALB"…

#> $ LBCAT    <chr> "CHEMISTRY", "CHEMISTRY", "CHEMISTRY", "CHEMISTRY", "CHEMISTR…

#> $ LBSTRESU <chr> "g/L", "g/L", "g/L", "g/L", "g/L", "g/L", "g/L", "g/L", "g/L"…
#> $ LBSTNRLO <dbl> 33, 33, 33, 33, 33, 33, 33, 33, 33, 33, 35, 35, 35, 35, 35, 3…
#> $ LBSTNRHI <dbl> 49, 49, 49, 49, 49, 49, 49, 49, 49, 49, 115, 115, 115, 115, 1…

# Get a summary of the LB (Laboratory) domain
lb %>%
  summarise(
    n_records = n(),
    n_subjects = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    n_test_types = n_distinct(LBTEST),
    n_visits = n_distinct(VISIT),
    date_range = paste(min(LBDTC), "to", max(LBDTC))
  )

#> # A tibble: 1 × 5
#>   n_records n_subjects n_test_types n_visits date_range                         
#>       <int>      <int>        <int>    <int> <chr>

# View unique lab test types

  distinct(LBTEST, LBCAT) %>%
  arrange(LBCAT, LBTEST)

print(unique_tests)

#> # A tibble: 47 × 2
#>    LBTEST                     LBCAT    
#>    <chr>                      <chr>    
#>  1 Alanine Aminotransferase   CHEMISTRY
#>  2 Albumin                    CHEMISTRY
#>  3 Alkaline Phosphatase       CHEMISTRY
#>  4 Aspartate Aminotransferase CHEMISTRY
#>  5 Bilirubin                  CHEMISTRY
#>  6 Blood Urea Nitrogen        CHEMISTRY

#>  8 Chloride                   CHEMISTRY
#>  9 Cholesterol                CHEMISTRY
#> 10 Creatine Kinase            CHEMISTRY

3.6.3 Using Pharmaversesdtm for ADaM Development
The pharmaversesdtm data can be used directly with the {admiral} package to create analysis datasets:

library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(tidyverse)

# Load SDTM domains
data("dm")
data("lb")

# Create a simple ADLB (laboratory analysis dataset)
# using admiral functions

adlb <- lb %>%
  # Join with demographics for subject-level variables
  left_join(dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM, ACTARMCD),
            by = "USUBJID") %>%
  # Calculate change from baseline
  group_by(USUBJID, LBTEST) %>%
  arrange(LBDTC) %>%
  mutate(
    LBBLFL = if_else(row_number() == 1, "Y", "N"),
    BASELINE = if_else(LBBLFL == "Y", LBSTRESN, NA_real_)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  fill(BASELINE, .direction = "down") %>%
  mutate(
    CHG = if_else(!is.na(BASELINE), LBSTRESN - BASELINE, NA_real_)
  ) %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBTEST, LBDTC, LBSTRESN, BASELINE, CHG, ARM, everything())

head(adlb)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 27
#>   USUBJID     LBTEST    LBDTC LBSTRESN BASELINE   CHG ARM   STUDYID DOMAIN LBSEQ

#> 1 01-716-1024 Albumin   2012…    41       41        0 Plac… CDISCP… LB         1
#> 2 01-716-1024 Alkaline… 2012…    68       68        0 Plac… CDISCP… LB         2
#> 3 01-716-1024 Alanine … 2012…    17       17        0 Plac… CDISCP… LB         3
#> 4 01-716-1024 Aspartat… 2012…    26       26        0 Plac… CDISCP… LB         4
#> 5 01-716-1024 Basophils 2012…     0.03     0.03     0 Plac… CDISCP… LB         5
#> 6 01-716-1024 Bilirubin 2012…    12.0     12.0      0 Plac… CDISCP… LB         6
#> # ℹ 17 more variables: LBTESTCD <chr>, LBCAT <chr>, LBORRES <chr>,
#> #   LBORRESU <chr>, LBORNRLO <chr>, LBORNRHI <chr>, LBSTRESC <chr>,
#> #   LBSTRESU <chr>, LBSTNRLO <dbl>, LBSTNRHI <dbl>, LBNRIND <chr>,
#> #   LBBLFL <chr>, VISITNUM <dbl>, VISIT <chr>, VISITDY <dbl>, LBDY <dbl>,
#> #   ACTARMCD <chr>

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.7 3.7 Writing Data Files

3.7.1 Exporting to CSV

# Write to CSV with base R
write.csv(dm_data, file = "dm_output.csv", row.names = FALSE)

# Write to CSV with readr (recommended)
library(readr)
write_csv(dm_data, file = "dm_output.csv")

# Write with custom separator
write.table(vs_data, file = "vs_output.txt",
            sep = "\t",
            row.names = FALSE)

# Write with custom NA representation
write_csv(ae_data, file = "ae_output.csv", na = "")

# Write specific columns
write_csv(dm_data %>% select(USUBJID, AGE, SEX),
          file = "dm_subset.csv")



3.7.2 Exporting to Excel

library(writexl)

# Write a single sheet
write_xlsx(dm_data, path = "output.xlsx")

# Write multiple sheets
write_xlsx(
  list(
    Demographics = dm_data,
    `Adverse Events` = ae_data,
    Laboratory = lb_data,
    `Vital Signs` = vs_data
  ),
  path = "clinical_data.xlsx"
)

# Note: writexl does NOT support formatting or formulas
# For complex Excel workbooks, consider openxlsx package



3.7.3 Exporting to SAS Format

library(haven)

# Write to SAS dataset (.sas7bdat)
write_sas(dm_data, path = "dm_output.sas7bdat")

# Add variable labels before writing
dm_data <- dm_data %>%
  set_variable_labels(
    USUBJID = "Unique Subject ID",
    AGE = "Age (years)",
    SEX = "Sex",
    TREATMENT = "Assigned Treatment"
  )

write_sas(dm_data, path = "dm_labeled.sas7bdat")



3.7.4 Exporting to XPT (SAS Transport)






FDA XPT Compliance: Writing XPT files for FDA submission requires strict adherence to standards. Use the {xportr} package from pharmaverse for full compliance, including: - 8-character variable name truncation - Proper variable ordering - Standard metadata - Label truncation to 40 characters




library(haven)

# Basic XPT write (careful with CDISC compliance)
write_xpt(dm_data, path = "dm.xpt")

# For FDA submissions, use xportr from pharmaverse
# install.packages("remotes")
# remotes::install_github("pharmaverse/xportr")

library(xportr)

# Create a specification of required variables
var_spec <- tibble(
  Variable = c("USUBJID", "AGE", "SEX"),
  Type = c("text", "numeric", "text"),
  `Variable Label` = c("Unique Subject ID", "Age (years)", "Sex"),
  Length = c(8, 8, 2)
)

# Write with full compliance checking
dm_xpt <- dm_data %>%
  xportr_type(var_spec) %>%
  xportr_label(var_spec) %>%
  xportr_order(var_spec)

write_xpt(dm_xpt, path = "dm.xpt")

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
text:
3.8 3.8 Handling Large Clinical Datasets

3.8.1 Data Size Considerations
Clinical datasets can be large: - A single SDTM LB (laboratory) domain from a 1000-subject trial with 50 labs per subject per visit across 10 visits = 500,000 rows - Full text files with imaging data can be gigabytes


3.8.2 Using data.table for Performance
For very large datasets, {data.table} is faster than {dplyr}:

library(data.table)

# Read large CSV with data.table
# Much faster than read.csv() or read_csv()
lb_large <- fread("lb_large.csv")

# Efficient subsetting
# Filter to specific lab tests
hematology <- lb_large[LBCAT == "HEMATOLOGY"]

# Group by calculations
summary_by_test <- lb_large[, .(
  n = .N,
  mean_value = mean(LBSTRESN, na.rm = TRUE),
  sd_value = sd(LBSTRESN, na.rm = TRUE)
), by = LBTEST]

print(summary_by_test)

# Write efficiently
fwrite(lb_large, file = "lb_output.csv")



3.8.3 Chunked Reading for Memory Efficiency

library(readr)

# Read a large file in chunks
process_large_file <- function(filename, chunk_size = 10000) {
  # Create a connection
  f <- file(filename, "r")

  # Read chunks
  chunk_num <- 0
  while (TRUE) {
    # Read chunk_size lines
    chunk <- read_csv(f, n_max = chunk_size, progress = FALSE)

    if (nrow(chunk) == 0) break  # End of file

    chunk_num <- chunk_num + 1
    cat("Processing chunk", chunk_num, "with", nrow(chunk), "rows\n")

    # Process this chunk (example: write to output)
    # your_analysis_function(chunk)
  }

  close(f)
}

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
section: 3.9 3.9 Complete Data Import Workflow for a Clinical Trial
text:
3.9 3.9 Complete Data Import Workflow for a Clinical Trial
Let’s integrate everything into a realistic clinical data workflow:

library(readr)
library(haven)
library(tidyverse)

# Define study parameters
study_id <- "EUSTC1"
data_folder <- "./data/raw"

# Function to load and validate SDTM domains
load_sdtm_domains <- function(study_id, data_folder) {

  # Define expected domains
  domains <- list(
    DM = "Demographics",
    AE = "Adverse Events",
    LB = "Laboratory",
    VS = "Vital Signs",
    EX = "Exposure"
  )

  # Load each domain
  sdtm <- list()

  for (domain_code in names(domains)) {
    # Try reading as SAS first
    sas_file <- file.path(data_folder, paste0(tolower(domain_code), ".sas7bdat"))

    if (file.exists(sas_file)) {
      cat("Loading", domain_code, "from SAS...\n")
      sdtm[[domain_code]] <- read_sas(sas_file)
    } else {
      # Try CSV as fallback
      csv_file <- file.path(data_folder, paste0(tolower(domain_code), ".csv"))
      if (file.exists(csv_file)) {
        cat("Loading", domain_code, "from CSV...\n")
        sdtm[[domain_code]] <- read_csv(csv_file)
      } else {
        warning(paste("Could not find data for domain", domain_code))
      }
    }
  }

  return(sdtm)
}

# Load the data
sdtm_data <- load_sdtm_domains(study_id, data_folder)

# Extract individual domains
dm <- sdtm_data$DM
ae <- sdtm_data$AE
lb <- sdtm_data$LB
vs <- sdtm_data$VS
ex <- sdtm_data$EX

# Perform validation checks
cat("=== Data Quality Summary ===\n")
cat("DM (Demographics):", nrow(dm), "records for", n_distinct(dm$USUBJID), "subjects\n")
cat("AE (Adverse Events):", nrow(ae), "records\n")
cat("LB (Laboratory):", nrow(lb), "records\n")
cat("VS (Vital Signs):", nrow(vs), "records\n")
cat("EX (Exposure):", nrow(ex), "records\n")

# Check for missing data
cat("\n=== Missing Data Check ===\n")
missing_by_domain <- list(
  DM = colSums(is.na(dm)),
  AE = colSums(is.na(ae)),
  LB = colSums(is.na(lb))
)

print(missing_by_domain)

# Export cleaned data in multiple formats
output_folder <- "./data/processed"
dir.create(output_folder, showWarnings = FALSE)

# Export to CSV (for transparency)
write_csv(dm, file.path(output_folder, "dm.csv"))
write_csv(ae, file.path(output_folder, "ae.csv"))
write_csv(lb, file.path(output_folder, "lb.csv"))

# Export to XPT (for regulatory submission)
write_xpt(dm, file.path(output_folder, "dm.xpt"))
write_xpt(ae, file.path(output_folder, "ae.xpt"))
write_xpt(lb, file.path(output_folder, "lb.xpt"))

cat("\nData import and export complete.\n")
cat("Output saved to:", output_folder, "\n")

title: 3  Data Import and Export in Clinical Research
section: 3.10 3.10 Best Practices for Clinical Data Import/Export
text:
3.10 3.10 Best Practices for Clinical Data Import/Export

Always validate after import: Check dimensions, data types, missing values
Use consistent naming: Follow CDISC conventions (8-character variable names for submission)
Preserve metadata: Keep variable labels and formats when possible
Version your raw data: Never overwrite original files; keep dated backups
Document transformations: Record which files were loaded, when, and any transformations applied
Use reproducible scripts: Write R scripts that can reload data identically
Test on sample data first: Before processing 500,000 rows, test on a subset
Check for encoding issues: Clinical data may come from different locales; watch for encoding problems
Validate against source systems: Compare record counts and key statistics with the source system
Archive processed data: Keep exported datasets for audit trails and reproducibility

text:
3.11 Exercises

3.11.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 3.1: Reading and Comparing File Formats
Using the {pharmaversesdtm} package: a) Load the dm (Demographics) domain b) Export it to three formats: CSV, Excel, and SAS (.sas7bdat) c) Read each file back into R d) Verify that the dimensions are identical for all three formats e) Check if any data types have changed
Exercise 3.2: Handling Missing Data During Import
Create a CSV file with intentional missing values:
USUBJID,AGE,SEX,TREATMENT,BASELINE_HGB
001-101,62,F,Active,13.5
001-102,,M,Placebo,12.8
001-103,55,F,,14.2
001-104,56,,Active,

Read this file using read_csv() with default settings
Identify which cells have missing values
Re-read the file specifying that missing values should also be represented as “.”
Check how the data has changed

Exercise 3.3: Multi-Sheet Excel Import
Create an Excel file with three sheets (DM, AE, LB) containing sample clinical data: a) Read all three sheets into a single list b) Access each sheet individually from the list c) Verify that column names are identical across sheets where expected d) Export the list to three separate CSV files


3.11.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 3.4: Batch Import of SDTM Domains
Write a function import_all_sdtm_domains() that: a) Takes a folder path as input b) Automatically detects all SAS files in that folder c) Reads each file using the domain code from the filename (e.g., dm.sas7bdat → DM) d) Returns a named list of all domains found e) Provides a summary report: number of subjects per domain, record counts, date ranges
Exercise 3.5: Data Export Pipeline
Using data from pharmaversesdtm: a) Load the DM and LB domains b) Create a merged analysis dataset combining demographics with baseline lab values c) Export to four formats: CSV, Excel (single file, multiple sheets), SAS, and XPT d) Verify that all files can be read back correctly e) Document (in code comments) what information is preserved/lost in each format

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.1 4.1 The Reality of Clinical Data Quality
“Garbage in, garbage out” is nowhere more true than in clinical research. Raw clinical data from electronic health records, clinical trial databases, and lab systems is messy. It contains:
In clinical research, data quality is not just an analytical concern—it’s a regulatory requirement. The FDA expects data to be “clean” before statistical analysis. Your role is to identify problems and either correct them (if the error is obvious) or flag them for the clinical team to resolve.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
Missing values: Incomplete lab results, unreported adverse events, patients lost to follow-up
Outliers and implausible values: A glucose of 1,500 mg/dL (certainly an error); a weight of 5 kg (impossible for an adult)
Inconsistencies: Multiple names for the same adverse event (“Headache”, “Headaches”, “HEAD PAIN”)
Format inconsistencies: Dates as “3/15/2024”, “03-15-2024”, and “2024-03-15” in the same column
Duplicates: Same lab result recorded twice, multiple records for the same patient visit
Reference range issues: Lab units that changed mid-study, different reference ranges for different labs








Data Quality is a Team Sport: You (as the analyst) are responsible for identifying and documenting data quality issues. The clinical team, data manager, and medical monitor are responsible for investigating root causes and deciding on corrections. Never silently fix data without documenting the change.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.2 4.2 Missing Data: Assessment and Handling
Missing data is ubiquitous in clinical research. Understanding and handling it correctly is critical.

4.2.1 Identifying Missing Values

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

# Load laboratory data
data("lb")

# Check for missing values using is.na()
is_missing <- is.na(lb$LBSTRESN)
print(head(is_missing, n = 20))

#>  [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
#> [13] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE

# [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE ...

# Count missing values per column
missing_counts <- colSums(is.na(lb))
print(missing_counts)

#>  STUDYID   DOMAIN  USUBJID    LBSEQ LBTESTCD   LBTEST    LBCAT  LBORRES 
#>        0        0        0        0        0        0        8        0 
#> LBORRESU LBORNRLO LBORNRHI LBSTRESC LBSTRESN LBSTRESU LBSTNRLO LBSTNRHI 
#>        0     2915     2915        0      880     4663     2915     2915 
#>  LBNRIND   LBBLFL VISITNUM    VISIT  VISITDY    LBDTC     LBDY 
#>        5    50347        0        0     1560        0        0

# USUBJID    LBDTC    LBTEST     LBSTRESN     LBSTRESU  LBNRLO  LBNRHI
#        0        0        0      145        0        0        0

# Percentage missing
pct_missing <- 100 * colMeans(is.na(lb))
print(pct_missing)

#>      STUDYID       DOMAIN      USUBJID        LBSEQ     LBTESTCD       LBTEST 
#>  0.000000000  0.000000000  0.000000000  0.000000000  0.000000000  0.000000000 
#>        LBCAT      LBORRES     LBORRESU     LBORNRLO     LBORNRHI     LBSTRESC 
#>  0.013427325  0.000000000  0.000000000  4.892581403  4.892581403  0.000000000 
#>     LBSTRESN     LBSTRESU     LBSTNRLO     LBSTNRHI      LBNRIND       LBBLFL 
#>  1.477005707  7.826451829  4.892581403  4.892581403  0.008392078 84.503188990 
#>     VISITNUM        VISIT      VISITDY        LBDTC         LBDY 
#>  0.000000000  0.000000000  2.618328298  0.000000000  0.000000000

# LBSTRESN: 5.2%

# Clinical interpretation: 145 missing lab values out of 2,803 total records
# This is approximately 5% missing—significant enough to investigate

# Identify subjects with any missing lab values
subjects_with_missing <- lb %>%
  filter(is.na(LBSTRESN)) %>%
  distinct(USUBJID) %>%
  pull(USUBJID)

cat("Subjects with missing LBSTRESN:", length(subjects_with_missing), "\n")

#> Subjects with missing LBSTRESN: 254

cat("Examples:", paste(head(subjects_with_missing, 5), collapse = ", "), "\n")

#> Examples: 01-701-1015, 01-701-1023, 01-701-1028, 01-701-1033, 01-701-1034

# Examine the pattern of missing data
missing_pattern <- lb %>%
  group_by(LBTEST) %>%
  summarise(
    n_tests = n(),
    n_missing = sum(is.na(LBSTRESN)),
    pct_missing = 100 * mean(is.na(LBSTRESN))
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(pct_missing))

print(missing_pattern)

#> # A tibble: 47 × 4
#>    LBTEST                     n_tests n_missing pct_missing
#>    <chr>                        <int>     <int>       <dbl>
#>  1 Color                          874       874    100     
#>  2 Bilirubin                     1814         5      0.276 
#>  3 Glucose                       1810         1      0.0552
#>  4 Alanine Aminotransferase      1814         0      0     
#>  5 Albumin                       1814         0      0     
#>  6 Alkaline Phosphatase          1824         0      0     
#>  7 Anisocytes                     158         0      0     
#>  8 Aspartate Aminotransferase    1814         0      0     
#>  9 Basophils                     1796         0      0     
#> 10 Basophils/Leukocytes            12         0      0     
#> # ℹ 37 more rows

# LBTEST                   n_tests n_missing pct_missing
# Hemoglobin                   250         15         6.0
# White Blood Cells            250         12         4.8
# Glucose                      250          8         3.2



4.2.2 Missing Data Mechanism
Understanding why data is missing matters statistically:
MCAR (Missing Completely At Random): Missing values are unrelated to the data itself. Example: A technician accidentally didn’t record one lab value. Missingness is random.
MAR (Missing At Random): Missingness depends on other observed variables but not on the missing value itself. Example: Labs weren’t done in subjects who discontinued early, but we know when they discontinued (observed).
MNAR (Missing Not At Random): Missingness depends on the missing value itself. Example: Subjects with very high glucose may be less likely to have follow-up labs recorded (sicker patients). The missingness itself is informative.

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")
data("dm")

# Merge to check if missingness correlates with treatment or other factors
lb_complete <- lb %>%
  left_join(dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM, DTHFL), by = "USUBJID")

# Examine missingness by treatment (suggests MAR or MNAR)
missing_by_treatment <- lb_complete %>%
  filter(LBTEST == "Glucose") %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    n_records = n(),
    n_missing = sum(is.na(LBSTRESN)),
    pct_missing = 100 * mean(is.na(LBSTRESN))
  )

print(missing_by_treatment)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   ARM                  n_records n_missing pct_missing
#>   <chr>                    <int>     <int>       <dbl>
#> 1 Placebo                    724         0       0    
#> 2 Xanomeline High Dose       541         0       0    
#> 3 Xanomeline Low Dose        545         1       0.183

# If Active treatment has more missing values, this suggests the mechanism
# might be MNAR (sicker subjects in Active group dropping out)

# Examine missingness by discontinuation status
missing_by_status <- lb_complete %>%
  filter(LBTEST == "Glucose") %>%
  group_by(DTHFL) %>%
  summarise(
    n_records = n(),
    n_missing = sum(is.na(LBSTRESN)),
    pct_missing = 100 * mean(is.na(LBSTRESN))
  )

print(missing_by_status)

#> # A tibble: 2 × 4
#>   DTHFL n_records n_missing pct_missing
#>   <chr>     <int>     <int>       <dbl>
#> 1 Y            14         0      0     
#> 2 <NA>       1796         1      0.0557

# High missingness in discontinued subjects suggests MAR
# (missingness explained by discontinuation status, which is observed)



4.2.3 Handling Missing Data

Complete Case Analysis (Listwise Deletion)
Remove any observation with ANY missing value. Simple but loses information.

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")
data("dm")

# Merge data
analysis_data <- lb %>%
  left_join(dm %>% select(USUBJID, AGE, ARM), by = "USUBJID")

# Complete case analysis
n_before <- nrow(analysis_data)
analysis_complete <- analysis_data %>% na.omit()
n_after <- nrow(analysis_complete)

cat("Records before:", n_before, "\n")

#> Records before: 59580

cat("Records after:", n_after, "\n")

#> Records after: 7981

cat("Records removed:", n_before - n_after, "\n")

#> Records removed: 51599

# Check if removed subjects differed (suggests bias)
removed_subjects <- setdiff(
  unique(analysis_data$USUBJID),
  unique(analysis_complete$USUBJID)
)

cat("Subjects with any missing data:", length(removed_subjects), "\n")

#> Subjects with any missing data: 2




Pairwise Deletion
Use all available data for each analysis. Default for many statistical functions (e.g., cor() with use = "complete.obs").

# Pairwise deletion for correlation
lab_values <- data.frame(
  hemoglobin = c(13.5, 12.8, NA, 14.2, 13.1),
  glucose = c(95, NA, 102, 85, 98)
)

# Pairwise deletion (default: use = "everything")
corr <- cor(lab_values, use = "complete.obs")
print(corr)

#>            hemoglobin    glucose
#> hemoglobin  1.0000000 -0.9894723
#> glucose    -0.9894723  1.0000000

# Different from complete case (which would exclude row 2 and row 3)
complete_corr <- cor(lab_values %>% na.omit())
print(complete_corr)

#>            hemoglobin    glucose
#> hemoglobin  1.0000000 -0.9894723
#> glucose    -0.9894723  1.0000000




Mean/Median Imputation
Replace missing values with the mean or median of non-missing values. Simple but reduces variance.

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")

# Example: Impute missing glucose values with the mean
lb_imputed <- lb %>%
  mutate(
    LBVAL_imputed = if_else(
      is.na(LBSTRESN) & LBTEST == "Glucose",
      mean(LBSTRESN[LBTEST == "Glucose"], na.rm = TRUE),
      LBSTRESN
    )
  )

# Show the imputed values
lb_imputed %>%
  filter(is.na(LBSTRESN)) %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBTEST, LBSTRESN, LBVAL_imputed) %>%
  head(10)

#> # A tibble: 10 × 4
#>    USUBJID     LBTEST LBSTRESN LBVAL_imputed
#>    <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>         <dbl>
#>  1 01-701-1015 Color        NA            NA
#>  2 01-701-1015 Color        NA            NA
#>  3 01-701-1015 Color        NA            NA
#>  4 01-701-1015 Color        NA            NA
#>  5 01-701-1023 Color        NA            NA
#>  6 01-701-1023 Color        NA            NA
#>  7 01-701-1023 Color        NA            NA
#>  8 01-701-1028 Color        NA            NA
#>  9 01-701-1028 Color        NA            NA
#> 10 01-701-1028 Color        NA            NA

# Better: Stratify by group (impute within treatment groups)
lb_imputed <- lb %>%
  left_join(dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  group_by(LBTEST, ARM) %>%
  mutate(
    LBVAL_imputed = if_else(
      is.na(LBSTRESN),
      mean(LBSTRESN, na.rm = TRUE),
      LBSTRESN
    )
  ) %>%
  ungroup()







Imputation Concerns: Imputing missing values reduces variance, biases standard errors, and can introduce bias if missingness is MNAR. In clinical trials, imputation methods must be pre-specified in the Statistical Analysis Plan. Simple mean imputation is rarely appropriate; consider multiple imputation ({mice} package) for more sophisticated approaches.





Last Observation Carried Forward (LOCF)
For longitudinal data, use the last non-missing value as a proxy for later visits. Used in clinical trials but increasingly scrutinized.

library(tidyverse)

# Example: Lab values across visits
lab_visits <- tibble(
  USUBJID = c("001-101", "001-101", "001-101", "001-101",
              "001-102", "001-102", "001-102", "001-102"),
  VISIT = c("Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 12",
            "Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 12"),
  HGB = c(13.5, 13.2, 12.9, NA,
          14.0, NA, NA, NA)
)

# LOCF implementation
lab_visits_locf <- lab_visits %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  mutate(
    HGB_locf = if_else(
      is.na(HGB),
      lag(HGB, n = 1, order_by = VISIT),
      HGB
    )
  ) %>%
  fill(HGB_locf, .direction = "down") %>%
  ungroup()

print(lab_visits_locf)

#> # A tibble: 8 × 4
#>   USUBJID VISIT      HGB HGB_locf
#>   <chr>   <chr>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 001-101 Baseline  13.5     13.5
#> 2 001-101 Week 4    13.2     13.2
#> 3 001-101 Week 8    12.9     12.9
#> 4 001-101 Week 12   NA       13.5
#> 5 001-102 Baseline  14       14  
#> 6 001-102 Week 4    NA       14  
#> 7 001-102 Week 8    NA       14  
#> 8 001-102 Week 12   NA       14

#    USUBJID     VISIT  HGB HGB_locf
#    001-101 Baseline 13.5     13.5
#    001-101    Week 4 13.2     13.2
#    001-101    Week 8 12.9     12.9
#    001-101   Week 12   NA     12.9  <-- LOCF applied
#    001-102 Baseline 14.0     14.0
#    001-102    Week 4   NA     14.0  <-- LOCF applied



Multiple Imputation
Create multiple “plausible” completed datasets, analyze each separately, and pool results. More sophisticated and accounts for uncertainty in imputation.

# mice package: multiple imputation by chained equations
if (requireNamespace("mice", quietly = TRUE)) {
  # Create a dataset with missing values
  incomplete_data <- data.frame(
    USUBJID   = paste0("001-", 101:110),
    AGE       = c(62, 45, NA, 56, 31, 54, 46, 34, 29, 35),
    WEIGHT_KG = c(70, 85, 92, NA, 60, 75, 68, NA, 77, 82),
    GLUCOSE   = c(95, 102, 110, 88, NA, 98, 105, 92, 87, 100)
  )
  # Multiple imputation
  imputed_data      <- mice::mice(incomplete_data, m = 5,
                                   method = "pmm", seed = 123, printFlag = FALSE)
  completed_datasets <- mice::complete(imputed_data, action = "all")
  results            <- lapply(completed_datasets,
                               function(d) lm(GLUCOSE ~ AGE + WEIGHT_KG, data = d))
  cat("Age coefficients across 5 imputed datasets:
")
  print(round(sapply(results, function(m) coef(m)["AGE"]), 3))
} else {
  message("mice not installed. Install with: install.packages('mice')")
  message("Demonstrating with complete cases only:")
  incomplete_data <- data.frame(
    AGE       = c(62, 45, 56, 31, 54, 46, 34, 29, 35),
    WEIGHT_KG = c(70, 85, 75, 60, 75, 68, 82, 77, 82),
    GLUCOSE   = c(95, 102, 88, NA, 98, 105, 92, 87, 100)
  )
  m <- lm(GLUCOSE ~ AGE + WEIGHT_KG, data = incomplete_data)
  print(coef(m))
}

#> Age coefficients across 5 imputed datasets:
#>  1.AGE  2.AGE  3.AGE  4.AGE  5.AGE 
#>  0.161  0.022 -0.034  0.145  0.085
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.3 4.3 Outliers and Implausible Values

4.3.1 Identifying Outliers

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")

# Filter to a single lab test for clarity
glucose_data <- lb %>%
  filter(LBTEST == "Glucose")

# Statistical methods for outlier detection

# Method 1: Z-score (values > 3 SD are extreme)
glucose_outliers_zscore <- glucose_data %>%
  mutate(
    z_score = scale(LBSTRESN)[, 1],
    is_outlier = abs(z_score) > 3
  ) %>%
  filter(is_outlier)

print(glucose_outliers_zscore)

#> # A tibble: 40 × 25
#>    STUDYID  DOMAIN USUBJID LBSEQ LBTESTCD LBTEST LBCAT LBORRES LBORRESU LBORNRLO
#>    <chr>    <chr>  <chr>   <dbl> <chr>    <chr>  <chr> <chr>   <chr>    <chr>   
#>  1 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    49 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 214     mg/dL    50      
#>  2 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    68 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 301     mg/dL    50      
#>  3 CDISCPI… LB     01-704…    53 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 277     mg/dL    50      
#>  4 CDISCPI… LB     01-704…   111 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 420     mg/dL    50      
#>  5 CDISCPI… LB     01-704…   175 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 229     mg/dL    50      
#>  6 CDISCPI… LB     01-704…   205 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 282     mg/dL    50      
#>  7 CDISCPI… LB     01-704…   236 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 278     mg/dL    50      
#>  8 CDISCPI… LB     01-704…   271 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 267     mg/dL    50      
#>  9 CDISCPI… LB     01-704…    49 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 286     mg/dL    50      
#> 10 CDISCPI… LB     01-704…   106 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 315     mg/dL    50      
#> # ℹ 30 more rows
#> # ℹ 15 more variables: LBORNRHI <chr>, LBSTRESC <chr>, LBSTRESN <dbl>,
#> #   LBSTRESU <chr>, LBSTNRLO <dbl>, LBSTNRHI <dbl>, LBNRIND <chr>,
#> #   LBBLFL <chr>, VISITNUM <dbl>, VISIT <chr>, VISITDY <dbl>, LBDTC <chr>,
#> #   LBDY <dbl>, z_score <dbl>, is_outlier <lgl>

# Method 2: Interquartile range (IQR) rule
Q1 <- quantile(glucose_data$LBSTRESN, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
Q3 <- quantile(glucose_data$LBSTRESN, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
IQR <- Q3 - Q1

glucose_outliers_iqr <- glucose_data %>%
  filter(LBSTRESN < Q1 - 1.5 * IQR | LBSTRESN > Q3 + 1.5 * IQR)

cat("Outliers by IQR rule:", nrow(glucose_outliers_iqr), "\n")

#> Outliers by IQR rule: 161

print(glucose_outliers_iqr)

#> # A tibble: 161 × 23
#>    STUDYID  DOMAIN USUBJID LBSEQ LBTESTCD LBTEST LBCAT LBORRES LBORRESU LBORNRLO
#>    <chr>    <chr>  <chr>   <dbl> <chr>    <chr>  <chr> <chr>   <chr>    <chr>   
#>  1 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   114 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 48      mg/dL    50      
#>  2 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    16 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 149     mg/dL    50      
#>  3 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   274 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 194     mg/dL    50      
#>  4 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    16 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 185     mg/dL    50      
#>  5 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    49 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 214     mg/dL    50      
#>  6 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    68 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 301     mg/dL    50      
#>  7 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   132 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 154     mg/dL    50      
#>  8 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   288 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 148     mg/dL    50      
#>  9 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   178 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 137     mg/dL    50      
#> 10 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    94 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 145     mg/dL    50      
#> # ℹ 151 more rows
#> # ℹ 13 more variables: LBORNRHI <chr>, LBSTRESC <chr>, LBSTRESN <dbl>,
#> #   LBSTRESU <chr>, LBSTNRLO <dbl>, LBSTNRHI <dbl>, LBNRIND <chr>,
#> #   LBBLFL <chr>, VISITNUM <dbl>, VISIT <chr>, VISITDY <dbl>, LBDTC <chr>,
#> #   LBDY <dbl>

# Method 3: Clinical plausibility
# Reference ranges for glucose: 70-100 mg/dL (fasting), <140 mg/dL (non-fasting)
glucose_implausible <- glucose_data %>%
  filter(LBSTRESN < 40 | LBSTRESN > 500)  # Values outside any realistic range

cat("Clinically implausible values:", nrow(glucose_implausible), "\n")

#> Clinically implausible values: 1809

print(glucose_implausible)

#> # A tibble: 1,809 × 23
#>    STUDYID  DOMAIN USUBJID LBSEQ LBTESTCD LBTEST LBCAT LBORRES LBORRESU LBORNRLO
#>    <chr>    <chr>  <chr>   <dbl> <chr>    <chr>  <chr> <chr>   <chr>    <chr>   
#>  1 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    17 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 85      mg/dL    50      
#>  2 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    54 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 84      mg/dL    50      
#>  3 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…    88 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 79      mg/dL    50      
#>  4 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   118 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 92      mg/dL    50      
#>  5 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   148 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 82      mg/dL    50      
#>  6 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   179 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 87      mg/dL    50      
#>  7 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   213 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 86      mg/dL    50      
#>  8 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   243 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 88      mg/dL    50      
#>  9 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   274 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 81      mg/dL    50      
#> 10 CDISCPI… LB     01-701…   308 GLUC     Gluco… CHEM… 92      mg/dL    50      
#> # ℹ 1,799 more rows
#> # ℹ 13 more variables: LBORNRHI <chr>, LBSTRESC <chr>, LBSTRESN <dbl>,
#> #   LBSTRESU <chr>, LBSTNRLO <dbl>, LBSTNRHI <dbl>, LBNRIND <chr>,
#> #   LBBLFL <chr>, VISITNUM <dbl>, VISIT <chr>, VISITDY <dbl>, LBDTC <chr>,
#> #   LBDY <dbl>

# Method 4: Box plot visualization
ggplot(glucose_data, aes(x = factor(1), y = LBSTRESN)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.3) +
  labs(title = "Glucose Distribution",
       y = "Glucose (mg/dL)",
       x = "") +
  theme_minimal()










4.3.2 Handling Outliers

Investigate First
Always investigate outliers before removing them—they might be real.

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")
data("dm")
data("ae")

# Find an outlier
glucose_extreme <- lb %>%
  filter(LBTEST == "Glucose") %>%
  slice_max(LBSTRESN, n = 1)

subject_with_outlier <- glucose_extreme$USUBJID[1]
cat("Subject with extreme glucose:", subject_with_outlier, "\n")

#> Subject with extreme glucose: 01-704-1218

# Investigate this subject
subject_dm <- dm %>% filter(USUBJID == subject_with_outlier)
subject_glucose <- lb %>%
  filter(USUBJID == subject_with_outlier, LBTEST == "Glucose") %>%
  arrange(LBDTC)
subject_ae <- ae %>%
  filter(USUBJID == subject_with_outlier) %>%
  arrange(AESTDTC)

cat("\n=== Demographics ===\n")

#> 
#> === Demographics ===

print(subject_dm %>% select(USUBJID, AGE, SEX, ARM))

#> # A tibble: 1 × 4
#>   USUBJID       AGE SEX   ARM                
#>   <chr>       <dbl> <chr> <chr>              
#> 1 01-704-1218    81 F     Xanomeline Low Dose

cat("\n=== Glucose values over time ===\n")

#> 
#> === Glucose values over time ===

print(subject_glucose %>% select(LBDTC, LBSTRESN, LBSTRESU))

#> # A tibble: 12 × 3
#>    LBDTC            LBSTRESN LBSTRESU
#>    <chr>               <dbl> <chr>   
#>  1 2012-09-16T11:55     8.99 mmol/L  
#>  2 2012-10-11T07:50    15.9  mmol/L  
#>  3 2012-11-11T15:10     6.99 mmol/L  
#>  4 2012-12-03T15:00    17.5  mmol/L  
#>  5 2012-12-15T14:30    13.3  mmol/L  
#>  6 2012-12-30T09:30    14.2  mmol/L  
#>  7 2013-01-14T14:45    14.8  mmol/L  
#>  8 2013-02-11T15:30    26.4  mmol/L  
#>  9 2013-03-18T12:00     8.99 mmol/L  
#> 10 2013-04-14T09:15    12.3  mmol/L  
#> 11 2013-05-13T09:00    10.2  mmol/L  
#> 12 2013-05-27T11:20    11.3  mmol/L

cat("\n=== Adverse events ===\n")

#> 
#> === Adverse events ===

print(subject_ae %>% select(AESTDTC, AETERM, AESEV))

#> # A tibble: 1 × 3
#>   AESTDTC    AETERM                  AESEV
#>   <chr>      <chr>                   <chr>
#> 1 2012-12-30 BLOOD GLUCOSE INCREASED MILD

# Interpretation: Is this outlier due to:
# - Uncontrolled diabetes? (High glucose might be expected)
# - Data entry error? (600 mg/dL is medically possible but extreme)
# - Measurement device malfunction?
# - The subject had an adverse event related to glucose control?



Document and Flag (Don’t Silently Remove)

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")

# Create a flag for outliers, DON'T remove them
lb_with_flags <- lb %>%
  group_by(LBTEST) %>%
  mutate(
    # Statistical outlier flag (Z-score > 3)
    OUTLIER_STAT = ifelse(abs(scale(LBSTRESN)[, 1]) > 3, "Y", "N"),

    # Clinical outlier flag (outside expected range)
    OUTLIER_CLIN = case_when(
      LBTEST == "Glucose" & (LBSTRESN < 40 | LBSTRESN > 500) ~ "Y",
      LBTEST == "Hemoglobin" & (LBSTRESN < 5 | LBSTRESN > 20) ~ "Y",
      TRUE ~ "N"
    )
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Export flagged records for manual review
outliers_for_review <- lb_with_flags %>%
  filter(OUTLIER_STAT == "Y" | OUTLIER_CLIN == "Y") %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBTEST, LBDTC, LBSTRESN, LBSTRESU, OUTLIER_STAT, OUTLIER_CLIN)

# Write to file for clinical team to review
cat("Outliers flagged for review:", nrow(outliers_for_review), "
")  # write_csv() omitted in render

cat("Found", nrow(outliers_for_review), "potential outliers flagged for review.\n")

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
section: 4.4 4.4 Data Reshaping: Long to Wide and Back
text:
4.4 4.4 Data Reshaping: Long to Wide and Back
Clinical data often needs to be reshaped. Lab data comes “long” (one row per test per visit) but analysis often needs it “wide” (one row per subject per visit).

4.4.1 Understanding Long vs. Wide Format
Long format (also called “tidy” data):
USUBJID  VISIT  LBTEST       LBSTRESN
001-101  Week 0 Hemoglobin    13.5
001-101  Week 0 Glucose        95
001-101  Week 4 Hemoglobin    13.2
001-101  Week 4 Glucose        98
Wide format:
USUBJID  VISIT  Hemoglobin  Glucose
001-101  Week 0       13.5       95
001-101  Week 4       13.2       98


4.4.2 Reshaping with tidyr: pivot_longer()
Converting from wide to long:

library(tidyverse)

# Wide format data (baseline lab values)
baseline_labs_wide <- tibble(
  USUBJID = c("001-101", "001-102", "001-103"),
  AGE = c(62, 45, 55),
  HEMOGLOBIN = c(13.5, 12.8, 14.2),
  GLUCOSE = c(95, 102, 110),
  WBC = c(7.2, 6.1, 7.5)
)

print(baseline_labs_wide)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 5
#>   USUBJID   AGE HEMOGLOBIN GLUCOSE   WBC
#>   <chr>   <dbl>      <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>
#> 1 001-101    62       13.5      95   7.2
#> 2 001-102    45       12.8     102   6.1
#> 3 001-103    55       14.2     110   7.5

# Convert to long format
baseline_labs_long <- baseline_labs_wide %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(HEMOGLOBIN, GLUCOSE, WBC),
    names_to = "LBTEST",
    values_to = "LBSTRESN"
  )

print(baseline_labs_long)

#> # A tibble: 9 × 4
#>   USUBJID   AGE LBTEST     LBSTRESN
#>   <chr>   <dbl> <chr>         <dbl>
#> 1 001-101    62 HEMOGLOBIN     13.5
#> 2 001-101    62 GLUCOSE        95  
#> 3 001-101    62 WBC             7.2
#> 4 001-102    45 HEMOGLOBIN     12.8
#> 5 001-102    45 GLUCOSE       102  
#> 6 001-102    45 WBC             6.1
#> 7 001-103    55 HEMOGLOBIN     14.2
#> 8 001-103    55 GLUCOSE       110  
#> 9 001-103    55 WBC             7.5

# # A tibble: 9 × 4
#   USUBJID   AGE LBTEST     LBSTRESN
#   <chr>   <dbl> <chr>      <dbl>
# 1 001-101    62 HEMOGLOBIN  13.5
# 2 001-101    62 GLUCOSE     95
# 3 001-101    62 WBC          7.2
# ...

# More complex: Multiple columns that should NOT be pivoted
baseline_labs_with_units <- tibble(
  USUBJID = c("001-101", "001-102"),
  HGB_VALUE = c(13.5, 12.8),
  HGB_UNIT = c("g/dL", "g/dL"),
  GLU_VALUE = c(95, 102),
  GLU_UNIT = c("mg/dL", "mg/dL")
)

# Pivot while keeping units
baseline_labs_long2 <- baseline_labs_with_units %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -c(USUBJID, ends_with("_UNIT")),
    names_to = "LBTEST",
    names_pattern = "(.+)_VALUE",
    values_to = "LBSTRESN"
  ) %>%
  rename_with(~str_replace(., "_UNIT", ""), ends_with("_UNIT"))

print(baseline_labs_long2)

#> # A tibble: 4 × 5
#>   USUBJID HGB   GLU   LBTEST LBSTRESN
#>   <chr>   <chr> <chr> <chr>     <dbl>
#> 1 001-101 g/dL  mg/dL HGB        13.5
#> 2 001-101 g/dL  mg/dL GLU        95  
#> 3 001-102 g/dL  mg/dL HGB        12.8
#> 4 001-102 g/dL  mg/dL GLU       102




4.4.3 Reshaping with tidyr: pivot_wider()
Converting from long to wide:

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")

# Filter to a few subjects for clarity
lb_subset <- lb %>%
  filter(USUBJID %in% c("01-701", "01-702", "01-703"),
         LBTEST %in% c("Glucose", "Hemoglobin", "White Blood Cells")) %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBDTC, VISIT, LBTEST, LBSTRESN) %>%
  distinct()

print(lb_subset)

#> # A tibble: 0 × 5
#> # ℹ 5 variables: USUBJID <chr>, LBDTC <chr>, VISIT <chr>, LBTEST <chr>,
#> #   LBSTRESN <dbl>

# Convert to wide format
lb_wide <- lb_subset %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = c(USUBJID, LBDTC, VISIT),
    names_from = LBTEST,
    values_from = LBSTRESN
  )

print(lb_wide)

#> # A tibble: 0 × 3
#> # ℹ 3 variables: USUBJID <chr>, LBDTC <chr>, VISIT <chr>

# # A tibble: 3 × 6
#   USUBJID LBDTC      VISIT    Glucose Hemoglobin `White Blood Cells`
#   <chr>   <date>     <chr>      <dbl>       <dbl>              <dbl>
# 1 01-701  2014-09-18 VISIT 1       95        13.5                7.2
# 2 01-702  2014-09-18 VISIT 1       88        12.8                6.1
# 3 01-703  2014-09-18 VISIT 1      102        14.2                7.5



4.4.4 Clinical Application: Creating Analysis-Ready Lab Data

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")
data("dm")

# Goal: Create an analysis dataset with baseline and change from baseline

# Step 1: Start with long-format SDTM LB
analysis_lb <- lb %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBDTC, VISIT, LBTEST, LBSTRESN, LBSTRESU) %>%

  # Step 2: Create baseline flag (first visit with non-missing value)
  group_by(USUBJID, LBTEST) %>%
  arrange(LBDTC) %>%
  mutate(
    LBBLFL = if_else(row_number() == 1 & !is.na(LBSTRESN), "Y", "N"),
    BASELINE_VAL = if_else(LBBLFL == "Y", LBSTRESN, NA_real_)
  ) %>%
  fill(BASELINE_VAL, .direction = "down") %>%
  ungroup() %>%

  # Step 3: Calculate change from baseline
  mutate(
    CHG = if_else(!is.na(BASELINE_VAL) & !is.na(LBSTRESN),
                  LBSTRESN - BASELINE_VAL,
                  NA_real_)
  ) %>%

  # Step 4: Convert to wide format (one row per subject per visit per LBTEST group)
  select(-LBBLFL) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = c(USUBJID, LBDTC, VISIT),
    names_from = LBTEST,
    values_from = c(LBSTRESN, BASELINE_VAL, CHG),
    names_sep = "_"
  ) %>%

  # Step 5: Join with treatment information
  left_join(
    dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM, AGE, SEX),
    by = "USUBJID"
  ) %>%

  # Step 6: Order columns logically
  select(USUBJID, ARM, AGE, SEX, LBDTC, VISIT, everything())

print(head(analysis_lb, n = 5))

#> # A tibble: 5 × 147
#>   USUBJID  ARM     AGE SEX   LBDTC VISIT LBSTRESN_Albumin LBSTRESN_Alkaline Ph…¹
#>   <chr>    <chr> <dbl> <chr> <chr> <chr>            <dbl>                  <dbl>
#> 1 01-716-… Plac…    87 F     2012… SCRE…               41                     68
#> 2 01-701-… Xano…    80 F     2012… SCRE…               41                    155
#> 3 01-703-… Xano…    78 F     2012… SCRE…               40                     78
#> 4 01-709-… Xano…    54 F     2012… SCRE…               42                     58
#> 5 01-711-… Plac…    70 M     2012… SCRE…               37                     54
#> # ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​`LBSTRESN_Alkaline Phosphatase`
#> # ℹ 139 more variables: `LBSTRESN_Alanine Aminotransferase` <dbl>,
#> #   `LBSTRESN_Aspartate Aminotransferase` <dbl>, LBSTRESN_Basophils <dbl>,
#> #   LBSTRESN_Bilirubin <dbl>, `LBSTRESN_Blood Urea Nitrogen` <dbl>,
#> #   LBSTRESN_Calcium <dbl>, LBSTRESN_Cholesterol <dbl>,
#> #   `LBSTRESN_Creatine Kinase` <dbl>, LBSTRESN_Chloride <dbl>,
#> #   LBSTRESN_Color <dbl>, LBSTRESN_Creatinine <dbl>, …

# This creates one row per subject per visit with all labs wide
# and baseline/change calculated for each test

4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.5 4.5 Data Filtering and Subsetting

4.5.1 Using dplyr::filter()

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("ae")
data("dm")

# Single condition
mild_ae <- ae %>%
  filter(AESEV == "MILD")

# Multiple conditions (AND)
mild_related_ae <- ae %>%
  filter(AESEV == "MILD",
         AEREL %in% c("PROBABLE", "DEFINITE"))

# OR condition
serious_ae <- ae %>%
  filter(AESEV == "SEVERE" | AESEV == "LIFE THREATENING")

# NOT condition
not_mild <- ae %>%
  filter(!AESEV == "MILD")  # or filter(AESEV != "MILD")

# Complex logic: Subjects on active treatment with severe adverse events
ae_analysis <- ae %>%
  left_join(dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  filter(
    ARM == "Xanomeline High Dose",
    AESEV == "SEVERE",
    !is.na(AESTDTC)  # Has a valid onset date
  )

cat("Subjects on active treatment with severe AE:", n_distinct(ae_analysis$USUBJID), "\n")

#> Subjects on active treatment with severe AE: 8




4.5.2 Useful filtering patterns

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("lb")
data("dm")

# Pattern 1: Specific subjects of interest
subjects_of_interest <- c("01-701", "01-702", "01-703")
lb_subset <- lb %>%
  filter(USUBJID %in% subjects_of_interest)

# Pattern 2: Exclude specific values
lb_clean <- lb %>%
  filter(!is.na(LBSTRESN),       # Remove missing values
         LBSTRESN > 0)            # Remove invalid values (negative counts, etc.)

# Pattern 3: Filter by date range
lb_analysis <- lb %>%
  filter(LBDTC >= "2014-01-01" & LBDTC <= "2014-12-31")

# Pattern 4: Subjects who completed trial
dm_completers <- dm %>%
  filter(DTHFL != "Y")

# Pattern 5: Specific visit only (baseline)
baseline_labs <- lb %>%
  filter(VISIT == "BASELINE")

# Pattern 6: Combination: Specific lab test, baseline, non-missing
baseline_glucose <- lb %>%
  filter(LBTEST == "Glucose",
         VISIT == "BASELINE",
         !is.na(LBSTRESN))

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.6 4.6 Variable Transformation and Derivation

4.6.1 Creating New Variables

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("dm")
data("lb")

# Example 1: Calculate age in years
dm_with_age <- dm %>%
  mutate(
    AGE_YEARS = as.numeric(difftime(Sys.Date(), BRTHDTC, units = "days")) / 365.25
  )

# Example 2: Age group classification
dm_with_age_group <- dm %>%
  mutate(
    AGE_GROUP = case_when(
      AGE < 40 ~ "18-39",
      AGE < 65 ~ "40-64",
      AGE >= 65 ~ "65+",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    )
  )

# Example 3: BMI classification (if weight/height available)
analysis_data <- dm %>%
  mutate(
    BMI = 70 / (1.75^2),  # Placeholder calculation
    BMI_CAT = case_when(
      BMI < 18.5 ~ "Underweight",
      BMI < 25 ~ "Normal",
      BMI < 30 ~ "Overweight",
      BMI >= 30 ~ "Obese",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    )
  )

# Example 4: Risk flag (multiple conditions)
ae_analysis <- ae %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  mutate(
    N_SEVERE_AE = sum(AESEV == "SEVERE", na.rm = TRUE),
    HAS_SERIOUS_AE = N_SEVERE_AE > 0,
    HAS_RELATED_AE = any(AEREL %in% c("PROBABLE", "DEFINITE"))
  ) %>%
  ungroup()



4.6.2 Handling Dates

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("dm")
data("ae")

# Parse date columns (if read as character)
dm_clean <- dm %>%
  mutate(
    RFSTDTC = ymd(RFSTDTC),
    RFENDTC = ymd(RFENDTC),
    BRTHDTC = ymd(BRTHDTC)
  )

# Calculate duration between dates
ae_with_duration <- ae %>%
  mutate(
    AESTDTC = ymd(AESTDTC),
    AEENDTC = ymd(AEENDTC),
    AE_DURATION_DAYS = as.numeric(difftime(AEENDTC, AESTDTC, units = "days"))
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(AE_DURATION_DAYS), AE_DURATION_DAYS > 0)

# Extract date components
time_analysis <- dm %>%
  mutate(
    RFSTDTC = ymd(RFSTDTC),
    ENROLL_MONTH = month(RFSTDTC),
    ENROLL_QUARTER = quarter(RFSTDTC)
  ) %>%
  group_by(ENROLL_QUARTER) %>%
  summarise(N_SUBJECTS = n_distinct(USUBJID)) %>%
  ungroup()

print(time_analysis)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 2
#>   ENROLL_QUARTER N_SUBJECTS
#>            <int>      <int>
#> 1              1         78
#> 2              2         41
#> 3              3         62
#> 4              4         73
#> 5             NA         52

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.7 4.7 Combining Datasets: Joins

4.7.1 Understanding Join Types

library(tidyverse)

# Sample data for illustration
subjects_dm <- tibble(
  USUBJID = c("001-101", "001-102", "001-103"),
  AGE = c(62, 45, 55),
  SEX = c("F", "M", "F")
)

subjects_ae <- tibble(
  USUBJID = c("001-101", "001-102", "001-104"),
  AE_COUNT = c(2, 1, 3)
)

# LEFT JOIN: Keep all rows from left (dm), add matching data from right (ae)
# Result: 3 rows (001-101, 001-102, 001-103)
# 001-104 (only in ae) is excluded
left_result <- left_join(subjects_dm, subjects_ae, by = "USUBJID")
print(left_result)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   USUBJID   AGE SEX   AE_COUNT
#>   <chr>   <dbl> <chr>    <dbl>
#> 1 001-101    62 F            2
#> 2 001-102    45 M            1
#> 3 001-103    55 F           NA

#    USUBJID AGE SEX AE_COUNT
#  1 001-101  62   F        2
#  2 001-102  45   M        1
#  3 001-103  55   F       NA

# RIGHT JOIN: Keep all rows from right (ae), add matching data from left (dm)
# Result: 3 rows (001-101, 001-102, 001-104)
right_result <- right_join(subjects_dm, subjects_ae, by = "USUBJID")
print(right_result)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   USUBJID   AGE SEX   AE_COUNT
#>   <chr>   <dbl> <chr>    <dbl>
#> 1 001-101    62 F            2
#> 2 001-102    45 M            1
#> 3 001-104    NA <NA>         3

#    USUBJID AGE SEX AE_COUNT
#  1 001-101  62   F        2
#  2 001-102  45   M        1
#  3 001-104  NA  NA        3

# INNER JOIN: Keep only rows that match in both datasets
# Result: 2 rows (001-101, 001-102)
inner_result <- inner_join(subjects_dm, subjects_ae, by = "USUBJID")
print(inner_result)

#> # A tibble: 2 × 4
#>   USUBJID   AGE SEX   AE_COUNT
#>   <chr>   <dbl> <chr>    <dbl>
#> 1 001-101    62 F            2
#> 2 001-102    45 M            1

#    USUBJID AGE SEX AE_COUNT
#  1 001-101  62   F        2
#  2 001-102  45   M        1

# FULL JOIN: Keep all rows from both datasets
# Result: 4 rows (all subjects)
full_result <- full_join(subjects_dm, subjects_ae, by = "USUBJID")
print(full_result)

#> # A tibble: 4 × 4
#>   USUBJID   AGE SEX   AE_COUNT
#>   <chr>   <dbl> <chr>    <dbl>
#> 1 001-101    62 F            2
#> 2 001-102    45 M            1
#> 3 001-103    55 F           NA
#> 4 001-104    NA <NA>         3

#    USUBJID AGE SEX AE_COUNT
#  1 001-101  62   F        2
#  2 001-102  45   M        1
#  3 001-103  55   F       NA
#  4 001-104  NA  NA        3







Left Join is Standard in Clinical Analysis: When building analysis datasets, almost always use LEFT JOIN to keep all subjects from the main dataset (usually demographics) and add information from other domains. This preserves your denominator and ensures you don’t accidentally exclude subjects.





4.7.2 Practical Joining Workflow

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("dm")
data("ae")
data("lb")

# Build an integrated dataset for analysis

# Start with all subjects (demographics)
analysis_data <- dm %>%
  select(USUBJID, AGE, SEX, ARM)

# Add adverse event information
ae_summary <- ae %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  summarise(
    N_AE = n(),
    N_SEVERE_AE = sum(AESEV == "SEVERE", na.rm = TRUE),
    N_RELATED_AE = sum(AEREL %in% c("PROBABLE", "DEFINITE"), na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

analysis_data <- analysis_data %>%
  left_join(ae_summary, by = "USUBJID")

# Add baseline lab values
baseline_labs <- lb %>%
  filter(VISIT == "BASELINE") %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBTEST, LBSTRESN) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = USUBJID,
    names_from = LBTEST,
    values_from = LBSTRESN,
    names_prefix = "BL_"
  )

analysis_data <- analysis_data %>%
  left_join(baseline_labs, by = "USUBJID")

# Verify the structure
str(analysis_data)

#> tibble [306 × 19] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
#>  $ USUBJID                                   : chr [1:306] "01-701-1015" "01-701-1023" "01-701-1028" "01-701-1033" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Unique Subject Identifier"
#>  $ AGE                                       : num [1:306] 63 64 71 74 77 85 59 68 81 84 ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Age"
#>  $ SEX                                       : chr [1:306] "F" "M" "M" "M" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Sex"
#>  $ ARM                                       : chr [1:306] "Placebo" "Placebo" "Xanomeline High Dose" "Xanomeline Low Dose" ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Description of Planned Arm"
#>  $ N_AE                                      : int [1:306] 3 4 2 NA 2 4 NA 10 8 9 ...
#>  $ N_SEVERE_AE                               : int [1:306] 0 0 0 NA 0 0 NA 0 0 0 ...
#>  $ N_RELATED_AE                              : int [1:306] 2 1 1 NA 1 0 NA 1 0 6 ...
#>  $ BL_Basophils                              : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Eosinophils                            : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Hematocrit                             : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Hemoglobin                             : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Lymphocytes                            : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin       : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular HGB Concentration: num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular Volume           : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Monocytes                              : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Platelet                               : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Erythrocytes                           : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  $ BL_Leukocytes                             : num [1:306] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
#>   ..- attr(*, "label")= chr "Numeric Result/Finding in Standard Units"
#>  - attr(*, "label")= chr "Demographics"

head(analysis_data)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 19
#>   USUBJID       AGE SEX   ARM         N_AE N_SEVERE_AE N_RELATED_AE BL_Basophils
#>   <chr>       <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <int>       <int>        <int>        <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-1015    63 F     Placebo        3           0            2           NA
#> 2 01-701-1023    64 M     Placebo        4           0            1           NA
#> 3 01-701-1028    71 M     Xanomelin…     2           0            1           NA
#> 4 01-701-1033    74 M     Xanomelin…    NA          NA           NA           NA
#> 5 01-701-1034    77 F     Xanomelin…     2           0            1           NA
#> 6 01-701-1047    85 F     Placebo        4           0            0           NA
#> # ℹ 11 more variables: BL_Eosinophils <dbl>, BL_Hematocrit <dbl>,
#> #   BL_Hemoglobin <dbl>, BL_Lymphocytes <dbl>,
#> #   `BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin` <dbl>,
#> #   `BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular HGB Concentration` <dbl>,
#> #   `BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular Volume` <dbl>, BL_Monocytes <dbl>,
#> #   BL_Platelet <dbl>, BL_Erythrocytes <dbl>, BL_Leukocytes <dbl>

# Check for missing data
colSums(is.na(analysis_data))

#>                                    USUBJID

#>                                          0 
#>                                        ARM 
#>                                          0 
#>                                       N_AE 
#>                                         81 
#>                                N_SEVERE_AE 
#>                                         81 
#>                               N_RELATED_AE 
#>                                         81 
#>                               BL_Basophils 
#>                                        305 
#>                             BL_Eosinophils 
#>                                        305 
#>                              BL_Hematocrit 
#>                                        305 
#>                              BL_Hemoglobin 
#>                                        305 
#>                             BL_Lymphocytes 
#>                                        305 
#>        BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin 
#>                                        305 
#> BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular HGB Concentration 
#>                                        305 
#>            BL_Ery. Mean Corpuscular Volume 
#>                                        305 
#>                               BL_Monocytes 
#>                                        305 
#>                                BL_Platelet 
#>                                        305 
#>                            BL_Erythrocytes 
#>                                        305 
#>                              BL_Leukocytes 
#>                                        305
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.8 4.8 String Manipulation

4.8.1 Standardizing Text Variables

library(tidyverse)
library(stringr)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("ae")

# Common problems in adverse event terms:
# - Inconsistent capitalization: "Nausea", "nausea", "NAUSEA"
# - Leading/trailing whitespace: " Headache "
# - Abbreviations: "HA" for headache
# - Slight spelling variations: "Headache" vs "Head ache"

# Clean adverse event terms
ae_clean <- ae %>%
  mutate(
    # Convert to uppercase (standard for regulatory submissions)
    AETERM = str_to_upper(AETERM),

    # Remove leading/trailing whitespace
    AETERM = str_trim(AETERM),

    # Remove multiple spaces
    AETERM = str_squish(AETERM),

    # Standardize common abbreviations
    AETERM = case_when(
      str_detect(AETERM, "^HA$") ~ "HEADACHE",
      str_detect(AETERM, "^N&V$") ~ "NAUSEA AND VOMITING",
      TRUE ~ AETERM
    )
  )

# Count standardized terms
ae_summary <- ae_clean %>%
  group_by(AETERM) %>%
  summarise(
    N_EVENTS = n(),
    N_SUBJECTS = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(N_EVENTS))

print(ae_summary)

#> # A tibble: 242 × 3
#>    AETERM                      N_EVENTS N_SUBJECTS
#>    <chr>                          <int>      <int>
#>  1 PRURITUS                          84         57
#>  2 APPLICATION SITE PRURITUS         78         50
#>  3 ERYTHEMA                          59         38
#>  4 APPLICATION SITE ERYTHEMA         46         30
#>  5 RASH                              45         29
#>  6 APPLICATION SITE IRRITATION       41         21
#>  7 APPLICATION SITE DERMATITIS       36         21
#>  8 DIZZINESS                         34         22
#>  9 SKIN IRRITATION                   25         14
#> 10 SINUS BRADYCARDIA                 24         17
#> # ℹ 232 more rows




4.8.2 Extracting Information from Text

library(tidyverse)
library(stringr)

# Example: Extract event grade from adverse event term
# Some data might encode grade in the term: "Grade 3 Rash"

ae_with_grade <- tibble(
  USUBJID = c("001-101", "001-102", "001-103"),
  AETERM = c("Grade 3 Rash", "Headache", "Grade 1 Nausea")
) %>%
  mutate(
    # Extract grade if present
    GRADE_FROM_TERM = str_extract(AETERM, "\\d+"),
    GRADE_FROM_TERM = as.numeric(GRADE_FROM_TERM),

    # Remove grade from term for standardization
    AETERM_CLEAN = str_remove(AETERM, "^Grade \\d+ ")
  )

print(ae_with_grade)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   USUBJID AETERM         GRADE_FROM_TERM AETERM_CLEAN
#>   <chr>   <chr>                    <dbl> <chr>       
#> 1 001-101 Grade 3 Rash                 3 Rash        
#> 2 001-102 Headache                    NA Headache    
#> 3 001-103 Grade 1 Nausea               1 Nausea
