title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.9 4.9 Complete Data Cleaning Workflow
Here’s a complete, realistic data cleaning pipeline:

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

# Load raw SDTM data
data("dm")
data("ae")
data("lb")

cat("=== DATA CLEANING WORKFLOW ===\n\n")

#> === DATA CLEANING WORKFLOW ===

# STEP 1: Initial assessment
cat("STEP 1: Initial Data Assessment\n")

#> STEP 1: Initial Data Assessment

cat("DM records:", nrow(dm), "| Unique subjects:", n_distinct(dm$USUBJID), "\n")

#> DM records: 306 | Unique subjects: 306

cat("AE records:", nrow(ae), "| Subjects with AE:", n_distinct(ae$USUBJID), "\n")

#> AE records: 1191 | Subjects with AE: 225

cat("LB records:", nrow(lb), "| Subjects with labs:", n_distinct(lb$USUBJID), "\n\n")

#> LB records: 59580 | Subjects with labs: 254

# STEP 2: Check for missing data
cat("STEP 2: Missing Data Assessment\n")

#> STEP 2: Missing Data Assessment

missing_dm <- colSums(is.na(dm))
missing_ae <- colSums(is.na(ae))
missing_lb <- colSums(is.na(lb))
cat("DM missing:", sum(missing_dm > 0), "columns\n")

#> DM missing: 10 columns

cat("AE missing:", sum(missing_ae > 0), "columns\n")

#> AE missing: 11 columns

cat("LB missing:", sum(missing_lb > 0), "columns\n\n")

#> LB missing: 10 columns

# STEP 3: Check for duplicates
cat("STEP 3: Duplicate Check\n")

#> STEP 3: Duplicate Check

dm_dups <- dm %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  filter(n() > 1)
cat("Duplicate subjects in DM:", n_distinct(dm_dups$USUBJID), "\n\n")

#> Duplicate subjects in DM: 0

# STEP 4: Clean adverse event terms
cat("STEP 4: Standardizing AE Terms\n")

#> STEP 4: Standardizing AE Terms

ae_clean <- ae %>%
  mutate(
    AETERM = str_to_upper(str_trim(AETERM)),
    AESEV = str_to_upper(str_trim(AESEV))
  ) %>%
  # Flag unusual severity values
  mutate(
    AESEV_VALID = AESEV %in% c("MILD", "MODERATE", "SEVERE", "LIFE THREATENING"),
    FLAG_INVALID_SEVERITY = if_else(!AESEV_VALID, "Y", "N")
  )

cat("Invalid severity values flagged:", sum(ae_clean$FLAG_INVALID_SEVERITY == "Y"), "\n\n")

#> Invalid severity values flagged: 0

# STEP 5: Lab data quality checks
cat("STEP 5: Lab Data Quality\n")

#> STEP 5: Lab Data Quality

lb_clean <- lb %>%
  mutate(
    LBVAL_MISSING = is.na(LBSTRESN),
    # Flag clinically implausible values
    FLAG_IMPLAUSIBLE = case_when(
      LBTEST == "Glucose" & (LBSTRESN < 30 | LBSTRESN > 500) ~ "Y",
      LBTEST == "Hemoglobin" & (LBSTRESN < 5 | LBSTRESN > 20) ~ "Y",
      TRUE ~ "N"
    )
  )

cat("Lab records with implausible values:", sum(lb_clean$FLAG_IMPLAUSIBLE == "Y"), "\n\n")

#> Lab records with implausible values: 1809

# STEP 6: Build analysis dataset
cat("STEP 6: Building Analysis Dataset\n")

#> STEP 6: Building Analysis Dataset

analysis_data <- dm %>%
  # Only include subjects with valid enrollment
  filter(!is.na(RFSTDTC)) %>%
  # Add AE summary
  left_join(
    ae_clean %>%
      filter(FLAG_INVALID_SEVERITY == "N") %>%
      group_by(USUBJID) %>%
      summarise(
        N_AE = n(),
        N_SEVERE_AE = sum(AESEV == "SEVERE", na.rm = TRUE),
        .groups = "drop"
      ),
    by = "USUBJID"
  ) %>%
  # Add baseline labs
  left_join(
    lb_clean %>%
      filter(FLAG_IMPLAUSIBLE == "N", !LBVAL_MISSING) %>%
      filter(VISIT == "BASELINE") %>%
      select(USUBJID, LBTEST, LBSTRESN) %>%
      pivot_wider(
        id_cols = USUBJID,
        names_from = LBTEST,
        values_from = LBSTRESN,
        names_prefix = "BL_"
      ),
    by = "USUBJID"
  ) %>%
  # Replace NA counts with 0 (no adverse events recorded)
  mutate(
    N_AE = replace_na(N_AE, 0),
    N_SEVERE_AE = replace_na(N_SEVERE_AE, 0)
  )

# STEP 7: Final quality checks
cat("STEP 7: Final Quality Checks\n")

#> STEP 7: Final Quality Checks

cat("Analysis dataset:", nrow(analysis_data), "subjects\n")

#> Analysis dataset: 254 subjects

cat("Complete cases (no missing):", sum(complete.cases(analysis_data)), "\n")

#> Complete cases (no missing): 0

cat("Records with any missing:", sum(!complete.cases(analysis_data)), "\n\n")

#> Records with any missing: 254

# STEP 8: Export flagged records for review
flags_for_review <- ae_clean %>%
  filter(FLAG_INVALID_SEVERITY == "Y") %>%
  select(USUBJID, AETERM, AESEV, FLAG_INVALID_SEVERITY)

if (nrow(flags_for_review) > 0) {
  cat("Flagged AE records:", nrow(flags_for_review), "rows (write_csv omitted in render)\n")
}

implausible_labs <- lb_clean %>%
  filter(FLAG_IMPLAUSIBLE == "Y") %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBTEST, LBSTRESN, FLAG_IMPLAUSIBLE)

if (nrow(implausible_labs) > 0) {
  cat("Implausible lab values:", nrow(implausible_labs), "rows (write_csv omitted in render)\n")
}

#> Implausible lab values: 1809 rows (write_csv omitted in render)

cat("\nCLEANING COMPLETE\n")

#> 
#> CLEANING COMPLETE

title: 4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research
text:
4.10 Exercises

4.10.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 4.1: Missing Data Assessment
Load the laboratory domain from pharmaversesdtm. For the “Glucose” test: a) Count the total number of missing values b) Calculate the percentage of missing data c) Identify which subjects have missing glucose values d) Determine if missingness differs by treatment group
Exercise 4.2: Outlier Detection
Using the glucose lab values: a) Calculate the mean and standard deviation b) Identify values more than 3 standard deviations from the mean c) Identify values outside the clinical range (40–500 mg/dL) d) Create a flag variable indicating which values are outliers
Exercise 4.3: Data Reshaping
Create a wide-format dataset with: a) One row per subject b) Columns for baseline hemoglobin, glucose, and WBC c) Include treatment group and age d) Compare the number of rows/columns with the original long-format data


4.10.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 4.4: Complete Data Cleaning Workflow
Using pharmaversesdtm domains (dm, ae, lb): a) Identify and document all data quality issues in the adverse events domain b) Standardize adverse event terms (uppercase, remove extra spaces) c) Identify subjects with missing key variables d) Create a cleaned analysis dataset combining demographics, AE counts, and baseline labs e) Export flagged issues to a CSV file for review
Exercise 4.5: Multi-Domain Integration and Analysis-Ready Dataset Creation
Build a complete analysis-ready dataset that: a) Starts with all enrolled subjects (dm domain) b) Adds baseline and Week 4 lab values (lb domain) in wide format c) Calculates change from baseline for each lab d) Includes adverse event counts by treatment group e) Flags subjects with missing key analysis variables f) Produces a summary table: mean baseline values by treatment group g) Exports to both CSV and Excel formats

4  Data Cleaning and Transformation in Clinical Research

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
5.1 Introduction
Once data has been imported and cleaned, the next essential step is manipulation — transforming raw datasets into analysis-ready structures. The dplyr package provides a consistent, readable grammar for data manipulation built around a small set of core verbs. Each verb does one thing well, and they compose naturally through the pipe operator (|> or %>%).
This chapter covers dplyr’s core verbs in the context of clinical trial data:
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
filter() — Keep rows that meet conditions (patient selection criteria)
select() — Keep or drop columns (variable selection)
mutate() — Create or transform variables (derived endpoints)
summarise() + group_by() — Compute summaries by group (treatment arm summaries)
arrange() — Sort data
join functions — Combine datasets (merging ADSL with ADAE, etc.)
across() — Apply operations to multiple columns at once
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
5.2 1. Setup and Data

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

# Load key SDTM domains
data("dm")    # Demographics
data("ae")    # Adverse Events
data("lb")    # Laboratory
data("vs")    # Vital Signs
data("ex")    # Exposure

cat("Loaded SDTM domains:\n")

#> Loaded SDTM domains:

cat("  DM:", nrow(dm), "rows x", ncol(dm), "cols\n")

#>   DM: 306 rows x 28 cols

cat("  AE:", nrow(ae), "rows x", ncol(ae), "cols\n")

#>   AE: 1191 rows x 35 cols

cat("  LB:", nrow(lb), "rows x", ncol(lb), "cols\n")

#>   LB: 59580 rows x 23 cols

cat("  VS:", nrow(vs), "rows x", ncol(vs), "cols\n")

#>   VS: 29643 rows x 24 cols
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
5.3 2. filter() — Selecting Rows
filter() keeps rows where a logical condition is TRUE. In clinical data, this maps directly to subject selection criteria, population flags, and analysis windows.

5.3.1 2.1 Basic Filtering

# Select the safety population: all subjects who received at least one dose
# (ACTARM not equal to "Screen Failure")
safety_pop <- dm |>
  filter(ACTARM != "Screen Failure", !is.na(ACTARM))

cat("Safety population:", nrow(safety_pop), "subjects\n")

#> Safety population: 254 subjects

# Select treatment-emergent adverse events only
# AESTDTC >= EXSTDTC (AE started on or after first dose)
# We use a simple proxy: AESER == "Y" for serious AEs
serious_ae <- ae |>
  filter(AESER == "Y")

cat("Serious adverse events:", nrow(serious_ae), "\n")

#> Serious adverse events: 3

# Select subjects aged >= 65 (elderly sub-group)
elderly <- dm |>
  filter(AGE >= 65)
cat("Subjects aged ≥ 65:", nrow(elderly), "\n")

#> Subjects aged ≥ 65: 264




5.3.2 2.2 Compound Conditions

# AND: female subjects in the active arm
female_active <- dm |>
  filter(SEX == "F", ARM != "Placebo")
cat("Female active-arm subjects:", nrow(female_active), "\n")

#> Female active-arm subjects: 126

# OR: subjects with severe AEs
# (pharmaversesdtm uses AESEV; real studies often also carry AETOXGR for toxicity grade)
severe_ae <- ae |>
  filter(AESEV == "SEVERE")
cat("Severe AEs:", nrow(severe_ae), "\n")

#> Severe AEs: 43

# NOT: exclude screen failures and not-treated subjects
analysable <- dm |>
  filter(!ARM %in% c("Screen Failure", "Not Assigned"))
cat("Analysable subjects:", nrow(analysable), "\n")

#> Analysable subjects: 254




5.3.3 2.3 Filtering with Helper Functions

# between() — numeric range
lb_normal_range <- lb |>
  filter(LBTESTCD == "ALT", between(LBSTRESN, 0, 56))
cat("ALT values in normal range (0–56):", nrow(lb_normal_range), "\n")

#> ALT values in normal range (0–56): 1790

# str_detect() — pattern matching on character columns
ae_infect <- ae |>
  filter(str_detect(AEDECOD, regex("infection|infect", ignore_case = TRUE)))
cat("Infection-related AEs:", nrow(ae_infect), "\n")

#> Infection-related AEs: 34

# is.na() — missing value filter
complete_sbp <- vs |>
  filter(VSTESTCD == "SYSBP", !is.na(VSSTRESN))
cat("Non-missing SBP records:", nrow(complete_sbp), "\n")

#> Non-missing SBP records: 8205
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
5.4 3. select() — Choosing Columns
In clinical datasets, domains can have 50+ columns. select() lets you work with only the variables you need.

# Select key demographic variables
dm_subset <- dm |>
  select(USUBJID, SUBJID, ARM, ACTARM, AGE, SEX, RACE, COUNTRY)

cat("DM columns selected:", names(dm_subset), "\n")

#> DM columns selected: USUBJID SUBJID ARM ACTARM AGE SEX RACE COUNTRY

# Select columns by pattern
ae_key <- ae |>
  select(USUBJID, starts_with("AE"), -ends_with("DTC"))  # AE vars, no dates

cat("AE columns:", ncol(ae_key), "\n")

#> AE columns: 30

# Drop specific columns
lb_no_dates <- lb |>
  select(-contains("DTC"), -contains("DTM"))

cat("LB without date columns:", ncol(lb_no_dates), "cols (was", ncol(lb), ")\n")

#> LB without date columns: 22 cols (was 23 )

# Rename during select
dm_clean <- dm |>
  select(
    subject_id   = USUBJID,
    arm          = ARM,
    age_years    = AGE,
    sex          = SEX
  )
head(dm_clean, 3)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   subject_id  arm                  age_years sex  
#>   <chr>       <chr>                    <dbl> <chr>
#> 1 01-701-1015 Placebo                     63 F    
#> 2 01-701-1023 Placebo                     64 M    
#> 3 01-701-1028 Xanomeline High Dose        71 M
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
5.5 4. mutate() — Creating and Transforming Variables
mutate() adds new columns or transforms existing ones — the primary tool for creating derived endpoints.

5.5.1 4.1 Numeric Transformations

# Create age groups (a common demographic stratification variable)
dm_with_agegroup <- dm |>
  mutate(
    age_group = case_when(
      AGE < 18            ~ "< 18",
      between(AGE, 18, 44) ~ "18–44",
      between(AGE, 45, 64) ~ "45–64",
      AGE >= 65           ~ "≥ 65",
      TRUE                ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |>
  select(USUBJID, AGE, age_group)

table(dm_with_agegroup$age_group, useNA = "ifany")

#> 
#>  ≥ 65 45–64 
#>   264    42



# Flag abnormal laboratory values based on reference ranges
lb_flagged <- lb |>
  filter(!is.na(LBSTRESN), !is.na(LBSTNRLO), !is.na(LBSTNRHI)) |>
  mutate(
    below_low  = LBSTRESN < LBSTNRLO,
    above_high = LBSTRESN > LBSTNRHI,
    abnormal   = below_low | above_high,
    # Relative deviation from reference range midpoint
    ref_midpoint = (LBSTNRLO + LBSTNRHI) / 2,
    pct_from_mid = 100 * (LBSTRESN - ref_midpoint) / ref_midpoint
  )

cat("Proportion of abnormal lab results:\n")

#> Proportion of abnormal lab results:

lb_flagged |>
  group_by(LBTESTCD) |>
  summarise(
    n          = n(),
    pct_abnorm = round(100 * mean(abnormal), 1)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(pct_abnorm)) |>
  head(10) |>
  print()

#> # A tibble: 10 × 3
#>    LBTESTCD     n pct_abnorm
#>    <chr>    <int>      <dbl>
#>  1 BASOLE      12      100  
#>  2 MONOLE      12       91.7
#>  3 EOSLE       12       75  
#>  4 LYMLE       12       50  
#>  5 MCV       1790       12.3
#>  6 RBC       1809        8.8
#>  7 VITB12     272        8.5
#>  8 HGB       1809        7.7
#>  9 ALP       1824        7  
#> 10 BUN       1828        7




5.5.2 4.2 String Operations

# Standardise AE preferred terms to title case
ae_clean <- ae |>
  mutate(
    AEDECOD_std = str_to_title(AEDECOD),
    AEBODSYS_std = str_to_title(AEBODSYS)
  ) |>
  select(USUBJID, AEDECOD, AEDECOD_std, AEBODSYS_std)

head(ae_clean, 5)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 4
#>   USUBJID     AEDECOD                              AEDECOD_std      AEBODSYS_std
#>   <chr>       <chr>                                <chr>            <chr>       
#> 1 01-701-1015 APPLICATION SITE ERYTHEMA            Application Sit… General Dis…
#> 2 01-701-1015 APPLICATION SITE PRURITUS            Application Sit… General Dis…
#> 3 01-701-1015 DIARRHOEA                            Diarrhoea        Gastrointes…
#> 4 01-701-1023 ATRIOVENTRICULAR BLOCK SECOND DEGREE Atrioventricula… Cardiac Dis…
#> 5 01-701-1023 ERYTHEMA                             Erythema         Skin And Su…




5.5.3 4.3 Conditional Variables with case_when()

# Classify AE severity for reporting
ae_severity <- ae |>
  mutate(
    severity_label = case_when(
      AESEV == "MILD"     ~ "Mild",
      AESEV == "MODERATE" ~ "Moderate",
      AESEV == "SEVERE"   ~ "Severe",
      AESER == "Y"        ~ "Serious",
      TRUE                ~ "Unknown"
    ),
    is_related = case_when(
      # pharmaversesdtm encodes AEREL as "Y" (related) / "N" (not related)
      AEREL == "Y" ~ TRUE,
      AEREL == "N" ~ FALSE,
      TRUE         ~ NA
    )
  )

cat("AE severity distribution:\n")

#> AE severity distribution:

print(table(ae_severity$severity_label, useNA = "ifany"))

#> 
#>     Mild Moderate   Severe 
#>      770      378       43

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
5.7 6. arrange() — Sorting Data

# Sort vital signs by subject then visit then test
vs_sorted <- vs |>
  arrange(USUBJID, VISITNUM, VSTESTCD)

head(vs_sorted |> select(USUBJID, VISITNUM, VSTESTCD, VSSTRESN), 8)

#> # A tibble: 8 × 4
#>   USUBJID     VISITNUM VSTESTCD VSSTRESN
#>   <chr>          <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-1015        1 DIABP         64 
#> 2 01-701-1015        1 DIABP         83 
#> 3 01-701-1015        1 DIABP         57 
#> 4 01-701-1015        1 HEIGHT       147.
#> 5 01-701-1015        1 PULSE         57 
#> 6 01-701-1015        1 PULSE         62 
#> 7 01-701-1015        1 PULSE         65 
#> 8 01-701-1015        1 SYSBP        131

# Sort AEs by onset date descending (most recent first)
ae_by_date <- ae |>
  filter(!is.na(AESTDTC)) |>
  arrange(desc(AESTDTC))

head(ae_by_date |> select(USUBJID, AEDECOD, AESTDTC, AESER), 5)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 4
#>   USUBJID     AEDECOD                                 AESTDTC    AESER
#>   <chr>       <chr>                                   <chr>      <chr>
#> 1 01-701-1317 DYSPHONIA                               2014-11-03 N    
#> 2 01-701-1034 FATIGUE                                 2014-11-02 N    
#> 3 01-704-1445 COMPLETED SUICIDE                       2014-10-31 N    
#> 4 01-709-1312 ELECTROCARDIOGRAM ST SEGMENT DEPRESSION 2014-10-19 N    
#> 5 01-701-1317 FATIGUE                                 2014-09-12 N
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
5.8 7. Joining Datasets
Combining datasets (DM + AE, ADSL + ADAE) is fundamental to clinical data analysis. dplyr provides clear, explicit join functions.

# Left join: add demographic info to every AE record
ae_dm <- ae |>
  left_join(
    dm |> select(USUBJID, ARM, AGE, SEX),
    by = "USUBJID"
  )

cat("AE with demographics:", nrow(ae_dm), "rows\n")

#> AE with demographics: 1191 rows

cat("AEs without matched subject:", sum(is.na(ae_dm$ARM)), "\n")

#> AEs without matched subject: 0

# Inner join: only subjects who have both AEs AND lab data
# (subjects with at least one record in both domains)
ae_subjects <- distinct(ae, USUBJID)
lb_subjects <- distinct(lb, USUBJID)
both_domains <- inner_join(ae_subjects, lb_subjects, by = "USUBJID")
cat("Subjects with both AE and LB data:", nrow(both_domains), "\n")

#> Subjects with both AE and LB data: 225

# Anti-join: subjects in DM but NOT in AE (no AEs reported)
no_ae_subjects <- dm |>
  anti_join(ae, by = "USUBJID")
cat("Subjects with no AEs:", nrow(no_ae_subjects), "\n")

#> Subjects with no AEs: 81

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
section: 5.9 8. across() — Applying Functions to Multiple Columns
text:
5.9 8. across() — Applying Functions to Multiple Columns
across() eliminates repetitive code when the same operation needs to be applied to several columns — useful for standardising lab values or converting multiple date columns.

# Compute mean and SD for all numeric vital sign measurements per subject
vs_numeric_summary <- vs |>
  filter(!is.na(VSSTRESN)) |>
  group_by(USUBJID, VSTESTCD) |>
  summarise(
    across(VSSTRESN, list(mean = mean, sd = sd, n = \(x) sum(!is.na(x)))),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

head(vs_numeric_summary, 6)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 5
#>   USUBJID     VSTESTCD VSSTRESN_mean VSSTRESN_sd VSSTRESN_n
#>   <chr>       <chr>            <dbl>       <dbl>      <int>
#> 1 01-701-1015 DIABP             62.2       6.66          42
#> 2 01-701-1015 HEIGHT           147.       NA              1
#> 3 01-701-1015 PULSE             59.0       3.72          42
#> 4 01-701-1015 SYSBP            136.        9.13          42
#> 5 01-701-1015 TEMP              36.3       0.221         14
#> 6 01-701-1015 WEIGHT            53.4       0.501         11

# Convert multiple character columns to upper case
ae_upper <- ae |>
  mutate(across(c(AEBODSYS, AEDECOD, AESEV), str_to_upper))

cat("Standardised AE terms (first row):\n")

#> Standardised AE terms (first row):

ae_upper |> select(AEBODSYS, AEDECOD, AESEV) |> head(2) |> print()

#> # A tibble: 2 × 3
#>   AEBODSYS                                             AEDECOD             AESEV
#>   <chr>                                                <chr>               <chr>
#> 1 GENERAL DISORDERS AND ADMINISTRATION SITE CONDITIONS APPLICATION SITE E… MILD 
#> 2 GENERAL DISORDERS AND ADMINISTRATION SITE CONDITIONS APPLICATION SITE P… MILD

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
section: 5.10 9. Practical Workflow: Building an Analysis Dataset
text:
5.10 9. Practical Workflow: Building an Analysis Dataset
Combining all verbs into a complete data-preparation pipeline:

# Build a subject-level safety dataset (ADSL-like)
adsl_safety <- dm |>
  # Keep randomised subjects only
  filter(!ARM %in% c("Screen Failure", "Not Assigned"), !is.na(ARM)) |>
  # Select and rename key variables
  select(
    USUBJID, SUBJID,
    ARM, ACTARM,
    AGE, SEX, RACE, COUNTRY
  ) |>
  # Add derived variables
  mutate(
    age_group = case_when(
      AGE < 65  ~ "< 65",
      AGE >= 65 ~ "≥ 65"
    ),
    female_flag = (SEX == "F")
  ) |>
  # Join AE summary
  left_join(
    ae |>
      group_by(USUBJID) |>
      summarise(
        n_ae       = n(),
        any_serious = any(AESER == "Y", na.rm = TRUE),
        .groups    = "drop"
      ),
    by = "USUBJID"
  ) |>
  # Replace NA (no AEs) with 0 / FALSE
  mutate(
    n_ae        = replace_na(n_ae, 0L),
    any_serious = replace_na(any_serious, FALSE)
  ) |>
  arrange(USUBJID)

cat("Analysis dataset dimensions:", nrow(adsl_safety), "×", ncol(adsl_safety), "\n")

#> Analysis dataset dimensions: 254 × 12

cat("Subjects with any SAE:", sum(adsl_safety$any_serious), "\n")

#> Subjects with any SAE: 3

cat("Mean AEs per subject:", round(mean(adsl_safety$n_ae), 2), "\n")

#> Mean AEs per subject: 4.69

glimpse(adsl_safety)

#> Rows: 254
#> Columns: 12
#> $ USUBJID     <chr> "01-701-1015", "01-701-1023", "01-701-1028", "01-701-1033"…
#> $ SUBJID      <chr> "1015", "1023", "1028", "1033", "1034", "1047", "1097", "1…
#> $ ARM         <chr> "Placebo", "Placebo", "Xanomeline High Dose", "Xanomeline …
#> $ ACTARM      <chr> "Placebo", "Placebo", "Xanomeline High Dose", "Xanomeline …
#> $ AGE         <dbl> 63, 64, 71, 74, 77, 85, 68, 81, 84, 52, 84, 81, 75, 57, 79…
#> $ SEX         <chr> "F", "M", "M", "M", "F", "F", "M", "F", "M", "M", "M", "F"…
#> $ RACE        <chr> "WHITE", "WHITE", "WHITE", "WHITE", "WHITE", "WHITE", "WHI…
#> $ COUNTRY     <chr> "USA", "USA", "USA", "USA", "USA", "USA", "USA", "USA", "U…
#> $ age_group   <chr> "< 65", "< 65", "≥ 65", "≥ 65", "≥ 65", "≥ 65", "≥ 65", "≥…
#> $ female_flag <lgl> TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE,…
#> $ n_ae        <int> 3, 4, 2, 0, 2, 4, 10, 8, 9, 1, 8, 4, 11, 10, 2, 9, 1, 8, 1…
#> $ any_serious <lgl> FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FA…

title: 5  Data Manipulation with dplyr: Filtering, Selecting, Mutating, Grouping, and Summarising Clinical Data
text:
5.11 Summary
dplyr’s six core verbs — filter(), select(), mutate(), summarise(), group_by(), and arrange() — together with the join functions, cover the vast majority of clinical data manipulation tasks:

filter() implements inclusion/exclusion criteria and analysis population flags
select() shapes datasets to contain only the needed variables
mutate() creates derived endpoints, group flags, and normalised values
group_by() + summarise() produces the subject-level and group-level summary tables that appear in clinical study reports
arrange() orders records for review, QC, and listing creation
join() functions merge across SDTM domains and ADaM datasets
across() applies the same transformation to many variables at once, eliminating repetitive code


Best practice: Write manipulation code as a single pipe chain from raw data to analysis-ready dataset. This makes the data lineage transparent, reproducible, and easy to audit — all essential for regulatory submissions.

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
text:
6.1 Introduction
Data wrangling — transforming data between wide and long formats, handling nested structures, and resolving shape mismatches — is a daily task in clinical data programming. Clinical trial data is particularly challenging because:
The tidyr package provides the tools to reshape data efficiently and consistently.
This chapter covers:
crumbs: Part I: Foundations of R Programming
6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
text:
SDTM stores lab and vital sign data in long format (one row per test per visit)
Statistical analysis often requires wide format (one row per subject, one column per timepoint)
ADaM datasets require specific structures that differ from SDTM
Regulatory outputs (listings, tables) demand yet another shape




pivot_longer() and pivot_wider() — reshaping between long and wide
separate() and unite() — splitting and combining columns
complete() — filling in missing combinations
nest() and unnest() — working with list-columns and grouped models
fill() and replace_na() — handling structural missing values
Real SDTM-to-ADaM reshaping workflows

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
text:
6.2 1. Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("dm")
data("lb")
data("vs")
data("ae")

cat("Domains loaded.\n")

#> Domains loaded.

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
text:
6.3 2. Tidy Data Principles
Before reshaping, it helps to understand what “tidy” means:

Every variable has its own column
Every observation has its own row
Every value has its own cell

Most clinical data issues arise from violations of these rules. For example, a lab dataset with BASELINE, WEEK4, WEEK8, WEEK12 as separate columns violates rule 2 — the four columns represent four observations of the same variable.

# SDTM LB is already tidy (long): one row per subject × test × visit
lb |>
  select(USUBJID, LBTESTCD, VISITNUM, LBSTRESN) |>
  filter(LBTESTCD == "ALT", USUBJID == unique(USUBJID)[1]) |>
  head(8)

#> # A tibble: 8 × 4
#>   USUBJID     LBTESTCD VISITNUM LBSTRESN
#>   <chr>       <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-1015 ALT             1       27
#> 2 01-701-1015 ALT             4       41
#> 3 01-701-1015 ALT             5       18
#> 4 01-701-1015 ALT             7       26
#> 5 01-701-1015 ALT             8       22
#> 6 01-701-1015 ALT             9       27
#> 7 01-701-1015 ALT            10       17
#> 8 01-701-1015 ALT            11       21

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
text:
6.5 4. pivot_wider() — Long to Wide
pivot_wider() spreads key–value pairs across multiple columns. Critical for:

Creating change-from-baseline (CFB) datasets where baseline and post-baseline are side by side
Building subject-level datasets with one column per timepoint
Constructing cross-tabulations for regulatory tables


6.5.1 4.1 Lab Data: One Column per Visit

# Create an ALT profile table: one column per visit
alt_profile <- lb |>
  filter(LBTESTCD == "ALT",
         !is.na(LBSTRESN),
         VISITNUM %in% c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) |>
  select(USUBJID, VISITNUM, LBSTRESN) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from   = VISITNUM,
    values_from  = LBSTRESN,
    names_prefix = "Visit_"
  )

cat("ALT by visit (wide format — first 6 subjects):\n")

#> ALT by visit (wide format — first 6 subjects):

print(head(alt_profile, 6))

#> # A tibble: 6 × 4
#>   USUBJID     Visit_1 Visit_4 Visit_5
#>   <chr>         <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-1015      27      41      18
#> 2 01-701-1023      23      30      38
#> 3 01-701-1028      26      30      29
#> 4 01-701-1033      16      35      14
#> 5 01-701-1034      15      19      21
#> 6 01-701-1047      22      16      20

cat("Dimensions:", nrow(alt_profile), "×", ncol(alt_profile), "\n")

#> Dimensions: 254 × 4




6.5.2 4.2 Computing Change from Baseline

# Compute change from baseline (CFB) and percent CFB for SBP
sbp_cfb <- vs |>
  filter(VSTESTCD == "SYSBP", !is.na(VSSTRESN)) |>
  select(USUBJID, VISITNUM, VSSTRESN) |>
  # Create BASELINE and POST-BASELINE columns
  mutate(
    timepoint = if_else(VISITNUM == 1, "Baseline", paste0("Visit_", VISITNUM))
  ) |>
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols      = USUBJID,
    names_from   = timepoint,
    values_from  = VSSTRESN,
    values_fn    = mean   # average if multiple baseline readings
  ) |>
  # Compute CFB for each post-baseline visit
  mutate(
    across(
      starts_with("Visit_"),
      list(
        CFB    = \(x) x - Baseline,
        PCTCFB = \(x) 100 * (x - Baseline) / Baseline
      ),
      .names = "{.col}_{.fn}"
    )
  )

cat("SBP with CFB (first 3 subjects):\n")

#> SBP with CFB (first 3 subjects):

sbp_cfb |>
  select(USUBJID, Baseline, starts_with("Visit_2"), starts_with("Visit_3")) |>
  head(3) |>
  print()

#> # A tibble: 3 × 17
#>   USUBJID    Baseline Visit_2 Visit_201 Visit_2_CFB Visit_2_PCTCFB Visit_201_CFB
#>   <chr>         <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>         <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-10…     136.    140        NA         4.33          3.19           NA  
#> 2 01-701-10…     134     133.      122.       -1.33         -0.995         -12.3
#> 3 01-701-10…     138.    132        NA        -6.33         -4.58           NA  
#> # ℹ 10 more variables: Visit_201_PCTCFB <dbl>, Visit_3 <dbl>, Visit_3.5 <dbl>,
#> #   Visit_3.1 <dbl>, Visit_3_CFB <dbl>, Visit_3_PCTCFB <dbl>,
#> #   Visit_3.5_CFB <dbl>, Visit_3.5_PCTCFB <dbl>, Visit_3.1_CFB <dbl>,
#> #   Visit_3.1_PCTCFB <dbl>

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
section: 6.6 5. separate() and unite() — Splitting and Combining Columns
text:
6.6 5. separate() and unite() — Splitting and Combining Columns
SDTM date-time columns (ISO 8601: 2023-04-15T09:30:00) are stored as strings and often need splitting. Conversely, separate pieces of information sometimes need joining.

6.6.1 5.1 separate() — Split a Column

# Split SDTM ISO date into date and time components
lb_dates <- lb |>
  filter(!is.na(LBDTC)) |>
  select(USUBJID, LBTESTCD, LBDTC) |>
  separate(
    col  = LBDTC,
    into = c("LBDT", "LBTM"),
    sep  = "T",
    fill = "right",    # fill right if no time component
    remove = FALSE
  ) |>
  separate(
    col  = LBDT,
    into = c("LBYR", "LBMO", "LBDY"),
    sep  = "-",
    fill = "right"
  )

head(lb_dates |> select(USUBJID, LBDTC, LBYR, LBMO, LBDY, LBTM), 5)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 6
#>   USUBJID     LBDTC            LBYR  LBMO  LBDY  LBTM 
#>   <chr>       <chr>            <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr>
#> 1 01-701-1015 2013-12-26T14:45 2013  12    26    14:45
#> 2 01-701-1015 2014-01-16T13:17 2014  01    16    13:17
#> 3 01-701-1015 2014-01-30T08:50 2014  01    30    08:50
#> 4 01-701-1015 2014-02-12T12:56 2014  02    12    12:56
#> 5 01-701-1015 2014-03-05T12:25 2014  03    05    12:25




6.6.2 5.2 unite() — Combine Columns

# Create a unique visit-test identifier for pivot keys
lb_key <- lb |>
  select(USUBJID, LBTESTCD, VISITNUM, LBSTRESN) |>
  unite(
    col    = "visit_test",
    VISITNUM, LBTESTCD,
    sep    = "_V",
    remove = FALSE
  ) |>
  mutate(visit_test = paste0("V", visit_test))

head(lb_key |> select(USUBJID, VISITNUM, LBTESTCD, visit_test, LBSTRESN), 5)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 5
#>   USUBJID     VISITNUM LBTESTCD visit_test LBSTRESN
#>   <chr>          <dbl> <chr>    <chr>         <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-1015        1 ALB      V1_VALB          38
#> 2 01-701-1015        4 ALB      V4_VALB          39
#> 3 01-701-1015        5 ALB      V5_VALB          38
#> 4 01-701-1015        7 ALB      V7_VALB          37
#> 5 01-701-1015        8 ALB      V8_VALB          38

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
section: 6.7 6. complete() — Making Implicit Missing Values Explicit
text:
6.7 6. complete() — Making Implicit Missing Values Explicit
complete() ensures that all combinations of variables are present in the dataset, inserting NA for missing combinations. This is essential for creating LOCF (Last Observation Carried Forward) datasets and for identifying subjects missing planned assessments.

# Identify which subjects are missing planned VS assessments
# Planned: all subjects should have SYSBP at visits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
subjects <- dm |>
  filter(!is.na(ARM), ARM != "Screen Failure") |>
  pull(USUBJID)

planned_visits <- 1:5

sbp_observed <- vs |>
  filter(VSTESTCD == "SYSBP",
         USUBJID %in% subjects,
         VISITNUM %in% planned_visits) |>
  select(USUBJID, VISITNUM, VSSTRESN)

# complete() fills in missing USUBJID × VISITNUM combinations
sbp_complete <- sbp_observed |>
  complete(
    USUBJID  = subjects,
    VISITNUM = planned_visits
  )

n_missing <- sum(is.na(sbp_complete$VSSTRESN))
cat("Missing planned SBP assessments:", n_missing, "\n")

#> Missing planned SBP assessments: 38

cat("Out of:", nrow(sbp_complete), "planned assessments\n")

#> Out of: 3734 planned assessments

cat("Missingness rate:", round(100 * n_missing / nrow(sbp_complete), 1), "%\n")

#> Missingness rate: 1 %

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
section: 6.9 8. nest() and unnest() — List-Columns and Grouped Models
text:
6.9 8. nest() and unnest() — List-Columns and Grouped Models
nest() stores sub-datasets as list-column elements, enabling per-group modelling workflows — fitting a separate model for each lab test, subgroup, or country.

6.9.1 8.1 Nested Linear Models by Lab Test

library(broom)

# Fit a linear time trend for each lab test
lab_trends <- lb |>
  filter(!is.na(LBSTRESN), !is.na(VISITNUM)) |>
  group_by(LBTESTCD) |>
  filter(n() >= 30) |>  # require at least 30 records
  nest() |>
  mutate(
    model  = map(data, \(d) lm(LBSTRESN ~ VISITNUM, data = d)),
    tidy   = map(model, broom::tidy),
    glance = map(model, broom::glance)
  )

# Extract slope (time trend) for each test
lab_slopes <- lab_trends |>
  unnest(tidy) |>
  filter(term == "VISITNUM") |>
  select(LBTESTCD, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  arrange(p.value) |>
  head(10)

cat("Lab tests with significant time trends (top 10):\n")

#> Lab tests with significant time trends (top 10):

print(lab_slopes)

#> # A tibble: 10 × 4
#> # Groups:   LBTESTCD [10]
#>    LBTESTCD  estimate std.error  p.value
#>    <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>
#>  1 SODIUM    8.76e- 2  1.10e- 2 2.62e-15
#>  2 SPGRAV    8.21e- 5  2.89e- 5 4.56e- 3
#>  3 MCHC     -6.69e- 3  3.32e- 3 4.43e- 2
#>  4 CHOL     -7.66e- 3  3.87e- 3 4.77e- 2
#>  5 ANISO     1.90e-17  1.08e-17 8.04e- 2
#>  6 ALT      -7.23e- 2  4.19e- 2 8.47e- 2
#>  7 WBC      -1.18e- 2  7.01e- 3 9.31e- 2
#>  8 MCV       3.61e- 2  2.20e- 2 1.00e- 1
#>  9 BASO     -1.53e- 4  1.08e- 4 1.55e- 1
#> 10 ALB       1.54e- 2  1.17e- 2 1.89e- 1




6.9.2 8.2 Unnesting for Reporting

# Unnest model summaries for a clean R² table
lab_fit <- lab_trends |>
  unnest(glance) |>
  select(LBTESTCD, r.squared, adj.r.squared, p.value) |>
  arrange(desc(r.squared)) |>
  head(8)

cat("Model fit by lab test:\n")

#> Model fit by lab test:

print(lab_fit)

#> # A tibble: 8 × 4
#> # Groups:   LBTESTCD [8]
#>   LBTESTCD r.squared adj.r.squared  p.value
#>   <chr>        <dbl>         <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 MACROCY    0.505        0.500    6.11e-17
#> 2 ANISO      0.500        0.497    2.92e-25
#> 3 SODIUM     0.0340       0.0335   2.62e-15
#> 4 SPGRAV     0.00919      0.00805  4.56e- 3
#> 5 TSH        0.00290     -0.000806 3.77e- 1
#> 6 MCHC       0.00226      0.00170  4.43e- 2
#> 7 CHOL       0.00215      0.00160  4.77e- 2
#> 8 ALT        0.00164      0.00109  8.47e- 2

title: 6  Data Wrangling with tidyr: Reshaping, Pivoting, Nesting, and Structuring Clinical Data
text:
6.11 Summary
tidyr and dplyr together form the backbone of clinical data wrangling in R:

pivot_longer() converts wide formats (e.g., one column per visit) into tidy long format required by most statistical models
pivot_wider() produces wide formats needed for CFB calculations, listings, and regulatory tables
separate() / unite() handle the messy ISO date strings and compound identifiers ubiquitous in SDTM
complete() makes implicit missingness explicit — essential for identifying protocol deviations and unplanned missing assessments
fill() implements LOCF/BOCF imputation (as sensitivity analysis only)
nest() / unnest() enable scalable per-group modelling — running the same analysis across all lab parameters or subgroups with a single pipeline


Regulatory note: Any reshaping or imputation applied to the data must be documented in the Statistical Analysis Plan and the dataset specification. The transformation logic should be captured in production-quality, version-controlled R code that can be reviewed and reproduced by statisticians, programmers, and regulators.

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
text:
7.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will: - Understand why CDISC standards are critical for regulatory submissions - Navigate SDTM domain structures and naming conventions - Recognize key ADaM datasets and their relationships to SDTM - Build and derive analysis datasets from SDTM domains - Implement metadata and traceability in clinical data workflows - Use R packages (pharmaversesdtm, admiral) to work with standards-compliant data
crumbs: Part II: Clinical Data Standards and Structures
7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
text:
7.2 5.1 Why CDISC Standards Matter

7.2.1 The Regulatory Imperative
When Moderna submitted data for the COVID-19 vaccine trial to the FDA in late 2020, every table, figure, and listing (TFL) submitted was built from standardized data formatted according to CDISC standards. The same was true for the Pfizer-BioNTech submission. This isn’t an accident or best practice—it’s a regulatory requirement.
The Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium (CDISC) publishes standards that:

Ensure regulatory acceptance: FDA, EMA, PMDA, and Health Canada accept CDISC-formatted data
Enable data sharing: Multiple sites, CROs, and statisticians work with consistent definitions
Facilitate audits: Regulators and auditors can trace any analysis result back to source data
Reduce transcription errors: Automated validation catches inconsistencies
Enable re-use: Future analyses (meta-analyses, real-world studies) can leverage existing data







ImportantFDA Guidance



The FDA’s guidance on “Providing Regulatory Submissions in Electronic Format—Certain Human and Animal Drug Applications” (2013, updated 2018) explicitly recommends CDISC standards for submissions. Many recent submissions now use CDISC as a requirement, not a suggestion.




7.2.2 Real-World Context: A Hypertension Trial
Imagine you’re a statistician at a 12-week randomized controlled trial comparing three antihypertensive drugs (treatment arms A, B, C) in 300 patients across 15 sites. Data comes from:

Electronic Case Report Forms (eCRFs) at each site
Laboratory systems (automated uploads of lab values)
Vital sign machines (automated BP readings)
Pharmacy systems (dosing and medication history)
Safety reporting forms (adverse events)

Without standardization, merging these sources would be chaotic. CDISC solves this by providing: - A common vocabulary (what does “baseline” mean? Visit 1? First post-dose? The median of 3 pre-dose readings?) - Consistent variable names (SYSTOLIC vs SBP vs SBPRES?) - Traceable data flow (SDTM → ADaM → TLF)
crumbs: Part II: Clinical Data Standards and Structures
7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
text:
7.3 5.2 SDTM: Study Data Tabulation Model

7.3.1 Overview
The SDTM is the FDA-preferred format for submitting raw and derived clinical data. Think of SDTM as the “source of truth”—it documents exactly what happened in the trial, with minimal interpretation.
Key principles: - One observation per row: Each row represents one test, one adverse event, one medication administration, etc. - Domains: Data is organized by type (demographics, adverse events, labs, vital signs) - Standardized variable naming: Variables follow consistent patterns - No derived calculations: Values are directly from the case report form (CRF) or automatically captured systems - Traceability: Each value can be traced back to its source


7.3.2 SDTM Domains
Major domains include:









Domain
Full Name
Content
Rows




DM
Demographics
Age, sex, ethnicity, enrollment date
One per subject


AE
Adverse Events
All reported adverse events
One per event


LB
Laboratory
All lab test results
One per test per visit


VS
Vital Signs
Blood pressure, heart rate, temperature
One per type per visit


EX
Exposure
Drug doses, administration times
One per dose per day


CM
Concomitant Medications
Other drugs taken during trial
One per medication per date range


MH
Medical History
Pre-trial medical conditions
One per condition


SV
Subject Visits
Planned visits
One per visit


PE
Physical Examination
Exam findings
One per exam



For our hypertension example, key domains are DM (baseline characteristics), VS (blood pressure readings), LB (kidney function, potassium), AE (side effects), EX (drug doses), and CM (background medications like other antihypertensives).


7.3.3 SDTM Variable Naming Conventions
SDTM uses a systematic naming structure:
Prefix–Suffix pattern: - STUDYID: Study identifier (e.g., “HT-2024-001”) - USUBJID: Unique subject identifier (e.g., “HT-2024-001-0042”) - –STDTC / –DTC: Start/occurrence date-time in ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD’T’HH:MM:SS) - Example: “2024-03-15” or “2024-03-15T09:30:00” - –DTCF: Date imputation flag (shows if date was complete or imputed) - DOMAIN: Two-letter domain code (DM, AE, LB, etc.) - –SEQ: Sequence number (row ID within domain for a subject) - –TESTCD: Short test code (e.g., “SYSBP” for systolic BP) - –TEST: Long test name (e.g., “Systolic Blood Pressure”) - –RESULT: The actual value - –UNIT: Unit of measurement (e.g., “mmHg”, “g/dL”) - –VISIT: Visit name (e.g., “Week 0”, “Week 4”) - –VISITNUM: Visit sequence (0 = baseline, 1 = week 2, etc.)
Example: In the VS domain for systolic blood pressure: - VSDTC = “2024-03-15T09:30:00” (date/time of measurement — ISO 8601) - VSTEST = “Systolic Blood Pressure” (full test name) - VSTESTCD = “SYSBP” (short code) - VSSTRESN = 148 (standardised numeric result) - VSSTRESU = “mmHg” (standardised unit)
This structure ensures any analyst can understand the data without additional documentation.






TipCDISC Controlled Terminology



The CDISC Controlled Terminology (CT) defines allowed values for many variables. For example, SEXN (sex numeric) uses 1 = Male, 2 = Female. The CT is updated annually; always reference the correct version used in your trial protocol.
crumbs: Part II: Clinical Data Standards and Structures
7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
text:
7.4 5.3 ADaM: Analysis Data Model

7.4.1 Overview and Relationship to SDTM
While SDTM captures raw data, analysis requires derived variables. This is where ADaM comes in.
ADaM (Analysis Data Model) is built from SDTM but adds: - Derived variables: Calculated fields like change from baseline, treatment-emergent status - Subject-level data: Demographics merged with outcomes - Analysis-ready format: One row = one analysis unit (subject, subject-visit, subject-event) - Audit trail: Variables show which SDTM records contributed to each ADaM row
The SDTM → ADaM → TLF flow:
Raw Data (CRF, Labs, EHR)
        ↓
SDTM (standardized raw data, one observation per event)
        ↓
ADaM (derived analysis data, one row = one analysis unit)
        ↓
TLF (Tables, Listings, Figures for submission)


7.4.2 Key ADaM Datasets
1. ADSL: Subject-Level Analysis Dataset - One row per subject - Contains: demographics, baseline characteristics, treatment assignment, trial status - Example variables: - USUBJID, STUDYID - TRT01A (actual treatment assigned) - TRT01PN (treatment sequence number) - TRTSTDT (treatment start date) - TRTENDT (treatment end date) - SAFFL (safety population flag, 1 if in analysis) - EFFFL (efficacy population flag) - DTHFL (death flag) / RFENDTC (end of reference period - used to derive discontinuation) - BASE (baseline value of primary outcome) - AGE, SEX, ETHNIC, RACE
2. ADAE: Adverse Events Analysis Dataset - One row per adverse event per subject (or one row per event for detailed analysis) - Built from SDTM AE domain - Added variables: - AESEV (severity: mild, moderate, severe) - AEREL (relationship to study drug: unrelated, remote, possible, probable, definite) - AESER (serious adverse event flag) - AETOXGR (toxicity grade, often 1–5) - AESTDTC (start date) - AEENDTC (end date) - TRTSDT (study treatment start date, for relatedness determination)
3. ADLB: Laboratory Analysis Dataset - One row per lab test per visit per subject - Built from SDTM LB domain - Added variables: - AVAL (analysis value, e.g., 142 for serum potassium) - BASE (baseline lab value) - CHG (change from baseline: AVAL - BASE) - PCHG (percent change: (CHG / BASE) × 100) - ANRIND (abnormality indicator: “L” for low, “H” for high, “N” for normal) - ABLFL (baseline flag, “Y” for baseline visit)
4. ADTTE: Time-to-Event Analysis Dataset - One row per subject - Built from SDTM domains (AE, LB, DM) and external events - Variables: - AVAL (time from baseline to event in days) - CNSR (censoring flag: 0 = event occurred, 1 = censored/did not occur by study end) - ADEVENT (event description) - EVENTDTC (event date)


7.4.3 ADaM Variable Conventions
Key ADaM variables follow strict patterns:

AVAL: The analysis value (primary outcome value at visit)
BASE: Baseline value of the same variable
CHG: Change from baseline = AVAL - BASE
PCHG: Percent change = (CHG / BASE) × 100
DTYPE: Data type flag

“C” = Calculated derived variable
“D” = Dummy record (added for consistency)

PARAM / PARAMCD: Parameter and parameter code

Example: PARAMCD=“SYSBP”, PARAM=“Systolic Blood Pressure”

ABLFL: Analysis baseline flag (“Y” = baseline, “N” or missing = not baseline)
ANRIND: Abnormality indicator for lab values (“L”, “H”, “N”)

7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
section: 7.5 5.4 From SDTM to ADaM in R
text:
7.5 5.4 From SDTM to ADaM in R
Let’s work with real CDISC data. We’ll use the pharmaversesdtm package (contains example SDTM datasets) and admiral package (tools for ADaM derivation).

7.5.1 Installation and Data Load

# Install packages if needed
# install.packages("pharmaversesdtm")
# install.packages("admiral")
# install.packages("dplyr")
# install.packages("lubridate")

library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

# Load SDTM domains
data(dm)    # Demographics
data(ae)    # Adverse Events
data(ex)    # Exposure (drug doses)
data(vs)    # Vital Signs
data(lb)    # Laboratory

# Inspect the DM domain (demographics)
head(dm, 3)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 28
#>   STUDYID      DOMAIN USUBJID   SUBJID RFSTDTC RFENDTC RFXSTDTC RFXENDTC RFICDTC
#>   <chr>        <chr>  <chr>     <chr>  <chr>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  
#> 1 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1015   2014-0… 2014-0… 2014-01… 2014-07… <NA>   
#> 2 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1023   2012-0… 2012-0… 2012-08… 2012-09… <NA>   
#> 3 CDISCPILOT01 DM     01-701-1… 1028   2013-0… 2014-0… 2013-07… 2014-01… <NA>   
#> # ℹ 19 more variables: RFPENDTC <chr>, DTHDTC <chr>, DTHFL <chr>, SITEID <chr>,
#> #   BRTHDTC <chr>, AGE <dbl>, AGEU <chr>, SEX <chr>, RACE <chr>, ETHNIC <chr>,
#> #   ARMCD <chr>, ARM <chr>, ACTARMCD <chr>, ACTARM <chr>, COUNTRY <chr>,
#> #   DMDTC <chr>, DMDY <dbl>, ARMNRS <chr>, ACTARMUD <chr>


Output (conceptual, showing structure):
STUDYID USUBJID   SUBJID AGE AGEU SEX RACE  ETHNIC    ARMCD    ARM
STUDY01 STUDY01-1 001   65   YEARS  M WHITE NOT HISP  1        Drug A
STUDY01 STUDY01-2 002   72   YEARS  F WHITE NOT HISP  2        Drug B
STUDY01 STUDY01-3 003   58   YEARS  M BLACK NOT HISP  3        Drug C


7.5.2 Understanding the EX Domain (Exposure)
The EX domain tells us exactly what drug each subject received.

# Explore EX domain
head(ex, 3)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 17
#>   STUDYID     DOMAIN USUBJID EXSEQ EXTRT EXDOSE EXDOSU EXDOSFRM EXDOSFRQ EXROUTE
#>   <chr>       <chr>  <chr>   <dbl> <chr>  <dbl> <chr>  <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  
#> 1 CDISCPILOT… EX     01-701…     1 PLAC…      0 mg     PATCH    QD       TRANSD…
#> 2 CDISCPILOT… EX     01-701…     2 PLAC…      0 mg     PATCH    QD       TRANSD…
#> 3 CDISCPILOT… EX     01-701…     3 PLAC…      0 mg     PATCH    QD       TRANSD…
#> # ℹ 7 more variables: VISITNUM <dbl>, VISIT <chr>, VISITDY <dbl>,
#> #   EXSTDTC <chr>, EXENDTC <chr>, EXSTDY <dbl>, EXENDY <dbl>

# Key EX variables:
# - EXSTDTC: drug start date
# - EXENDTC: drug end date
# - EXTRT: treatment name (Drug A, Drug B, etc.)
# - EXDOSE: dose amount
# - EXDOSU: dose unit (mg, mL, etc.)

# Summarize treatment by arm
ex %>%
  distinct(USUBJID, EXTRT) %>%
  group_by(EXTRT) %>%
  summarise(N = n(), .groups = 'drop')

#> # A tibble: 2 × 2
#>   EXTRT          N
#>   <chr>      <int>
#> 1 PLACEBO       86
#> 2 XANOMELINE   168




7.5.3 Building ADSL: Subject-Level Analysis Dataset

# Step 1: Start with DM domain
adsl <- dm %>%
  select(STUDYID, USUBJID, AGE, SEX, RACE, ETHNIC, ARMCD, ARM) %>%
  rename(TRT01A = ARM,
         TRT01AN = ARMCD) %>%
  mutate(
    # Create subject-level identifiers
    SAFFL = "Y",  # All subjects are in safety population
    EFFFL = ifelse(AGE >= 18, "Y", "N"),  # Efficacy pop: age >= 18

    # Add baseline characteristics
    AGEGR1 = case_when(
      AGE < 40 ~ "<40",
      AGE >= 40 & AGE < 65 ~ "40-64",
      AGE >= 65 ~ ">=65"
    ),

    SEXN = ifelse(SEX == "M", 1, 2),

    # Add treatment dates (from EX domain)
    TRTSTDT = min(as.Date(ex$EXSTDTC), na.rm = TRUE),
    TRTENDT = max(as.Date(ex$EXENDTC), na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID)

# Verify ADSL structure
head(adsl, 3)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 14
#>   STUDYID     USUBJID   AGE SEX   RACE  ETHNIC TRT01AN TRT01A SAFFL EFFFL AGEGR1
#>   <chr>       <chr>   <dbl> <chr> <chr> <chr>  <chr>   <chr>  <chr> <chr> <chr> 
#> 1 CDISCPILOT… 01-701…    63 F     WHITE HISPA… Pbo     Place… Y     Y     40-64 
#> 2 CDISCPILOT… 01-701…    64 M     WHITE HISPA… Pbo     Place… Y     Y     40-64 
#> 3 CDISCPILOT… 01-701…    71 M     WHITE NOT H… Xan_Hi  Xanom… Y     Y     >=65  
#> # ℹ 3 more variables: SEXN <dbl>, TRTSTDT <date>, TRTENDT <date>

nrow(adsl)  # Should match number of subjects

#> [1] 306




7.5.4 Merging ADSL with Lab Baseline

# Step 2: Get baseline lab values (e.g., systolic BP from VS domain)
baseline_labs <- vs %>%
  filter(VISITNUM == 0) %>%  # Baseline visit
  select(USUBJID, VSTESTCD, VSSTRESN) %>%
  rename(TESTCD_BASE = VSTESTCD,
         BASE = VSSTRESN) %>%
  mutate(BASE = as.numeric(BASE))

# For systolic blood pressure (SYSBP)
baseline_sysbp <- baseline_labs %>%
  filter(TESTCD_BASE == "SYSBP") %>%
  select(USUBJID, BASE) %>%
  rename(BASE_SBP = BASE)

# Merge into ADSL
adsl <- adsl %>%
  left_join(baseline_sysbp, by = "USUBJID") %>%
  mutate(BASE_SBP = as.numeric(BASE_SBP))

head(adsl[, c("USUBJID", "AGE", "TRT01A", "BASE_SBP")], 5)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 4
#>   USUBJID       AGE TRT01A               BASE_SBP
#>   <chr>       <dbl> <chr>                   <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-1015    63 Placebo                    NA
#> 2 01-701-1023    64 Placebo                    NA
#> 3 01-701-1028    71 Xanomeline High Dose       NA
#> 4 01-701-1033    74 Xanomeline Low Dose        NA
#> 5 01-701-1034    77 Xanomeline High Dose       NA




7.5.5 Building ADLB: Lab Analysis Dataset

# Step 3: Build ADLB from VS domain (using vital signs as example labs)
# Correct SDTM VS columns: VSSTRESU (units), VSDTC (date), VSSTRESN (numeric result)
adlb <- vs %>%
  select(STUDYID, USUBJID, VSTESTCD, VSTEST, VSSTRESN, VSSTRESU,
         VISITNUM, VISIT, VSDTC) %>%
  rename(PARAMCD = VSTESTCD,
         PARAM   = VSTEST,
         AVAL    = VSSTRESN,
         UNIT    = VSSTRESU) %>%
  mutate(
    # Parse date from ISO 8601 character
    VISITDT = as.Date(VSDTC),
    AVAL    = as.numeric(AVAL),

    # Add analysis flags
    ABLFL = ifelse(VISITNUM == 0, "Y", "N"),  # Baseline flag
    DTYPE = NA_character_                       # Not a calculated variable (raw SDTM)
  )

# Merge with baseline values
adlb <- adlb %>%
  left_join(
    adlb %>%
      filter(ABLFL == "Y") %>%
      select(USUBJID, PARAMCD, AVAL) %>%
      rename(BASE = AVAL),
    by = c("USUBJID", "PARAMCD")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # Derive change from baseline
    CHG = ifelse(is.na(BASE), NA_real_, AVAL - BASE),
    PCHG = ifelse(is.na(BASE) | BASE == 0, NA_real_,
                  (CHG / BASE) * 100)
  )

# View structure
head(adlb[, c("USUBJID", "PARAMCD", "VISITNUM", "AVAL", "BASE", "CHG", "PCHG")], 8)

#> # A tibble: 8 × 7
#>   USUBJID     PARAMCD VISITNUM  AVAL  BASE   CHG  PCHG
#>   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-1015 DIABP          1    64    NA    NA    NA
#> 2 01-701-1015 DIABP          1    83    NA    NA    NA
#> 3 01-701-1015 DIABP          1    57    NA    NA    NA
#> 4 01-701-1015 DIABP          2    68    NA    NA    NA
#> 5 01-701-1015 DIABP          2    59    NA    NA    NA
#> 6 01-701-1015 DIABP          2    71    NA    NA    NA
#> 7 01-701-1015 DIABP          3    56    NA    NA    NA
#> 8 01-701-1015 DIABP          3    51    NA    NA    NA




7.5.6 Building ADAE: Adverse Events Analysis Dataset

# Step 4: Build ADAE from AE domain
adae <- ae %>%
  select(STUDYID, USUBJID, AETERM, AESOC, AESTDTC, AEENDTC, AESEV) %>%
  rename(AETEXT = AETERM,
         AEBODY = AESOC) %>%
  mutate(
    # Parse dates
    AESTDTC = as.Date(AESTDTC),
    AEENDTC = as.Date(AEENDTC),

    # Add analysis-level variables
    AETOXGR = case_when(
      AESEV == "MILD" ~ 1,
      AESEV == "MODERATE" ~ 2,
      AESEV == "SEVERE" ~ 3,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    ),

    # Treatment-emergent flag (started after treatment start date)
    TRTEMFL = "Y"  # For simplicity; normally compare to TRTSTDT
  ) %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  mutate(AESEQ = row_number()) %>%
  ungroup()

# View sample
head(adae[, c("USUBJID", "AETEXT", "AESEV", "AETOXGR", "AESTDTC")], 6)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 5
#>   USUBJID     AETEXT                               AESEV    AETOXGR AESTDTC   
#>   <chr>       <chr>                                <chr>      <dbl> <date>    
#> 1 01-701-1015 APPLICATION SITE ERYTHEMA            MILD           1 2014-01-03
#> 2 01-701-1015 APPLICATION SITE PRURITUS            MILD           1 2014-01-03
#> 3 01-701-1015 DIARRHOEA                            MILD           1 2014-01-09
#> 4 01-701-1023 ATRIOVENTRICULAR BLOCK SECOND DEGREE MILD           1 2012-08-26
#> 5 01-701-1023 ERYTHEMA                             MILD           1 2012-08-07
#> 6 01-701-1023 ERYTHEMA                             MODERATE       2 2012-08-07




7.5.7 Verification and Quality Checks

# Quality check: Verify all subjects in ADSL have a baseline record
cat("ADSL subjects:", nrow(adsl), "\n")

#> ADSL subjects: 306

cat("Subjects with baseline SBP:", sum(!is.na(adsl$BASE_SBP)), "\n")

#> Subjects with baseline SBP: 0

# Check ADLB completeness
adlb_baselines <- adlb %>%
  filter(ABLFL == "Y") %>%
  distinct(USUBJID, PARAMCD) %>%
  nrow()
cat("ADLB baseline records:", adlb_baselines, "\n")

#> ADLB baseline records: 0

# Verify treatment assignment
table(adsl$TRT01A)

#> 
#>              Placebo       Screen Failure Xanomeline High Dose 
#>                   86                   52                   84 
#>  Xanomeline Low Dose 
#>                   84

# Check AE data
cat("Total adverse events:", nrow(adae), "\n")

#> Total adverse events: 1191

cat("Subjects with AEs:", n_distinct(adae$USUBJID), "\n")

#> Subjects with AEs: 225

7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
text:
7.6 5.5 Metadata and Traceability

7.6.1 Why Metadata Matters
In a regulatory submission, the FDA asks: “Where did this number come from?” Metadata—data about data—provides the answer.
Essential metadata for each ADaM variable: - Variable name and label: AVAL = “Analysis Value” - Definition: How was it calculated? - Source: Which SDTM variables contributed? - Derivation: Formula (e.g., CHG = AVAL - BASE) - Validation rules: What values are valid?

# Example: Create a metadata data frame for ADLB
adlb_metadata <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Variable, ~Label, ~Definition, ~Source_Domain, ~Derivation,
  "USUBJID", "Unique Subject ID", "Subject identifier", "SDTM AE", "From AE domain",
  "PARAMCD", "Parameter Code", "Lab test code", "SDTM VS", "VSTESTCD from VS",
  "AVAL", "Analysis Value", "Result value at visit", "SDTM VS", "VSSTRESN from VS",
  "BASE", "Baseline Value", "Value at baseline visit", "SDTM VS", "AVAL where VISITNUM=0",
  "CHG", "Change from Baseline", "Difference from baseline", "SDTM VS", "AVAL - BASE",
  "PCHG", "Percent Change", "Percent difference from baseline", "SDTM VS", "(CHG/BASE)*100"
)

print(adlb_metadata)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 5
#>   Variable Label                Definition              Source_Domain Derivation
#>   <chr>    <chr>                <chr>                   <chr>         <chr>     
#> 1 USUBJID  Unique Subject ID    Subject identifier      SDTM AE       From AE d…
#> 2 PARAMCD  Parameter Code       Lab test code           SDTM VS       VSTESTCD …
#> 3 AVAL     Analysis Value       Result value at visit   SDTM VS       VSSTRESN …
#> 4 BASE     Baseline Value       Value at baseline visit SDTM VS       AVAL wher…
#> 5 CHG      Change from Baseline Difference from baseli… SDTM VS       AVAL - BA…
#> 6 PCHG     Percent Change       Percent difference fro… SDTM VS       (CHG/BASE…








WarningDocumentation is Compliance



In FDA audits, inadequate documentation of data derivations is a common 483 observation. Document every derived variable, every filter, every assumption.

7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
section: 7.7 5.6 The Admiral Package for Streamlined ADaM Derivation
text:
7.7 5.6 The Admiral Package for Streamlined ADaM Derivation
The admiral package (Analysis Data Model in R Asset Library) automates common ADaM derivations. While a full admiral tutorial is beyond scope, here’s a taste:

# Example: Using admiral to add analysis flags

# admiral contains functions like:
# - derive_vars_dt() : Add date variables
# - derive_vars_base() : Add baseline comparators
# - derive_vars_chg() : Calculate change from baseline

# Simple example with ADLB:
adlb_admiral <- adlb %>%
  # Convert character dates to date type
  mutate(
    across(c(VSDTC), as.Date)
  ) %>%
  # Derive flag for treatment-emergent lab abnormality
  mutate(
    ANRIND = case_when(
      PARAMCD == "SYSBP" & AVAL > 140 ~ "H",  # High BP
      PARAMCD == "SYSBP" & AVAL < 90 ~ "L",   # Low BP
      TRUE ~ "N"
    )
  )

head(adlb_admiral[, c("USUBJID", "PARAMCD", "AVAL", "BASE", "CHG", "ANRIND")], 6)

#> # A tibble: 6 × 6
#>   USUBJID     PARAMCD  AVAL  BASE   CHG ANRIND
#>   <chr>       <chr>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr> 
#> 1 01-701-1015 DIABP      64    NA    NA N     
#> 2 01-701-1015 DIABP      83    NA    NA N     
#> 3 01-701-1015 DIABP      57    NA    NA N     
#> 4 01-701-1015 DIABP      68    NA    NA N     
#> 5 01-701-1015 DIABP      59    NA    NA N     
#> 6 01-701-1015 DIABP      71    NA    NA N

7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
section: 7.9 5.8 Practical Example: A Complete SDTM-to-ADaM Workflow
text:
7.9 5.8 Practical Example: A Complete SDTM-to-ADaM Workflow
Let’s build a comprehensive example for a hypothetical hypertension trial:

# ============================================
# COMPLETE WORKFLOW: Hypertension Trial
# ============================================

# 1. Load and inspect SDTM domains
head(dm[, c("USUBJID", "AGE", "SEX", "ARM")], 3)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   USUBJID       AGE SEX   ARM                 
#>   <chr>       <dbl> <chr> <chr>               
#> 1 01-701-1015    63 F     Placebo             
#> 2 01-701-1023    64 M     Placebo             
#> 3 01-701-1028    71 M     Xanomeline High Dose

head(ex[, c("USUBJID", "EXTRT", "EXSTDTC", "EXENDTC")], 3)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   USUBJID     EXTRT   EXSTDTC    EXENDTC   
#>   <chr>       <chr>   <chr>      <chr>     
#> 1 01-701-1015 PLACEBO 2014-01-02 2014-01-16
#> 2 01-701-1015 PLACEBO 2014-01-17 2014-06-18
#> 3 01-701-1015 PLACEBO 2014-06-19 2014-07-02

head(vs[, c("USUBJID", "VSTESTCD", "VSSTRESN", "VISITNUM")], 3)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   USUBJID     VSTESTCD VSSTRESN VISITNUM
#>   <chr>       <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 01-701-1015 DIABP          64        1
#> 2 01-701-1015 DIABP          83        1
#> 3 01-701-1015 DIABP          57        1

# 2. Build ADSL (subject-level)
adsl_complete <- dm %>%
  select(STUDYID, USUBJID, AGE, SEX, RACE, ETHNIC, ARMCD, ARM) %>%
  mutate(
    # Treatment assignment
    TRT01A = ARM,
    TRT01AN = as.numeric(factor(ARMCD)),

    # Eligibility flags
    SAFFL = "Y",
    EFFFL = "Y",

    # Demographics
    AGEGR1 = case_when(
      AGE < 40 ~ "<40",
      AGE >= 40 & AGE < 65 ~ "40-64",
      AGE >= 65 ~ ">=65"
    ),
    SEXN = ifelse(SEX == "M", 1, 2),

    # Treatment dates
    TRTSTDT = as.Date("2024-01-15"),  # Common start
    TRTENDT = as.Date("2024-03-15")
  ) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID)

# 3. Build ADLB (vital signs as lab analogue)
adlb_complete <- vs %>%
  # Filter to key parameters
  filter(VSTESTCD %in% c("SYSBP", "DIABP", "PULSE")) %>%
  # SDTM VS: VSSTRESU = standardised unit; VSDTC = date of measurement
  select(STUDYID, USUBJID, VSTESTCD, VSTEST, VSSTRESN, VSSTRESU,
         VISITNUM, VISIT, VSDTC) %>%
  rename(PARAMCD = VSTESTCD,
         PARAM   = VSTEST,
         AVAL    = VSSTRESN) %>%
  mutate(
    AVAL    = as.numeric(AVAL),
    VISITDT = as.Date(VSDTC),
    ABLFL   = ifelse(VISITNUM == 0, "Y", "N")
  ) %>%
  # Add baseline values
  left_join(
    vs %>%
      filter(VSTESTCD %in% c("SYSBP", "DIABP", "PULSE"),
             VISITNUM == 0) %>%
      select(USUBJID, VSTESTCD, VSSTRESN) %>%
      mutate(VSSTRESN = as.numeric(VSSTRESN)) %>%
      rename(PARAMCD = VSTESTCD, BASE = VSSTRESN),
    by = c("USUBJID", "PARAMCD")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    CHG = AVAL - BASE,
    PCHG = ifelse(BASE != 0, (CHG / BASE) * 100, NA_real_)
  ) %>%
  select(STUDYID, USUBJID, PARAMCD, PARAM, VISITNUM, VISIT, VISITDT,
         AVAL, BASE, CHG, PCHG, ABLFL)

# 4. Summary: Baseline characteristics
cat("=== BASELINE DEMOGRAPHICS ===\n")

#> === BASELINE DEMOGRAPHICS ===

adsl_complete %>%
  group_by(TRT01A) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Mean_Age = mean(AGE),
    SD_Age = sd(AGE),
    Male_N = sum(SEX == "M"),
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

#> # A tibble: 4 × 5
#>   TRT01A                   N Mean_Age SD_Age Male_N
#>   <chr>                <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>  <int>
#> 1 Placebo                 86     75.2   8.59     33
#> 2 Screen Failure          52     75.1   9.70     16
#> 3 Xanomeline High Dose    84     74.4   7.89     44
#> 4 Xanomeline Low Dose     84     75.7   8.29     34

# 5. Summary: Baseline vitals by treatment
cat("\n=== BASELINE SYSTOLIC BP BY ARM ===\n")

#> 
#> === BASELINE SYSTOLIC BP BY ARM ===

adlb_complete %>%
  filter(PARAMCD == "SYSBP", ABLFL == "Y") %>%
  left_join(adsl_complete %>% select(USUBJID, TRT01A), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  group_by(TRT01A) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Mean = mean(AVAL),
    SD = sd(AVAL),
    Min = min(AVAL),
    Max = max(AVAL),
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

#> # A tibble: 0 × 6
#> # ℹ 6 variables: TRT01A <chr>, N <int>, Mean <dbl>, SD <dbl>, Min <dbl>,
#> #   Max <dbl>

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
text:
7.10 Summary: CDISC Standards in Context
CDISC standards (SDTM and ADaM) are not bureaucratic hurdles—they’re the skeleton that holds clinical data together, enabling:

Regulatory acceptance (FDA submissions)
Data reuse (meta-analyses, subgroup analyses)
Quality assurance (validation rules, metadata)
Collaboration (multiple sites, CROs, statisticians speak the same language)

In the chapters ahead, we’ll apply these standardized datasets to exploratory analysis (Chapter 6) and visualization (Chapter 7).

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
text:
7.11 Exercises

7.11.1 Beginner Exercises
5.1 SDTM Domain Matching
Match each SDTM domain to its content: 1. DM → _____ (A: Lab test results) 2. AE → _____ (B: Demographics and enrollment info) 3. LB → _____ (C: Adverse events)
Answer: 1-B, 2-C, 3-A
5.2 Variable Interpretation
What does each variable tell us? - USUBJID = “STUDY-001-0042” - VSTESTCD = “SYSBP” - AVAL = “145” - UNIT = “mmHg”
Write a sentence describing what these values represent.
Answer: Subject 0042 from study STUDY-001 had a systolic blood pressure measurement of 145 mmHg.
5.3 Change from Baseline Calculation
If a subject’s baseline systolic BP (from SDTM VS at VISITNUM=0) was 150 mmHg, and at Week 4 (VISITNUM=1) it was 138 mmHg, calculate: - CHG = ? - PCHG = ?
Answer: CHG = 138 - 150 = -12 mmHg; PCHG = (-12/150) × 100 = -8%



7.11.2 Advanced Exercises
5.4 ADSL Derivation and Quality Control
Using the pharmaversesdtm package: 1. Load the DM and EX domains 2. Build an ADSL dataset with these variables: - USUBJID, STUDYID, AGE, SEX, ARM (renamed to TRT01A) - TRTSTDT (first dose date from EX) - TRTENDT (last dose date from EX) - SAFFL = “Y” for all subjects - EFFFL = “Y” for subjects with AGE >= 18

Validate: How many subjects are in ADSL? How many are in EFFFL?

Hint code:

# Load data
data(dm); data(ex)

# Start with DM
adsl <- dm %>% select(USUBJID, STUDYID, AGE, SEX, ARM) %>%
  # Your derivations here

5.5 ADLB Build and Analysis
Using pharmaversesdtm VS domain: 1. Filter to VSTESTCD == “SYSBP” (systolic blood pressure) 2. Create ADLB with VISITNUM, AVAL, and derived ABLFL 3. Add baseline values (BASE) by merging baseline records 4. Derive CHG and PCHG 5. Calculate mean change from baseline by treatment arm and visit
Bonus: Identify subjects with baseline SBP > 150 (hypertension).

title: 7  Chapter 5: CDISC Standards (SDTM & ADaM)
text:
7.12 Key Takeaways

CDISC standards are FDA-required frameworks for clinical data in regulatory submissions
SDTM organizes raw data; ADaM organizes analysis data
Traceability is essential: every number must trace back to SDTM source
R packages (pharmaversesdtm, admiral) streamline SDTM-to-ADaM workflows
Metadata documentation is compliance; don’t skip it
