title: 12  Parametric Statistical Tests in Clinical Trials
text:
12.14 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: One-Sample t-Test on BMI



Using the dm dataset (if BMIBL is available) or weight-related variables from vs:

Extract body weight (VSTESTCD == "WEIGHT") from the vs dataset at baseline.
Test whether the mean weight differs from 70 kg using a one-sample t-test.
Check the normality assumption using Shapiro-Wilk and a Q-Q plot.
Write two sentences interpreting the result as you would in a clinical study report.









NoteExercise 2: Two-Sample t-Test




Filter dm_clean to Placebo and Xanomeline Low Dose arms only.
Compare mean AGE between these two arms using a Welch t-test.
First check whether variances are equal using Levene’s test.
Create a publication-ready box plot with the p-value annotated.









NoteExercise 3: One-Way ANOVA with Post-Hoc




Extract diastolic BP (VSTESTCD == "DIABP") at VISITNUM == 1.
Run a one-way ANOVA comparing diastolic BP across all three ARM groups.
Check the four standard ANOVA assumptions.
If ANOVA is significant, run Tukey HSD and visualise the confidence intervals.
Which pairs of arms differ significantly?









NoteExercise 4: ANCOVA Analysis




Use VSTESTCD == "DIABP" at VISITNUM 1 (baseline) and VISITNUM 3 (post-treatment).
Fit an ANCOVA model: V3 ~ ARM + V1.
Interpret the ARM coefficient and the baseline covariate coefficient.
Compare the p-value for ARM from plain ANOVA vs ANCOVA — which has more power? Why?









NoteExercise 5: Two-Way ANOVA




Use baseline SYSBP data across all three arms.
Fit a two-way ANOVA: VSSTRESN ~ ARM * SEX.
Interpret the interaction term.
Create an interaction plot showing mean SBP by ARM and SEX with 95% CI error bars.
Discuss the clinical implications if the interaction were statistically significant.









NoteChallenge Exercise: Complete Parametric Analysis Pipeline



Write a single R function parametric_pipeline(data, outcome_var, group_var, baseline_var = NULL) that:

Checks normality (Shapiro-Wilk) for each group.
Checks variance equality (Levene’s test).
Selects and runs the appropriate test (t-test, Welch t-test, ANOVA, Welch ANOVA, or ANCOVA).
Runs post-hoc tests if ANOVA is significant.
Returns a list with test name, statistic, p-value, and a ggplot visualisation.

Test your function on at least two different variables from the vs dataset.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
12  Parametric Statistical Tests in Clinical Trials

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.1 Introduction
Non-parametric (distribution-free) tests make fewer assumptions about the underlying data distribution. They are particularly valuable in clinical research when:
This chapter covers:
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
Sample sizes are small (n < 30) and normality cannot be verified
Data are ordinal (e.g., pain scores, quality-of-life ratings)
Distributions are heavily skewed or contain outliers
Outcomes are censored counts or time-to-event with small samples



Wilcoxon signed-rank test: Paired or one-sample comparisons
Mann–Whitney U test: Two independent groups
Kruskal–Wallis test: Multiple independent groups
Friedman test: Repeated measures across groups
Spearman correlation: Monotonic association between variables
Permutation tests: Exact non-parametric inference
Choosing between parametric and non-parametric approaches
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.2 1. When to Use Non-Parametric Tests

library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(gt)
set.seed(42)

# Generate clinical dataset: pain score (0-10, ordinal/skewed)
n_per_arm <- 30
pain_data <- tibble(
  subject_id = paste0("PT-", sprintf("%03d", 1:(n_per_arm * 2))),
  arm        = rep(c("Active", "Placebo"), each = n_per_arm),
  pain_base  = c(round(rbeta(n_per_arm, 3, 1.5) * 10),
                 round(rbeta(n_per_arm, 3, 1.5) * 10)),
  pain_week8 = c(pmax(0, round(rbeta(n_per_arm, 1.5, 3) * 10)),   # Active: lower
                 pmax(0, round(rbeta(n_per_arm, 2.5, 2) * 10)))    # Placebo: moderate
) |>
  mutate(pain_chg = pain_week8 - pain_base)

# Check normality
cat("=== Normality Assessment ===\n")

#> === Normality Assessment ===

for (arm_name in c("Active", "Placebo")) {
  vals <- pain_data |> filter(arm == arm_name) |> pull(pain_chg)
  sw   <- shapiro.test(vals)
  cat(arm_name, ": W =", round(sw$statistic, 4),
      ", p =", round(sw$p.value, 4), "\n")
}

#> Active : W = 0.9772 , p = 0.7483 
#> Placebo : W = 0.9345 , p = 0.0648



pain_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pain_chg, fill = arm)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, alpha = 0.75, colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ arm, ncol = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Active" = "#2C6E9E", "Placebo" = "#E07B39")) +
  labs(
    x = "Change in Pain Score (Week 8 – Baseline)",
    y = "Count",
    fill = "Treatment",
    title = "Distribution of Pain Score Changes by Treatment Arm",
    subtitle = "Skewed distributions suggest non-parametric methods"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")




Pain score changes: departure from normality motivates non-parametric tests
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.3 2. Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test

13.3.1 2.1 Concept and Hypotheses
The Wilcoxon signed-rank test evaluates whether the median of paired differences (or a one-sample median) equals a hypothesised value. It replaces the paired t-test when the normality assumption fails.
Procedure: 1. Compute pairwise differences \(d_i = x_{i,\text{post}} - x_{i,\text{pre}}\) 2. Rank the absolute values \(|d_i|\) (excluding zeros) 3. Assign signs to ranks based on the sign of \(d_i\) 4. Test statistic: \(W^+ = \sum_{d_i > 0} \text{rank}(|d_i|)\)
Hypotheses (two-tailed): \(H_0: \tilde{d} = 0\) vs \(H_1: \tilde{d} \neq 0\)

# Paired comparison: pain score baseline vs. week 8 (Active arm only)
active_pain <- pain_data |> filter(arm == "Active")

# Parametric paired t-test for comparison
t_paired <- t.test(active_pain$pain_week8, active_pain$pain_base, paired = TRUE)

# Wilcoxon signed-rank test
w_signed <- wilcox.test(active_pain$pain_week8, active_pain$pain_base,
                        paired = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE)

cat("=== Paired Comparison: Pain Score (Active Arm) ===\n\n")

#> === Paired Comparison: Pain Score (Active Arm) ===

cat("Parametric paired t-test:\n")

#> Parametric paired t-test:

cat("  t =", round(t_paired$statistic, 3),
    ", p =", round(t_paired$p.value, 4), "\n")

#>   t = -4.929 , p = 0

cat("  95% CI:", round(t_paired$conf.int[1], 2), "to",
    round(t_paired$conf.int[2], 2), "\n\n")

#>   95% CI: -3.96 to -1.64

cat("Wilcoxon signed-rank test:\n")

#> Wilcoxon signed-rank test:

cat("  V =", w_signed$statistic,
    ", p =", round(w_signed$p.value, 4), "\n")

#>   V = 38 , p = 3e-04

cat("  95% CI (pseudomedian):", round(w_signed$conf.int[1], 2),
    "to", round(w_signed$conf.int[2], 2), "\n")

#>   95% CI (pseudomedian): -4.5 to -2




13.3.2 2.2 Visualising Paired Data

active_pain |>
  pivot_longer(c(pain_base, pain_week8),
               names_to = "timepoint",
               values_to = "score") |>
  mutate(timepoint = case_match(timepoint,
                               "pain_base"  ~ "Baseline",
                               "pain_week8" ~ "Week 8")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = timepoint, y = score, group = subject_id)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.35, colour = "#2C6E9E") +
  geom_point(aes(colour = timepoint), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  stat_summary(aes(group = 1), fun = median, geom = "line",
               linewidth = 1.5, colour = "#D32F2F") +
  stat_summary(aes(group = 1), fun = median, geom = "point",
               size = 4, colour = "#D32F2F", shape = 18) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Baseline" = "#757575", "Week 8" = "#2C6E9E")) +
  labs(
    x = "Timepoint",
    y = "Pain Score (0–10)",
    colour = NULL,
    title = "Individual and Median Pain Scores (Active Arm)",
    subtitle = "Red line = median trajectory"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")




Paired pain scores before and after treatment (Active arm) with connecting lines
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.4 3. Mann–Whitney U Test (Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test)

13.4.1 3.1 Concept
The Mann–Whitney U test (equivalent to the Wilcoxon rank-sum test) compares the distribution of outcomes between two independent groups. It tests whether one group tends to have higher values than the other.
Hypotheses: \(H_0\): The two populations are identically distributed \(H_1\): One population tends to produce larger values
The test statistic \(U\) counts the number of times an observation from group 1 exceeds an observation from group 2.

# Compare pain changes between Active and Placebo
mw_result <- wilcox.test(pain_chg ~ arm, data = pain_data,
                         conf.int = TRUE, conf.level = 0.95)

# For comparison, parametric test
t_indep <- t.test(pain_chg ~ arm, data = pain_data, var.equal = FALSE)

cat("=== Pain Change: Active vs. Placebo ===\n\n")

#> === Pain Change: Active vs. Placebo ===

cat("Medians:\n")

#> Medians:

pain_data |>
  group_by(arm) |>
  summarise(median = median(pain_chg), IQR = IQR(pain_chg)) |>
  print()

#> # A tibble: 2 × 3
#>   arm     median   IQR
#>   <chr>    <dbl> <dbl>
#> 1 Active    -3    3.5 
#> 2 Placebo   -0.5  2.75

cat("\nMann-Whitney U test:\n")

#> 
#> Mann-Whitney U test:

cat("  W =", mw_result$statistic,
    ", p =", round(mw_result$p.value, 4), "\n")

#>   W = 214.5 , p = 5e-04

cat("  Hodges-Lehmann estimate:", round(mw_result$estimate, 2), "\n")

#>   Hodges-Lehmann estimate: -3

cat("  95% CI:", round(mw_result$conf.int[1], 2), "to",
    round(mw_result$conf.int[2], 2), "\n\n")

#>   95% CI: -4 to -1

cat("Two-sample t-test (for comparison):\n")

#> Two-sample t-test (for comparison):

cat("  t =", round(t_indep$statistic, 3),
    ", p =", round(t_indep$p.value, 4), "\n")

#>   t = -3.477 , p = 0.001




13.4.2 3.2 The Probability of Superiority
A useful effect size for the Mann–Whitney test is the common language effect size (CLES), also called the probability of superiority:
\[P(\text{Active} < \text{Placebo}) = \frac{U}{n_1 \cdot n_2}\]

active_vals  <- pain_data |> filter(arm == "Active")  |> pull(pain_chg)
placebo_vals <- pain_data |> filter(arm == "Placebo") |> pull(pain_chg)

# CLES: P(Active < Placebo) for pain (lower = better)
u_stat <- mw_result$statistic
cles   <- as.numeric(u_stat) / (length(active_vals) * length(placebo_vals))

cat("Probability that Active patient has lower pain change than Placebo patient:\n")

#> Probability that Active patient has lower pain change than Placebo patient:

cat("  CLES =", round(cles, 3), "\n")

#>   CLES = 0.238

cat("  Interpretation: In", round(cles * 100, 1),
    "% of Active–Placebo pairs, the Active patient had more pain reduction\n")

#>   Interpretation: In 23.8 % of Active–Placebo pairs, the Active patient had more pain reduction



pain_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = arm, y = pain_chg, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.75, width = 0.5, outlier.shape = 21) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Active" = "#2C6E9E", "Placebo" = "#E07B39")) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = max(pain_data$pain_chg) * 0.95,
           label = paste0("Mann-Whitney p = ", round(mw_result$p.value, 3)),
           size = 3.5, fontface = "italic") +
  labs(
    x = "Treatment Arm",
    y = "Change in Pain Score",
    fill = NULL,
    title = "Pain Score Changes: Active vs. Placebo",
    subtitle = "Box shows median and IQR; points show individual values"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")




Pain score changes by treatment arm with median and IQR
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.5 4. Kruskal–Wallis Test

13.5.1 4.1 Extension to Multiple Groups
The Kruskal–Wallis test extends the Mann–Whitney approach to three or more independent groups. It tests whether the population medians are equal.
\[H = \frac{12}{N(N+1)} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{R_i^2}{n_i} - 3(N+1)\]
where \(R_i\) is the sum of ranks in group \(i\), \(n_i\) is the group size, and \(N\) is the total sample size. Under \(H_0\), \(H\) follows a \(\chi^2\) distribution with \(k-1\) df.

# Three-group comparison: dose-response study
set.seed(55)
dose_data <- tibble(
  subject_id = paste0("DPT-", sprintf("%03d", 1:75)),
  dose       = rep(c("Placebo", "Low Dose", "High Dose"), each = 25),
  response   = c(
    round(rbeta(25, 2.5, 2) * 10),        # Placebo
    round(rbeta(25, 2, 2.5) * 10),        # Low dose
    round(rbeta(25, 1.5, 3.5) * 10)       # High dose
  )
)

# Order factor
dose_data <- dose_data |>
  mutate(dose = factor(dose, levels = c("Placebo", "Low Dose", "High Dose")))

kw_result <- kruskal.test(response ~ dose, data = dose_data)

cat("Kruskal-Wallis Test: Response by Dose Group\n")

#> Kruskal-Wallis Test: Response by Dose Group

cat("  H =", round(kw_result$statistic, 3),
    ", df =", kw_result$parameter,
    ", p =", round(kw_result$p.value, 4), "\n\n")

#>   H = 15.83 , df = 2 , p = 4e-04

dose_data |>
  group_by(dose) |>
  summarise(n = n(), median = median(response), IQR = IQR(response)) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Summary Statistics by Dose Group")







Summary Statistics by Dose Group


dose
n
median
IQR




Placebo
25
5
4


Low Dose
25
4
2


High Dose
25
3
2









13.5.2 4.2 Post-Hoc Pairwise Comparisons (Dunn’s Test)

# Pairwise Wilcoxon tests with Bonferroni correction
pairwise_results <- pairwise.wilcox.test(
  x       = dose_data$response,
  g       = dose_data$dose,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni"
)

cat("Pairwise comparisons (Wilcoxon, Bonferroni-adjusted):\n")

#> Pairwise comparisons (Wilcoxon, Bonferroni-adjusted):

print(round(pairwise_results$p.value, 4))

#>           Placebo Low Dose
#> Low Dose    1.000       NA
#> High Dose   0.001   0.0051



dose_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = dose, y = response, fill = dose)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6, trim = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, fill = "white", alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Placebo"   = "#757575",
    "Low Dose"  = "#2C6E9E",
    "High Dose" = "#1A3E5C"
  )) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = max(dose_data$response),
           label = paste0("KW p = ", round(kw_result$p.value, 4)),
           size = 3.5, fontface = "italic") +
  labs(
    x = "Dose Group",
    y = "Response Score",
    fill = NULL,
    title = "Dose-Response: Violin + Box Plot",
    subtitle = "Kruskal-Wallis test across three groups"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")




Dose-response: response scores by dose group with Kruskal-Wallis p-value
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.6 5. Friedman Test: Repeated Measures
The Friedman test is the non-parametric equivalent of repeated-measures ANOVA. It is used when the same subjects are measured under \(k\) conditions.

# Repeated measures: pain scores at 3 timepoints (within subject)
set.seed(77)
n_subj <- 30
long_pain <- tibble(
  subject_id = rep(paste0("PT-", sprintf("%03d", 1:n_subj)), times = 3),
  timepoint  = rep(c("Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8"), each = n_subj),
  pain_score = c(
    round(rbeta(n_subj, 3, 1.5) * 10),         # Baseline
    round(rbeta(n_subj, 2.5, 2) * 10),          # Week 4
    round(rbeta(n_subj, 1.5, 3.5) * 10)         # Week 8
  )
) |>
  mutate(timepoint = factor(timepoint,
                            levels = c("Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8")))

# Wide format for Friedman
wide_pain <- long_pain |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = timepoint, values_from = pain_score)

friedman_result <- friedman.test(
  y = as.matrix(wide_pain[, c("Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8")])
)

cat("Friedman Test: Pain Scores at 3 Timepoints\n")

#> Friedman Test: Pain Scores at 3 Timepoints

cat("  χ² =", round(friedman_result$statistic, 3),
    ", df =", friedman_result$parameter,
    ", p =", round(friedman_result$p.value, 4), "\n")

#>   χ² = 23.636 , df = 2 , p = 0

# Descriptive statistics
long_pain |>
  group_by(timepoint) |>
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    Median = median(pain_score),
    IQR    = IQR(pain_score),
    Mean   = round(mean(pain_score), 1)
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Pain Scores by Timepoint")

crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.7 6. Spearman Rank Correlation
Spearman’s \(\rho\) measures the strength of a monotonic (not necessarily linear) relationship between two variables. It is the correlation of the ranked values:
\[r_s = 1 - \frac{6 \sum d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}\]
where \(d_i\) is the difference in ranks for observation \(i\).

set.seed(101)
n_corr <- 60
cor_data <- tibble(
  age         = runif(n_corr, 40, 80),
  pain_score  = 2 + 0.05 * age + rnorm(n_corr, 0, 1.5),   # monotonic but noisy
  crp         = exp(0.03 * age + rnorm(n_corr, 0, 0.5))     # non-linear (log-normal)
)

r_pearson  <- cor(cor_data$age, cor_data$crp, method = "pearson")
r_spearman <- cor(cor_data$age, cor_data$crp, method = "spearman")
cor_test   <- cor.test(cor_data$age, cor_data$crp, method = "spearman")

cat("Pearson r:   ", round(r_pearson,  3), "\n")

#> Pearson r:    0.546

cat("Spearman rho:", round(r_spearman, 3),
    " (p =", round(cor_test$p.value, 4), ")\n")

#> Spearman rho: 0.608  (p = 0 )

cor_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age, y = crp)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#2C6E9E", alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "red",   linetype = "dashed",
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", colour = "#E07B39",
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.9) +
  annotate("text", x = 42, y = max(cor_data$crp) * 0.95,
           label = paste0("Pearson r = ", round(r_pearson, 2),
                          "\nSpearman ρ = ", round(r_spearman, 2)),
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "CRP (mg/L)",
    title = "Age vs. CRP: Spearman Captures the Monotonic Relationship",
    subtitle = "Red dashed = linear fit; orange = LOESS"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)




Spearman vs. Pearson correlation: Spearman captures monotonic but non-linear relationships
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.8 7. Permutation Tests
Permutation tests provide exact non-parametric p-values by computing the test statistic under all (or a random sample of) permutations of group labels.

set.seed(123)
obs_diff   <- mean(active_vals) - mean(placebo_vals)
all_values <- c(active_vals, placebo_vals)
n1         <- length(active_vals)
n_perm     <- 5000

perm_diffs <- replicate(n_perm, {
  shuffled <- sample(all_values)
  mean(shuffled[1:n1]) - mean(shuffled[(n1 + 1):length(all_values)])
})

perm_p <- mean(abs(perm_diffs) >= abs(obs_diff))

cat("Observed mean difference:", round(obs_diff, 3), "\n")

#> Observed mean difference: -2.567

cat("Permutation p-value:      ", round(perm_p, 4), "\n")

#> Permutation p-value:       2e-04

cat("Mann-Whitney p-value:     ", round(mw_result$p.value, 4), "\n")

#> Mann-Whitney p-value:      5e-04

tibble(perm_diff = perm_diffs) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = perm_diff)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#757575", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept =  obs_diff, colour = "red",
             linewidth = 1.1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -obs_diff, colour = "red",
             linewidth = 1.1, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = obs_diff + 0.2, y = 400,
           label = paste0("Observed\n", round(obs_diff, 2)),
           colour = "red", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    x = "Permutation Mean Difference",
    y = "Count",
    title = "Permutation Distribution of Mean Difference",
    subtitle = paste0(n_perm, " permutations; p = ", round(perm_p, 4))
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)




Permutation distribution of mean difference; observed value shown in red
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
section: 13.9 8. Choosing Between Parametric and Non-Parametric Tests
text:

tibble(
  Situation = c(
    "One-sample, test median",
    "Paired samples",
    "Two independent groups",
    "3+ independent groups",
    "Repeated measures",

  )
) |>

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.10 9. Practical Example: Patient-Reported Outcome Comparison

# PRO data: EQ-5D utility score (0-1, ordinal-like, bounded)
set.seed(88)
pro_data <- tibble(
  subject_id = paste0("SUBJ-", sprintf("%03d", 1:100)),
  arm        = rep(c("Active", "Placebo"), each = 50),
  eq5d_base  = round(rbeta(100, 3, 2), 2),
  eq5d_w12   = c(
    pmin(1, round(rbeta(50, 4, 1.5) + 0.05, 2)),  # Active: improvement
    round(rbeta(50, 3, 2.5), 2)                    # Placebo: minimal change
  )
) |>
  mutate(eq5d_chg = eq5d_w12 - eq5d_base)

# Test
mw_pro <- wilcox.test(eq5d_chg ~ arm, data = pro_data, conf.int = TRUE)

cat("=== EQ-5D Utility Change: Active vs. Placebo ===\n\n")

#> === EQ-5D Utility Change: Active vs. Placebo ===

pro_data |>
  group_by(arm) |>
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    Median = round(median(eq5d_chg), 3),
    IQR    = round(IQR(eq5d_chg), 3),
    Mean   = round(mean(eq5d_chg), 3)
  ) |>
  print()

#> # A tibble: 2 × 5
#>   arm         n Median   IQR   Mean
#>   <chr>   <int>  <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>
#> 1 Active     50  0.215 0.388  0.184
#> 2 Placebo    50 -0.075 0.395 -0.112

cat("\nMann-Whitney U test:\n")

#> 
#> Mann-Whitney U test:

cat("  W =", mw_pro$statistic, ", p =", round(mw_pro$p.value, 4), "\n")

#>   W = 1936.5 , p = 0

cat("  HL estimate (median difference):", round(mw_pro$estimate, 3), "\n")

#>   HL estimate (median difference): 0.31

cat("  95% CI:", round(mw_pro$conf.int[1], 3), "to",
    round(mw_pro$conf.int[2], 3), "\n")

#>   95% CI: 0.19 to 0.42
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research
text:
13.11 Summary
Non-parametric tests are indispensable tools in the clinical researcher’s toolkit:

Use them when data are ordinal, heavily skewed, or when sample sizes are too small to verify normality
Wilcoxon signed-rank replaces the paired t-test; Mann–Whitney U replaces the independent t-test
Kruskal–Wallis handles three or more independent groups; follow with pairwise Wilcoxon post-hoc tests
Friedman test handles repeated measures when normality fails
Spearman ρ captures monotonic (non-linear) associations; appropriate for ordinal variables
Permutation tests provide exact p-values with no distributional assumptions
Non-parametric tests lose some power relative to parametric tests when normality holds — but in clinical research with small or ordinal data, the extra robustness is worthwhile


Regulatory note: For PRO endpoints scored on ordinal scales (e.g., Likert scales, VAS), regulatory guidance often requires pre-specification of whether parametric or non-parametric analysis will be used in the SAP, with justification based on distributional assumptions.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
13  Non-Parametric Tests in Clinical Research

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.1 Setup and Data Loading
Regression models are among the most widely used statistical tools in clinical research. They allow us to quantify relationships between a response variable and one or more predictors, adjust for confounders, and make predictions. This chapter covers linear regression, logistic regression, and Poisson regression, all illustrated with the pharmaversesdtm clinical datasets.
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

data("dm")
data("ae")
data("vs")
data("lb")

# Clean ARM variable throughout
dm_clean <- dm |>
  filter(!is.na(ARM), ARM != "", ARM != "Screen Failure") |>
  mutate(ARM = factor(ARM,
                      levels = c("Placebo",
                                 "Xanomeline Low Dose",
                                 "Xanomeline High Dose")))

cat("Subjects in dm_clean:", nrow(dm_clean), "\n")

#> Subjects in dm_clean: 254

cat("ARM distribution:\n")

#> ARM distribution:

print(table(dm_clean$ARM))

#> 
#>              Placebo  Xanomeline Low Dose Xanomeline High Dose 
#>                   86                   84                   84
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.2 1. Simple Linear Regression
We predict systolic blood pressure (SBP) from subject age. SBP is stored in the vs dataset where VSTESTCD == "SYSBP".

14.2.1 1.1 Preparing the SBP Dataset

library(dplyr)

# Extract baseline SBP values (VISIT == "BASELINE" or first available)
sbp_data <- vs |>
  filter(VSTESTCD == "SYSBP", !is.na(VSSTRESN)) |>
  group_by(USUBJID) |>
  slice_min(order_by = VISITNUM, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(USUBJID, SBP = VSSTRESN)

# Join to demographics
analysis_df <- dm_clean |>
  inner_join(sbp_data, by = "USUBJID") |>
  filter(!is.na(AGE), !is.na(SBP)) |>
  mutate(SEX = factor(SEX),
         RACE = factor(RACE))

cat("Analytic dataset rows:", nrow(analysis_df), "\n")

#> Analytic dataset rows: 254

cat("SBP range:", range(analysis_df$SBP), "\n")

#> SBP range: 100 208

cat("AGE range:", range(analysis_df$AGE), "\n")

#> AGE range: 51 89




14.2.2 1.2 Fitting the Simple Linear Model

model_simple <- lm(SBP ~ AGE, data = analysis_df)
summary(model_simple)

#> 
#> Call:
#> lm(formula = SBP ~ AGE, data = analysis_df)
#> 
#> Residuals:
#>    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
#> -37.07 -13.09  -0.26  13.18  63.18 
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
#> (Intercept)  99.2652    10.4127   9.533  < 2e-16 ***
#> AGE           0.5624     0.1379   4.080 6.05e-05 ***
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> Residual standard error: 18.08 on 252 degrees of freedom
#> Multiple R-squared:  0.06196,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.05824 
#> F-statistic: 16.65 on 1 and 252 DF,  p-value: 6.047e-05


Interpretation: The slope coefficient for AGE represents the expected change in SBP (mmHg) for each additional year of age. A positive coefficient indicates SBP tends to rise with age, consistent with known physiology.


14.2.3 1.3 Visualising the Relationship

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(analysis_df, aes(x = AGE, y = SBP)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, colour = "#2166ac") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "#d6604d") +
  labs(title = "Systolic Blood Pressure vs Age",
       subtitle = "Simple linear regression with 95% confidence band",
       x = "Age (years)",
       y = "Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.3 2. Multiple Linear Regression
Adding SEX, RACE, and treatment ARM as additional predictors provides an adjusted estimate of the age effect and allows us to test whether these variables are independently associated with SBP.

14.3.1 2.1 Fitting the Multiple Regression Model

model_multi <- lm(SBP ~ AGE + SEX + RACE + ARM, data = analysis_df)
summary(model_multi)

#> 
#> Call:
#> lm(formula = SBP ~ AGE + SEX + RACE + ARM, data = analysis_df)
#> 
#> Residuals:
#>     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
#> -38.417 -11.179  -0.594  11.578  58.651 
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
#> (Intercept)                    91.1105    19.8040   4.601 6.74e-06 ***
#> AGE                             0.5050     0.1358   3.719 0.000248 ***
#> SEXM                           -9.6054     2.2655  -4.240 3.16e-05 ***
#> RACEBLACK OR AFRICAN AMERICAN  15.2683    18.1185   0.843 0.400217    
#> RACEWHITE                      17.3347    17.7968   0.974 0.330992    
#> ARMXanomeline Low Dose         -1.9414     2.6967  -0.720 0.472255    
#> ARMXanomeline High Dose         0.6910     2.7219   0.254 0.799796    
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> Residual standard error: 17.57 on 247 degrees of freedom
#> Multiple R-squared:  0.1323, Adjusted R-squared:  0.1112 
#> F-statistic: 6.277 on 6 and 247 DF,  p-value: 3.714e-06




14.3.2 2.2 Extracting Coefficients and Confidence Intervals

coef_tbl <- as.data.frame(confint(model_multi))
coef_tbl$Estimate <- coef(model_multi)
coef_tbl <- coef_tbl[, c("Estimate", "2.5 %", "97.5 %")]
names(coef_tbl) <- c("Estimate", "Lower_95", "Upper_95")
print(round(coef_tbl, 3))

#>                               Estimate Lower_95 Upper_95
#> (Intercept)                     91.111   52.104  130.117
#> AGE                              0.505    0.238    0.772
#> SEXM                            -9.605  -14.068   -5.143
#> RACEBLACK OR AFRICAN AMERICAN   15.268  -20.418   50.955
#> RACEWHITE                       17.335  -17.718   52.388
#> ARMXanomeline Low Dose          -1.941   -7.253    3.370
#> ARMXanomeline High Dose          0.691   -4.670    6.052




14.3.3 2.3 Comparing Models with ANOVA

anova(model_simple, model_multi)

#> Analysis of Variance Table
#> 
#> Model 1: SBP ~ AGE
#> Model 2: SBP ~ AGE + SEX + RACE + ARM
#>   Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq      F  Pr(>F)   
#> 1    252 82385                               
#> 2    247 76207  5    6178.1 4.0049 0.00164 **
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


A significant F-test indicates that adding the extra predictors improves model fit beyond what chance would produce.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.4 3. Regression Diagnostics
Before drawing conclusions, we must verify that the four key assumptions of linear regression hold:

Linearity – the relationship is approximately linear.
Independence – residuals are not correlated.
Homoscedasticity – residuals have constant variance.
Normality – residuals are approximately normally distributed.


14.4.1 3.1 Residual Plots

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model_multi, which = 1:4)







par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


Residuals vs Fitted: checks linearity and homoscedasticity — a random scatter around zero is ideal.
Normal Q-Q: checks normality — points should lie close to the diagonal.
Scale-Location: another view of homoscedasticity.
Cook’s Distance / Residuals vs Leverage: identifies influential observations.



14.4.2 3.2 Cook’s Distance — Identifying Influential Points

library(dplyr)

cooks_d <- cooks.distance(model_multi)
influential_threshold <- 4 / nrow(analysis_df)

influential_df <- data.frame(
  USUBJID = analysis_df$USUBJID,
  CooksD   = cooks_d
) |>
  filter(CooksD > influential_threshold) |>
  arrange(desc(CooksD))

cat("Number of potentially influential observations:", nrow(influential_df), "\n")

#> Number of potentially influential observations: 8

if (nrow(influential_df) > 0) print(head(influential_df, 10))

#>         USUBJID     CooksD
#> 91  01-705-1280 0.02823846
#> 111 01-708-1158 0.02673703
#> 116 01-708-1236 0.02628236
#> 98  01-705-1377 0.02517005
#> 32  01-701-1360 0.02468091
#> 94  01-705-1292 0.02207412
#> 20  01-701-1203 0.01692763
#> 251 01-718-1328 0.01629363




14.4.3 3.3 Cook’s Distance Plot

library(ggplot2)

data.frame(
  Index  = seq_along(cooks_d),
  CooksD = cooks_d
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Index, y = CooksD)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = Index, yend = 0), colour = "grey60") +
  geom_point(aes(colour = CooksD > influential_threshold), size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#2166ac", "TRUE" = "#d6604d"),
                      name = "Influential") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = influential_threshold, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#d6604d") +
  labs(title = "Cook's Distance — Multiple Regression Model",
       x = "Observation Index",
       y = "Cook's Distance") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.5 4. Model Selection

14.5.1 4.1 AIC Comparison
AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) penalises model complexity. Lower AIC indicates a better balance between fit and parsimony.

models <- list(
  "Age only"       = lm(SBP ~ AGE, data = analysis_df),
  "Age + Sex"      = lm(SBP ~ AGE + SEX, data = analysis_df),
  "Age + Sex + Race" = lm(SBP ~ AGE + SEX + RACE, data = analysis_df),
  "Full model"     = model_multi
)

aic_tbl <- data.frame(
  Model = names(models),
  AIC   = sapply(models, AIC),
  BIC   = sapply(models, BIC),
  R2    = sapply(models, \(m) summary(m)$r.squared)
)
aic_tbl <- aic_tbl[order(aic_tbl$AIC), ]
aic_tbl[, -1] <- round(aic_tbl[, -1], 3)
print(aic_tbl)

#>                             Model      AIC      BIC    R2
#> Age + Sex               Age + Sex 2179.717 2193.866 0.125
#> Age + Sex + Race Age + Sex + Race 2182.633 2203.857 0.129
#> Full model             Full model 2185.606 2213.904 0.132
#> Age only                 Age only 2195.405 2206.017 0.062




14.5.2 4.2 Stepwise Variable Selection

# Start from full model; step() uses AIC by default
full_model <- lm(SBP ~ AGE + SEX + RACE + ARM, data = analysis_df)
step_model  <- step(full_model, direction = "both", trace = 0)
cat("Selected model formula:\n")

#> Selected model formula:

print(formula(step_model))

#> SBP ~ AGE + SEX

summary(step_model)$coefficients |> round(4)

#>             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
#> (Intercept) 106.7165    10.2275 10.4343    0e+00
#> AGE           0.5182     0.1338  3.8726    1e-04
#> SEXM         -9.4459     2.2201 -4.2547    0e+00
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.6 5. Polynomial Regression — Quadratic Age Effect
A quadratic term allows for a curved (non-linear) relationship between age and SBP.

model_poly <- lm(SBP ~ poly(AGE, 2, raw = TRUE), data = analysis_df)
summary(model_poly)

#> 
#> Call:
#> lm(formula = SBP ~ poly(AGE, 2, raw = TRUE), data = analysis_df)
#> 
#> Residuals:
#>     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
#> -36.481 -13.310  -0.271  13.187  63.187 
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
#> (Intercept)               64.727859  73.494090   0.881    0.379
#> poly(AGE, 2, raw = TRUE)1  1.533333   2.049796   0.748    0.455
#> poly(AGE, 2, raw = TRUE)2 -0.006724   0.014163  -0.475    0.635
#> 
#> Residual standard error: 18.11 on 251 degrees of freedom
#> Multiple R-squared:  0.06281,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.05534 
#> F-statistic:  8.41 on 2 and 251 DF,  p-value: 0.0002915



14.6.1 5.1 Comparing Linear vs Quadratic Fits

anova(model_simple, model_poly)

#> Analysis of Variance Table
#> 
#> Model 1: SBP ~ AGE
#> Model 2: SBP ~ poly(AGE, 2, raw = TRUE)
#>   Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)
#> 1    252 82385                           
#> 2    251 82311  1    73.908 0.2254 0.6354



library(ggplot2)

age_seq <- data.frame(AGE = seq(min(analysis_df$AGE),
                                 max(analysis_df$AGE),
                                 length.out = 100))

age_seq$Linear    <- predict(model_simple, newdata = age_seq)
age_seq$Quadratic <- predict(model_poly,   newdata = age_seq)

pred_long <- tidyr::pivot_longer(age_seq, cols = c("Linear", "Quadratic"),
                                  names_to = "Model", values_to = "SBP")

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = analysis_df, aes(x = AGE, y = SBP),
             alpha = 0.35, colour = "grey50") +
  geom_line(data = pred_long, aes(x = AGE, y = SBP, colour = Model),
            linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Linear" = "#2166ac",
                                  "Quadratic" = "#d6604d")) +
  labs(title = "Linear vs Quadratic Age Effect on SBP",
       x = "Age (years)", y = "Systolic BP (mmHg)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.7 6. Logistic Regression — Predicting AE Occurrence
Logistic regression models the probability of a binary outcome. Here, we ask: which baseline characteristics predict whether a subject experienced any adverse event?

14.7.1 6.1 Creating the Binary Outcome

library(dplyr)

# Subjects with at least one AE
ae_flag <- ae |>
  filter(!is.na(USUBJID)) |>
  distinct(USUBJID) |>
  mutate(ANY_AE = 1L)

# Join to analysis dataset
logit_df <- dm_clean |>
  left_join(ae_flag, by = "USUBJID") |>
  mutate(ANY_AE = if_else(is.na(ANY_AE), 0L, ANY_AE)) |>
  filter(!is.na(AGE)) |>
  mutate(SEX  = factor(SEX),
         RACE = factor(RACE),
         ANY_AE = factor(ANY_AE, levels = c(0, 1),
                         labels = c("No AE", "AE")))

cat("AE occurrence:\n")

#> AE occurrence:

print(table(logit_df$ANY_AE, logit_df$ARM))

#>        
#>         Placebo Xanomeline Low Dose Xanomeline High Dose
#>   No AE      17                   7                    5
#>   AE         69                  77                   79




14.7.2 6.2 Fitting the Logistic Regression Model

model_logit <- glm(ANY_AE ~ AGE + SEX + ARM,
                   data   = logit_df,
                   family = binomial(link = "logit"))
summary(model_logit)

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = ANY_AE ~ AGE + SEX + ARM, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
#>     data = logit_df)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                         Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
#> (Intercept)             -0.28872    1.81451  -0.159   0.8736  
#> AGE                      0.01845    0.02375   0.777   0.4372  
#> SEXM                     0.95305    0.46442   2.052   0.0402 *
#> ARMXanomeline Low Dose   0.99995    0.48364   2.068   0.0387 *
#> ARMXanomeline High Dose  1.28243    0.54092   2.371   0.0177 *
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 180.42  on 253  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 166.63  on 249  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 176.63
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5




14.7.3 6.3 Odds Ratios and Confidence Intervals
Exponentiate the log-odds coefficients to obtain odds ratios (OR). An OR > 1 means increased odds of AE occurrence.

or_tbl <- data.frame(
  OR       = exp(coef(model_logit)),
  Lower_95 = exp(confint.default(model_logit)[, 1]),
  Upper_95 = exp(confint.default(model_logit)[, 2])
)
print(round(or_tbl, 3))

#>                            OR Lower_95 Upper_95
#> (Intercept)             0.749    0.021   26.250
#> AGE                     1.019    0.972    1.067
#> SEXM                    2.594    1.044    6.445
#> ARMXanomeline Low Dose  2.718    1.053    7.014
#> ARMXanomeline High Dose 3.605    1.249   10.408




14.7.4 6.4 Forest Plot of Odds Ratios

library(ggplot2)

or_plot_df <- or_tbl[-1, ]   # drop intercept
or_plot_df$Term <- rownames(or_plot_df)

ggplot(or_plot_df, aes(x = OR, y = reorder(Term, OR))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = Lower_95, xmax = Upper_95),
                 height = 0.25, colour = "#2166ac") +
  geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#d6604d") +
  labs(title = "Odds Ratios for AE Occurrence",
       x = "Odds Ratio (95% CI)",
       y = NULL) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.8 7. Model Evaluation

14.8.1 7.1 Confusion Matrix
We use a 0.5 probability threshold to classify predictions.

pred_probs <- predict(model_logit, newdata = logit_df, type = "response")
pred_class <- factor(if_else(pred_probs >= 0.5, "AE", "No AE"),
                     levels = c("No AE", "AE"))
actual     <- factor(as.character(logit_df$ANY_AE), levels = c("No AE", "AE"))

conf_mat <- table(
  Predicted = pred_class,
  Actual    = actual
)
print(conf_mat)

#>          Actual
#> Predicted No AE  AE
#>     No AE     0   0
#>     AE       29 225

# Derived metrics (with safe fallbacks for zero cells)
tp <- conf_mat["AE", "AE"]
tn <- conf_mat["No AE", "No AE"]
fp <- conf_mat["AE", "No AE"]
fn <- conf_mat["No AE", "AE"]

sensitivity <- if ((tp + fn) > 0) tp / (tp + fn) else NA_real_
specificity  <- if ((tn + fp) > 0) tn / (tn + fp) else NA_real_
accuracy     <- (tp + tn) / sum(conf_mat)

cat(sprintf("\nSensitivity : %.3f\n", sensitivity))

#> 
#> Sensitivity : 1.000

cat(sprintf("Specificity : %.3f\n", specificity))

#> Specificity : 0.000

cat(sprintf("Accuracy    : %.3f\n", accuracy))

#> Accuracy    : 0.886




14.8.2 7.2 ROC Curve Concept
The ROC curve plots sensitivity vs (1 - specificity) across all classification thresholds. Here we build it manually without additional packages.

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

roc_df <- data.frame(
  prob   = pred_probs,
  actual = logit_df$ANY_AE == "AE"
) |>
  arrange(desc(prob))

thresholds <- sort(unique(roc_df$prob), decreasing = TRUE)

roc_points <- lapply(thresholds, function(thr) {
  pred_pos <- roc_df$prob >= thr
  tp_i  <- sum( pred_pos &  roc_df$actual)
  fp_i  <- sum( pred_pos & !roc_df$actual)
  fn_i  <- sum(!pred_pos &  roc_df$actual)
  tn_i  <- sum(!pred_pos & !roc_df$actual)
  data.frame(
    TPR = tp_i / (tp_i + fn_i),
    FPR = fp_i / (fp_i + tn_i)
  )
}) |>
  dplyr::bind_rows()

# Add (0,0) and (1,1)
roc_curve <- rbind(data.frame(TPR = 0, FPR = 0),
                    roc_points,
                    data.frame(TPR = 1, FPR = 1))

ggplot(roc_curve, aes(x = FPR, y = TPR)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2166ac", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
              colour = "grey60") +
  labs(title = "ROC Curve — Logistic Regression (AE Occurrence)",
       x = "1 - Specificity (False Positive Rate)",
       y = "Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)") +
  coord_equal() +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
section: 14.9 8. Poisson Regression — Count of AEs per Subject
text:
14.9 8. Poisson Regression — Count of AEs per Subject
When the outcome is a count (e.g., number of AEs), Poisson regression is appropriate.

14.9.1 8.1 Building the Count Outcome

library(dplyr)

ae_counts <- ae |>
  filter(!is.na(USUBJID)) |>
  group_by(USUBJID) |>
  summarise(N_AE = n(), .groups = "drop")

poisson_df <- dm_clean |>
  left_join(ae_counts, by = "USUBJID") |>
  mutate(N_AE = if_else(is.na(N_AE), 0L, as.integer(N_AE)),
         SEX  = factor(SEX),
         RACE = factor(RACE)) |>
  filter(!is.na(AGE))

cat("AE count summary:\n")

#> AE count summary:

print(summary(poisson_df$N_AE))

#>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
#>   0.000   2.000   4.000   4.689   7.000  23.000




14.9.2 8.2 Fitting the Poisson Model

model_poisson <- glm(N_AE ~ AGE + SEX + ARM,
                     data   = poisson_df,
                     family = poisson(link = "log"))
summary(model_poisson)

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = N_AE ~ AGE + SEX + ARM, family = poisson(link = "log"), 
#>     data = poisson_df)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                          Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
#> (Intercept)              1.362972   0.272426   5.003 5.64e-07 ***
#> AGE                     -0.002697   0.003520  -0.766 0.443644    
#> SEXM                     0.223815   0.058517   3.825 0.000131 ***
#> ARMXanomeline Low Dose   0.388643   0.075017   5.181 2.21e-07 ***
#> ARMXanomeline High Dose  0.403324   0.074767   5.394 6.87e-08 ***
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for poisson family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 859.88  on 253  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 802.67  on 249  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 1552.6
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5




14.9.3 8.3 Incidence Rate Ratios
Exponentiating Poisson coefficients gives Incidence Rate Ratios (IRR).

irr_tbl <- data.frame(
  IRR      = exp(coef(model_poisson)),
  Lower_95 = exp(confint.default(model_poisson)[, 1]),
  Upper_95 = exp(confint.default(model_poisson)[, 2])
)
print(round(irr_tbl, 3))

#>                           IRR Lower_95 Upper_95
#> (Intercept)             3.908    2.291    6.665
#> AGE                     0.997    0.990    1.004
#> SEXM                    1.251    1.115    1.403
#> ARMXanomeline Low Dose  1.475    1.273    1.709
#> ARMXanomeline High Dose 1.497    1.293    1.733




14.9.4 8.4 Checking for Overdispersion
A key assumption of Poisson regression is that the mean equals the variance. We test for overdispersion by computing the ratio of residual deviance to degrees of freedom.

disp_ratio <- model_poisson$deviance / model_poisson$df.residual
cat(sprintf("Dispersion ratio: %.3f\n", disp_ratio))

#> Dispersion ratio: 3.224

cat("A ratio >> 1 suggests overdispersion; consider negative binomial model.\n")

#> A ratio >> 1 suggests overdispersion; consider negative binomial model.




14.9.5 8.5 AE Count Distribution by ARM

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(poisson_df, aes(x = ARM, y = N_AE, fill = ARM)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.alpha = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Placebo"               = "#4393c3",
                                "Xanomeline Low Dose"   = "#f4a582",
                                "Xanomeline High Dose"  = "#d6604d")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of AE Counts by Treatment Arm",
       x = NULL, y = "Number of Adverse Events") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.10 9. Coefficient Interpretation Reference








Model
Coefficient
Interpretation




Linear
beta
Change in mean outcome per 1-unit increase in predictor


Logistic
exp(beta)
Odds ratio: multiplicative change in odds of outcome


Poisson
exp(beta)
Incidence rate ratio: multiplicative change in rate




# Collect all key estimates in one table
cat("=== Linear Regression: AGE coefficient ===\n")

#> === Linear Regression: AGE coefficient ===

cat(sprintf("  Beta = %.4f (per year of age, SBP in mmHg)\n",
            coef(model_multi)["AGE"]))

#>   Beta = 0.5050 (per year of age, SBP in mmHg)

cat("\n=== Logistic Regression: AGE odds ratio ===\n")

#> 
#> === Logistic Regression: AGE odds ratio ===

cat(sprintf("  OR = %.4f (per year of age, odds of any AE)\n",
            exp(coef(model_logit)["AGE"])))

#>   OR = 1.0186 (per year of age, odds of any AE)

cat("\n=== Poisson Regression: AGE incidence rate ratio ===\n")

#> 
#> === Poisson Regression: AGE incidence rate ratio ===

cat(sprintf("  IRR = %.4f (per year of age, AE count rate)\n",
            exp(coef(model_poisson)["AGE"])))

#>   IRR = 0.9973 (per year of age, AE count rate)

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.11 Summary
This chapter demonstrated:

Simple and multiple linear regression to explain SBP variation using age, sex, race and treatment arm.
Model diagnostics including residual plots, Q-Q plots, and Cook’s distance.
Model selection via AIC and stepwise procedures.
Polynomial regression to capture non-linear age effects.
Logistic regression to predict binary AE occurrence, with odds ratios and a manually constructed ROC curve.
Poisson regression to model count outcomes (AE frequency per subject) with incidence rate ratios.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
14.12 Exercises

Fit a simple linear regression of diastolic blood pressure (VSTESTCD == "DIABP") on AGE. Compare the slope to the SBP result.
Extend the multiple linear regression for SBP by including a RACE × SEX interaction term. Does the interaction improve model fit (use anova())? Interpret any significant interaction.
Examine the Cook’s distance plot for the multiple regression model. Remove the most influential observation and refit the model. How do the coefficients change?
Using step(), perform forward stepwise selection starting from an intercept-only model for SBP. Do you arrive at the same model as backward selection?
Fit a logistic regression predicting serious AEs (AESER == "Y" in the ae dataset) from age and ARM. Compute and interpret the odds ratio for the Xanomeline High Dose arm relative to Placebo.
The Poisson dispersion ratio you computed may exceed 1. Refit the AE-count model using glm.nb() from the MASS package (call it as MASS::glm.nb()). Compare AIC values to decide which model is preferable.
Add WEIGHTBL (baseline weight) as a covariate to the SBP multiple regression if that variable is available in dm. Interpret its coefficient.
Construct a calibration plot for the logistic regression model: bin subjects by predicted probability (e.g., deciles), compute the observed proportion of AEs in each bin, and plot observed vs predicted. Does the model appear well calibrated?
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
14  Regression Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.1 Introduction
Many of the most important clinical outcomes are categorical: a patient either responds or does not; they experience an adverse event or remain event-free; they are cured, improved, stable, or worse. Categorical data analysis provides the statistical tools to summarise, test, and model these outcomes.
This chapter covers:
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
2×2 contingency tables: Cross-tabulation of binary variables
Chi-square and Fisher’s exact tests: Testing independence
Measures of association: Odds ratio, relative risk, number needed to treat
McNemar’s test: Paired binary outcomes
Ordinal outcomes: Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel and proportional odds
Logistic regression: Modelling binary outcomes with covariates
Stratified analysis and the CMH test: Controlling for confounders
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.2 1. Setting Up Categorical Clinical Data

library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(gt)
set.seed(42)

# Simulated Phase III RCT: binary endpoint (responder yes/no)
n_per_arm <- 150
trial_data <- tibble(
  subject_id = paste0("SUBJ-", sprintf("%03d", 1:(n_per_arm * 2))),
  arm        = rep(c("Active", "Placebo"), each = n_per_arm),
  age        = round(rnorm(n_per_arm * 2, mean = 52, sd = 12)),
  sex        = sample(c("Female", "Male"), n_per_arm * 2, replace = TRUE,
                      prob = c(0.52, 0.48)),
  responder  = c(
    rbinom(n_per_arm, 1, prob = 0.58),   # Active: 58% response
    rbinom(n_per_arm, 1, prob = 0.38)    # Placebo: 38% response
  ),
  ae_serious = c(
    rbinom(n_per_arm, 1, prob = 0.08),   # Active: 8% SAE
    rbinom(n_per_arm, 1, prob = 0.05)    # Placebo: 5% SAE
  ),
  disease_severity = sample(c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"),
                            n_per_arm * 2, replace = TRUE,
                            prob = c(0.3, 0.5, 0.2))
)

# Quick summary
cat("=== Trial Summary ===\n")

#> === Trial Summary ===

trial_data |>
  group_by(arm) |>
  summarise(
    N             = n(),
    Responders    = sum(responder),
    Response_rate = paste0(round(mean(responder) * 100, 1), "%"),
    SAE_n         = sum(ae_serious),
    SAE_rate      = paste0(round(mean(ae_serious) * 100, 1), "%")
  ) |>
  print()

#> # A tibble: 2 × 6
#>   arm         N Responders Response_rate SAE_n SAE_rate
#>   <chr>   <int>      <int> <chr>         <int> <chr>   
#> 1 Active    150         95 63.3%             9 6%      
#> 2 Placebo   150         57 38%               7 4.7%
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.3 2. Contingency Tables

15.3.1 2.1 The 2×2 Table
A 2×2 contingency table cross-classifies two binary variables:




Response: Yes
Response: No
Total




Active
a
b
a+b


Placebo
c
d
c+d


Total
a+c
b+d
N




# Build 2x2 table
ct <- table(trial_data$arm, trial_data$responder,
            dnn = c("Arm", "Responder"))
colnames(ct) <- c("No", "Yes")

cat("=== 2×2 Contingency Table ===\n")

#> === 2×2 Contingency Table ===

print(ct)

#>          Responder
#> Arm       No Yes
#>   Active  55  95
#>   Placebo 93  57

cat("\nRow proportions:\n")

#> 
#> Row proportions:

print(round(prop.table(ct, margin = 1) * 100, 1))

#>          Responder
#> Arm         No  Yes
#>   Active  36.7 63.3
#>   Placebo 62.0 38.0



tibble(
  arm       = c("Active", "Active", "Placebo", "Placebo"),
  response  = c("Responder", "Non-responder", "Responder", "Non-responder"),
  n         = c(ct["Active", "Yes"], ct["Active", "No"],
                ct["Placebo", "Yes"], ct["Placebo", "No"])
) |>
  mutate(
    arm      = factor(arm,      levels = c("Placebo", "Active")),
    response = factor(response, levels = c("Non-responder", "Responder"))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = arm, y = n, fill = response)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", alpha = 0.85, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(n, "\n(", round(n / sum(n[arm == arm]) * 100, 1), "%)")),
    position = position_fill(vjust = 0.5), size = 3.5, colour = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Responder" = "#2C6E9E", "Non-responder" = "#D32F2F")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(
    x = "Treatment Arm",
    y = "Proportion",
    fill = NULL,
    title = "Response Rates by Treatment Arm",
    subtitle = "Stacked bar shows proportion of responders"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")




Mosaic plot: proportion of responders by treatment arm
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.4 3. Chi-Square Test of Independence

15.4.1 3.1 Pearson’s Chi-Square
The \(\chi^2\) test evaluates whether two categorical variables are independent. The test statistic:
\[\chi^2 = \sum_{i,j} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}\]
where \(E_{ij} = \frac{(\text{row total}_i)(\text{col total}_j)}{N}\).
Assumptions: \(E_{ij} \geq 5\) for all cells; if not, use Fisher’s exact test.

chi_result <- chisq.test(ct, correct = FALSE)

cat("=== Chi-Square Test of Independence ===\n")

#> === Chi-Square Test of Independence ===

cat("χ² =", round(chi_result$statistic, 4),
    ", df =", chi_result$parameter,
    ", p =", round(chi_result$p.value, 4), "\n\n")

#> χ² = 19.2568 , df = 1 , p = 0

cat("Expected frequencies:\n")

#> Expected frequencies:

print(round(chi_result$expected, 1))

#>          Responder
#> Arm       No Yes
#>   Active  74  76
#>   Placebo 74  76

cat("\nAll expected values ≥ 5:", all(chi_result$expected >= 5), "\n")

#> 
#> All expected values ≥ 5: TRUE




15.4.2 3.2 Fisher’s Exact Test
When expected cell counts are small (< 5), Fisher’s exact test is preferred. It computes exact probabilities based on the hypergeometric distribution.

# Small-sample example: SAE comparison
sae_ct <- table(trial_data$arm, trial_data$ae_serious,
                dnn = c("Arm", "SAE"))
colnames(sae_ct) <- c("No SAE", "SAE")

cat("=== SAE Contingency Table ===\n")

#> === SAE Contingency Table ===

print(sae_ct)

#>          SAE
#> Arm       No SAE SAE
#>   Active     141   9
#>   Placebo    143   7

cat("\nExpected frequencies:\n")

#> 
#> Expected frequencies:

print(round(chisq.test(sae_ct)$expected, 1))

#>          SAE
#> Arm       No SAE SAE
#>   Active     142   8
#>   Placebo    142   8

# Use Fisher's exact test
fisher_result <- fisher.test(sae_ct)
cat("\nFisher's Exact Test:\n")

#> 
#> Fisher's Exact Test:

cat("  OR =", round(fisher_result$estimate, 3),
    ", p =", round(fisher_result$p.value, 4), "\n")

#>   OR = 0.768 , p = 0.7982

cat("  95% CI: [", round(fisher_result$conf.int[1], 3), ",",
    round(fisher_result$conf.int[2], 3), "]\n")

#>   95% CI: [ 0.236 , 2.388 ]
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.5 4. Measures of Association

15.5.1 4.1 Relative Risk, Odds Ratio, and NNT

# Extract cell values from response table
a <- ct["Active",  "Yes"]   # Active responders
b <- ct["Active",  "No"]    # Active non-responders
c <- ct["Placebo", "Yes"]   # Placebo responders
d <- ct["Placebo", "No"]    # Placebo non-responders

# Compute measures
rr   <- (a / (a + b)) / (c / (c + d))
or   <- (a * d) / (b * c)
rd   <- (a / (a + b)) - (c / (c + d))   # Risk difference (ARD)
nnt  <- 1 / rd                            # Number needed to treat

# 95% CI for RR (log method)
se_log_rr <- sqrt(b / (a * (a + b)) + d / (c * (c + d)))
rr_ci     <- exp(log(rr) + c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.975) * se_log_rr)

# 95% CI for OR
se_log_or <- sqrt(1/a + 1/b + 1/c + 1/d)
or_ci     <- exp(log(or) + c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.975) * se_log_or)

tibble(
  Measure     = c("Response rate (Active)", "Response rate (Placebo)",
                  "Risk Difference (ARD)", "Relative Risk (RR)",
                  "Odds Ratio (OR)", "Number Needed to Treat (NNT)"),
  Estimate    = c(
    paste0(round(a / (a + b) * 100, 1), "%"),
    paste0(round(c / (c + d) * 100, 1), "%"),
    paste0(round(rd * 100, 1), "% (", round(rd * 100, 1), " pp)"),
    round(rr, 3),
    round(or, 3),
    round(nnt, 1)
  ),
  `95% CI` = c(
    "—", "—", "—",
    paste0("[", round(rr_ci[1], 3), ", ", round(rr_ci[2], 3), "]"),
    paste0("[", round(or_ci[1], 3), ", ", round(or_ci[2], 3), "]"),
    "—"
  )
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Measures of Association: Response Endpoint")







Measures of Association: Response Endpoint


Measure
Estimate
95% CI




Response rate (Active)
63.3%
—


Response rate (Placebo)
38%
—


Risk Difference (ARD)
25.3% (25.3 pp)
—


Relative Risk (RR)
1.667
[1.314, 2.114]


Odds Ratio (OR)
2.818
[1.765, 4.5]


Number Needed to Treat (NNT)
3.9
—









15.5.2 4.2 Interpreting Odds Ratios vs. Relative Risks

cat("Key distinction: OR vs. RR\n\n")

#> Key distinction: OR vs. RR

cat("When response rates are high (>10%), OR overestimates RR:\n")

#> When response rates are high (>10%), OR overestimates RR:

cat("  Active response rate:", round(a / (a + b) * 100, 1), "%\n")

#>   Active response rate: 63.3 %

cat("  Placebo response rate:", round(c / (c + d) * 100, 1), "%\n")

#>   Placebo response rate: 38 %

cat("  RR =", round(rr, 3), "(Active", round(rr, 1), "× more likely to respond)\n")

#>   RR = 1.667 (Active 1.7 × more likely to respond)

cat("  OR =", round(or, 3), "(inflated because outcomes are common)\n\n")

#>   OR = 2.818 (inflated because outcomes are common)

cat("Rule of thumb: when event rate < 10%, OR ≈ RR.\n")

#> Rule of thumb: when event rate < 10%, OR ≈ RR.

cat("For rare events (SAEs), OR and RR are nearly identical.\n")

#> For rare events (SAEs), OR and RR are nearly identical.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
section: 15.6 5. McNemar’s Test for Paired Binary Data
text:
15.6 5. McNemar’s Test for Paired Binary Data
When the same subject is assessed before and after treatment, or matched pairs are compared, use McNemar’s test.

# Crossover study: response at Period 1 (Active) and Period 2 (Placebo)
set.seed(55)
n_crossover <- 100
crossover_data <- tibble(
  subject_id  = paste0("XO-", sprintf("%03d", 1:n_crossover)),
  period1_act = rbinom(n_crossover, 1, 0.60),   # Active period
  period2_pbo = rbinom(n_crossover, 1, 0.42)    # Placebo period
)

# McNemar table
mcn_table <- table(
  Active  = crossover_data$period1_act,
  Placebo = crossover_data$period2_pbo
)
cat("McNemar Table (Active period vs. Placebo period):\n")

#> McNemar Table (Active period vs. Placebo period):

print(mcn_table)

#>       Placebo
#> Active  0  1
#>      0 28 19
#>      1 32 21

mcn_result <- mcnemar.test(mcn_table, correct = FALSE)
cat("\nMcNemar's Test:\n")

#> 
#> McNemar's Test:

cat("  χ² =", round(mcn_result$statistic, 3),
    ", df =", mcn_result$parameter,
    ", p =", round(mcn_result$p.value, 4), "\n")

#>   χ² = 3.314 , df = 1 , p = 0.0687

cat("\nNote: Only discordant pairs (01 and 10) contribute information.\n")

#> 
#> Note: Only discordant pairs (01 and 10) contribute information.

cat("  Active yes / Placebo no (b):", mcn_table[2, 1], "\n")

#>   Active yes / Placebo no (b): 32

cat("  Active no / Placebo yes (c):", mcn_table[1, 2], "\n")

#>   Active no / Placebo yes (c): 19
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.7 6. Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel Test: Stratified Analysis
The CMH test extends the chi-square test to stratified 2×2 tables, allowing control of a categorical confounder.

# Stratify by disease severity
cmh_data <- trial_data |>
  mutate(
    responder_f  = factor(responder, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("No", "Yes")),
    severity_f   = factor(disease_severity,
                          levels = c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"))
  )

# 3-dimensional table: arm × response × severity
cmh_table <- xtabs(~ arm + responder_f + severity_f, data = cmh_data)

cmh_result <- mantelhaen.test(cmh_table)
cat("=== Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel Test ===\n")

#> === Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel Test ===

cat("Stratification variable: Disease Severity\n\n")

#> Stratification variable: Disease Severity

cat("CMH χ² =", round(cmh_result$statistic, 3),
    ", df =", cmh_result$parameter,
    ", p =", round(cmh_result$p.value, 4), "\n")

#> CMH χ² = 17.749 , df = 1 , p = 0

cat("Common OR estimate:", round(cmh_result$estimate, 3), "\n")

#> Common OR estimate: 0.356

cat("95% CI: [", round(cmh_result$conf.int[1], 3), ",",
    round(cmh_result$conf.int[2], 3), "]\n")

#> 95% CI: [ 0.222 , 0.571 ]

# Stratum-specific response rates
cat("\nResponse rates by stratum:\n")

#> 
#> Response rates by stratum:

cmh_data |>
  group_by(arm, disease_severity) |>
  summarise(n = n(), resp = sum(responder),
            rate = paste0(round(mean(responder) * 100, 1), "%"),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = arm, values_from = c(n, resp, rate)) |>
  print()

#> # A tibble: 3 × 7
#>   disease_severity n_Active n_Placebo resp_Active resp_Placebo rate_Active
#>   <chr>               <int>     <int>       <int>        <int> <chr>      
#> 1 Mild                   48        40          32           20 66.7%      
#> 2 Moderate               76        80          43           26 56.6%      
#> 3 Severe                 26        30          20           11 76.9%      
#> # ℹ 1 more variable: rate_Placebo <chr>
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.8 7. Logistic Regression for Binary Outcomes

15.8.1 7.1 The Logistic Model
When we want to adjust for covariates in a binary outcome analysis, we use logistic regression:
\[\log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p\]
The exponentiated coefficients \(e^{\beta_j}\) are adjusted odds ratios.

# Logistic regression: response ~ arm + age + sex + disease_severity
logit_model <- glm(
  responder ~ arm + age + sex + disease_severity,
  data   = trial_data,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

tidy(logit_model, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(across(c(estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value),
                \(x) round(x, 4))) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Logistic Regression: Adjusted Odds Ratios",
    subtitle = "Outcome: Clinical Responder"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    term      = "Predictor",
    estimate  = "Adjusted OR",
    std.error = "SE (log scale)",
    statistic = "z",
    p.value   = "p-value",
    conf.low  = "95% CI Lower",
    conf.high = "95% CI Upper"
  )







Logistic Regression: Adjusted Odds Ratios


Outcome: Clinical Responder


Predictor
Adjusted OR
SE (log scale)
z
p-value
95% CI Lower
95% CI Upper




armPlacebo
0.3539
0.24163322
-4.2989330

15.8.2 7.2 Model Diagnostics and Goodness of Fit

# Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit (manual decile calculation)
trial_data_fitted <- trial_data |>
  mutate(fitted_prob = fitted(logit_model))

hl_data <- trial_data_fitted |>
  mutate(decile = ntile(fitted_prob, 10)) |>
  group_by(decile) |>
  summarise(
    n_obs  = n(),
    obs    = sum(responder),
    exp    = sum(fitted_prob),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Hosmer-Lemeshow diagnostic (observed vs. expected by decile):\n")

#> Hosmer-Lemeshow diagnostic (observed vs. expected by decile):

hl_data |>
  mutate(
    obs_rate = paste0(round(obs / n_obs * 100, 1), "%"),
    exp_rate = paste0(round(exp / n_obs * 100, 1), "%")
  ) |>
  select(decile, n_obs, obs_rate, exp_rate) |>
  print()

#> # A tibble: 10 × 4
#>    decile n_obs obs_rate exp_rate
#>     <int> <int> <chr>    <chr>   
#>  1      1    30 36.7%    30.3%   
#>  2      2    30 30%      32.3%   
#>  3      3    30 23.3%    36.8%   
#>  4      4    30 50%      44%     
#>  5      5    30 50%      46.7%   
#>  6      6    30 53.3%    55.1%   
#>  7      7    30 53.3%    57.4%   
#>  8      8    30 73.3%    63.5%   
#>  9      9    30 73.3%    69.1%   
#> 10     10    30 63.3%    71.5%



# ROC curve using sorted fitted probabilities
roc_data <- trial_data_fitted |>
  arrange(desc(fitted_prob)) |>
  mutate(
    tp = cumsum(responder),
    fp = cumsum(1 - responder),
    tpr = tp / sum(responder),
    fpr = fp / sum(1 - responder)
  )

auc_val <- with(roc_data, {
  dx <- diff(fpr)
  dy <- (tpr[-1] + tpr[-length(tpr)]) / 2
  abs(sum(dx * dy))
})

roc_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fpr, y = tpr)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2C6E9E", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.6, y = 0.3,
           label = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc_val, 3)),
           size = 4, fontface = "bold", colour = "#2C6E9E") +
  labs(
    x = "False Positive Rate (1 – Specificity)",
    y = "True Positive Rate (Sensitivity)",
    title = "ROC Curve: Logistic Regression Model",
    subtitle = "Predicting clinical response"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  coord_equal()




ROC curve for the logistic regression model predicting clinical response






15.8.3 7.3 Predicted Probabilities by Treatment Arm

trial_data_fitted |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fitted_prob, fill = arm)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.65) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Active" = "#2C6E9E", "Placebo" = "#E07B39")) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "black") +
  labs(
    x     = "Predicted Probability of Response",
    y     = "Density",
    fill  = "Treatment",
    title = "Predicted Response Probabilities by Arm",
    subtitle = "Vertical line = 0.5 decision threshold"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")




Distribution of predicted response probabilities by treatment arm
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.9 8. Ordinal Outcomes: Proportional Odds Model
When outcomes have more than two ordered categories (e.g., much worse / worse / same / better / much better), the proportional odds logistic regression model is appropriate.

library(MASS)

# Convert severity to ordered outcome
outcomes <- c("Much worse", "Worse", "No change", "Better", "Much better")
ordinal_data <- trial_data |>
  mutate(
    outcome_ord = factor(
      sapply(arm, function(a) {
        probs <- if (a == "Active") c(0.03, 0.07, 0.15, 0.45, 0.30)
                 else               c(0.05, 0.15, 0.30, 0.35, 0.15)
        sample(outcomes, 1, prob = probs)
      }),
      levels = outcomes,
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  )

# Proportional odds model
po_model <- polr(outcome_ord ~ arm + age + sex,
                 data    = ordinal_data,
                 Hess    = TRUE,
                 method  = "logistic")

# Coefficients as ORs
po_coefs <- exp(coef(po_model))
po_ci    <- exp(confint(po_model, level = 0.95))

cat("=== Proportional Odds Model ===\n")

#> === Proportional Odds Model ===

cat("Cumulative Odds Ratios:\n")

#> Cumulative Odds Ratios:

tibble(
  Term     = names(po_coefs),
  OR       = round(po_coefs, 3),
  CI_lower = round(po_ci[, 1], 3),
  CI_upper = round(po_ci[, 2], 3)
) |> print()

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   Term          OR CI_lower CI_upper
#>   <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 armPlacebo 0.261    0.167    0.405
#> 2 age        1.00     0.985    1.02 
#> 3 sexMale    0.713    0.47     1.08
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
15.10 Summary
Categorical data analysis is central to clinical trial evaluation. Key takeaways:

2×2 tables with chi-square or Fisher’s exact test are the workhorse for binary outcomes; choose Fisher’s when expected counts are < 5
Odds ratios are natural parameters for logistic regression and case-control studies; relative risks are more interpretable for RCTs with common outcomes
NNT (number needed to treat) translates statistical results into clinical practice language
McNemar’s test handles paired binary data correctly — never use chi-square for matched data
CMH test controls for confounders in stratified analyses, producing an adjusted common OR
Logistic regression allows adjustment for multiple covariates simultaneously; report adjusted ORs with 95% CIs
Proportional odds models are the appropriate tool for ordered categorical outcomes


Regulatory note: For confirmatory trials with a binary primary endpoint, the analysis method (chi-square, CMH, logistic regression), stratification factors, and covariate adjustment strategy must be pre-specified in the Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP). Post-hoc choice of analysis method is not acceptable.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
15  Categorical Data Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
16.1 Introduction
Sample size determination is one of the most consequential decisions in clinical trial design. An underpowered study risks missing a real treatment effect; an overpowered study wastes resources and exposes more patients than necessary to experimental treatments. Regulatory agencies require prospective justification of sample size in every confirmatory trial protocol.
This chapter covers:
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
16  Sample Size and Power Calculations

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
Fundamental concepts: Effect size, power, Type I and II errors
Two-sample t-test: Continuous endpoints
Two proportions: Binary endpoints
Survival endpoints: Log-rank test and event-driven designs
Equivalence and non-inferiority: TOST and one-sided designs
Multiple comparisons: Adjusting for multiplicity
Sensitivity analyses: Handling uncertainty in design assumptions
R packages: pwr, pwrss, gsDesign, rpact
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
16  Sample Size and Power Calculations

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
16.2 1. Core Concepts

16.2.1 1.1 The Four Design Parameters
Any sample size calculation involves four interdependent quantities. Fix any three and the fourth is determined:








Parameter
Symbol
Typical value




Significance level (Type I error rate)
\(\alpha\)
0.05 (two-sided)


Power (1 − Type II error rate)
\(1-\beta\)
0.80 or 0.90


Effect size (standardised or absolute)
\(\delta\)
Clinically meaningful difference


Sample size
\(n\)
To be determined




library(tidyverse)
library(pwr)
library(gt)
set.seed(42)

# Illustrate power as a function of sample size
alpha  <- 0.05
delta  <- 0.5    # medium standardised effect (Cohen's d)
n_vals <- seq(10, 300, by = 5)

power_vals <- sapply(n_vals, function(n) {
  pwr.t.test(n = n, d = delta, sig.level = alpha,
             type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")$power
})

tibble(n = n_vals, power = power_vals) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = power)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2C6E9E", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#D32F2F") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.90, linetype = "dotted", colour = "#E07B39") +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = pwr.t.test(d = delta, sig.level = alpha,
                             power = 0.80, type = "two.sample")$n,
    linetype = "dashed", colour = "#D32F2F", alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = 5, y = 0.82, label = "80% power",
           colour = "#D32F2F", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 5, y = 0.92, label = "90% power",
           colour = "#E07B39", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(
    x     = "Sample Size per Arm",
    y     = "Power",
    title = "Statistical Power vs. Sample Size",
    subtitle = paste0("Two-sample t-test, δ = ", delta, " (Cohen's d), α = ", alpha)
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)










16.2.2 1.2 Effect Size: The Most Critical Input
The effect size must reflect the minimum clinically important difference (MCID) — the smallest difference that would matter to patients or clinicians — not the largest plausible effect.

d_vals <- seq(0.1, 1.5, by = 0.05)
n_needed <- sapply(d_vals, function(d) {
  ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = d, sig.level = 0.05,
                     power = 0.80, type = "two.sample")$n)
})

tibble(effect_size = d_vals, n_per_arm = n_needed) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = effect_size, y = n_per_arm)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2C6E9E", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = tibble(
    effect_size = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.8),
    n_per_arm   = c(
      ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.2, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = "two.sample")$n),
      ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = "two.sample")$n),
      ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.8, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = "two.sample")$n)
    ),
    label = c("Small (0.2)\nn=393", "Medium (0.5)\nn=64", "Large (0.8)\nn=26")
  ), aes(x = effect_size, y = n_per_arm), colour = "#D32F2F", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.8), y = c(450, 120, 80),
           label = c("Small (0.2)\nn=393", "Medium (0.5)\nn=64", "Large (0.8)\nn=26"),
           colour = "#D32F2F", size = 3) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    x     = "Standardised Effect Size (Cohen's d)",
    y     = "Sample Size per Arm (log scale)",
    title = "Effect Size Has a Dramatic Impact on Sample Size",
    subtitle = "Two-sample t-test, 80% power, α = 0.05"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)




Sample size per arm as a function of effect size (two-sample t-test, 80% power, α = 0.05)
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
16  Sample Size and Power Calculations

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
16.3 2. Sample Size for Continuous Endpoints

16.3.1 2.1 Two-Sample t-Test
The required sample size per arm:
\[n = \frac{2\sigma^2(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2}{\delta^2}\]
where \(\delta\) is the mean difference, \(\sigma\) is the common standard deviation.

# Example: Systolic blood pressure trial
# MCID = 5 mmHg reduction; SD = 15 mmHg; α = 0.05 (two-sided); 90% power

delta_sbp <- 5       # MCID: 5 mmHg
sigma_sbp <- 15      # assumed SD
alpha_sbp <- 0.05
power_sbp <- 0.90

ss_sbp <- pwr.t.test(
  d          = delta_sbp / sigma_sbp,   # Cohen's d
  sig.level  = alpha_sbp,
  power      = power_sbp,
  type       = "two.sample",
  alternative = "two.sided"
)

n_sbp <- ceiling(ss_sbp$n)

cat("=== SBP Trial Sample Size ===\n")

#> === SBP Trial Sample Size ===

cat("MCID (δ):      ", delta_sbp, "mmHg\n")

#> MCID (δ):       5 mmHg

cat("Assumed SD:    ", sigma_sbp, "mmHg\n")

#> Assumed SD:     15 mmHg

cat("Cohen's d:     ", round(delta_sbp / sigma_sbp, 3), "\n")

#> Cohen's d:      0.333

cat("α (two-sided): ", alpha_sbp, "\n")

#> α (two-sided):  0.05

cat("Power (1-β):   ", power_sbp, "\n")

#> Power (1-β):    0.9

cat("n per arm:     ", n_sbp, "\n")

#> n per arm:      191

cat("Total N:       ", n_sbp * 2, "\n\n")

#> Total N:        382

# Sensitivity: vary SD assumption
cat("Sensitivity to SD assumption:\n")

#> Sensitivity to SD assumption:

tibble(
  SD       = c(10, 12, 15, 18, 20),
  n_per_arm = sapply(c(10, 12, 15, 18, 20), function(sd) {
    ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = delta_sbp / sd, sig.level = alpha_sbp,
                       power = power_sbp, type = "two.sample")$n)
  }),
  Total_N  = sapply(c(10, 12, 15, 18, 20), function(sd) {
    2 * ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = delta_sbp / sd, sig.level = alpha_sbp,
                            power = power_sbp, type = "two.sample")$n)
  })
) |>
  mutate(Cohen_d = round(delta_sbp / SD, 3)) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Sensitivity Analysis: Varying SD Assumption",
             subtitle = paste0("δ = ", delta_sbp, " mmHg, α = ", alpha_sbp,
                               ", power = ", power_sbp))
