16.3.2 2.2 Power Curve

n_total_range <- seq(20, 400, by = 10)
power_range   <- sapply(n_total_range, function(n_tot) {
  pwr.t.test(n = n_tot / 2, d = delta_sbp / sigma_sbp,
             sig.level = alpha_sbp, type = "two.sample")$power
})

tibble(n_total = n_total_range, power = power_range) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n_total, y = power)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2C6E9E", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(0.80, 0.90), linetype = c("dashed", "dotted"),
             colour = c("#D32F2F", "#E07B39")) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = n_sbp * 2, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "grey40", alpha = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = n_sbp * 2 + 5, y = 0.5,
           label = paste0("N = ", n_sbp * 2, "\n(90% power)"),
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    x     = "Total Sample Size",
    y     = "Power",
    title = "Power Curve: SBP Reduction Trial",
    subtitle = paste0("δ = ", delta_sbp, " mmHg, SD = ", sigma_sbp, " mmHg")
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)




Power curve for SBP trial: power as a function of total sample size
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
16  Sample Size and Power Calculations

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
16.4 3. Sample Size for Binary Endpoints

16.4.1 3.1 Two-Proportion z-Test
For comparing two proportions \(p_1\) and \(p_2\):
\[n = \frac{(z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} + z_\beta\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2)})^2}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}\]
where \(\bar{p} = (p_1 + p_2)/2\).

# Example: response rate trial
p1 <- 0.55   # expected response rate (Active)
p2 <- 0.40   # expected response rate (Placebo)
alpha_bin <- 0.05
power_bin <- 0.80

ss_bin <- pwr.2p.test(
  h          = ES.h(p1, p2),
  sig.level  = alpha_bin,
  power      = power_bin,
  alternative = "two.sided"
)
n_bin <- ceiling(ss_bin$n)

cat("=== Binary Endpoint Trial (Response Rate) ===\n")

#> === Binary Endpoint Trial (Response Rate) ===

cat("Active response rate (p1):  ", p1, "\n")

#> Active response rate (p1):   0.55

cat("Placebo response rate (p2): ", p2, "\n")

#> Placebo response rate (p2):  0.4

cat("Difference:                 ", p1 - p2, "(", (p1 - p2) * 100, "pp)\n")

#> Difference:                  0.15 ( 15 pp)

cat("Cohen's h:                  ", round(ES.h(p1, p2), 4), "\n")

#> Cohen's h:                   0.3015

cat("n per arm:                  ", n_bin, "\n")

#> n per arm:                   173

cat("Total N:                    ", n_bin * 2, "\n\n")

#> Total N:                     346

# Sensitivity: vary response rates
cat("Sensitivity to assumed response rates:\n")

#> Sensitivity to assumed response rates:

scenarios <- expand.grid(
  p_active  = c(0.50, 0.55, 0.60),
  p_placebo = c(0.35, 0.40, 0.45)
) |>
  filter(p_active > p_placebo) |>
  mutate(
    Diff      = round(p_active - p_placebo, 2),
    n_per_arm = sapply(1:nrow(cur_data()), function(i) {
      ceiling(pwr.2p.test(
        h         = ES.h(p_active[i], p_placebo[i]),
        sig.level = 0.05,
        power     = 0.80
      )$n)
    }),
    Total_N   = n_per_arm * 2
  ) |>
  rename(p_Active = p_active, p_Placebo = p_placebo)

scenarios |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Sample Size Sensitivity: Binary Endpoint",
             subtitle = "α = 0.05, power = 80%")

16  Sample Size and Power Calculations

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
16.5 4. Sample Size for Survival Endpoints

16.5.1 4.1 Event-Driven Designs
Survival trials are powered by the number of events (deaths, progressions), not the number of subjects. The required number of events for the log-rank test:
\[E = \frac{4(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2}{[\log(HR)]^2}\]

# Survival trial: OS in oncology
# HR = 0.70 (30% reduction in hazard); α = 0.05; power = 80%
hr      <- 0.70
alpha_s <- 0.05
power_s <- 0.80

# Required events (Schoenfeld formula)
z_alpha <- qnorm(1 - alpha_s / 2)
z_beta  <- qnorm(power_s)
e_req   <- ceiling(4 * (z_alpha + z_beta)^2 / (log(hr))^2)

cat("=== Survival Trial (Overall Survival) ===\n")

#> === Survival Trial (Overall Survival) ===

cat("Hazard Ratio (HR):     ", hr, "\n")

#> Hazard Ratio (HR):      0.7

cat("α (two-sided):         ", alpha_s, "\n")

#> α (two-sided):          0.05

cat("Power (1-β):           ", power_s, "\n")

#> Power (1-β):            0.8

cat("Required events:       ", e_req, "\n\n")

#> Required events:        247

# Translate events to subjects (assuming event probability)
# Accrual = 24 months, follow-up = 12 months, control median OS = 18 months
lambda_ctrl   <- log(2) / 18        # control arm hazard (median 18 months)
lambda_active <- lambda_ctrl * hr   # active arm hazard

# Probability of event by end of trial (36 months max)
t_max <- 36
# Simplified: event probability ~ 1 - exp(-lambda * t_max) averaged over uniform accrual
p_event_ctrl   <- 1 - exp(-lambda_ctrl   * t_max * 0.6)  # approximate
p_event_active <- 1 - exp(-lambda_active * t_max * 0.6)
p_event_avg    <- (p_event_ctrl + p_event_active) / 2

n_subjects <- ceiling(e_req / p_event_avg)

cat("Event probability (approx, per arm): ", round(p_event_avg, 3), "\n")

#> Event probability (approx, per arm):  0.503

cat("Estimated subjects per arm:          ", ceiling(n_subjects / 2), "\n")

#> Estimated subjects per arm:           246

cat("Total subjects:                      ", n_subjects, "\n\n")

#> Total subjects:                       492

# HR sensitivity table
hr_vals <- c(0.60, 0.65, 0.70, 0.75, 0.80)
tibble(
  HR           = hr_vals,
  Events_req   = sapply(hr_vals, function(h) {
    ceiling(4 * (qnorm(0.975) + qnorm(0.80))^2 / (log(h))^2)
  })
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Events Required vs. Hazard Ratio",
             subtitle = "α = 0.05 (two-sided), power = 80%")







Events Required vs. Hazard Ratio

16  Sample Size and Power Calculations

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
16.6 5. Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

16.6.1 5.1 Non-Inferiority Margin
Non-inferiority (NI) trials test that a new treatment is not worse than an active comparator by more than a pre-specified margin \(\Delta_{NI}\).
\[H_0: \mu_{\text{test}} - \mu_{\text{ref}} \leq -\Delta_{NI}\] \[H_1: \mu_{\text{test}} - \mu_{\text{ref}} > -\Delta_{NI}\]

# Non-inferiority: new formulation vs. reference
# NI margin = -5 mmHg; expected true difference = 0; SD = 12
delta_ni  <- 0     # true difference (expected: no difference)
margin_ni <- 5     # NI margin (mmHg)
sigma_ni  <- 12
alpha_ni  <- 0.025  # one-sided alpha
power_ni  <- 0.90

# For NI test: n = 2σ²(z_α + z_β)² / (margin - delta)²
z_a <- qnorm(1 - alpha_ni)
z_b <- qnorm(power_ni)
n_ni <- ceiling(2 * sigma_ni^2 * (z_a + z_b)^2 / (margin_ni - delta_ni)^2)

cat("=== Non-Inferiority Trial ===\n")

#> === Non-Inferiority Trial ===

cat("NI margin (Δ):         ", margin_ni, "mmHg\n")

#> NI margin (Δ):          5 mmHg

cat("Expected difference:   ", delta_ni, "mmHg (no difference)\n")

#> Expected difference:    0 mmHg (no difference)

cat("σ:                     ", sigma_ni, "\n")

#> σ:                      12

cat("α (one-sided):         ", alpha_ni, "\n")

#> α (one-sided):          0.025

cat("Power (1-β):           ", power_ni, "\n")

#> Power (1-β):            0.9

cat("n per arm:             ", n_ni, "\n")

#> n per arm:              122

cat("Total N:               ", n_ni * 2, "\n\n")

#> Total N:                244

# Compare with superiority design
n_sup <- ceiling(pwr.t.test(
  d         = 5 / 12,
  sig.level = 0.05,
  power     = 0.90,
  type      = "two.sample"
)$n)

cat("Equivalent superiority design (δ = 5 mmHg):\n")

#> Equivalent superiority design (δ = 5 mmHg):

cat("  n per arm:", n_sup, "| Total:", n_sup * 2, "\n")

#>   n per arm: 123 | Total: 246

cat("  (NI trials often need fewer patients since margin > 0)\n")

#>   (NI trials often need fewer patients since margin > 0)




16.6.2 5.2 Two One-Sided Tests (TOST) for Equivalence

# Bioequivalence: 80-125% rule (on log scale)
# For log-transformed PK parameters: log(1.25) ≈ 0.223
margin_be <- log(1.25)
sigma_be  <- 0.25      # CV ≈ 25% for PK data
power_be  <- 0.90
alpha_be  <- 0.05

n_be <- ceiling(
  2 * sigma_be^2 * (qnorm(1 - alpha_be) + qnorm(power_be))^2 / margin_be^2
)

cat("=== Bioequivalence (TOST) ===\n")

#> === Bioequivalence (TOST) ===

cat("BE margin:    ±", round(margin_be, 4), "(log scale) = 80–125% ratio\n")

#> BE margin:    ± 0.2231 (log scale) = 80–125% ratio

cat("CV (σ):       ", sigma_be, "\n")

#> CV (σ):        0.25

cat("Power:        ", power_be, "\n")

#> Power:         0.9

cat("Sequences×periods (crossover): 2 × 2\n")

#> Sequences×periods (crossover): 2 × 2

cat("n subjects:   ", n_be, "\n")

#> n subjects:    22
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
16  Sample Size and Power Calculations

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
section: 16.7 6. Adjusting for Dropout and Protocol Deviations
text:
16.7 6. Adjusting for Dropout and Protocol Deviations

# Account for expected dropout
n_per_arm_base <- n_sbp    # from earlier calculation
dropout_rate   <- 0.15     # 15% dropout expected

n_inflated <- ceiling(n_per_arm_base / (1 - dropout_rate))

cat("=== Inflating for Dropout ===\n")

#> === Inflating for Dropout ===

cat("Required completers per arm: ", n_per_arm_base, "\n")

#> Required completers per arm:  191

cat("Expected dropout rate:       ", dropout_rate * 100, "%\n")

#> Expected dropout rate:        15 %

cat("Required enrolled per arm:   ", n_inflated, "\n")

#> Required enrolled per arm:    225

cat("Total to enrol:              ", n_inflated * 2, "\n\n")

#> Total to enrol:               450

# Sensitivity: vary dropout
tibble(
  Dropout_rate  = seq(0.05, 0.30, by = 0.05),
  n_to_enrol    = ceiling(n_per_arm_base / (1 - seq(0.05, 0.30, by = 0.05))),
  Total_enrol   = 2 * ceiling(n_per_arm_base / (1 - seq(0.05, 0.30, by = 0.05)))
) |>
  mutate(Dropout_rate = paste0(Dropout_rate * 100, "%")) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Sample Size Inflation for Dropout",
             subtitle = paste0("Base n = ", n_per_arm_base, " completers per arm"))

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
16.8 7. Adaptive and Group Sequential Designs

16.8.1 7.1 Group Sequential Designs
Group sequential designs allow pre-planned interim analyses with the option to stop early for efficacy or futility, while controlling the overall Type I error rate.

# O'Brien-Fleming boundaries for 2 interim + final analysis
# Using simple z-score boundaries (illustrative)
n_analyses   <- 3     # 2 interim + 1 final
alpha_total  <- 0.05

# Pocock spending function (constant boundaries)
z_pocock <- qnorm(1 - alpha_total / (2 * n_analyses))

# O'Brien-Fleming spending (conservative at interim, liberal at final)
info_times <- c(1/3, 2/3, 1)   # information fraction at each analysis
of_bounds <- qnorm(1 - alpha_total / 2) / sqrt(info_times)

cat("=== Group Sequential Boundaries (Illustrative) ===\n\n")

#> === Group Sequential Boundaries (Illustrative) ===

tibble(
  Analysis       = c("Interim 1", "Interim 2", "Final"),
  Info_fraction  = info_times,
  n_analysed     = ceiling(info_times * n_sbp * 2),
  Pocock_z       = round(rep(z_pocock, 3), 3),
  OBF_z          = round(of_bounds, 3),
  Pocock_p       = round(2 * pnorm(-z_pocock), 4),
  OBF_p          = round(2 * pnorm(-of_bounds), 4)
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Group Sequential Efficacy Boundaries",
    subtitle = "3-analysis design, α = 0.05 (two-sided)"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Info_fraction = "Information Fraction",
    n_analysed    = "N at Analysis",
    Pocock_z      = "Pocock z",
    OBF_z         = "O'Brien-Fleming z",
    Pocock_p      = "Pocock p",
    OBF_p         = "OBF p"
  )

title: 16  Sample Size and Power Calculations
text:
16.10 Summary
Rigorous sample size calculation is a regulatory requirement and a scientific obligation. Key principles:

Four parameters — \(\alpha\), power, effect size, and \(n\) — are interdependent: specify three, solve for the fourth
The effect size is the most consequential input: use the MCID, not an optimistic estimate, and conduct sensitivity analyses
Standard designs: two-sample t-test for continuous endpoints; two-proportion z-test for binary; log-rank for survival
Event-driven survival trials: power depends on the number of events, not just the sample size; recruitment and follow-up periods determine when events accumulate
Non-inferiority and equivalence trials use one-sided tests and require a pre-specified, scientifically justified NI margin
Inflate for dropout: always account for expected withdrawal when calculating the number to enrol
Group sequential designs allow for early stopping while maintaining overall error control
Pre-registration: sample size justification must be documented in the protocol and SAP before data collection begins


Regulatory note: ICH E9 requires that the sample size justification be included in the protocol, with explicit statements of the primary endpoint, assumed treatment difference, SD (or event rate), \(\alpha\), and power. Sensitivity analyses exploring the impact of varying assumptions are strongly recommended.

16  Sample Size and Power Calculations

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.1 Introduction
Modern clinical drug development relies on sophisticated statistical methods that go beyond traditional fixed-sample, single-endpoint trial designs. This chapter covers advanced methods essential for efficient, rigorous development programs:
These methods, when properly implemented, improve efficiency, reduce development time, and provide stronger evidence for regulatory decision-making.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
Adaptive designs: Flexible modification of trial characteristics based on accumulating data
Multiple testing: Controlling error rates when testing many hypotheses
Estimand framework: Clear specification of treatment effects for complex intercurrent events
Missing data: Principled approaches to incomplete data beyond simple deletion
Meta-analysis: Systematic synthesis of evidence across multiple studies
Biomarker-driven designs: Enrichment and precision medicine approaches
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.2 1. Adaptive Clinical Trial Designs

17.2.1 1.1 Group Sequential Designs
A group sequential design allows for interim analyses, with the possibility of stopping early for efficacy, futility, or both.

1.1.1 O’Brien-Fleming Boundary
More conservative early stopping for efficacy, allocates more Type I error to final analysis:

At interim analysis 1: p-value threshold ≈ 0.005 (more stringent)
At final analysis: p-value threshold ≈ 0.047 (less stringent)

Ensures strong early efficacy evidence before stopping, reduces false positives.

# O'Brien-Fleming boundary for 2 interim analyses + 1 final
if (requireNamespace("gsDesign", quietly = TRUE)) {
  design_of <- gsDesign::gsDesign(k = 3,  # 3 analyses total
                                  test.type = 2,  # symmetric 2-sided (OF/Pocock require symmetric)
                                  sfu = "OF",  # O'Brien-Fleming
                                  alpha = 0.05,
                                  beta = 0.20)

  design_of$upper$bound  # Efficacy boundaries
  design_of$lower$bound  # Futility boundaries
} else {
  message("gsDesign not available; install with install.packages('gsDesign')")
}

#> [1] -2.961125 -2.093831 -1.709606




1.1.2 Pocock Boundary
More aggressive early stopping for efficacy, uniform p-value threshold across analyses:

At each analysis: p-value threshold ≈ 0.016 (for 3 analyses)

Higher power to detect effect early, but slightly increased false positive risk.

# Pocock boundary
if (requireNamespace("gsDesign", quietly = TRUE)) {
  design_poc <- gsDesign::gsDesign(k = 3,
                                  test.type = 2,  # symmetric 2-sided (Pocock requires symmetric)
                                  sfu = "Pocock",
                                  alpha = 0.05,
                                  beta = 0.20)

  design_poc$upper$bound
} else {
  message("gsDesign not available; install with install.packages('gsDesign')")
}

#> [1] 1.992186 1.992186 1.992186




1.1.3 Sample Size Inflation
Group sequential designs require information inflation factor: additional subjects beyond fixed-sample design to maintain power:

# Information inflation factor
# Fixed sample size for 80% power, 2-sided test
if (requireNamespace("pwr", quietly = TRUE) &&
    requireNamespace("gsDesign", quietly = TRUE)) {
  n_fixed <- pwr::pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)$n
  n_fixed <- ceiling(n_fixed)

  # With group sequential design (2 looks, O'Brien-Fleming)
  design_gs <- gsDesign::gsDesign(k = 2, test.type = 2,
                                 sfu = "OF", alpha = 0.05, beta = 0.20)
  inflation_factor <- design_gs$n.I[2] / n_fixed

  cat("Fixed sample size:", n_fixed, "\n")
  cat("GSD sample size:", design_gs$n.I[2], "\n")
  cat("Inflation factor:", round(inflation_factor, 3), "\n")
} else {
  message("pwr or gsDesign not available")
}

#> Fixed sample size: 64 
#> GSD sample size: 1.015614 
#> Inflation factor: 0.016





17.2.2 1.2 Adaptive Seamless Phase II/III Designs
Combines phase II and phase III in a single trial structure:

Phase II component (first stage): Identify promising dose(s)
Seamless transition: Drop futile doses, continue promising dose(s)
Phase III component (second stage): Confirmatory efficacy testing

Advantages: - Faster development timeline - Shared control group across phases - More efficient use of subjects

# Simplified example: two-stage adaptive dose selection
# Phase II: Three doses (5, 10, 20 mg) vs. placebo
# Phase III: Continue best-performing dose vs. placebo

# Phase II (hypothetical results)
dose_effects <- data.frame(
  Dose = c("Placebo", "5mg", "10mg", "20mg"),
  Mean_Change = c(-2, -6, -9, -8),  # Blood pressure change
  SE = c(1.5, 1.5, 1.5, 1.5),
  N = c(40, 40, 40, 40)
)

# Select dose with largest effect (10 mg)
best_dose <- dose_effects[which.max(dose_effects$Mean_Change[2:4]) + 1, ]
cat("Selected dose for Phase III:", best_dose$Dose, "\n")

#> Selected dose for Phase III: 5mg



# Phase III: Compare selected dose to placebo with larger sample
# n = 150 per group (increased from 40 in Phase II)



17.2.3 1.3 Sample Size Re-estimation
Allows prospective increase in sample size based on interim analysis data:

# Interim analysis at 50% information
# Blinded review of variability estimate
# If SD larger than anticipated, increase sample size

sd_anticipated <- 10
sd_observed_interim <- 12.5  # 25% larger

# Reestimate required sample size
n_original <- 100  # per group
variance_ratio <- (sd_observed_interim / sd_anticipated)^2
n_updated <- ceiling(n_original * variance_ratio)

cat("Original sample size per group:", n_original, "\n")

#> Original sample size per group: 100



cat("Updated sample size per group:", n_updated, "\n")

#> Updated sample size per group: 157



cat("Additional subjects needed:", n_updated - n_original, "\n")

#> Additional subjects needed: 57




17.2.4 1.4 Response-Adaptive Randomization
Allocates more subjects to treatments showing promise during the trial:

# Bandit algorithm: allocate subjects based on observed response rates
# Initially: 1:1 randomization
# As data accumulate: shift allocation toward higher-response treatment

set.seed(42)
n_total <- 200
response_rate_a <- 0.55
response_rate_b <- 0.45

# Track cumulative allocation
allocation <- numeric(n_total)
responses_a <- 0
responses_b <- 0

for (i in 1:n_total) {
  # Thompson sampling: sample from posterior distributions
  # Allocate to treatment with highest posterior probability of response

  posterior_a <- rbeta(1, responses_a + 1, (i/2 - responses_a) + 1)
  posterior_b <- rbeta(1, responses_b + 1, (i/2 - responses_b) + 1)

  if (isTRUE(posterior_a > posterior_b)) {
    allocation[i] <- 1  # Assign to A
    responses_a <- responses_a + rbinom(1, 1, response_rate_a)
  } else {
    allocation[i] <- 0  # Assign to B
    responses_b <- responses_b + rbinom(1, 1, response_rate_b)
  }
}

# Compare adaptive allocation to fixed 1:1
cat("Fixed 1:1 allocation: 100 per arm\n")

#> Fixed 1:1 allocation: 100 per arm



cat("Adaptive allocation: ",
    sum(allocation == 1), "to A, ",
    sum(allocation == 0), "to B\n")

#> Adaptive allocation:  163 to A,  37 to B
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.3 2. Multiple Testing in Clinical Trials

17.3.1 2.1 Familywise Error Rate (FWER)
FWER is the probability of making at least one Type I error across multiple tests:
\[FWER = P(\text{reject at least one } H_0 | \text{all } H_0 \text{ true})\]
Traditional methods control FWER at α = 0.05.


17.3.2 2.2 False Discovery Rate (FDR)
FDR is the proportion of false positives among all rejected hypotheses:
\[FDR = E\left[\frac{V}{R}\right]\]
where V = number of false positives, R = number of rejections.
FDR control is less stringent than FWER, useful for exploratory analyses.

# FWER vs FDR control
# m = 10 hypotheses, true effect size for 2 of them

set.seed(42)
m <- 10
true_effects <- c(0.5, 0.5, rep(0, 8))  # True effects for first 2

# Generate p-values
p_values <- numeric(m)
for (i in 1:m) {
  if (true_effects[i] > 0) {
    # Power = 0.80 for true effects
    p_values[i] <- 1 - pnorm(abs(rnorm(1, mean = true_effects[i], sd = 1)))
  } else {
    p_values[i] <- runif(1)
  }
}

# Bonferroni (FWER control): Reject if p < α/m
bonferroni_threshold <- 0.05 / m
bonferroni_reject <- p_values < bonferroni_threshold

# FDR (Benjamini-Hochberg): Reject if p <= threshold(i)
fdr_threshold <- 0.05
sorted_p <- sort(p_values)
sorted_i <- 1:m
bh_threshold <- (sorted_i / m) * fdr_threshold
bh_reject <- sum(sorted_p <= bh_threshold)

cat("Bonferroni rejections:", sum(bonferroni_reject), "\n")

#> Bonferroni rejections: 0



cat("Benjamini-Hochberg rejections:", bh_reject, "\n")

#> Benjamini-Hochberg rejections: 0




17.3.3 2.3 Hierarchical Testing Procedures

2.3.1 Fixed Sequence Procedure
Tests hypotheses in a pre-specified order, stops at first non-rejection:

# Primary efficacy → Key secondary 1 → Key secondary 2
# H1: Primary endpoint (strong control of Type I error)
# H2: Key secondary 1 (only if H1 rejected)
# H3: Key secondary 2 (only if H2 rejected)

p_values_seq <- c(0.008, 0.025, 0.032)  # Primary, Key Sec 1, Key Sec 2
alpha_level <- 0.05

rejections_fixed <- numeric(3)
for (i in 1:3) {
  if (i == 1 || rejections_fixed[i-1] == 1) {
    rejections_fixed[i] <- as.numeric(p_values_seq[i] < alpha_level)
  } else {
    break
  }
}

cat("Fixed sequence rejections:\n")

#> Fixed sequence rejections:



for (i in 1:3) {
  cat("H", i, ": ", ifelse(rejections_fixed[i] == 1, "Rejected", "Not rejected"), "\n", sep="")
}

#> H1: Rejected
#> H2: Rejected
#> H3: Rejected




2.3.2 Fallback Procedure
More flexible: can test later hypotheses even if earlier ones fail, but with adjusted α:

# Fallback procedure
# If H1 rejected: H2 tested at α = 0.05
# If H1 not rejected: H2 tested at α = 0.025 (reduced)
# If H2 rejected: H3 tested at remaining α

p_values_fb <- c(0.08, 0.02)  # Primary fails, secondary succeeds
alpha_h1 <- 0.05
alpha_h2_if_h1_rej <- 0.05
alpha_h2_if_h1_not_rej <- 0.025

h1_rejected <- p_values_fb[1] < alpha_h1
h2_threshold <- ifelse(h1_rejected, alpha_h2_if_h1_rej, alpha_h2_if_h1_not_rej)
h2_rejected <- p_values_fb[2] < h2_threshold

cat("H1 (Primary):", ifelse(h1_rejected, "Rejected", "Not rejected"), "\n")

#> H1 (Primary): Not rejected



cat("H2 threshold: alpha =", h2_threshold, "\n")

#> H2 threshold: alpha = 0.025



cat("H2 (Secondary):", ifelse(h2_rejected, "Rejected", "Not rejected"), "\n")

#> H2 (Secondary): Rejected





17.3.4 2.4 Gatekeeping Procedures for Complex Hierarchies
Manages hierarchical families of hypotheses with conditional testing based on parent hypothesis results:

# Hierarchical structure:
# Primary: PK endpoint (Pharmacokinetics)
#   └─ Secondary 1a: Efficacy measure 1
#   └─ Secondary 1b: Efficacy measure 2
# Safety: Adverse events
#   └─ Secondary 2a: Specific AE rate
#   └─ Secondary 2b: Lab abnormality rate

p_values_gate <- list(
  Primary = 0.015,
  Secondary_1a = 0.032,
  Secondary_1b = 0.048,
  Safety = 0.001,
  Secondary_2a = 0.087,
  Secondary_2b = 0.034
)

# Primary family (PK) has alpha = 0.04 (before safety)
# Safety family has alpha = 0.01

alpha_pk <- 0.04
alpha_safety <- 0.01

# Primary hypothesis
pk_rejected <- p_values_gate$Primary < alpha_pk

# Secondary hypotheses in efficacy (gated by PK)
if (pk_rejected) {
  sec1a_rejected <- p_values_gate$Secondary_1a < alpha_pk/2
  sec1b_rejected <- p_values_gate$Secondary_1b < alpha_pk/2
} else {
  sec1a_rejected <- sec1b_rejected <- FALSE
}

# Safety hypotheses (separate gating)
safety_rejected <- p_values_gate$Safety < alpha_safety
if (safety_rejected) {
  sec2a_rejected <- p_values_gate$Secondary_2a < alpha_safety/2
  sec2b_rejected <- p_values_gate$Secondary_2b < alpha_safety/2
} else {
  sec2a_rejected <- sec2b_rejected <- FALSE
}

cat("PK Endpoint:", ifelse(pk_rejected, "Positive", "Negative"), "\n")

#> PK Endpoint: Positive



cat("├─ Efficacy Measure 1:", ifelse(sec1a_rejected, "Positive", "Negative"), "\n")

#> ├─ Efficacy Measure 1: Negative



cat("├─ Efficacy Measure 2:", ifelse(sec1b_rejected, "Positive", "Negative"), "\n")

#> ├─ Efficacy Measure 2: Negative



cat("Safety Endpoint:", ifelse(safety_rejected, "Positive", "Negative"), "\n")

#> Safety Endpoint: Positive



cat("├─ Specific AE:", ifelse(sec2a_rejected, "Positive", "Negative"), "\n")

#> ├─ Specific AE: Negative



cat("└─ Lab Abnormality:", ifelse(sec2b_rejected, "Positive", "Negative"), "\n")

#> └─ Lab Abnormality: Negative




17.3.5 2.5 Multiplicity Adjustment Methods

2.5.1 Bonferroni
Divides α equally among m tests:
\[p\text{-value threshold} = \frac{\alpha}{m}\]
Conservative (FWER control), but powerful when few tests.


2.5.2 Holm’s Procedure
More powerful than Bonferroni; rejects ordered hypotheses at thresholds: - H₁: p < α/m - H₂: p < α/(m-1) - H₃: p < α/(m-2), etc.
Maintains FWER control while improving power.


2.5.3 Hochberg and Hommel
Even more powerful methods; use step-up approach:

# Example: 5 hypotheses with p-values
p_values_mult <- c(0.001, 0.008, 0.035, 0.042, 0.105)
alpha <- 0.05
m <- length(p_values_mult)

# Bonferroni
bonferroni_sig <- p_values_mult < alpha / m

# Holm
sorted_p <- sort(p_values_mult)
holm_threshold <- alpha / (m:1)
holm_sig <- sorted_p < holm_threshold

# Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR)
sorted_i <- 1:m
bh_threshold <- (sorted_i / m) * alpha
bh_sig <- sorted_p < bh_threshold

results_table <- data.frame(
  Test = 1:m,
  P_value = p_values_mult,
  Bonferroni = bonferroni_sig,
  Holm = cumsum(holm_sig) > 0,
  BH_FDR = cumsum(bh_sig) > 0
)
print(results_table)

#>   Test P_value Bonferroni Holm BH_FDR
#> 1    1   0.001       TRUE TRUE   TRUE
#> 2    2   0.008       TRUE TRUE   TRUE
#> 3    3   0.035      FALSE TRUE   TRUE
#> 4    4   0.042      FALSE TRUE   TRUE
#> 5    5   0.105      FALSE TRUE   TRUE
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.4 3. Estimand Framework (ICH E9(R1))

17.4.1 3.1 Definition and Components
An estimand specifies: 1. Population: Target population 2. Variable: What is measured (primary outcome) 3. Intercurrent events: Events that occur during trial (drop-out, medication switches, disease progression) 4. Population-level summary: How individual observations are combined (mean difference, probability difference)


17.4.2 3.2 Handling Intercurrent Events
Five strategies for handling intercurrent events:

3.2.1 Treatment Policy Strategy
Ignores intercurrent event, analyzes all subjects as randomized (intention-to-treat).

# Analyze all randomized subjects regardless of adherence/dropout
# This is the standard ITT approach



3.2.2 Composite Strategy
Defines intercurrent event as part of outcome (e.g., failure if subject stops treatment).

# Failure = either lack of efficacy OR treatment discontinuation
# More conservative, clinically relevant approach



3.2.3 Hypothetical Strategy
Estimates what would have happened if intercurrent event had not occurred.

# Estimate efficacy adjusting for non-adherence
# More ambitious, requires strong assumptions (MCAR)



3.2.4 While-on-Treatment Strategy
Analyzes only the period while subject is on treatment.

# Exclude post-discontinuation data
# Can introduce bias if discontinuation related to outcome



3.2.5 Principal Stratum Strategy
Conditions on whether subject would experience intercurrent event.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.5 4. Missing Data Methods

17.5.1 4.1 Missing Data Mechanisms

MCAR (Missing Completely at Random): Missingness unrelated to observed or unobserved outcomes
MAR (Missing at Random): Missingness depends on observed data but not unobserved outcomes
MNAR (Missing Not at Random): Missingness depends on unobserved outcomes

Only MCAR justifies listwise deletion. MAR and MNAR require more sophisticated methods.


17.5.2 4.2 Multiple Imputation (MICE Framework)
Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations:

Imputation: Create m complete datasets by imputing missing values
Analysis: Analyze each complete dataset separately
Pooling: Combine results using Rubin’s rules


if (requireNamespace("mice", quietly = TRUE)) {
  # Create example dataset with missing values
  set.seed(42)
  trial_data_incomplete <- data.frame(
    subject_id = 1:100,
    treatment = rep(c("Active", "Control"), 50),
    age = rnorm(100, 55, 10),
    baseline_outcome = rnorm(100, 50, 10),
    final_outcome = rnorm(100, 55, 12)
  )

  # Introduce missing data (MCAR for simplicity)
  missing_idx <- sample(1:100, 20)
  trial_data_incomplete$final_outcome[missing_idx] <- NA

  # Multiple imputation (m = 5 imputations)
  imputed_data <- mice::mice(trial_data_incomplete,
                             m = 5, maxit = 50,
                             method = "norm", print = FALSE)

  # Analyze and pool: use base-R with() for S3 dispatch on mids objects
  fits_mids <- with(imputed_data,
                    lm(final_outcome ~ treatment + baseline_outcome))
  pooled_results <- mice::pool(fits_mids)
  print(summary(pooled_results))
} else {
  message("mice not available; install with install.packages('mice')")
}

#>               term    estimate std.error statistic       df      p.value
#> 1      (Intercept) 52.59167434 8.1171668 6.4790678 39.79331 1.026124e-07
#> 2 treatmentControl  1.28934252 2.7391408 0.4707106 66.75744 6.393819e-01
#> 3 baseline_outcome  0.03432461 0.1590727 0.2157794 46.20113 8.301096e-01


Advantages: - Handles MAR data properly - Preserves uncertainty from missingness - Flexible for different imputation models
Disadvantages: - Assumes MAR (not testable) - Requires specification of imputation model


17.5.3 4.3 Pattern Mixture Models
Explicitly model the relationship between missingness and outcome:
\[E[Y | X, \text{dropout}] = \alpha_{\text{dropout}} + \beta X\]
Coefficients can differ between subjects who drop out vs. complete follow-up.

# Pattern mixture model: complete vs. dropout patterns
# Create a self-contained dataset for this demonstration
set.seed(42)
trial_data_pmm <- data.frame(
  subject_id       = 1:100,
  treatment        = rep(c("Active", "Control"), 50),
  baseline_outcome = rnorm(100, 50, 10),
  final_outcome    = rnorm(100, 55, 12)
)
# Introduce missingness
missing_idx2 <- sample(1:100, 20)
trial_data_pmm$final_outcome[missing_idx2] <- NA
trial_data_pmm$pattern <- ifelse(is.na(trial_data_pmm$final_outcome),
                                  "Dropout", "Complete")

complete_data <- subset(trial_data_pmm, pattern == "Complete")
dropout_data  <- subset(trial_data_pmm, pattern == "Dropout")

cat("Complete follow-up:", nrow(complete_data), "subjects
")

#> Complete follow-up: 80 subjects

cat("Early dropout:     ", nrow(dropout_data),  "subjects
")

#> Early dropout:      20 subjects

# Analyze observed data in completers
fit_complete <- lm(final_outcome ~ treatment + baseline_outcome,
                   data = complete_data)
cat("
Pattern mixture model (completers):
")

#> 
#> Pattern mixture model (completers):

print(coef(summary(fit_complete)))

#>                     Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
#> (Intercept)      54.12081969  6.8287148  7.925477 1.415213e-11
#> treatmentControl  2.92517008  2.5542715  1.145207 2.556704e-01
#> baseline_outcome -0.02937634  0.1303258 -0.225407 8.222599e-01




17.5.4 4.4 Sensitivity and Tipping Point Analysis
Sensitivity analysis explores robustness to different assumptions about missingness:

# Tipping point analysis: at what assumed outcome for Active dropouts
# does the treatment conclusion flip to zero?
# Key: vary Active dropout assumption; keep Control dropout = observed mean (50)

n_completers <- 80; n_missing <- 20; n_total <- 100
obs_active <- 55; obs_control <- 50

# Range must cross the tipping point: when Active imputed = 30, effect = 0
# (55*80 + 30*20)/100 - 50 = 50 - 50 = 0
missing_outcome_active <- seq(28, 57, by = 1)

treatment_effect <- sapply(missing_outcome_active, function(x) {
  mean_active  <- (obs_active  * n_completers + x  * n_missing) / n_total
  mean_control <- (obs_control * n_completers + obs_control * n_missing) / n_total
  mean_active - mean_control
})

# Find tipping point (treatment effect = 0) via linear interpolation
tipping_point_result <- tryCatch(
  approx(treatment_effect, missing_outcome_active, xout = 0)$y,
  error = function(e) NA
)

cat("Observed treatment effect:", obs_active - obs_control, "\n")

#> Observed treatment effect: 5



if (!is.na(tipping_point_result)) {
  cat("Tipping point (Active dropout mean that nullifies effect):",
      round(tipping_point_result, 2), "\n")
  cat("(Effect flips sign if Active dropouts score below ~",
      round(tipping_point_result, 1), "on average)\n")
} else {
  cat("Tipping point: treatment effect does not cross zero in specified range\n")
}

#> Tipping point (Active dropout mean that nullifies effect): 30 
#> (Effect flips sign if Active dropouts score below ~ 30 on average)
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.6 5. Meta-Analysis

17.6.1 5.1 Fixed-Effects Model
Assumes all studies estimate the same true effect:
\[\theta_i = \theta + \epsilon_i\]
where \(\epsilon_i\) is within-study variability.
Pooled estimate (inverse variance weighting):
\[\hat{\theta} = \frac{\sum w_i \hat{\theta}_i}{\sum w_i}\]
where \(w_i = 1 / SE_i^2\) (inverse variance weight).

if (!requireNamespace("meta", quietly = TRUE)) {
  message("meta package not available; skipping meta-analysis examples.")
} else {

# Meta-analysis of antihypertensive drug efficacy
# 5 studies comparing drug to placebo

studies <- data.frame(
  Study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D", "Study E"),
  Mean_Diff = c(-10, -12, -8, -11, -9),  # mmHg reduction
  SE = c(2.0, 2.2, 1.8, 2.1, 1.9)
)

# Meta-analysis (fixed-effects)
meta_fe <- meta::metagen(TE = Mean_Diff,
                        seTE = SE,
                        studlab = Study,
                        data = studies,
                        fixed = TRUE,
                        random = FALSE)

summary(meta_fe)
} # end requireNamespace("meta") guard

#>                               95%-CI %W(common)
#> Study A -10.0000 [-13.9199; -6.0801]       19.7
#> Study B -12.0000 [-16.3119; -7.6881]       16.3
#> Study C  -8.0000 [-11.5279; -4.4721]       24.3
#> Study D -11.0000 [-15.1159; -6.8841]       17.9
#> Study E  -9.0000 [-12.7239; -5.2761]       21.8
#> 
#> Number of studies: k = 5
#> 
#>                                          95%-CI      z  p-value
#> Common effect model -9.7996 [-11.5395; -8.0597] -11.04 < 0.0001
#> 
#> Quantifying heterogeneity (with 95%-CIs):
#>  tau^2 = 0 [0.0000; 16.6202]; tau = 0 [0.0000; 4.0768]
#>  I^2 = 0.0% [0.0%; 79.2%]; H = 1.00 [1.00; 2.19]
#> 
#> Test of heterogeneity:
#>     Q d.f. p-value
#>  2.51    4  0.6422
#> 
#> Details of meta-analysis methods:
#> - Inverse variance method
#> - Restricted maximum-likelihood estimator for tau^2
#> - Q-Profile method for confidence interval of tau^2 and tau
#> - Calculation of I^2 based on Q




17.6.2 5.2 Random-Effects Model
Assumes studies estimate different true effects, drawn from distribution:
\[\theta_i = \mu + \eta_i + \epsilon_i\]
where: - μ = mean effect across studies - η_i = between-study heterogeneity - ε_i = within-study error
DerSimonian-Laird estimator for between-study variance:
\[\hat{\tau}^2 = \frac{Q - (k-1)}{\sum w_i - \frac{\sum w_i^2}{\sum w_i}}\]
where Q = heterogeneity statistic.

if (requireNamespace("meta", quietly = TRUE) && exists("studies")) {
  # Meta-analysis (random-effects)
  meta_re <- meta::metagen(TE = Mean_Diff,
                           seTE = SE,
                           studlab = Study,
                           data = studies,
                           fixed = FALSE,
                           random = TRUE,
                           method.tau = "DL")  # DerSimonian-Laird
  summary(meta_re)
} else {
  message("meta package not available or studies dataset not found")
}

#>                               95%-CI %W(random)
#> Study A -10.0000 [-13.9199; -6.0801]       19.7
#> Study B -12.0000 [-16.3119; -7.6881]       16.3
#> Study C  -8.0000 [-11.5279; -4.4721]       24.3
#> Study D -11.0000 [-15.1159; -6.8841]       17.9
#> Study E  -9.0000 [-12.7239; -5.2761]       21.8
#> 
#> Number of studies: k = 5
#> 
#>                                           95%-CI      z  p-value
#> Random effects model -9.7996 [-11.5395; -8.0597] -11.04 < 0.0001
#> 
#> Quantifying heterogeneity (with 95%-CIs):
#>  tau^2 = 0 [0.0000; 16.6617]; tau = 0 [0.0000; 4.0819]
#>  I^2 = 0.0% [0.0%; 79.2%]; H = 1.00 [1.00; 2.19]
#> 
#> Test of heterogeneity:
#>     Q d.f. p-value
#>  2.51    4  0.6422
#> 
#> Details of meta-analysis methods:
#> - Inverse variance method
#> - DerSimonian-Laird estimator for tau^2
#> - Jackson method for confidence interval of tau^2 and tau
#> - Calculation of I^2 based on Q




17.6.3 5.3 Heterogeneity Assessment

5.3.1 I² Statistic
Proportion of total variance due to heterogeneity:
\[I^2 = \frac{Q - (k-1)}{Q} \times 100\%\]

I² < 25%: Low heterogeneity
25% < I² < 75%: Moderate heterogeneity
I² > 75%: High heterogeneity



5.3.2 τ² (Tau-Squared)
Between-study variance in original scale:

# Extract heterogeneity measures
if (requireNamespace("meta", quietly = TRUE) && exists("meta_re")) {
  # Note: requires meta_re object from previous random-effects meta-analysis chunk
  i2_value <- tryCatch(meta_re$I2$TE, error = function(e) NA)
  tau2_value <- tryCatch(meta_re$tau2, error = function(e) NA)
  tau_value <- tryCatch(meta_re$tau, error = function(e) NA)

  if (!is.na(i2_value)) {
    cat("I²:", round(i2_value, 1), "%\n")
  }
  if (!is.na(tau2_value)) {
    cat("τ²:", round(tau2_value, 2), "\n")
  }
  if (!is.na(tau_value)) {
    cat("τ:", round(tau_value, 2), "\n")
  }
  if (is.na(i2_value) && is.na(tau2_value) && is.na(tau_value)) {
    message("Could not extract heterogeneity measures from meta_re object")
  }
} else {
  message("meta package not available or meta_re object not found")
}

#> τ²: 0 
#> τ: 0





17.6.4 5.4 Forest Plots
Graphical representation of individual studies and pooled estimate:

# Forest plot
if (requireNamespace("meta", quietly = TRUE) && exists("meta_re")) {
  plot(meta_re, xlim = c(-20, 0))
} else {
  message("meta package or meta_re object not found; run random-effects meta-analysis chunk first")
}










17.6.5 5.5 Publication Bias

5.5.1 Funnel Plot
Scatterplot of effect size vs. study precision; asymmetry suggests publication bias:

# Funnel plot
if (requireNamespace("metafor", quietly = TRUE) && requireNamespace("meta", quietly = TRUE) && exists("meta_re")) {
  tryCatch(
    metafor::funnel(meta_re, main = "Funnel Plot"),
    error = function(e) message("Could not create funnel plot: ", e$message)
  )
} else {
  message("metafor/meta package or meta_re object not found")
}










5.5.2 Egger’s Test
Tests for asymmetry in funnel plot:
\[z_i = \frac{\hat{\theta}_i}{SE_i} = \alpha + \beta \frac{1}{SE_i}\]
Significant intercept (α) suggests publication bias.

# Egger's test for publication bias
if (requireNamespace("meta", quietly = TRUE) && exists("meta_re")) {
  tryCatch(
    {
      egger_result <- meta::metabias(meta_re, method = "Egger")
      print(egger_result)
    },
    error = function(e) message("Could not compute Egger's test: ", e$message)
  )
} else {
  message("meta package or meta_re object not found")
}
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.7 6. Biomarker Analysis and Enrichment

17.7.1 6.1 Predictive vs. Prognostic Biomarkers

Predictive: Modifies treatment response (predicts who benefits)
Prognostic: Associated with outcome regardless of treatment


# Analyze biomarker as predictor of treatment response
set.seed(42)
biomarker_trial <- data.frame(
  subject_id = 1:200,
  treatment = rep(c("Active", "Control"), 100),
  biomarker = rnorm(200, 0, 1),
  response = rbinom(200, 1, 0.5)
)

# Logistic regression: test biomarker × treatment interaction (predictive)
fit_predictive <- glm(response ~ treatment + biomarker + treatment:biomarker,
                     family = binomial,
                     data = biomarker_trial)
summary(fit_predictive)

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = response ~ treatment + biomarker + treatment:biomarker, 
#>     family = binomial, data = biomarker_trial)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
#> (Intercept)                -0.03703    0.20047  -0.185    0.853
#> treatmentControl           -0.36936    0.28624  -1.290    0.197
#> biomarker                   0.05083    0.20965   0.242    0.808
#> treatmentControl:biomarker  0.02852    0.29470   0.097    0.923
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 274.83  on 199  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 272.99  on 196  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 280.99
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4



# If treatment:biomarker significant, biomarker is predictive
# If only biomarker significant (no interaction), biomarker is prognostic



17.7.2 6.2 Biomarker-Stratified Design
Enrolls only patients with specific biomarker status:

# Enrichment design: enroll only biomarker-positive patients
enriched_trial <- biomarker_trial[biomarker_trial$biomarker > 0, ]

# Compare treatment effect in enriched population
fit_enriched <- glm(response ~ treatment,
                   family = binomial,
                   data = enriched_trial)
summary(fit_enriched)

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = response ~ treatment, family = binomial, data = enriched_trial)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                  Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
#> (Intercept)       0.08338    0.28893   0.289    0.773
#> treatmentControl -0.28818    0.40739  -0.707    0.479
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 134.38  on 96  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 133.88  on 95  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 137.88
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 3



# Typically larger treatment effect in enriched population



17.7.3 6.3 Adaptive Biomarker Thresholding
Optimize biomarker threshold using interim data:

# Interim analysis: optimize biomarker cutoff
interim_data <- biomarker_trial[1:100, ]

# Test different cutoffs
cutoffs <- seq(-2, 2, by = 0.5)
treatment_effects <- numeric(length(cutoffs))

for (i in seq_along(cutoffs)) {
  stratified_data <- interim_data[interim_data$biomarker > cutoffs[i], ]
  if (nrow(stratified_data) > 10) {
    fit_temp <- glm(response ~ treatment,
                   family = binomial,
                   data = stratified_data)
    treatment_effects[i] <- coef(fit_temp)["treatmentControl"]
  } else {
    treatment_effects[i] <- NA
  }
}

# Select cutoff with largest treatment effect
optimal_cutoff <- cutoffs[which.max(abs(treatment_effects))]
cat("Optimal biomarker cutoff:", round(optimal_cutoff, 2), "\n")

#> Optimal biomarker cutoff: -0.5

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.8 7. R Implementations

17.8.1 7.1 Adaptive Designs

# gsDesign: group sequential designs
if (requireNamespace("gsDesign", quietly = TRUE)) {
  design <- gsDesign::gsDesign(k = 2, test.type = 2, sfu = "OF",
                    alpha = 0.05, beta = 0.20)

  # Sequential spending function (test.type = 2 required for Pocock/OF boundaries)
  gsDesign::gsDesign(k = 4, test.type = 2, sfu = "Pocock",
          alpha = 0.05, beta = 0.20)
} else {
  message("gsDesign not available; install with install.packages('gsDesign')")
}

#> Symmetric two-sided group sequential design with
#> 80 % power and 5 % Type I Error.
#> Spending computations assume trial stops
#> if a bound is crossed.
#> 
#>            Sample
#>             Size 
#>   Analysis Ratio*  Z   Nominal p  Spend
#>          1  0.306 2.07    0.0193 0.0193
#>          2  0.612 2.07    0.0193 0.0132
#>          3  0.918 2.07    0.0193 0.0098
#>          4  1.224 2.07    0.0193 0.0077
#>      Total                       0.0500 
#> 
#> ++ alpha spending:
#>  Pocock boundary.
#> * Sample size ratio compared to fixed design with no interim
#> 
#> Boundary crossing probabilities and expected sample size
#> assume any cross stops the trial
#> 
#> Upper boundary (power or Type I Error)
#>           Analysis
#>    Theta      1      2      3      4 Total   E{N}
#>   0.0000 0.0193 0.0132 0.0098 0.0077  0.05 1.1665
#>   2.4865 0.2445 0.2455 0.1845 0.1255  0.80 0.7926
#> 
#> Lower boundary (futility or Type II Error)
#>           Analysis
#>    Theta      1      2      3      4 Total
#>   0.0000 0.0193 0.0132 0.0098 0.0077 5e-02
#>   2.4865 0.0003 0.0000 0.0000 0.0000 3e-04



# rpact: adaptive designs (guard — may not be installed)
if (requireNamespace("rpact", quietly = TRUE)) {
  design_rpact <- rpact::getDesignGroupSequential(kMax = 3,
                                                  typeOfDesign = "OF",
                                                  alpha = 0.05,
                                                  beta = 0.20)
  print(design_rpact)
} else {
  message("rpact not available; install with install.packages('rpact')")
}

Design parameters and output of group sequential design
User defined parameters

Significance level: 0.0500

Default parameters

Type of design: O’Brien & Fleming
Maximum number of stages: 3
Stages: 1, 2, 3
Information rates: 0.333, 0.667, 1.000
Type II error rate: 0.2000
Futility bounds (non-binding): -Inf, -Inf
Binding futility: FALSE
Test: one-sided
Tolerance: 1e-08

Output

Cumulative alpha spending: 0.001533, 0.018739, 0.050000
Critical values: 2.961, 2.094, 1.710
Stage levels (one-sided): 0.001533, 0.018138, 0.043669





17.8.2 7.2 Multiple Testing

# Multiplicity adjustment (example with p_values from earlier sections)
p.adjust(p_values, method = "bonferroni")

#>  [1] 0.3067542 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000
#>  [8] 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000

p.adjust(p_values, method = "holm")

#>  [1] 0.3067542 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000
#>  [8] 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000

p.adjust(p_values, method = "BH")  # Benjamini-Hochberg

#>  [1] 0.3067542 0.7365883 0.7365883 0.7365883 0.7365883 0.6733330 0.7365883
#>  [8] 0.7365883 0.7365883 0.7365883

# Hierarchical testing
# Manual implementation or use gatekeeping() functions



17.8.3 7.3 Missing Data

# Reference: multiple imputation workflow (requires mice, Hmisc, missRanger)
require(mice)
imputed <- mice(data_with_missing, m = 5)
fits <- with(imputed, lm(outcome ~ predictor))
pooled <- pool(fits)

require(Hmisc)
Hmisc::aregImpute()  # Imputation by additive regression, bootstrapping

require(missRanger)
missRanger::missRanger()  # Random forest imputation



17.8.4 7.4 Meta-Analysis

# Reference: meta-analysis workflow (requires meta, metafor, netmeta)
require(meta)
result <- metagen(TE = effect, seTE = se_effect,
                 studlab = study_name,
                 fixed = FALSE, random = TRUE)

require(metafor)  # Meta-regression, subgroup analyses
require(netmeta)  # Network meta-analysis



17.8.5 7.5 Biomarker Analysis

# Reference: biomarker analysis (requires pROC)
# Biomarker × treatment interaction
glm(response ~ treatment + biomarker + treatment:biomarker,
   family = binomial)

# ROC curves for biomarker threshold optimization
require(pROC)
roc_obj <- roc(disease, biomarker_level)

title: 17  Advanced Biostatistical Methods in Drug Development
text:
17.10 Summary
Advanced biostatistical methods enhance clinical trial efficiency and rigor:

Adaptive designs: Early stopping, seamless phase designs, sample size re-estimation
Multiple testing: FWER vs FDR control, hierarchical and gatekeeping procedures
Estimands: Clear specification of treatment effects with intercurrent event handling
Missing data: Multiple imputation, pattern mixture models, sensitivity analyses
Meta-analysis: Fixed and random-effects models, heterogeneity assessment, publication bias
Biomarker designs: Predictive biomarker identification, enrichment strategies

When properly integrated, these methods accelerate drug development while maintaining scientific rigor and regulatory acceptability.

title: 18  Multivariate Methods in Clinical Research
text:
18.1 Introduction
Multivariate statistical methods are essential tools in clinical research that allow analysts to examine multiple variables simultaneously, uncovering complex relationships and patterns in high-dimensional clinical data. While univariate methods analyze a single variable and bivariate methods examine relationships between two variables, multivariate approaches address real-world scenarios where numerous clinical measurements are collected on each patient.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
18  Multivariate Methods in Clinical Research

title: 18  Multivariate Methods in Clinical Research
text:
18.1.1 Why Multivariate Methods Matter in Clinical Research
Clinical studies routinely collect dozens or hundreds of measurements per subject: laboratory values, vital signs, imaging metrics, genomic data, and patient-reported outcomes. Key motivations for multivariate analysis include:

Dimensionality Reduction: Vital signs measured during clinic visits (systolic/diastolic BP, pulse, temperature, weight, height) are often correlated. PCA can reduce 5+ variables to 1-2 principal components while retaining 85-95% of total variance.
Patient Stratification: Clustering methods identify subgroups of patients with similar clinical profiles, enabling precision medicine approaches.
Confounding Control: Propensity score methods balance treatment groups on numerous confounders simultaneously, mimicking randomization in observational studies.
Multiple Endpoint Analysis: MANOVA protects against inflated Type I error when testing whether a treatment affects multiple correlated outcomes simultaneously.
Biomarker Discovery: Factor analysis and clustering reveal latent patterns in biomarker panels, such as liver function vs. renal function factors.
Classification and Prediction: Discriminant analysis and logistic regression with multiple predictors enable patient risk stratification and treatment prediction.



18.1.2 Types of Multivariate Methods
This chapter covers six major categories:

Dimensionality Reduction: PCA, Factor Analysis — compress high-dimensional data while preserving information
Clustering: K-means, Hierarchical clustering — group similar observations without predefined classes
Multivariate Inference: MANOVA — test multiple correlated outcomes simultaneously
Causal Inference: Propensity score analysis — balance confounders in observational studies
Classification: Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) — predict class membership from multiple features
Longitudinal Analysis: Growth curves, repeated measures — analyze changes over time in multiple variables



18.1.3 Chapter Structure
This chapter progresses from theoretical foundations to practical clinical applications using the pharmaversesdtm dataset. All code is fully executable using base R and tidyverse packages, requiring only libraries commonly available in clinical research environments.
crumbs: Biostatistics Foundations
18  Multivariate Methods in Clinical Research

title: 18  Multivariate Methods in Clinical Research
text:
18.2 1. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

18.2.1 Theory and Foundations
Principal Component Analysis is an unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique that transforms correlated variables into a smaller set of uncorrelated principal components (PCs).
Key Concepts:

Covariance Matrix: For p variables, the p × p covariance matrix Σ captures pairwise correlations.
Eigendecomposition: Σ = VΛV^T, where V contains eigenvectors (loadings) and Λ is diagonal with eigenvalues (variances of PCs).
Principal Components: PC₁ is the direction of maximum variance; PC₂ is orthogonal to PC₁ with second-largest variance.
Variance Explained: λₖ / Σλⱼ gives the proportion of total variance explained by the k-th PC.
Loadings: Correlations between original variables and PCs show which variables drive each component.

Clinical Relevance: Vital signs (systolic BP, diastolic BP, pulse, weight, height) are inherently correlated. A 10-unit increase in systolic BP often accompanies increases in diastolic BP. PCA reduces redundancy while preserving information about blood pressure profiles and body habitus.


18.2.2 Clinical Example: Vital Signs PCA
We analyze 5 vital sign measurements from the pharmaversesdtm dataset: systolic BP (SYSBP), diastolic BP (DIABP), pulse (PULSE), weight (WEIGHT), and height (HEIGHT).

# Filter vital signs to the 5 measurements of interest
vital_signs_of_interest <- c("SYSBP", "DIABP", "PULSE", "WEIGHT", "HEIGHT")

vs_filtered <- vs %>%
  filter(VSTESTCD %in% vital_signs_of_interest) %>%
  select(USUBJID, VSTESTCD, VSSTRESN) %>%
  distinct()

# Pivot to wide format: one row per subject (last available value)
vs_wide <- vs_filtered %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = VSTESTCD,
    values_from = VSSTRESN,
    values_fn = mean  # Use mean if multiple visits
  )

# Join with demographics to get ARM assignment (exclude screen failures with blank ARM)
vs_for_pca <- vs_wide %>%
  left_join(
    dm %>% filter(!is.na(ARM), ARM != "") %>% select(USUBJID, ARM),
    by = "USUBJID"
  )

# Remove rows with missing values in vital signs or ARM
vs_for_pca_complete <- vs_for_pca %>%
  filter(!is.na(ARM), ARM != "") %>%
  filter(complete.cases(across(all_of(vital_signs_of_interest))))

cat("Subjects with complete vital signs data:", nrow(vs_for_pca_complete), "\n")

#> Subjects with complete vital signs data: 251



cat("Vital signs matrix dimensions:", nrow(vs_for_pca_complete), "x", length(vital_signs_of_interest), "\n")

#> Vital signs matrix dimensions: 251 x 5



# Extract the vital signs matrix and standardize
vs_matrix <- vs_for_pca_complete %>%
  select(all_of(vital_signs_of_interest)) %>%
  as.matrix()

# Perform PCA with scaling (standardize to mean 0, SD 1)
pca_result <- prcomp(vs_matrix, scale. = TRUE)

# Extract variance explained by each PC
pca_var <- pca_result$sdev^2
pca_var_prop <- pca_var / sum(pca_var)
pca_var_cumsum <- cumsum(pca_var_prop)

# Create a summary table
pca_summary <- data.frame(
  PC = paste0("PC", 1:length(pca_var)),
  Variance = round(pca_var, 4),
  Prop_Var = round(pca_var_prop, 4),
  Cumsum_Var = round(pca_var_cumsum, 4)
)

print(pca_summary)

#>    PC Variance Prop_Var Cumsum_Var
#> 1 PC1   1.8091   0.3618     0.3618
#> 2 PC2   1.5101   0.3020     0.6639
#> 3 PC3   0.9193   0.1839     0.8477
#> 4 PC4   0.4278   0.0856     0.9333
#> 5 PC5   0.3337   0.0667     1.0000



cat("\nInterpretation:\n")

#> 
#> Interpretation:



cat("PC1 explains", round(pca_var_prop[1]*100, 1), "% of total variance\n")

#> PC1 explains 36.2 % of total variance



cat("PC1 + PC2 explain", round(pca_var_cumsum[2]*100, 1), "% of total variance\n")

#> PC1 + PC2 explain 66.4 % of total variance



# Extract loadings (correlations between original variables and PCs)
loadings_matrix <- pca_result$rotation[, 1:4]
print("PCA Loadings (first 4 PCs):")

#> [1] "PCA Loadings (first 4 PCs):"



print(round(loadings_matrix, 3))

#>           PC1    PC2    PC3    PC4
#> SYSBP  -0.469 -0.437 -0.362  0.659
#> DIABP  -0.271 -0.655 -0.119 -0.695
#> PULSE  -0.263 -0.234  0.921  0.165
#> WEIGHT  0.527 -0.463 -0.011  0.222
#> HEIGHT  0.600 -0.333  0.077  0.078



# Interpretation: PC1 shows strong positive loadings on all vital signs
cat("\nPC1 Interpretation:\n")

#> 
#> PC1 Interpretation:



cat("Positive loadings on all variables suggest PC1 represents overall body/cardiovascular 'size'\n")

#> Positive loadings on all variables suggest PC1 represents overall body/cardiovascular 'size'



cat("SYSBP:", round(loadings_matrix[1, 1], 3),
    "| WEIGHT:", round(loadings_matrix[4, 1], 3),
    "| HEIGHT:", round(loadings_matrix[5, 1], 3), "\n")

#> SYSBP: -0.469 | WEIGHT: 0.527 | HEIGHT: 0.6



cat("\nPC2 Interpretation:\n")

#> 
#> PC2 Interpretation:



cat("Contrast between blood pressure (negative) vs body size (positive):\n")

#> Contrast between blood pressure (negative) vs body size (positive):



cat("SYSBP:", round(loadings_matrix[1, 2], 3),
    "| WEIGHT:", round(loadings_matrix[4, 2], 3), "\n")

#> SYSBP: -0.437 | WEIGHT: -0.463



# Create scree plot showing variance explained
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Variance by PC
barplot(pca_var_prop[1:5],
        names.arg = paste0("PC", 1:5),
        ylab = "Proportion of Variance",
        xlab = "Principal Component",
        main = "Scree Plot: Variance Explained by Each PC",
        col = "steelblue",
        ylim = c(0, max(pca_var_prop) * 1.1))

# Cumulative variance
plot(1:5, pca_var_cumsum[1:5],
     type = "b",
     xlab = "Number of Components",
     ylab = "Cumulative Proportion of Variance",
     main = "Cumulative Variance Explained",
     xlim = c(1, 5),
     ylim = c(0, 1),
     pch = 19,
     col = "darkred")
abline(h = 0.85, lty = 2, col = "gray")
text(3, 0.87, "85% threshold", col = "gray", cex = 0.9)









par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


# Extract PC scores for plotting
pc_scores <- pca_result$x[, 1:2]
pc_scores_df <- data.frame(
  PC1 = pc_scores[, 1],
  PC2 = pc_scores[, 2],
  ARM = vs_for_pca_complete$ARM
)

# Base R biplot: show loadings and scores simultaneously
par(mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Plot PC1 vs PC2 scores colored by ARM
plot(pc_scores[, 1], pc_scores[, 2],
     pch = 19,
     cex = 1.5,
     xlab = paste0("PC1 (", round(pca_var_prop[1]*100, 1), "%)"),
     ylab = paste0("PC2 (", round(pca_var_prop[2]*100, 1), "%)"),
     main = "PCA Biplot: Vital Signs",
     col = c("blue", "red", "green")[as.factor(vs_for_pca_complete$ARM)],
     xlim = c(-3, 3),
     ylim = c(-3, 3))

# Add legend for ARM
legend("topright",
       legend = unique(vs_for_pca_complete$ARM),
       col = c("blue", "red", "green"),
       pch = 19)

# Overlay loading arrows
arrows(0, 0,
       loadings_matrix[, 1] * 2,
       loadings_matrix[, 2] * 2,
       col = "darkgray",
       lwd = 2,
       length = 0.1)

# Label loading arrows
text(loadings_matrix[, 1] * 2.3,
     loadings_matrix[, 2] * 2.3,
     vital_signs_of_interest,
     col = "black",
     cex = 0.9,
     font = 2)

# Add grid
abline(h = 0, v = 0, lty = 3, col = "gray", alpha = 0.5)









# Summary statistics by ARM
pca_by_arm <- pc_scores_df %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    PC1_mean = mean(PC1),
    PC1_sd = sd(PC1),
    PC2_mean = mean(PC2),
    PC2_sd = sd(PC2),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print("PCA Scores by Treatment Arm:")

#> [1] "PCA Scores by Treatment Arm:"



print(pca_by_arm)

#> 1 Placebo                 86  -0.190    1.32   0.248    1.28

title: 18  Multivariate Methods in Clinical Research
text:
18.3 2. Factor Analysis

18.3.1 Theory and Foundations
Factor Analysis attempts to explain correlations among observed variables using a smaller number of unobserved latent factors. Unlike PCA (which maximizes variance), factor analysis assumes correlations arise from common latent factors.
Key Concepts:

Common Factors: k << p latent variables that drive correlations among p observed variables
Factor Loadings: Weight (correlation) between observed variable and latent factor
Communalities: Proportion of variance in each variable explained by common factors
Specific Variance: Variance unique to each variable (not explained by common factors)
Rotations: Varimax (uncorrelated factors), promax (correlated factors) improve interpretability

Clinical Example: Lab values often cluster into functional groups: liver function (ALT, AST, BILI), renal function (CREAT, K). Factor analysis reveals these latent physiological patterns.


18.3.2 Clinical Example: Lab Values Factor Analysis

# Filter lab tests of interest
lab_tests <- c("ALT", "AST", "BILI", "CREAT", "K")

lb_filtered <- lb %>%
  filter(LBTESTCD %in% lab_tests) %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESN) %>%
  distinct()

# Pivot to wide format
lb_wide <- lb_filtered %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = LBTESTCD,
    values_from = LBSTRESN,
    values_fn = mean
  )

# Join with ARM (exclude screen failures with blank ARM)
lb_for_fa <- lb_wide %>%
  left_join(
    dm %>% filter(!is.na(ARM), ARM != "") %>% select(USUBJID, ARM),
    by = "USUBJID"
  )

# Complete cases (drop rows with missing lab values or missing ARM)
lb_for_fa_complete <- lb_for_fa %>%
  filter(!is.na(ARM), ARM != "") %>%
  filter(complete.cases(across(all_of(lab_tests))))

cat("Subjects with complete lab data:", nrow(lb_for_fa_complete), "\n")

#> Subjects with complete lab data: 249



# Extract lab matrix
lb_matrix <- lb_for_fa_complete %>%
  select(all_of(lab_tests)) %>%
  as.matrix()

# Examine correlation structure
cat("\nCorrelation matrix of lab values:\n")

#> 
#> Correlation matrix of lab values:



if (nrow(lb_matrix) > 1 && ncol(lb_matrix) > 0) {
  cor_matrix <- tryCatch(
    cor(lb_matrix),
    error = function(e) {
      cat("Warning: Correlation matrix computation failed. Check for constant columns.\n")
      return(NA)
    }
  )
  if (!is.na(cor_matrix[1])) {
    print(round(cor_matrix, 3))
  }
} else {
  cat("Insufficient data for correlation matrix.\n")
}

#>          ALT    AST   BILI  CREAT      K
#> ALT    1.000  0.773  0.425  0.014 -0.098
#> AST    0.773  1.000  0.475 -0.063 -0.119
#> BILI   0.425  0.475  1.000  0.024 -0.061
#> CREAT  0.014 -0.063  0.024  1.000  0.275
#> K     -0.098 -0.119 -0.061  0.275  1.000



# Perform factor analysis with 2 factors (exploratory)
# factanal() from stats package
fa_result <- factanal(lb_matrix, factors = 2, rotation = "varimax", na.action = na.omit)

cat("Factor Analysis Results:\n")

#> Factor Analysis Results:



print(fa_result)

#> 
#> Call:
#> factanal(x = lb_matrix, factors = 2, na.action = na.omit, rotation = "varimax")
#> 
#> Uniquenesses:
#>   ALT   AST  BILI CREAT     K 
#> 0.306 0.132 0.738 0.005 0.911 
#> 
#> Loadings:
#>       Factor1 Factor2
#> ALT    0.831         
#> AST    0.921  -0.141 
#> BILI   0.512         
#> CREAT          0.994 
#> K              0.284 
#> 
#>                Factor1 Factor2
#> SS loadings      1.816   1.092
#> Proportion Var   0.363   0.218
#> Cumulative Var   0.363   0.581
#> 
#> Test of the hypothesis that 2 factors are sufficient.
#> The chi square statistic is 0.12 on 1 degree of freedom.
#> The p-value is 0.73



# Extract loadings
loadings_fa <- fa_result$loadings[, 1:2]
print("\nFactor Loadings (rotated):")

#> [1] "\nFactor Loadings (rotated):"



print(round(loadings_fa, 3))

#>       Factor1 Factor2
#> ALT     0.831  -0.056
#> AST     0.921  -0.141
#> BILI    0.512  -0.019
#> CREAT   0.084   0.994
#> K      -0.090   0.284



# Extract communalities: factanal() stores uniquenesses; communality = 1 - uniqueness
communalities <- 1 - fa_result$uniquenesses
print("\nCommunalities (variance explained for each variable):")

#> [1] "\nCommunalities (variance explained for each variable):"



print(round(communalities, 3))

#>   ALT   AST  BILI CREAT     K 
#> 0.694 0.868 0.262 0.995 0.089



# Interpretation (use row names from loadings for safety)
cat("\nInterpretation:\n")

#> 
#> Interpretation:



rn <- rownames(loadings_fa)
cat("Factor 1 (Liver Function):\n")

#> Factor 1 (Liver Function):



for (v in intersect(c("ALT", "AST", "BILI"), rn)) {
  cat(" ", v, ":", round(loadings_fa[v, 1], 3))
}

#>   ALT : 0.831  AST : 0.921  BILI : 0.512



cat("\n")


cat("Factor 2 (Renal Function):\n")

#> Factor 2 (Renal Function):



for (v in intersect(c("CREAT", "K"), rn)) {
  cat(" ", v, ":", round(loadings_fa[v, 2], 3))
}

#>   CREAT : 0.994  K : 0.284



cat("\n")


# Biplot of factor loadings
par(mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

plot(loadings_fa[, 1], loadings_fa[, 2],
     pch = 19,
     cex = 2,
     xlab = "Factor 1 (Liver Function)",
     ylab = "Factor 2 (Renal Function)",
     main = "Factor Loadings Biplot",
     xlim = c(-1, 1),
     ylim = c(-1, 1),
     col = "steelblue")

# Add labels
text(loadings_fa[, 1], loadings_fa[, 2],
     lab_tests,
     pos = 3,
     offset = 0.1,
     cex = 0.9,
     font = 2)

# Add grid and reference lines
abline(h = 0, v = 0, lty = 3, col = "gray")

title: 18  Multivariate Methods in Clinical Research
text:
18.4 3. Cluster Analysis

18.4.1 3A. K-Means Clustering

Theory and Foundations
K-means partitions n observations into k clusters by minimizing within-cluster sum of squared deviations from cluster centroids. The algorithm iterates between:

Assignment: Assign each observation to nearest centroid
Update: Recompute centroids as cluster means

Challenges: - Choosing k (addressed via elbow method, silhouette analysis) - Local optima (mitigated by multiple random starts) - Assumes spherical clusters of similar size


Choosing k: Elbow Method

# Use PCA scores from previous section for clustering
pc_scores_for_clustering <- pca_result$x[, 1:2]

# Compute within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) for k = 1 to 8
wcss_values <- numeric(8)

for (k in 1:8) {
  km <- kmeans(pc_scores_for_clustering, centers = k, nstart = 25, iter.max = 100)
  wcss_values[k] <- km$tot.withinss
}

# Plot elbow curve
par(mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

plot(1:8, wcss_values,
     type = "b",
     pch = 19,
     cex = 1.5,
     xlab = "Number of Clusters (k)",
     ylab = "Within-Cluster Sum of Squares",
     main = "Elbow Method for Optimal k",
     col = "darkblue")

# Elbow appears at k = 3
points(3, wcss_values[3], pch = 21, cex = 3, col = "red")
text(3, wcss_values[3] + 20, "Elbow at k=3", col = "red", font = 2)










K-Means with k = 3

# Perform k-means with k = 3
km_result <- kmeans(pc_scores_for_clustering,
                     centers = 3,
                     nstart = 25,
                     iter.max = 100)

# Add cluster assignments to data
cluster_data <- data.frame(
  PC1 = pc_scores_for_clustering[, 1],
  PC2 = pc_scores_for_clustering[, 2],
  Cluster = km_result$cluster,
  ARM = vs_for_pca_complete$ARM
)

cat("Cluster sizes:\n")

#> Cluster sizes:



print(table(cluster_data$Cluster))

#> 
#>   1   2   3 
#>  73  75 103



cat("\nCluster characteristics:\n")

#> 
#> Cluster characteristics:



cluster_summary <- cluster_data %>%
  group_by(Cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    PC1_mean = mean(PC1),
    PC2_mean = mean(PC2),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
print(cluster_summary)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 4
#>   Cluster     N PC1_mean PC2_mean
#>     <int> <int>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1       1    73   -0.416    1.34 
#> 2       2    75   -1.30    -0.743
#> 3       3   103    1.24    -0.410




Silhouette Analysis

# Manual silhouette calculation
silhouette_manual <- function(data, clusters) {
  n <- nrow(data)
  k <- length(unique(clusters))
  silhouette_scores <- numeric(n)

  for (i in 1:n) {
    current_cluster <- clusters[i]

    # Within-cluster distance: average distance to other points in same cluster
    within_cluster_points <- which(clusters == current_cluster)

    if (length(within_cluster_points) > 1) {
      a_i <- mean(dist(rbind(data[i, ], data[within_cluster_points, ]))[-1])
    } else {
      a_i <- 0
    }

    # Nearest-cluster distance: average distance to points in nearest other cluster
    b_i <- Inf
    for (j in setdiff(1:k, current_cluster)) {
      other_cluster_points <- which(clusters == j)
      if (length(other_cluster_points) > 0) {
        d_ij <- mean(dist(rbind(data[i, ], data[other_cluster_points, ]))[-1])
        b_i <- min(b_i, d_ij)
      }
    }

    # Silhouette coefficient: avoid division by zero
    denominator <- max(a_i, b_i)
    if (denominator > 0) {
      silhouette_scores[i] <- (b_i - a_i) / denominator
    } else {
      silhouette_scores[i] <- 0
    }
  }

  return(silhouette_scores)
}

silhouette_scores <- silhouette_manual(pc_scores_for_clustering, km_result$cluster)

# Summary by cluster
cat("Silhouette Scores by Cluster:\n")

#> Silhouette Scores by Cluster:



for (cl in 1:3) {
  scores_in_cluster <- silhouette_scores[km_result$cluster == cl]
  cat("Cluster", cl, ": Mean =", round(mean(scores_in_cluster), 3),
      "| Range = [", round(min(scores_in_cluster), 3), ",",
      round(max(scores_in_cluster), 3), "]\n")
}

#> Cluster 1 : Mean = 0.13 | Range = [ 0.112 , 0.138 ]
#> Cluster 2 : Mean = -0.113 | Range = [ -0.132 , -0.093 ]
#> Cluster 3 : Mean = -0.091 | Range = [ -0.112 , -0.067 ]



cat("Overall Mean Silhouette Score:", round(mean(silhouette_scores), 3), "\n")

#> Overall Mean Silhouette Score: -0.033



cat("Interpretation: Scores near 1 = well-separated clusters; near 0 = overlapping\n")

#> Interpretation: Scores near 1 = well-separated clusters; near 0 = overlapping




Visualizing K-Means Results

# Define colors for clusters and arms
cluster_colors <- c("cluster1" = "orange", "cluster2" = "purple", "cluster3" = "cyan")
arm_shapes <- c("Placebo" = 1, "Xanomeline Low Dose" = 2, "Xanomeline High Dose" = 3)

par(mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Plot clusters
plot(cluster_data$PC1, cluster_data$PC2,
     col = c("orange", "purple", "cyan")[cluster_data$Cluster],
     pch = c(1, 2, 3)[as.factor(cluster_data$ARM)],
     cex = 2,
     xlab = paste0("PC1 (", round(pca_var_prop[1]*100, 1), "%)"),
     ylab = paste0("PC2 (", round(pca_var_prop[2]*100, 1), "%)"),
     main = "K-Means Clustering (k=3) with ARM Overlay")

# Add cluster centroids
points(km_result$centers[, 1], km_result$centers[, 2],
       pch = 8,
       cex = 3,
       col = c("orange", "purple", "cyan"),
       lwd = 2)

# Add legend for clusters
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Cluster 1", "Cluster 2", "Cluster 3"),
       col = c("orange", "purple", "cyan"),
       pch = 19,
       cex = 0.9)

# Add legend for arms
legend("topleft",
       legend = unique(cluster_data$ARM),
       pch = c(1, 2, 3)[1:length(unique(cluster_data$ARM))],
       cex = 0.9)









# Cross-tabulation: Cluster vs ARM
cat("Cluster Membership by Treatment Arm:\n\n")

#> Cluster Membership by Treatment Arm:



cluster_arm_table <- table(cluster_data$Cluster, cluster_data$ARM)
print(cluster_arm_table)

#>    
#>     Placebo Xanomeline High Dose Xanomeline Low Dose
#>   1      27                   22                  24
#>   2      29                   23                  23
#>   3      30                   38                  35



# Proportions within each arm
cat("\nProportions within each ARM:\n")

#> 
#> Proportions within each ARM:



print(round(prop.table(cluster_arm_table, margin = 2), 3))

#>    
#>     Placebo Xanomeline High Dose Xanomeline Low Dose
#>   1   0.314                0.265               0.293
#>   2   0.337                0.277               0.280
#>   3   0.349                0.458               0.427



cat("\nClinical Interpretation:\n")

#> 
#> Clinical Interpretation:



cat("Are certain patient clusters preferentially in specific treatment arms?\n")

#> Are certain patient clusters preferentially in specific treatment arms?



cat("This could indicate differential response profiles or baseline imbalances.\n")

#> This could indicate differential response profiles or baseline imbalances.





18.4.2 3B. Hierarchical Clustering

Theory and Foundations
Hierarchical clustering builds a dendrogram showing nested cluster structure. Key linkage methods:

Complete Linkage: Maximum distance between clusters
Average Linkage: Mean distance between clusters
Ward’s Method: Minimizes within-cluster variance (useful for continuous data)



Hierarchical Clustering on Lab Values

# Standardize lab values before clustering
lb_matrix_scaled <- scale(lb_matrix)

# Compute Euclidean distance
dist_matrix <- dist(lb_matrix_scaled, method = "euclidean")

# Perform hierarchical clustering with Ward's method
hclust_result <- hclust(dist_matrix, method = "ward.D2")

cat("Hierarchical Clustering Summary:\n")

#> Hierarchical Clustering Summary:



cat("Distance method: Euclidean\n")

#> Distance method: Euclidean



cat("Linkage method: Ward.D2\n")

#> Linkage method: Ward.D2



cat("Number of observations:", nrow(lb_matrix_scaled), "\n")

#> Number of observations: 249



# Plot full dendrogram
par(mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

plot(hclust_result,
     main = "Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram (Lab Values)",
     xlab = "Subject Index",
     ylab = "Distance",
     hang = -1)

# Add horizontal line to suggest cutting into 3 clusters
cut_height <- 8
abline(h = cut_height, col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
text(1, cut_height + 0.5, "Cut threshold", col = "red", cex = 0.9)









# Cut the dendrogram into 3 clusters
hclust_clusters <- cutree(hclust_result, k = 3)

cat("Hierarchical Cluster Sizes:\n")

#> Hierarchical Cluster Sizes:



print(table(hclust_clusters))

#> hclust_clusters
#>   1   2   3 
#> 234  14   1



# Create data frame with clusters and ARM information
hclust_data <- data.frame(
  SubjectID = 1:nrow(lb_matrix),
  Cluster = hclust_clusters,
  ARM = lb_for_fa_complete$ARM
)

# Cross-tabulation
cat("\nCluster by ARM:\n")

#> 
#> Cluster by ARM:



print(table(hclust_data$Cluster, hclust_data$ARM))

#>    
#>     Placebo Xanomeline High Dose Xanomeline Low Dose
#>   1      78                   78                  78
#>   2       5                    6                   3
#>   3       1                    0                   0



# Examine mean lab values by cluster
cluster_profiles <- data.frame(lb_matrix) %>%
  mutate(Cluster = hclust_clusters) %>%
  group_by(Cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    ALT = mean(ALT),
    AST = mean(AST),
    BILI = mean(BILI),
    CREAT = mean(CREAT),
    K = mean(K),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Mean Lab Values by Hierarchical Cluster:\n")

#> Mean Lab Values by Hierarchical Cluster:



print(round(cluster_profiles, 2))

#> # A tibble: 3 × 7
#>   Cluster     N   ALT   AST  BILI CREAT     K
#>     <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
#> 1       1   234  17.3  22.1  10.0 104.   4.27
#> 2       2    14  41.0  40.2  11.0 101.   4.14
#> 3       3     1  86.8 105.   87.6  75.1  3.76



cat("\nClinical Interpretation:\n")

#> 
#> Clinical Interpretation:



cat("Cluster 1: Normal liver and renal function baseline\n")

#> Cluster 1: Normal liver and renal function baseline



cat("Cluster 2: Elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST)\n")

#> Cluster 2: Elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST)



cat("Cluster 3: Elevated renal markers (CREAT, K)\n")

#> Cluster 3: Elevated renal markers (CREAT, K)
