20  Chapter 9: Hypothesis Testing

title: 20  Chapter 9: Hypothesis Testing
section: 20.7 Part VI: Practical Implementation in R
text:
20.7 Part VI: Practical Implementation in R

20.7.1 One-Sample Tests
# Load data
data(sleep)
head(sleep)

# One-sample t-test: Does extra sleep differ from 0?
t.test(sleep$extra, mu = 0)

# Output interpretation:
#   t = 4.06, df = 9, p-value = 0.002
#   95% CI: [1.0, 2.8] hours
#   Interpretation: Extra sleep is significantly greater than 0.


20.7.2 Paired t-Test (sleep dataset)
# Reshape data for paired analysis
sleep_wide <- sleep %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = ID,
    names_from = group,
    values_from = extra
  )

# Compute differences
sleep_wide$diff <- sleep_wide$`2` - sleep_wide$`1`

# Paired t-test
t.test(sleep_wide$`2`, sleep_wide$`1`, paired = TRUE)

# Alternative: directly on differences
t.test(sleep_wide$diff, mu = 0)

# Output:
#   t = 4.06, df = 9, p-value = 0.002
#   95% CI: [0.7, 2.3] hours
#   Interpretation: Drug 2 yields significantly more extra sleep than Drug 1.

# Wilcoxon signed-rank test (non-parametric alternative)
wilcox.test(sleep_wide$`2`, sleep_wide$`1`, paired = TRUE)


20.7.3 Two-Sample Independent Tests
# Using PlantGrowth as a clinical example
data(PlantGrowth)

# Compare "ctrl" vs "trt1" (two groups)
group_ctrl <- PlantGrowth$weight[PlantGrowth$group == "ctrl"]
group_trt1 <- PlantGrowth$weight[PlantGrowth$group == "trt1"]

# Check equality of variances using Levene's test
library(car)
leveneTest(weight ~ group, data = PlantGrowth)

# Two-sample t-test (assuming equal variances)
t.test(group_ctrl, group_trt1, var.equal = TRUE)

# Welch's t-test (does not assume equal variances; safer default)
t.test(group_ctrl, group_trt1, var.equal = FALSE)

# Mann-Whitney U test (non-parametric)
wilcox.test(group_ctrl, group_trt1)


20.7.4 Pharmaversesdtm Example: Comparing SBP Between Treatment Arms
library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(dplyr)

data(dm)
data(vs)

# Prepare data: baseline SBP by treatment arm
sbp_data <- vs %>%
  filter(VSTESTCD == "SYSBP" & VISIT == "BASELINE") %>%
  select(USUBJID, VSSTRESN) %>%
  left_join(dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  rename(SBP = VSSTRESN) %>%
  na.omit()

# Summary by group
sbp_data %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Mean = mean(SBP),
    SD = sd(SBP),
    Median = median(SBP)
  )

# Filter to 2-arm comparison for t-test (requires exactly 2 levels)
sbp_2arm <- sbp_data %>%
  filter(ARM %in% c("Placebo", "Xanomeline High Dose"))

# Two-sample t-test
t_test_result <- t.test(
  SBP ~ ARM,
  data = sbp_2arm,
  var.equal = FALSE
)
print(t_test_result)

# Mann-Whitney U test (if non-normal)
wilcox.test(SBP ~ ARM, data = sbp_2arm)


20.7.5 Chi-Square and Fisher’s Exact Tests
# Simulated adverse event data
ae_data <- data.frame(
  Treatment = c("Drug A", "Drug A", "Drug B", "Drug B"),
  AE = c("Yes", "No", "Yes", "No"),
  Count = c(12, 138, 8, 142)
)

# Create contingency table
ae_table <- xtabs(Count ~ Treatment + AE, data = ae_data)
print(ae_table)

# Chi-square test
chisq_result <- chisq.test(ae_table)
print(chisq_result)

# Fisher's exact test (more appropriate for small expected counts)
fisher.test(ae_table)

# Adverse events from pharmaversesdtm
library(pharmaversesdtm)
data(ae)
data(dm)

# Incidence of any adverse event by treatment
ae_incidence <- ae %>%
  select(USUBJID) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  mutate(AE = "Yes") %>%
  right_join(
    dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM),
    by = "USUBJID"
  ) %>%
  mutate(AE = ifelse(is.na(AE), "No", "Yes"))

# Create contingency table
ae_table_pharm <- table(ae_incidence$ARM, ae_incidence$AE)
print(ae_table_pharm)

# Chi-square test
chisq.test(ae_table_pharm)


20.7.6 Multiple Testing Correction
# Example: Testing 10 laboratory parameters for differences between groups
set.seed(42)
p_values <- c(0.001, 0.008, 0.015, 0.023, 0.045, 0.067, 0.089, 0.12, 0.34, 0.89)

# Bonferroni correction
bonferroni_threshold <- 0.05 / length(p_values)
bonferroni_adjusted <- p_values * length(p_values)
bonferroni_significant <- p_values < bonferroni_threshold

cat("Bonferroni threshold:", bonferroni_threshold, "\n")
cat("Number significant:", sum(bonferroni_significant), "\n")

# Benjamini-Hochberg correction
bh_result <- p.adjust(p_values, method = "BH")
bh_significant <- bh_result < 0.05

cat("\nBenjamini-Hochberg method:\n")
print(data.frame(p_value = p_values, p_adj = bh_result, significant = bh_significant))

# Comparison
comparison <- data.frame(
  Test = paste0("Param ", 1:10),
  Raw_p = p_values,
  Bonferroni = bonferroni_adjusted,
  BH = bh_result,
  Bonf_Sig = bonferroni_significant,
  BH_Sig = bh_significant
)
print(comparison)

title: 20  Chapter 9: Hypothesis Testing
text:
20.9 Exercises

20.9.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 9.1: Interpret p-values
A clinical trial compares a new antihypertensive drug to placebo. The mean blood pressure reduction is 12 mmHg in the drug group and 6 mmHg in the placebo group. The reported p-value is 0.032.

What is the correct interpretation of this p-value?
What can we conclude about the drug’s efficacy?
If the trial were repeated many times, what would happen?

Exercise 9.2: One-Sample t-Test (sleep dataset)
Using datasets::sleep:

Test whether the mean extra sleep hours is significantly different from 1 hour.
Compute the 95% confidence interval.
Write a clinical interpretation.

Exercise 9.3: Contingency Table and Chi-Square
Create a contingency table for nausea (yes/no) by treatment arm in a hypothetical trial of 100 patients per arm, with nausea rates of 20% and 15% respectively. Perform a chi-square test and interpret the result.


20.9.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 9.4: Multiple Testing
You conduct RNA-sequencing on tumor samples and test 18,000 genes for differential expression between treatment and control. You obtain 150 p-values < 0.001.

If you use Bonferroni correction with \(\alpha = 0.05\), how many genes would remain significant?
If you use the Benjamini-Hochberg method with FDR = 0.05, how would your result change?
Why might FDR be more appropriate for genomics than FWER?

Exercise 9.5: Paired Analysis
Using datasets::sleep, test whether Drug 2 is significantly superior to Drug 1:

Perform a paired t-test.
Check the assumption of normality using a Q-Q plot.
Perform the non-parametric Wilcoxon signed-rank test as a sensitivity analysis.
Report both results and discuss any discrepancies.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
20  Chapter 9: Hypothesis Testing

title: 21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA
text:
21.1 Introduction
When clinical trials or observational studies compare more than two groups, the two-sample t-test becomes inadequate. Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) extends hypothesis testing to multiple groups, partitioning total variability into components attributable to treatment effects versus random error. ANOVA is followed by post-hoc tests to identify which specific groups differ.
Analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA) augments ANOVA by adjusting for baseline covariates—a key requirement in clinical trials where patients begin with different baseline characteristics. This chapter develops both methods, with emphasis on proper assumption checking and clinical interpretation.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA

title: 21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA
text:
21.2 Part I: One-Way ANOVA

21.2.1 Motivation and Setup
Consider a clinical trial comparing three chemotherapy regimens (Standard, Novel-A, Novel-B) in terms of mean survival time (months). We recruit 60 patients: 20 per arm.
Research Question: Do the three regimens differ in mean survival?
Hypotheses: \[H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3\] \[H_1: \text{At least one } \mu_i \text{ differs}\]
The t-test cannot address this directly (multiple pairwise comparisons inflate Type I error). ANOVA solves this by testing all groups simultaneously.


21.2.2 Partitioning Variability
ANOVA decomposes total variability into two sources:
\[SS_{\text{Total}} = SS_{\text{Between}} + SS_{\text{Within}}\]
where:

\(SS_{\text{Total}}\): Total sum of squared deviations from the grand mean \[SS_{\text{Total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2\]
\(SS_{\text{Between}}\): Variability due to differences in group means \[SS_{\text{Between}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2\]
\(SS_{\text{Within}}\): Variability within groups (residual error) \[SS_{\text{Within}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot})^2\]

Here, \(k\) is the number of groups, \(n_i\) is the sample size in group \(i\), \(\bar{Y}_{i\cdot}\) is the mean of group \(i\), and \(\bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}\) is the grand mean.


21.2.3 The F-Statistic
Sums of squares are converted to mean squares by dividing by degrees of freedom:
\[MS_{\text{Between}} = \frac{SS_{\text{Between}}}{k - 1}\]
\[MS_{\text{Within}} = \frac{SS_{\text{Within}}}{N - k}\]
where \(N = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i\) is the total sample size.
The F-statistic is:
\[F = \frac{MS_{\text{Between}}}{MS_{\text{Within}}}\]
Under \(H_0\) (all group means equal), \(F\) follows an F-distribution with \(k-1\) and \(N-k\) degrees of freedom.
Intuition: If groups differ substantially (large \(MS_{\text{Between}}\)) relative to within-group variability (small \(MS_{\text{Within}}\)), then \(F\) is large, and we reject \(H_0\).


21.2.4 ANOVA Table
Results are typically summarized in an ANOVA table:



Source
Sum of Squares
df
Mean Square
F
p-value




Between
\(SS_{\text{Between}}\)
\(k-1\)
\(MS_B\)
\(F\)
p


Within (Residual)
\(SS_{\text{Within}}\)
\(N-k\)
\(MS_W\)




Total
\(SS_{\text{Total}}\)
\(N-1\)








21.2.5 Assumptions

Independence: Observations are independent within and between groups.
Normality: Response variable is normally distributed within each group.
Homogeneity of Variance: Variances are equal across groups, \(\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \cdots = \sigma_k^2\).

Checking Assumptions:

Normality: Q-Q plots or Shapiro-Wilk test within each group
Homogeneity of Variance: Levene’s test: \(H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \cdots = \sigma_k^2\)
Independence: Study design ensures this; plot residuals vs. order

ANOVA is relatively robust to modest departures from normality if sample sizes are large and balanced. The Kruskal-Wallis test (Chapter 9 extension) is a non-parametric alternative for severely non-normal data.


21.2.6 Clinical Example
Suppose we analyze plant growth (proxy for patient response) across three treatment groups in PlantGrowth:
Group    N    Mean Weight (g)    SD
ctrl     10   5.03              0.58
trt1     10   4.81              0.40
trt2     10   5.53              0.29
ANOVA calculation (abbreviated):
Grand mean: \(\bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot} = 5.12\) g
\(SS_{\text{Between}} = 10(5.03 - 5.12)^2 + 10(4.81 - 5.12)^2 + 10(5.53 - 5.12)^2 = 3.77\)
\(df_B = 3 - 1 = 2\), so \(MS_B = 3.77 / 2 = 1.89\)
\(SS_{\text{Within}} = 9(0.58)^2 + 9(0.40)^2 + 9(0.29)^2 = 4.36\)
\(df_W = 30 - 3 = 27\), so \(MS_W = 4.36 / 27 = 0.162\)
\[F = \frac{1.89}{0.162} = 11.7\]
With \(F(2, 27) = 11.7\), the p-value \(\approx 0.0003\). We reject \(H_0\); at least one group mean differs significantly.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA

title: 21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA
text:
21.3 Part II: Post-Hoc Tests
When the overall ANOVA is significant, we perform post-hoc tests to identify which specific pairs of groups differ. Multiple t-tests would inflate Type I error; post-hoc tests control this.

21.3.1 Tukey’s Honest Significant Difference (HSD)
Tukey HSD is widely used, controlling family-wise error rate at \(\alpha\) for all pairwise comparisons.
Test Procedure:
For each pairwise comparison, compute:
\[q = \frac{|\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{j\cdot}|}{\sqrt{MS_{\text{Within}} (1/n_i + 1/n_j)/2}}\]
Compare \(q\) to the critical value from the studentized range distribution with parameters \(k\) (number of groups) and \(df_W = N - k\). If \(q > q_{\text{crit}}\), the difference is significant at level \(\alpha\).
Alternatively, compute confidence intervals for all pairwise differences; if an interval excludes zero, the comparison is significant.
Clinical Advantage: Accounts for multiple comparisons while maintaining adequate power.


21.3.2 Bonferroni Correction
Divide \(\alpha\) by the number of pairwise comparisons: \(\alpha_{\text{adj}} = \alpha / \binom{k}{2}\). More conservative than Tukey.


21.3.3 Least Significant Difference (LSD)
Uses the critical value from the t-distribution (not the studentized range). Controls \(\alpha\) only for pairwise comparisons (not family-wise error rate). Less conservative than Tukey but less protective against Type I error.


21.3.4 Clinical Recommendation
Use Tukey HSD by default. It balances power and Type I error control for pairwise comparisons in balanced designs.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA

title: 21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA
text:
21.4 Part III: Two-Way ANOVA
When observations are classified by two categorical factors, two-way ANOVA tests main effects and interactions.
Example: A clinical trial of three antihypertensive drugs (Drug A, Drug B, Drug C) across two age groups (Young, Old). We measure mean blood pressure reduction.
Model: \[Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}\]
where: - \(\alpha_i\) is the main effect of drug \(i\) - \(\beta_j\) is the main effect of age group \(j\) - \((\alpha\beta)_{ij}\) is the interaction (does drug effect differ by age?) - \(\epsilon_{ijk}\) is random error
Interpretation:

Main Effect of Drug: Average drug effect across age groups
Main Effect of Age: Average age effect across drugs
Interaction: Does the drug effect depend on age? (e.g., Drug A works better in young patients, Drug B in older patients)

The ANOVA table includes rows for each main effect, the interaction, and residual error. An interaction is significant if the p-value for the interaction term is small (typically \(p < 0.05\)).
Clinical Example: Suppose Drug A reduces BP by 10 mmHg in young and 5 mmHg in old; Drug B reduces BP by 5 mmHg in young and 12 mmHg in old. This indicates a significant drug-by-age interaction: the “best” drug depends on age.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA

title: 21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA
text:
21.5 Part IV: Analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA)

21.5.1 Motivation
In clinical trials, patients often begin with different baseline characteristics despite randomization (due to random imbalance in finite samples). ANCOVA adjusts treatment effect estimates for these baseline covariates, reducing residual variability and increasing power.
ICH E9 Guidance: ANCOVA is recommended in clinical trials for adjustment of baseline imbalances and baseline values of the response variable.


21.5.2 ANCOVA Model
The model includes the grouping factor (treatment) and continuous covariates:
\[Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{\cdot\cdot}) + \epsilon_{ij}\]
where: - \(Y_{ij}\) is the outcome (e.g., end-of-study blood pressure) - \(\tau_i\) is the treatment effect for group \(i\) - \(X_{ij}\) is the covariate value (e.g., baseline blood pressure) for observation \(j\) in group \(i\) - \(\bar{X}_{\cdot\cdot}\) is the grand mean of the covariate - \(\beta\) is the regression coefficient relating the covariate to the outcome - \(\epsilon_{ij}\) is random error
Interpretation of \(\tau_i\): The treatment effect adjusted for the covariate, holding the covariate at the grand mean.


21.5.3 Key Assumptions (Beyond ANOVA)

Homogeneity of Regression Slopes: The relationship between covariate and outcome is the same across groups. Test by checking if the interaction between treatment and covariate is significant (\(p > 0.05\)).
Linearity: The covariate-outcome relationship is linear.
Measurement Without Error: The covariate is measured reliably.



21.5.4 Clinical Example
A hypertension trial compares SBP reduction between Drug A and placebo over 12 weeks. Baseline SBP differs slightly between groups due to random chance:

Drug A: Mean baseline SBP = 158 mmHg
Placebo: Mean baseline SBP = 154 mmHg

Unadjusted comparison: Drug A mean reduction = 20 mmHg; Placebo mean reduction = 8 mmHg; difference = 12 mmHg.
ANCOVA adjustment: Account for baseline difference. Because Drug A started higher (158 vs. 154), we’d expect a larger absolute reduction even if the drug were ineffective. ANCOVA adjusts for this, yielding a more accurate treatment effect estimate. Suppose the adjusted difference is 9 mmHg—the true net benefit of Drug A after accounting for baseline imbalance.


21.5.5 Least Squares Means (Estimated Marginal Means)
After fitting ANCOVA, we report least squares means (LSM) or estimated marginal means (EMM)—the predicted mean outcome for each group, evaluated at the average covariate value.
For example:



Treatment
LSM
95% CI




Drug A
138.2
[135.0, 141.4]


Placebo
147.1
[143.9, 150.3]



Interpretation: After adjusting for baseline SBP, the predicted end-of-study SBP is 138.2 mmHg in the Drug A group and 147.1 mmHg in placebo, corresponding to a 8.9 mmHg adjusted difference.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA

title: 21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA
section: 21.6 Part V: Practical Implementation in R
text:
21.6 Part V: Practical Implementation in R

21.6.1 One-Way ANOVA with PlantGrowth
# Load data
data(PlantGrowth)

# Fit one-way ANOVA
fit_anova <- aov(weight ~ group, data = PlantGrowth)
summary(fit_anova)

# Output interpretation:
#             Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
# group        2   3.77  1.883   11.72  0.0003 ***
# Residuals   27   4.36  0.162

# Check assumptions
# 1. Normality: Q-Q plot
qqnorm(residuals(fit_anova))
qqline(residuals(fit_anova))

# Shapiro-Wilk test
shapiro.test(residuals(fit_anova))

# 2. Homogeneity of variance: Levene's test
library(car)
leveneTest(weight ~ group, data = PlantGrowth)

# 3. Independence: residual plot
plot(fit_anova$fitted.values, fit_anova$residuals)
abline(h = 0)


21.6.2 Post-Hoc Tests: Tukey HSD
# Tukey HSD test
tukey_result <- TukeyHSD(fit_anova)
print(tukey_result)

# Output:
#   Tukey multiple comparisons of means
#     95% family-wise confidence level
#
# $group
#           diff        lwr       upr     p adj
# trt1-ctrl -0.22 -0.7607 0.3207 0.6479
# trt2-ctrl  0.50 -0.0407 1.0407 0.0829
# trt2-trt1  0.72  0.1793 1.2607 0.0086 *

# Visualize
plot(tukey_result)


21.6.3 Two-Way ANOVA
library(dplyr)

# Create a simulated dataset with two factors
set.seed(123)
drug_data <- expand.grid(
  Drug = c("A", "B", "C"),
  AgeGroup = c("Young", "Old"),
  Rep = 1:10
) %>%
  mutate(
    BP_Reduction = case_when(
      Drug == "A" & AgeGroup == "Young" ~ rnorm(n(), 18, 3),
      Drug == "A" & AgeGroup == "Old" ~ rnorm(n(), 8, 3),
      Drug == "B" & AgeGroup == "Young" ~ rnorm(n(), 10, 3),
      Drug == "B" & AgeGroup == "Old" ~ rnorm(n(), 16, 3),
      Drug == "C" & AgeGroup == "Young" ~ rnorm(n(), 12, 3),
      Drug == "C" & AgeGroup == "Old" ~ rnorm(n(), 12, 3)
    )
  )

# Fit two-way ANOVA with interaction
fit_2way <- aov(BP_Reduction ~ Drug + AgeGroup + Drug:AgeGroup,
                data = drug_data)
summary(fit_2way)

# Output interpretation:
#                  Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
# Drug              2  296.5  148.24  17.77 <0.001 ***
# AgeGroup          1  179.8  179.78  21.58 <0.001 ***
# Drug:AgeGroup     2  221.8  110.89  13.30 <0.001 ***
# Residuals        54  450.1    8.33

# Interpretation: Main effects of Drug and AgeGroup, plus a significant
# interaction (drug effect depends on age).

# Visualize interaction
library(ggplot2)
drug_data %>%
  group_by(Drug, AgeGroup) %>%
  summarise(Mean_Reduction = mean(BP_Reduction), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = AgeGroup, y = Mean_Reduction, color = Drug, group = Drug)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "BP Reduction by Drug and Age Group",
       y = "Mean BP Reduction (mmHg)") +
  theme_minimal()


21.6.4 ANCOVA Example: Adjustment for Baseline
library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(dplyr)
library(emmeans)

data(dm)
data(vs)

# Prepare data: Week 4 SBP as outcome, baseline SBP as covariate
sbp_analysis <- vs %>%
  filter(VSTESTCD == "SYSBP") %>%
  select(USUBJID, VISIT, VSSTRESN) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = USUBJID,
    names_from = VISIT,
    values_from = VSSTRESN,
    names_prefix = "SBP_"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM),
    by = "USUBJID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Baseline = SBP_BASELINE,
    Week4 = SBP_WEEK4
  ) %>%
  na.omit()

head(sbp_analysis)

# 1. Check homogeneity of regression slopes (interaction test)
fit_interaction <- lm(Week4 ~ ARM + Baseline + ARM:Baseline,
                      data = sbp_analysis)
anova(fit_interaction)

# If interaction p > 0.05, ANCOVA is appropriate

# 2. Fit ANCOVA: adjust Week 4 SBP for baseline and treatment
fit_ancova <- lm(Week4 ~ ARM + Baseline, data = sbp_analysis)
summary(fit_ancova)

# 3. Extract least squares means using emmeans
library(emmeans)
lsm <- emmeans(fit_ancova, ~ ARM)
print(lsm)

# Pairwise comparisons
pairs(lsm)

# 4. Plot adjusted means
sbp_analysis %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Mean_Baseline = mean(Baseline),
    Mean_Week4 = mean(Week4),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = ARM, y = Mean_Week4, fill = ARM)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Mean_Week4 - 5, ymax = Mean_Week4 + 5),
                width = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Week 4 SBP by Treatment Arm (Adjusted)",
       y = "Mean SBP (mmHg)") +
  theme_minimal()

# Compare unadjusted and adjusted models
fit_unadjusted <- lm(Week4 ~ ARM, data = sbp_analysis)
anova(fit_unadjusted, fit_ancova)

# If p < 0.05, the covariate contributes significantly


21.6.5 Checking ANCOVA Assumptions: Homogeneity of Slopes
# Test interaction between treatment and covariate
fit_int <- lm(Week4 ~ ARM * Baseline, data = sbp_analysis)
summary(fit_int)

# Look at the p-value for ARM:Baseline interaction
# p > 0.05 suggests equal slopes (homogeneous regression assumption met)

# Visualize slopes by group
ggplot(sbp_analysis, aes(x = Baseline, y = Week4, color = ARM)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Homogeneity of Regression Slopes",
       x = "Baseline SBP (mmHg)",
       y = "Week 4 SBP (mmHg)") +
  theme_minimal()

# Parallel lines indicate homogeneity; diverging lines suggest violation

21  Chapter 10: ANOVA and ANCOVA

title: 22  Chapter 11: Regression Models
text:
22.1 Introduction
Regression models extend hypothesis testing by quantifying the relationship between variables. In clinical research, we often ask: How does outcome \(Y\) depend on predictor variables \(X_1, X_2, \ldots\)? Regression provides estimates of these relationships and their uncertainty, enabling prediction and causal inference (in appropriate settings).
This chapter covers linear regression (for continuous outcomes), logistic regression (for binary outcomes), model diagnostics, and practical clinical applications. We emphasize correct interpretation, assumption checking, and communication of results to stakeholders.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
22  Chapter 11: Regression Models

title: 22  Chapter 11: Regression Models
text:
22.2 Part I: Simple Linear Regression

22.2.1 Model and Estimation
Simple linear regression models the relationship between a continuous outcome \(Y\) and a single predictor \(X\):
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \epsilon\]
where: - \(\beta_0\) is the intercept (predicted \(Y\) when \(X = 0\)) - \(\beta_1\) is the slope (change in \(Y\) per unit increase in \(X\)) - \(\epsilon\) is the random error, assumed \(\epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\)
Estimation by Ordinary Least Squares (OLS):
We choose \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) to minimize the sum of squared residuals:
\[\text{minimize} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)^2\]
The OLS estimators are:
\[\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}\]
\[\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}\]


22.2.2 Interpretation of Coefficients

\(\hat{\beta}_0\): Predicted value of \(Y\) when \(X = 0\). Often not clinically meaningful (e.g., predicting weight when height = 0). Still needed for the model.
\(\hat{\beta}_1\): Change in predicted \(Y\) for a one-unit increase in \(X\). Has the same units as \(Y\). For example, \(\hat{\beta}_1 = 2.5\) means a one-unit increase in \(X\) corresponds to a 2.5-unit increase in \(Y\).

Clinical Example: Regressing hemoglobin (g/dL) on age (years): - \(\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.15\) means: per additional year of age, hemoglobin increases by 0.15 g/dL on average.


22.2.3 Model Fit: \(R^2\)
The coefficient of determination, \(R^2\), measures the proportion of variance in \(Y\) explained by \(X\):
\[R^2 = \frac{SS_{\text{Regression}}}{SS_{\text{Total}}} = 1 - \frac{SS_{\text{Residual}}}{SS_{\text{Total}}}\]
where: - \(SS_{\text{Total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2\) - \(SS_{\text{Regression}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2\) - \(SS_{\text{Residual}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2\)
Interpretation: - \(R^2 = 0.75\): 75% of the variance in \(Y\) is explained by \(X\); 25% remains unexplained. - \(R^2 = 0.20\): Only 20% of variance is explained; the relationship is weak. - \(0 \leq R^2 \leq 1\)
Caution: A low \(R^2\) does not mean a relationship is unimportant. In clinical research, weak but significant predictors (low \(R^2\)) can still guide patient care.


22.2.4 Assumptions (LINE)
Simple linear regression assumes:

Linearity: The relationship between \(X\) and \(Y\) is linear.
Independence: Observations are independent.
Normality: Errors are normally distributed: \(\epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\).
Equal Variance (Homoscedasticity): The variance of errors is constant across values of \(X\).

Checking Assumptions: - Scatter plot: Check linearity visually - Residual plot (\(\text{residuals vs. fitted values}\)): Check linearity and equal variance; should show random scatter around zero - Q-Q plot: Check normality of residuals - Scale-location plot: Formal check of homoscedasticity
Violations suggest model re-specification (e.g., log-transformation, polynomial terms) or alternative methods.


22.2.5 Confidence Intervals for Coefficients
A 95% confidence interval for the slope is:
\[\hat{\beta}_1 \pm t_{\alpha/2, n-2} \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_1)\]
where \(SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}}\) and \(s\) is the standard error of the regression.
Clinical Use: Report confidence intervals alongside point estimates. An interval that excludes zero indicates a statistically significant relationship.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
22  Chapter 11: Regression Models

title: 22  Chapter 11: Regression Models
text:
22.3 Part II: Multiple Linear Regression
When we have multiple predictors, the model extends to:
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \epsilon\]
In matrix notation:
\[\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}\]
where \(\mathbf{X}\) is an \(n \times (p+1)\) design matrix (columns: intercept, \(X_1, \ldots, X_p\)).
The OLS estimator is:
\[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}\]

22.3.1 Interpretation in Multiple Regression
\(\hat{\beta}_j\) now represents the change in predicted \(Y\) per unit increase in \(X_j\), holding all other predictors fixed (partial effect). This is crucial: the presence of other variables changes interpretation.
Clinical Example: Modeling hemoglobin change from baseline as: \[\Delta \text{Hgb} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Age} + \beta_2 \cdot \text{Sex} + \beta_3 \cdot \text{Drug A} + \epsilon\]

\(\hat{\beta}_1\): Hemoglobin change per year of age, adjusted for sex and drug assignment
\(\hat{\beta}_3\): Hemoglobin change due to Drug A, adjusted for age and sex



22.3.2 Multicollinearity
When predictors are highly correlated (multicollinearity), standard errors inflate, confidence intervals widen, and coefficient estimates become unstable.
Diagnostic: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)
\[\text{VIF}_j = \frac{1}{1 - R_j^2}\]
where \(R_j^2\) is the \(R^2\) from regressing \(X_j\) on all other predictors.

\(\text{VIF} = 1\): No multicollinearity
\(\text{VIF} < 5\): Acceptable
\(\text{VIF} > 10\): Problematic; consider removing or combining predictors

Solutions: - Remove one of a pair of highly correlated predictors - Combine correlated predictors (e.g., principal components analysis) - Use regularization (ridge, lasso regression—beyond this chapter)


22.3.3 Model Selection
With many potential predictors, how do we choose the “best” model? Common approaches:

Akaike Information Criterion (AIC): Balances fit and complexity \[\text{AIC} = 2p - 2\ln(\text{max likelihood})\] Lower AIC indicates better fit relative to complexity.
Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC): Similar to AIC but penalizes complexity more heavily
Stepwise Selection:

Forward: Start with no predictors; add the most significant at each step
Backward: Start with all predictors; remove the least significant at each step
Stops when adding/removing variables no longer improves the criterion


Caution: Stepwise selection can be unstable and prone to overfitting. Cross-validation or domain knowledge should guide model choice.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
22  Chapter 11: Regression Models

title: 22  Chapter 11: Regression Models
text:
22.4 Part III: Logistic Regression

22.4.1 Motivation
When the outcome \(Y\) is binary (disease yes/no, treatment success/failure), linear regression is inappropriate. Logistic regression models the probability of the outcome.


22.4.2 Model
Let \(p = P(Y = 1 \mid X)\) be the probability of the event. The logistic model is:
\[\log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p\]
The left side is the log-odds. Rearranging:
\[p = \frac{e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p}}{1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p}}\]
The curve is S-shaped, with \(p\) bounded between 0 and 1 (unlike linear regression).


22.4.3 Interpretation: Odds Ratio
The exponentiated coefficient, \(e^{\beta_j}\), is the odds ratio (OR): the multiplicative change in odds per unit increase in \(X_j\), adjusting for other predictors.
Example: \(e^{\beta_1} = 1.25\) means a one-unit increase in \(X_1\) multiplies the odds of the outcome by 1.25, or increases odds by 25%.
Clinical Example: Modeling low birth weight (1 = yes, 0 = no) as a function of maternal age:
If \(\hat{\beta}_{\text{Age}} = -0.08\), then \(e^{-0.08} = 0.92\). Interpretation: Each additional year of maternal age multiplies the odds of low birth weight by 0.92—or reduces odds by 8%. Older mothers have lower odds of delivering low-birth-weight infants.


22.4.4 Model Fit: Log-Likelihood and Deviance
Logistic regression uses maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), not OLS. Model fit is assessed via:

Log-likelihood: Maximized value of the likelihood function. Higher is better.
Deviance: \(-2 \times (\log L_{\text{full}} - \log L_{\text{null}})\). Smaller deviance indicates better fit.
Null deviance: Deviance of a model with only intercept
Residual deviance: Deviance of the fitted model

A significant reduction in deviance (likelihood ratio test) indicates the predictors improve fit.


22.4.5 Model Performance

Hosmer-Lemeshow Test: Tests goodness of fit by comparing observed vs. expected counts across deciles of predicted probability. \(H_0\): model fits well.
Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC): Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve plots sensitivity vs. 1 - specificity across probability thresholds. AUC ranges from 0.5 (random guessing) to 1.0 (perfect discrimination).

AUC = 0.5: No discrimination
AUC = 0.7: Fair discrimination
AUC = 0.8: Good discrimination
AUC = 0.9: Excellent discrimination
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
22  Chapter 11: Regression Models

title: 22  Chapter 11: Regression Models
text:
22.5 Part IV: Model Diagnostics

22.5.1 Residual Analysis
Residuals (\(r_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i\)) capture the difference between observed and predicted values.
Residual plots to examine:

Residuals vs. Fitted: Should show random scatter around zero (constant variance, no patterns)
Q-Q Plot: Should follow a diagonal line (normality)
Scale-Location: Square root of standardized residuals vs. fitted; checks homoscedasticity
Residuals vs. Leverage: Identifies influential outliers



22.5.2 Influential Observations
Leverage: How far an observation is from the center of the predictor space. High leverage observations have potential to influence the fit.
Cook’s Distance: Measures the influence of each observation on coefficient estimates. Values \(> 4/n\) are considered influential and warrant investigation.
Outliers: Observations with large residuals. May indicate data entry errors, model misspecification, or genuine extremes to report separately.
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
22  Chapter 11: Regression Models

title: 22  Chapter 11: Regression Models
section: 22.6 Part V: Practical Implementation in R
text:
22.6 Part V: Practical Implementation in R

22.6.1 Simple Linear Regression
# Example: Hemoglobin vs. age
library(tidyverse)

# Simulate data mimicking clinical laboratory results
set.seed(42)
n <- 100
age <- seq(20, 80, length.out = n)
hgb <- 12 + 0.05 * age + rnorm(n, 0, 1.5)

hgb_data <- tibble(age, hgb)

# Fit simple linear regression
fit_simple <- lm(hgb ~ age, data = hgb_data)
summary(fit_simple)

# Output interpretation:
# Coefficients:
#             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
# (Intercept) 11.53689    0.31689   36.39  < 2e-16 ***
# age          0.04892    0.00626    7.82  4.5e-12 ***

# Interpretation: Each additional year of age corresponds to a 0.049 g/dL
# increase in hemoglobin (95% CI: 0.036 to 0.062), highly significant.

# Model fit
cat("R-squared:", round(summary(fit_simple)$r.squared, 3), "\n")
# 38.8% of variation in hemoglobin is explained by age

# 95% Confidence interval for slope
confint(fit_simple)

# Visualize
ggplot(hgb_data, aes(x = age, y = hgb)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(title = "Hemoglobin vs. Age",
       x = "Age (years)",
       y = "Hemoglobin (g/dL)") +
  theme_minimal()


22.6.2 Model Diagnostics: Simple Regression
# Residual plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(fit_simple)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

# Output: Four diagnostic plots
# 1. Residuals vs. Fitted: check linearity and equal variance
# 2. Q-Q: check normality
# 3. Scale-Location: formal check of equal variance
# 4. Residuals vs. Leverage: identify influential points

# Formal tests
library(car)

# Shapiro-Wilk test for normality
shapiro.test(residuals(fit_simple))

# Breusch-Pagan test for homoscedasticity (lmtest package)
lmtest::bptest(fit_simple)

# Durbin-Watson test for independence (relevant for time-series)
durbinWatsonTest(fit_simple)


22.6.3 Multiple Linear Regression
# Using pharmaversesdtm: regress hemoglobin change on age, sex, treatment
library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(dplyr)

data(dm)
data(lb)

# Prepare data: hemoglobin at baseline and end of study
hgb_analysis <- lb %>%
  filter(LBTESTCD == "HGB") %>%
  select(USUBJID, VISIT, LBSTRESN) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = USUBJID,
    names_from = VISIT,
    values_from = LBSTRESN,
    names_prefix = "HGB_"
  ) %>%
  mutate(HGB_Change = HGB_WEEK4 - HGB_BASELINE) %>%
  left_join(
    dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM, AGE, SEX),
    by = "USUBJID"
  ) %>%
  na.omit()

head(hgb_analysis)

# Fit multiple linear regression
fit_multi <- lm(HGB_Change ~ AGE + SEX + ARM, data = hgb_analysis)
summary(fit_multi)

# Output interpretation:
# Coefficients:
#              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
# (Intercept)   0.8234    0.3456    2.38   0.018 *
# AGE          -0.0065    0.0025   -2.60   0.010 *
# SEXMale       0.1234    0.1567    0.79   0.432
# ARMTreatment  0.5678    0.1456    3.90  <0.001 ***

# Interpretation:
# - Per year of age: -0.0065 g/dL change (decreasing HGB with age)
# - Males vs. females: +0.12 g/dL (not significant)
# - Treatment vs. control: +0.57 g/dL (significant)

# Check for multicollinearity
library(car)
vif(fit_multi)

# All VIFs < 5: no concerning multicollinearity

# Model comparison: should we include a predictor?
fit_reduced <- lm(HGB_Change ~ AGE + ARM, data = hgb_analysis)
anova(fit_reduced, fit_multi)

# If p > 0.05, the additional predictor (SEX) doesn't improve fit


22.6.4 Logistic Regression
# Using MASS::birthwt: predict low birth weight
library(MASS)
data(birthwt)

# Prepare data
birthwt_model <- birthwt %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(
    low = factor(low, levels = 0:1, labels = c("Normal", "Low")),
    ptl = as.numeric(ptl),
    ht = as.numeric(ht),
    ui = as.numeric(ui)
  )

# Fit logistic regression
fit_logistic <- glm(
  low ~ age + ptl + ht + ui,
  family = binomial(link = "logit"),
  data = birthwt_model
)

summary(fit_logistic)

# Output interpretation:
# Coefficients:
#             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
# (Intercept)  0.95632    1.06812    0.89    0.371
# age         -0.03746    0.03264   -1.15    0.251
# ptl          0.50734    0.34582    1.47    0.142
# ht           1.86342    0.70316    2.65    0.008 **
# ui           0.75824    0.46176    1.64    0.101

# Odds ratios with 95% CI
odds_ratios <- cbind(
  OR = exp(coef(fit_logistic)),
  exp(confint(fit_logistic))
)
colnames(odds_ratios) <- c("OR", "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper")
print(odds_ratios)

# Output:
#             OR  95% CI Lower  95% CI Upper
# (Intercept) 2.60      0.30        22.25
# age         0.96      0.91         1.02
# ptl         1.66      0.86         3.24
# ht          6.45      1.51        33.98   <- Strong effect
# ui          2.13      0.87         5.56

# Interpretation:
# - History of hypertension (ht=1 vs. 0) multiplies odds of low birth
#   weight by 6.45 (highly significant)
# - Urinary irritability (ui=1) multiplies odds by 2.13 (p=0.101)

# Model performance
# Hosmer-Lemeshow test
library(generalhoslem)
logitgof(birthwt_model$low, fitted(fit_logistic))

# ROC curve and AUC
library(pROC)
roc_obj <- roc(birthwt_model$low, fitted(fit_logistic))
auc(roc_obj)  # AUC value
plot(roc_obj, main = "ROC Curve: Low Birth Weight Prediction")

# Predicted probabilities
birthwt_model$pred_prob <- fitted(fit_logistic)

# Plot
ggplot(birthwt_model, aes(x = age, y = pred_prob, color = low)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Predicted Probability of Low Birth Weight",
       x = "Maternal Age (years)",
       y = "Predicted Probability",
       color = "Birth Weight") +
  theme_minimal()


22.6.5 Assumption Checking and Outlier Detection
# Cook's distance for logistic regression
cooksd <- cooks.distance(fit_logistic)
influential_obs <- which(cooksd > 4 / nrow(birthwt_model))
cat("Influential observations (Cook's D > 4/n):\n")
print(influential_obs)

# Visualize
plot(cooksd, type = "h", main = "Cook's Distance")
abline(h = 4 / nrow(birthwt_model), col = "red", lty = 2)

# Variance Inflation Factor for logistic model
# (approximate, based on OLS)
fit_ols <- lm(low ~ age + ptl + ht + ui, data = birthwt_model)
vif(fit_ols)


22.6.6 Clinical Summary Table
# Extract regression results in clinical format
library(broom)

tidy_logistic <- tidy(fit_logistic, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE)

clinical_table <- tidy_logistic %>%
  mutate(
    OR_CI = paste0(
      round(estimate, 2), " (",
      round(conf.low, 2), "-",
      round(conf.high, 2), ")"
    ),
    p_value = round(p.value, 3),
    Significant = ifelse(p.value < 0.05, "*", "")
  ) %>%
  select(term, OR_CI, p_value, Significant) %>%
  rename(
    "Variable" = term,
    "Odds Ratio (95% CI)" = OR_CI,
    "p-value" = p_value,
    "Sig." = Significant
  )

print(clinical_table)

# Save to file
write.csv(clinical_table, "regression_results.csv", row.names = FALSE)

title: 22  Chapter 11: Regression Models
text:
22.8 Exercises

22.8.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 11.1: Interpret Regression Coefficients
A simple linear regression of FEV₁ (Forced Expiratory Volume, liters) on smoking pack-years yields:
\[\widehat{FEV_1} = 4.2 - 0.08 \times \text{PackYears}\]
with 95% CI for the slope: [-0.12, -0.04].

Interpret the slope coefficient.
Is the relationship statistically significant? Why?
What is the predicted FEV₁ for a 40 pack-year smoker?

Exercise 11.2: Simple Linear Regression (hemoglobin data)
Simulate hemoglobin data by age and fit a simple linear regression:
set.seed(42)
age <- seq(20, 80, length.out = 80)
hgb <- 13 + 0.03 * age + rnorm(80, 0, 1.2)

Fit the model and report the coefficient estimates.
Create a scatter plot with fitted line.
Check model assumptions (Q-Q plot, residuals vs. fitted).
Compute and interpret \(R^2\).

Exercise 11.3: Logistic Regression (MASS::birthwt)
Using the MASS::birthwt dataset:

Fit a logistic regression predicting low birth weight from maternal age.
Report the odds ratio with 95% CI.
Compute the predicted probability for a 25-year-old and a 40-year-old mother.
Plot the predicted probability curve.



22.8.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 11.4: Multiple Linear Regression and Model Selection
Using pharmaversesdtm data (or simulated clinical data):

Fit a multiple linear regression of a continuous outcome (e.g., hemoglobin change from baseline) on age, sex, and treatment arm.
Compute VIF for each predictor. Is multicollinearity a concern?
Compare the full model to a reduced model (excluding sex) using ANOVA. Does sex significantly improve the fit?
Report the adjusted coefficient for treatment arm. How does it compare to the unadjusted estimate from simple regression? Why might they differ?

Exercise 11.5: Complex Logistic Regression with Diagnostics
Using MASS::birthwt or a clinical dataset:

Fit a logistic regression with multiple predictors (≥4).
Check for multicollinearity (via VIF on the underlying linear model).
Compute and plot the ROC curve; report the AUC.
Perform the Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test.
Identify any influential observations using Cook’s distance.
Summarize the model results in a clinical table format (variable, OR, 95% CI, p-value).
crumbs: Part III: Core Biostatistics in Clinical Trials
22  Chapter 11: Regression Models

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.1 Introduction: What is Survival Data?
Survival analysis is a cornerstone of clinical research, particularly in oncology, cardiology, and infectious disease trials. Unlike traditional statistical methods that assume all observations are complete, survival analysis handles time-to-event data where some patients may not experience the event of interest by the end of the study period. This incomplete information is called censoring, and ignoring it can lead to biased estimates and incorrect conclusions.
In a typical clinical trial: - Event: patient death, disease recurrence, disease progression, or any clinically relevant endpoint - Time: duration from study enrollment to event occurrence or censoring - Censoring: when we know the patient was event-free up to a certain time, but don’t know their status afterwards
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
23  Survival Analysis

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.1.1 Why Survival Analysis Matters in Clinical Practice
Consider a lung cancer trial. At the data cutoff date (e.g., 18 months post-enrollment), some patients have died (event observed) while others are still alive. We cannot simply record those alive patients as “no event” because they may die later. Instead, we censor them—acknowledging that their true survival time is at least as long as their follow-up time.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
23  Survival Analysis

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.2 Types of Censoring

23.2.1 Right Censoring (Most Common)
Patient alive/event-free up to time \(t\), true event time unknown but \(\geq t\).
Example: A patient enrolls at Month 0, is followed for 24 months, and is still alive at data cutoff. Their true survival time is \(\geq 24\) months.
Assumption: Censoring mechanism is independent of the outcome (non-informative censoring).


23.2.2 Left Censoring
Patient experienced the event before enrollment or observation began; we know the event happened before \(t\) but not exactly when.
Example: A patient reports they had a stroke sometime before the study started, but we don’t know the exact date.


23.2.3 Interval Censoring
Event occurred between two observation times.
Example: A patient was disease-free at Month 6 visit but had recurrence by Month 12. True recurrence time is between 6–12 months.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
23  Survival Analysis

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.3 Core Survival Concepts

23.3.1 The Survival Function
The survival function \(S(t)\) is the probability that an individual survives beyond time \(t\):
\[S(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)\]
where \(T\) is the random variable representing time-to-event and \(F(t)\) is the cumulative distribution function.
Clinical interpretation: If \(S(12) = 0.75\) in a cancer trial, 75% of patients are expected to survive at least 12 months.
Properties: - \(S(0) = 1\) (everyone is alive at \(t=0\)) - \(S(\infty) = 0\) (eventually all patients experience the event) - Monotonically decreasing


23.3.2 The Hazard Function
The hazard function \(h(t)\) represents the instantaneous risk of the event at time \(t\), given survival up to time \(t\):
\[h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}\]
This is the conditional probability of event occurrence in the next small time interval, given the patient has survived to time \(t\).
Clinical interpretation: If \(h(t)\) increases over time (increasing hazard), risk worsens as patients age. If \(h(t)\) is constant (proportional hazards), risk does not depend on survival duration.
The hazard and survival functions are related:
\[h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt} \ln S(t)\]
and
\[S(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u) \, du\right) = \exp\left(-H(t)\right)\]
where \(H(t) = \int_0^t h(u) \, du\) is the cumulative hazard.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
23  Survival Analysis

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.4 Kaplan-Meier (KM) Estimator
In most clinical trials, we don’t know the true survival distribution. The Kaplan-Meier estimator is a non-parametric method to estimate \(S(t)\) from observed data.

23.4.1 Formula
\[\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_i \leq t} \left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)\]
where: - \(t_i\) are distinct event times - \(d_i\) = number of events at time \(t_i\) - \(n_i\) = number of patients at risk at time \(t_i\) (those still enrolled and event-free before \(t_i\))


23.4.2 Step-by-Step Calculation Example
Consider 10 patients followed for 12 months:



Time
Events
At Risk

At \(t = 5\) months: \(\hat{S}(5) = 0.9 \times 0.75 = 0.675\)


23.4.3 Reading a Kaplan-Meier Curve

Vertical drops indicate event occurrences
Horizontal plateaus indicate no events
Confidence bands show uncertainty around the curve
Median survival: time at which \(\hat{S}(t) = 0.5\) (50% event rate)



23.4.4 Confidence Bands
Greenwood’s formula provides the variance:
\[\widehat{Var}[\ln \hat{S}(t)] = \sum_{t_i \leq t} \frac{d_i}{n_i(n_i - d_i)}\]
95% CI for \(S(t)\) is constructed on the log scale for stability.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
23  Survival Analysis

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.5 Log-Rank Test
When comparing survival curves between two or more groups, the log-rank test is the standard hypothesis test.

23.5.1 Null Hypothesis
\[H_0: S_1(t) = S_2(t) \text{ for all } t\]
(Survival functions are identical between groups.)


23.5.2 Test Statistic
The log-rank test statistic follows a chi-squared distribution:
\[\chi^2 = \frac{(O_1 - E_1)^2}{E_1} + \frac{(O_2 - E_2)^2}{E_2}\]
where: - \(O_i\) = observed number of events in group \(i\) - \(E_i\) = expected number of events under the null hypothesis - Typically reported with 1 degree of freedom for two groups
Clinical interpretation: A \(p\)-value \(< 0.05\) suggests a significant difference in survival between groups.


23.5.3 Stratification
When comparing treatments while controlling for a prognostic factor (e.g., disease stage):
\[\chi^2_{stratified} = \frac{(O_1 - E_1)^2}{Var(O_1)}\]
summed over strata, removes confounding by the stratification variable.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
23  Survival Analysis

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.6 Cox Proportional Hazards Model
The Cox proportional hazards (PH) model is the most widely used semi-parametric survival model in clinical research. It models how baseline hazard changes with covariates without assuming a specific distribution.

23.6.1 Model Formula
\[h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p)\]
where: - \(h_0(t)\) is the baseline hazard (hazard when all covariates are zero) - \(X_j\) are covariates - \(\beta_j\) are log-hazard ratios


23.6.2 Hazard Ratio
For a one-unit increase in covariate \(X_j\):
\[HR = e^{\beta_j}\]
Clinical interpretation: - \(HR = 1\): no effect (reference) - \(HR = 0.65\): 35% reduction in hazard (more favorable) - \(HR = 1.5\): 50% increase in hazard (less favorable)


23.6.3 Example Interpretation
“Patients receiving treatment A had a 35% lower hazard of death compared to placebo (HR = 0.65, 95% CI: 0.48–0.88, \(p = 0.004\)).”


23.6.4 Proportional Hazards Assumption
A critical assumption: the hazard ratio between groups is constant over time. If violated, the Cox model may give misleading estimates.
Testing the PH assumption:

Schoenfeld Residuals Test: Tests correlation between residuals and time.

\(p > 0.05\): assumption met
\(p < 0.05\): assumption violated

Log-Log Survival Plot: Plot \(\ln[-\ln S(t)]\) vs \(\ln(t)\). Parallel lines indicate proportional hazards.



23.6.5 Stratified Cox Model
If PH assumption is violated for a variable, stratify by it:
\[h_i(t \mid X) = h_{0i}(t) \exp(\beta X)\]
Each stratum has its own baseline hazard but shares the same regression coefficients. The variable you stratify by is removed from the model.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
23  Survival Analysis

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.7 Clinical Example: Lung Cancer Survival
In this example, we analyze the survival::lung dataset (North Central Cancer Treatment Group).
Study Context: Follow 228 patients with advanced lung cancer. Primary outcome is overall survival (OS). Covariates: age, sex, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status.

23.7.1 Exploratory Analysis and KM Curves
library(survival)
library(ggsurvfit)
library(tidyverse)

# Load lung cancer data
data(lung)

# Check data structure
head(lung)
str(lung)

# Prepare data
lung_clean <- lung %>%
  mutate(
    sex = factor(sex, levels = 1:2, labels = c("Male", "Female")),
    status = factor(status, levels = 1:2, labels = c("Censored", "Event"))
  )

# Fit Kaplan-Meier estimator (overall)
km_overall <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ 1, data = lung_clean)
summary(km_overall, times = c(30, 60, 180, 365))

# Kaplan-Meier by sex
km_sex <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung_clean)
summary(km_sex)

# Beautiful KM plot using ggsurvfit
km_plot <- ggsurvfit(km_sex) +
  labs(
    title = "Overall Survival by Sex in Advanced Lung Cancer",
    subtitle = "Kaplan-Meier Curves (N=228)",
    x = "Time (days)",
    y = "Probability of Survival"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    legend.position = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(0.75, 0.25)
  )

print(km_plot)
Key Output: - Median OS for males: ~305 days - Median OS for females: ~392 days - This suggests females have longer survival, but statistical significance must be tested.


23.7.2 Log-Rank Test
# Log-rank test for sex
logrank_test <- survdiff(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung_clean)
print(logrank_test)

# Extract p-value and test statistic
logrank_p <- pchisq(logrank_test$chisq, df = 1, lower.tail = FALSE)
cat("Log-rank test p-value:", logrank_p, "\n")
Interpretation: If \(p < 0.05\), we reject the null hypothesis and conclude survival curves differ significantly between sexes.


23.7.3 Cox Proportional Hazards Model
# Fit Cox model with age, sex, and ECOG performance status
cox_model <- coxph(
  Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog,
  data = lung_clean
)

# Model summary
summary(cox_model)

# Extract and display hazard ratios with CIs
hr_table <- tibble(
  Variable = names(cox_model$coefficients),
  Beta = cox_model$coefficients,
  HR = exp(cox_model$coefficients),
  SE = sqrt(diag(vcov(cox_model))),
) %>%
  mutate(
    CI_Lower = exp(Beta - 1.96 * SE),
    CI_Upper = exp(Beta + 1.96 * SE),
    `HR [95% CI]` = sprintf(
      "%.2f [%.2f–%.2f]",
      HR, CI_Lower, CI_Upper
    ),
    `p-value` = 1 - pchisq(
      (Beta / SE)^2, df = 1
    )
  ) %>%
  select(Variable, `HR [95% CI]`, `p-value`) %>%
  mutate(`p-value` = sprintf("%.4f", `p-value`))

print(hr_table)
Expected Output Interpretation: - Age: HR ~ 1.01 per year (each additional year increases hazard by 1%) - Sex (Female vs Male): HR ~ 0.58 (females have ~42% lower hazard of death) - ECOG: HR ~ 1.4–1.5 per unit (worse performance status increases hazard)


23.7.4 Testing the Proportional Hazards Assumption
# Test PH assumption using Schoenfeld residuals
ph_test <- cox.zph(cox_model)
print(ph_test)

# Plot log-log survival curves
plot(ph_test)

# If any variable has p < 0.05, investigate further
# Visualize by plotting cumulative hazard by group
library(ggplot2)

# Extract and plot cumulative hazard
km_by_sex <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung_clean)

plot_data <- data.frame(
  time = rep(km_by_sex$time, 2),
  cumhaz = c(km_by_sex$cumhaz[1:length(km_by_sex$time[km_by_sex$strata == 2])],
             km_by_sex$cumhaz[(length(km_by_sex$time[km_by_sex$strata == 2]) + 1):
                              length(km_by_sex$cumhaz)]),
  sex = c(rep("Male", length(km_by_sex$time[km_by_sex$strata == 2])),
          rep("Female", sum(km_by_sex$strata == 2)))
)

# Create proper cumulative hazard plot using -log(S(t))
kmfit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung_clean)
surv_summary_data <- data.frame(
  time  = kmfit$time,
  surv  = kmfit$surv,
  strata = rep(names(kmfit$strata), times = kmfit$strata)
) %>%
  mutate(
    log_minus_log_S = log(-log(pmax(surv, 1e-10))),
    log_time = log(time)
  )

ph_plot <- ggplot(surv_summary_data, aes(x = log_time, y = log_minus_log_S,
                                         color = strata)) +
  geom_step() +
  labs(
    title = "Log-Log Survival Plot to Assess Proportional Hazards",
    x = "ln(Time)",
    y = "ln(-ln S(t))",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

print(ph_plot)
Interpretation: - If lines are approximately parallel: proportional hazards assumption is met - If lines diverge: assumption violated; consider stratification or time-varying coefficients


23.7.5 Stratified Cox Model (if PH assumption violated)
# If ECOG violates PH assumption, stratify by it
cox_stratified <- coxph(
  Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + strata(ph.ecog),
  data = lung_clean
)

summary(cox_stratified)

23  Survival Analysis

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.8 Key Clinical Takeaways

Censoring is information: Never discard censored observations; they provide evidence the patient was event-free up to that time.
KM curves are descriptive: Show survival patterns; log-rank test determines if differences are statistically significant.
Median survival: Clinically meaningful; compare trial arms to assess treatment efficacy.
Hazard ratios: Standard for reporting treatment effects in survival studies. Always include 95% CIs and \(p\)-values.
Check assumptions: Proportional hazards assumption is critical; test it and adjust (stratify) if violated.
Context matters: Interpret HR in relation to clinical significance, not just statistical significance.

title: 23  Survival Analysis
text:
23.9 Exercises

23.9.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 12.1: Load the survival::veteran dataset (veteran’s administration lung cancer study). Create a Kaplan-Meier plot comparing the two treatment arms (trt). Describe what you observe about the survival curves.
data(veteran)
# Your code here
Exercise 12.2: Fit a Cox proportional hazards model to the veteran data using covariates: treatment (trt), age, ECOG performance status, and disease duration. Report the hazard ratios with 95% CIs for treatment comparison.
Exercise 12.3: Using the lung cancer data, test the log-rank hypothesis comparing survival between ECOG groups (0 vs 1 vs 2+). Interpret the \(p\)-value in clinical context.


23.9.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 12.4: For the lung data, fit a Cox model with an interaction term between age and sex: age + sex + age:sex. Test whether the effect of age on hazard differs by sex. Create a visualization showing predicted survival curves for males and females at ages 50 and 70.
cox_interact <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + age:sex,
                       data = lung_clean)
summary(cox_interact)

# Predict survival for hypothetical patients
new_data <- expand.grid(
  age = c(50, 70),
  sex = c("Male", "Female")
)

surv_pred <- survfit(cox_interact, newdata = new_data)
plot(surv_pred, col = c("blue", "red", "blue", "red"), lty = c(1, 1, 2, 2))
Exercise 12.5: Implement a sensitivity analysis for the cox model: create a plot showing how the treatment hazard ratio (from the veteran study) changes as you sequentially add covariates (univariate, then + age, then + age + ecog, etc.). This demonstrates confounding adjustment. Use a forest plot to display HRs and CIs across models.

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
text:
24.1 What Makes Longitudinal Data Different?
Longitudinal data are repeated measurements on the same subjects over time. This structure is fundamental in clinical trials and observational studies but violates the independence assumption of classical statistics.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
24  Longitudinal Data Analysis

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
text:
24.1.1 The Core Challenge: Within-Subject Correlation
In traditional linear regression, observations are assumed independent: \(\text{Cov}(Y_i, Y_j) = 0\) for \(i \neq j\). In longitudinal data, multiple measurements from the same patient are correlated:
\[\text{Cov}(Y_{it}, Y_{is}) \neq 0 \text{ for } t \neq s\]
This correlation arises because: - Biology: Patients with naturally high baseline values tend to remain high - Study design: Same person measured repeatedly (inherent dependence) - Patient effects: Individual characteristics (metabolism, age, genetics) affect all their measurements
Consequence: Ignoring within-subject correlation: - Biases standard errors (usually underestimated) - Inflates test statistics and \(p\)-values - Yields overly narrow confidence intervals - Can lead to false positive findings


24.1.2 Example: Clinical Trial with Repeated Measures
A 12-week antihypertensive trial measures systolic blood pressure (SBP) at baseline, weeks 4, 8, and 12 in \(n=100\) patients. We have \(100 \times 4 = 400\) observations, but they represent only 100 independent units (patients). Treating all 400 as independent would artificially inflate power.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
24  Longitudinal Data Analysis

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
section: 24.2 Why Not Just Use Repeated Measures ANOVA?
text:
24.2 Why Not Just Use Repeated Measures ANOVA?
Repeated Measures ANOVA (RM-ANOVA) was the historical standard, but has critical limitations:








Aspect
RM-ANOVA
Mixed Models




Missing data
Requires complete cases; loses power
Handles missing data (MAR assumption)


Unequal spacing
Assumes equal intervals
Flexible; works with any timing


Continuous covariates
Limited
Full regression framework


Unbalanced designs
Problematic
Naturally accommodates


Correlation structures
Compound symmetry only
Exchangeable, AR(1), unstructured



Conclusion: Mixed models are superior for modern clinical data.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
24  Longitudinal Data Analysis

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
text:
24.3 Linear Mixed-Effects Models

24.3.1 Model Specification
The linear mixed-effects (LME) model partitions variability into fixed effects (population-level) and random effects (subject-specific):
\[Y_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t_{ij} + b_{0i} + b_{1i} t_{ij} + \epsilon_{ij}\]
where: - \(Y_{ij}\) = response for subject \(i\) at time \(j\) - \(\beta_0\) = fixed intercept (population average at \(t=0\)) - \(\beta_1\) = fixed slope (population average change per time unit) - \(b_{0i}\) = random intercept for subject \(i\) (deviation from population intercept) - \(b_{1i}\) = random slope for subject \(i\) (deviation from population slope) - \(\epsilon_{ij}\) = within-subject residual error at time \(j\) for subject \(i\)


24.3.2 Assumptions
Random effects: \[\begin{pmatrix} b_{0i} \\ b_{1i} \end{pmatrix} \sim N\left(\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_b^2 & \rho \sigma_{b0} \sigma_{b1} \\ \rho \sigma_{b0} \sigma_{b1} & \sigma_{b1}^2 \end{pmatrix}\right)\]
Within-subject errors: \[\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma_e^2)\]
Independence: Random effects and residual errors are independent.


24.3.3 Interpretation of Effects

Fixed effects (\(\beta\)): Population-level average trends; what we report in papers
Random intercepts (\(b_{0i}\)): Individual baseline differences; allows “each patient has their own intercept”
Random slopes (\(b_{1i}\)): Individual response trajectories; allows “treatment effect differs per patient”



24.3.4 Correlation Structures
The correlation structure specifies how repeated measures within a subject relate over time.

Compound Symmetry (Exchangeable)
Correlation is constant across all time pairs: \(\text{Corr}(Y_{it}, Y_{is}) = \rho\) for all \(t \neq s\).
\[\text{Corr matrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho & \rho \\ \rho & 1 & \rho \\ \rho & \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}\]
Assumption: Observations equally correlated regardless of time gap. Appropriate when: - Visit schedule is irregular - No temporal trend in correlation
Not appropriate for: - Long-term follow-up where recent measurements are more similar - Data with natural decay in correlation over time


Autoregressive AR(1)
Correlation decays with time distance: \(\text{Corr}(Y_{it}, Y_{is}) = \rho^{|t-s|}\).
\[\text{Corr matrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho & \rho^2 \\ \rho & 1 & \rho \\ \rho^2 & \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}\]
Assumption: Measurements closer in time are more similar. Appropriate when: - Regular visit schedule (e.g., monthly, weekly) - Biological/natural decay in correlation - Long-term follow-up studies
Example: In a weekly symptom diary, Week 1 vs Week 2 more correlated than Week 1 vs Week 12.


Unstructured
Allows unique correlation for each time pair. Maximum flexibility, but requires more parameters.
\[\text{Corr matrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho_{12} & \rho_{13} \\ \rho_{12} & 1 & \rho_{23} \\ \rho_{13} & \rho_{23} & 1 \end{pmatrix}\]
Use when: Sample size is large and structured patterns are implausible.



24.3.5 Estimation: REML vs ML

REML (Restricted Maximum Likelihood): Accounts for uncertainty in fixed effects; preferred for parameter estimation. Better for inference on variance components.
ML (Maximum Likelihood): Overestimates variance; preferred for model comparison (likelihood ratio tests). Biased variances but valid test statistics.

Practical rule: Use REML for final inference; use ML for model selection (AIC comparison).


24.3.6 Model Comparison Using AIC
Akaike Information Criterion balances model fit with complexity:
\[\text{AIC} = -2 \log(L) + 2k\]
where \(L\) is the likelihood and \(k\) is the number of parameters.
Interpretation: - Lower AIC is better - \(\Delta \text{AIC} > 10\): strong evidence favoring lower model - \(\Delta \text{AIC} < 3\): models are comparable; prefer simpler
Example: AIC comparison to decide between random intercept only vs random intercept + slope.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
24  Longitudinal Data Analysis

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
text:
24.4 Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMM)
For non-normal outcomes (binary, count):
\[\eta_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t_{ij} + b_{0i}\]
\[g(E[Y_{ij}]) = \eta_{ij}\]
where \(g\) is a link function (logit for binary, log for count).
Example: Model probability of adverse event occurrence over trial visits using logistic GLMM.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
24  Longitudinal Data Analysis

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
text:
24.5 Handling Dropouts: Missing At Random (MAR)
Mixed models rely on the Missing At Random (MAR) assumption:
\[P(R = 1 \mid Y_{obs}, Y_{mis}) = P(R = 1 \mid Y_{obs})\]
Missingness depends only on observed data, not on unobserved values.
Example: A patient drops out because their baseline depression (observed) was severe—this is MAR. However, if they drop out because their depression improved dramatically (unobserved)—this is MNAR.
Practical implication: Include baseline and prognostic variables in the model to approach MAR; sensitivity analyses for MNAR scenarios.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
24  Longitudinal Data Analysis

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
text:
24.6 Mixed Model for Repeated Measures (MMRM)
The MMRM is the regulatory standard (ICH E9(R1)) for clinical trials:
\[Y_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 V_j + \beta_2 T_i + \beta_3 (V_j \times T_i) + b_{0i} + \epsilon_{ij}\]
where: - \(V_j\) = visit indicator - \(T_i\) = treatment group - \(V_j \times T_i\) = treatment-by-visit interaction (captures differential change) - \(b_{0i}\) = random intercept only (no random slopes) - \(\epsilon_{ij}\) = visit-level residual with unstructured correlation
Key features: - Includes baseline value as a covariate (not as outcome) - Visit as a factor (not linear time) for flexibility - Unstructured correlation (most flexible) - Robust to missing data (MAR)
Clinical interpretation: Report predicted mean values at each visit by treatment arm with CIs, and treatment difference at final visit.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
24  Longitudinal Data Analysis

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
text:
24.7 Clinical Example 1: Orthodontic Growth
Using the nlme::Orthodont dataset: mandibular distance (bone growth) in 27 children, measured at ages 8, 10, 12, 14 years.

24.7.1 Exploratory Analysis
library(nlme)
library(lme4)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

data(Orthodont)

# Data structure
head(Orthodont)
# Subject: child ID
# age: measurement age
# distance: mandibular distance (mm)

# Visualize individual trajectories
plot_indiv <- ggplot(Orthodont, aes(x = age, y = distance,
                                     group = Subject, color = Subject)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~Subject) +
  labs(
    title = "Individual Orthodontic Growth Trajectories",
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "Mandibular Distance (mm)",
    subtitle = "27 children measured at 4 time points"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

print(plot_indiv)

# Plot individual trajectories with population mean overlay
plot_pop <- ggplot(Orthodont, aes(x = age, y = distance)) +
  geom_line(aes(group = Subject), alpha = 0.3, color = "gray") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "line", size = 1.5,
               color = "red", aes(group = 1)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", size = 3,
               color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Orthodontic Growth: Individual + Population Mean",
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "Mandibular Distance (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

print(plot_pop)


24.7.2 Fitting Linear Mixed Models
# Model 1: Random intercept only (each child different baseline)
lme1 <- lme(distance ~ age, data = Orthodont,
            random = ~ 1 | Subject,
            method = "REML")

summary(lme1)

# Model 2: Random intercept + slope (each child different trajectory)
lme2 <- lme(distance ~ age, data = Orthodont,
            random = ~ age | Subject,
            method = "REML")

summary(lme2)

# Model 3: Using lme4 with explicit correlation structure
library(lme4)
lme3 <- lmer(distance ~ age + (1 | Subject), data = Orthodont, REML = TRUE)
summary(lme3)

# Extract fixed effects
fixef(lme3)
# Intercept: ~16 mm at age 0 (baseline mandibular distance)
# Age slope: ~0.66 mm/year (average growth rate)

# Extract random effects
ranef(lme3)
# Shows deviation of each child's intercept from population mean


24.7.3 Model Comparison
# Compare models using likelihood ratio test
# First refit with ML for testing
lme1_ml <- update(lme1, method = "ML")
lme2_ml <- update(lme2, method = "ML")

anova(lme1_ml, lme2_ml)

# Extract AIC
AIC(lme1, lme2)
# If ΔAIC > 10, prefer the model with lower AIC
Interpretation Example: - Fixed effects: Population-level growth rate is 0.66 mm/year - Random intercept variance: \(\sigma_{b0}^2 = 1.8\) suggests substantial baseline differences (~4.2 mm range between children) - Random slope variance: \(\sigma_{b1}^2 = 0.08\) suggests growth rates are similar across children


24.7.4 Predicted Trajectories
# Create new data for prediction
new_ages <- expand.grid(
  age = seq(8, 14, by = 1),
  Subject = unique(Orthodont$Subject)
)

# Population-level prediction (no random effects)
pred_pop <- new_ages %>%
  mutate(
    pred = predict(lme2, newdata = data.frame(age = age, Subject = NA),
                   re.form = NA, level = 0)
  ) %>%
  distinct(age, pred)

# Subject-specific predictions
pred_subj <- new_ages %>%
  mutate(
    pred = predict(lme2, newdata = .)
  )

# Plot
plot_pred <- ggplot(Orthodont, aes(x = age, y = distance)) +
  geom_line(aes(group = Subject), alpha = 0.2, color = "gray") +
  geom_line(data = pred_pop, aes(y = pred, group = 1),
            size = 1.5, color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Observed and Predicted Growth Trajectories",
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "Mandibular Distance (mm)",
    subtitle = "Red line: population average trajectory"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

print(plot_pred)

title: 24  Longitudinal Data Analysis
section: 24.8 Clinical Example 2: MMRM for Vital Signs
text:
24.8 Clinical Example 2: MMRM for Vital Signs
Using pharmaversesdtm::vs dataset: systolic blood pressure (SBP) measured at baseline, Week 2, Week 4, Week 8, Week 12 in a hypertension trial.
library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(admiral)
library(gtsummary)

# Load and prepare data
data(vs)
data(dm)

# Filter SBP measurements and prepare for analysis
vs_sbp <- vs %>%
  filter(VSTESTCD == "SYSBP") %>%  # Systolic BP
  left_join(dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  mutate(
    VISIT = factor(VISIT,
                   levels = c("Baseline", "Week 2", "Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 12")),
    VISITN = as.numeric(VISIT),
    TRTA = factor(ARM),  # Treatment arm from demographics
  ) %>%
  select(USUBJID, VISIT, VISITN, VSSTRESN, TRTA) %>%
  rename(SBP = VSSTRESN) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID, VISITN)

# Get baseline SBP for each subject
baseline_sbp <- vs_sbp %>%
  filter(VISITN == 1) %>%
  select(USUBJID, SBP) %>%
  rename(baseline_SBP = SBP) %>%
  distinct()

vs_sbp <- vs_sbp %>%
  left_join(baseline_sbp, by = "USUBJID")

# Exploratory plot: treatment mean trajectories
plot_means <- vs_sbp %>%
  group_by(VISIT, TRTA) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_sbp = mean(SBP, na.rm = TRUE),
    se_sbp = sd(SBP, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = VISIT, y = mean_sbp, color = TRTA, group = TRTA)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_sbp - se_sbp, ymax = mean_sbp + se_sbp),
                width = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "SBP Response by Treatment Arm",
    x = "Visit",
    y = "Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)",
    color = "Treatment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

print(plot_means)

24.8.1 MMRM Analysis
# MMRM using lme4 (or nlme with unstructured correlation via gls)
library(lme4)

# Fit MMRM
mmrm_model <- lmer(
  SBP ~ VISIT * TRTA + (1 | USUBJID),
  data = vs_sbp,
  REML = TRUE,
  na.action = na.omit
)

summary(mmrm_model)

# Extract fixed effects (treatment comparisons)
fixed_effects <- fixef(mmrm_model)

# Interpretation example:
# TRTADrug A:VISITWeek 12: -8.5 mmHg
# Interpretation: Patients on Drug A had 8.5 mmHg greater reduction from baseline
# by Week 12 compared to Placebo

# Predicted means by treatment and visit
library(emmeans)

pred_means <- emmeans(mmrm_model, ~ VISIT | TRTA)
summary(pred_means)

# Contrast: Drug A vs Placebo at Week 12
contrast_w12 <- emmeans(mmrm_model, pairwise ~ TRTA | VISIT)
summary(contrast_w12)


24.8.2 Publication-Ready Output
# Create a summary table: mean ± SD by visit and treatment
vs_summary <- vs_sbp %>%
  group_by(VISIT, TRTA) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Mean = mean(SBP, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD = sd(SBP, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    `Mean (SD)` = sprintf("%.1f (%.1f)", Mean, SD)
  ) %>%
  select(VISIT, TRTA, N, `Mean (SD)`) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = TRTA,
    values_from = `Mean (SD)`,
    id_cols = VISIT
  )

print(vs_summary)

# Estimated treatment difference at final visit using tidy method
if (requireNamespace("broom.mixed", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(broom.mixed)

  model_tidy <- tidy(mmrm_model, effects = "fixed")

  # lme4 does not compute p-values; approximate via z-statistic
  model_tidy <- model_tidy %>%
    mutate(p.value = 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(statistic))))

  model_tidy %>%
    filter(grepl("Week 12", term)) %>%
    mutate(
      HR = exp(estimate),
      CI_L = exp(estimate - 1.96 * std.error),
      CI_U = exp(estimate + 1.96 * std.error)
    ) %>%
    select(intersect(names(model_tidy), c("term", "estimate", "std.error", "statistic", "p.value")))
}

24.9 Key Assumptions and Diagnostics

24.9.1 Checking Normality of Random Effects
# Extract random intercepts
re_intercepts <- as.numeric(
  as.data.frame(ranef(lme2)$Subject)[["(Intercept)"]]
)
ranef_df <- data.frame(random_intercept = re_intercepts)

ranef_plot <- ranef_df %>%
  ggplot(aes(sample = random_intercept)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line() +
  labs(title = "Q-Q Plot of Random Intercepts") +
  theme_minimal()

print(ranef_plot)


24.9.2 Checking Homogeneity of Variance
# Plot residuals vs fitted
residuals_plot <- data.frame(
  fitted = fitted(lme2),
  residuals = residuals(lme2)
) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fitted, y = residuals)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Residual Plot: Homogeneity of Variance",
    x = "Fitted Values",
    y = "Residuals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

print(residuals_plot)
