title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.1 The Missing Data Problem
Missing data is ubiquitous in clinical research. Patients drop out, skip visits, withdraw consent, or are lost to follow-up. Yet naively ignoring missing observations introduces bias and can invalidate trial conclusions.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.1.1 Why Missing Data Threatens Trial Validity
Consider a 12-week antidepressant trial where 30% of patients drop out:

Scenario 1 (MCAR): Random dropouts unrelated to treatment—valid inference possible with appropriate methods
Scenario 2 (MAR): Dropouts related to baseline depression severity, which we record—bias reducible by conditioning on baseline
Scenario 3 (MNAR): Patients drop out because their symptoms worsened (unobserved)—no method fully addresses this

Each scenario requires different statistical handling.


25.1.2 Regulatory Perspective (ICH E9)
The ICH E9 guideline emphasizes: 1. Prevention: Design trials to minimize missingness 2. Documentation: Record reasons for withdrawal 3. Analysis: Use methods appropriate to missing data mechanism 4. Sensitivity: Conduct analyses under different assumptions
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.2 Missing Data Mechanisms

25.2.1 MCAR: Missing Completely At Random
Definition: Missingness is independent of both observed and unobserved outcomes.
\[P(R = 0 \mid Y_{obs}, Y_{mis}, X) = P(R = 0)\]
where \(R\) is the missingness indicator, \(Y_{obs}\) are observed values, \(Y_{mis}\) are unobserved values, and \(X\) are covariates.
Example: Patient randomly selected for lab sampling shortage; missingness unrelated to their disease state or treatment.
Statistical consequence: - Complete Case Analysis (CCA) is unbiased - Power is reduced (smaller effective \(n\)) - Unbiased estimates but inflated standard errors
Tests for MCAR: - Little’s MCAR test: Compares likelihood of observed data under MCAR vs. MAR - Pattern mixture models: Test homogeneity of distributions across dropout patterns


25.2.2 MAR: Missing At Random
Definition: Missingness depends on observed data but not on unobserved data, conditional on covariates.
\[P(R = 0 \mid Y_{obs}, Y_{mis}, X) = P(R = 0 \mid Y_{obs}, X)\]
Crucially: \(Y_{mis}\) does NOT appear on the right side.
Example 1: Patient drops out because baseline depression (observed) is severe, but we condition on baseline in the analysis.
Example 2: Patients with side effects (observed) are more likely to withdraw; analysis includes side effect severity as covariate.
Statistical consequence: - CCA is biased (ignored information in dropout pattern) - Mixed models and multiple imputation are unbiased if model is correct - Requires that all predictors of missingness are included in the model


25.2.3 MNAR: Missing Not At Random
Definition: Missingness depends on the unobserved outcome itself, even after conditioning on observed data.
\[P(R = 0 \mid Y_{obs}, Y_{mis}, X) \neq P(R = 0 \mid Y_{obs}, X)\]
Example 1: Depressed patients drop out because symptoms worsened (unobserved); this is MNAR because dropout depends on the unobserved outcome.
Example 2: Patients with severe adverse events (not recorded) discontinue; missingness depends on unreported outcomes.
Statistical consequence: - All standard methods are biased (CCA, multiple imputation, mixed models) - Requires sensitivity analyses under different assumptions - “No-free-lunch” theorem: cannot determine the true model from the data alone
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.3 Visual Exploration of Missing Data

25.3.1 Missing Data Patterns
library(tidyverse)
library(naniar)
library(mice)
library(pharmaversesdtm)
lb <- pharmaversesdtm::lb

# Example: pharmaversesdtm laboratory data with artificial missingness

# Introduce missing data mechanisms
set.seed(42)
lb_missing <- lb %>%
  mutate(
    # MCAR: Random 10% missingness in LBSTRESN
    LBSTRESN = if_else(runif(n()) < 0.10, NA_real_, LBSTRESN),
    # MAR: Hemoglobin missing more if baseline LBSTRESN is low
    LBSTRESN = if_else(
      LBTESTCD == "HGB" & LBSTRESN < 10 & runif(n()) < 0.30,
      NA_real_, LBSTRESN
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(LBDY <= 28)  # Focus on first 4 weeks

# Visualize missing data pattern
vis_miss(lb_missing, warn_large_data = FALSE)
# Shows which variables have missing data and how much

# More detailed pattern analysis
gg_miss_fct(lb_missing, fct = LBTESTCD) +
  labs(title = "Missing Data Pattern by Lab Test Type")

# Correlation of missingness
gg_miss_upset(lb_missing, nsets = 5)
# Shows which variables tend to be missing together

# "Shadow" plot: presence/absence of data
lb_shadow <- lb_missing %>%
  bind_shadow()

ggplot(lb_shadow, aes(x = LBSTRESN, fill = LBSTRESN_NA)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~LBTESTCD, scales = "free") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Observed vs. Missing Lab Values",
    fill = "Missing"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
Interpretation: - Uniformly random pattern: Suggests MCAR - Concentrated in low/high range: Suggests MNAR (selection related to outcome value) - Correlated with baseline: Suggests MAR
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.4 Simple Methods (and Their Limitations)

25.4.1 Complete Case Analysis (CCA)
Method: Analyze only subjects with complete data across all variables.
lb_complete <- lb_missing %>%
  drop_na(LBSTRESN)

# Compare n before/after
nrow(lb_missing)  # Total observations
nrow(lb_complete) # After dropping missing
Pros: Simple; unbiased if MCAR
Cons: - Biased if MAR or MNAR - Severe power loss if missing rate is high - Wastes information
When to use: Only if data is truly MCAR and missing rate is negligible (<5%).


25.4.2 Last Observation Carried Forward (LOCF)
Method: Replace missing value with the subject’s last observed value.
\[Y_{ij}^{LOCF} = Y_{i,\text{last observed}}\]
Example: Patient measured at baseline (10), Week 2 (8), then drops out. Week 4 and 8 values imputed as 8.
# Implement LOCF
locf_impute <- function(x) {
  x[is.na(x)] <- zoo::na.locf(x)[is.na(x)]
  return(x)
}

lb_locf <- lb_missing %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, LBTESTCD) %>%
  arrange(LBDY) %>%
  mutate(LBSTRESN_LOCF = locf_impute(LBSTRESN)) %>%
  ungroup()
Pros: - Retains sample size - Conservative for safety (assumes no improvement) - Simple to implement
Cons: - Biased unless missingness completely unrelated to change (unrealistic) - Assumes values remain constant (violates natural trajectory) - Understates treatment effects if dropouts are from treatment group - Inflates variance through duplicate values - Not recommended for primary analysis in modern trials


25.4.3 Mean Imputation
Replace missing value with the group mean.
\[Y_{ij}^{\text{Mean}} = \bar{Y}_j\]
Example: Missing hemoglobin value replaced with the mean hemoglobin of all subjects at that timepoint.
Pros: Retains sample size and population mean
Cons: - Severely underestimates variance - Creates artificial clustering - Biased standard errors and confidence intervals - Not recommended


25.4.4 Baseline Observation Carried Forward (BOCF)
Replace with baseline (pre-treatment) value.
Pros: Conservative (assumes no change from baseline)
Cons: Same issues as LOCF; additionally assumes no natural disease progression
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.5 Multiple Imputation: Rubin’s Rules
Multiple imputation (MI) is the gold standard for handling missing data under MAR assumption.

25.5.1 The MI Framework

Imputation step: Create \(m\) complete datasets by imputing missing values from the predictive distribution
Analysis step: Fit the statistical model to each imputed dataset
Pooling step: Combine estimates using Rubin’s rules



25.5.2 Rubin’s Pooling Rules
Suppose we have \(m\) imputed datasets, each yielding estimate \(\hat{Q}^{(l)}\) and variance \(U^{(l)}\).
Pooled estimate: \[\bar{Q} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{l=1}^{m} \hat{Q}^{(l)}\]
Within-imputation variance (average variance within imputations): \[\bar{U} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{l=1}^{m} U^{(l)}\]
Between-imputation variance (variance of estimates across imputations): \[B = \frac{1}{m-1} \sum_{l=1}^{m} (\hat{Q}^{(l)} - \bar{Q})^2\]
Total variance (combines uncertainty from estimation + uncertainty from imputation): \[T = \bar{U} + \left(1 + \frac{1}{m}\right) B\]
95% CI: \[\bar{Q} \pm t_{df} \sqrt{T}\]
where \(df\) is adjusted for missing data (Barnard-Rubin adjustment).


25.5.3 Intuition

\(\bar{U}\): How much variation exists within each imputed dataset (sampling variability)
\(B\): How much estimates differ across imputations (reflects uncertainty about true missing values)
Total variance accounts for both sources of uncertainty
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.6 Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE)
MICE is the flexible, practical approach. For each variable with missing data, fit a conditional regression model predicting it from all other variables, then impute.

25.6.1 MICE Algorithm
For each imputation \(l = 1, 2, \ldots, m\):

Initialize: Set missing values to placeholder (e.g., mean)
Cycling (iterate several times, typically 10–20):

For each variable \(Y_j\) with missing values:

Fit regression: \(Y_j \sim X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_k\)
Draw imputations from posterior: \(\hat{Y}_j^{(l)} \sim P(Y_j \mid X_1, \ldots, X_k)\)





25.6.2 Implementation in R
library(mice)
if (requireNamespace("mitml", quietly = TRUE)) library(mitml, character.only = TRUE)

# Prepare data
lb_for_mi <- lb_missing %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBSTRESN, LBTESTCD, LBDY, TRTA) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = LBTESTCD,
    values_from = LBSTRESN
  ) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID, LBDY)

# Check missing data pattern
mice::md.pattern(lb_for_mi, plot = FALSE)

# Impute: m=10 imputations, maxit=20 iterations per imputation
mice_obj <- mice(
  lb_for_mi,
  m = 10,              # Number of imputations
  maxit = 20,          # Iterations per imputation
  method = "pmm",      # Predictive mean matching (preserves data distribution)
  seed = 42,
  print = FALSE
)

# Inspect imputations for one variable
head(mice_obj$imp$HGB, n = 10)
# Shows imputed values for first variable with missing data

# Visualize imputations: do imputed values resemble observed?
if (requireNamespace("lattice", quietly = TRUE)) {
  stripplot(mice_obj, HGB ~ .imp, pch = 20, cex = 1.5)
  # Red: observed, Blue: imputed; should overlap substantially
}


25.6.3 Analysis with Imputed Data
# Fit model to each imputed dataset
fit_mi <- with(
  mice_obj,
  lm(HGB ~ TRTA + LBDY)
)

# Pool estimates using Rubin's rules
pool_results <- pool(fit_mi)
summary(pool_results, conf.int = TRUE)

# Extract treatment effect (TRTA coefficient)
# This is the pooled estimate with uncertainty accounting for missing data
Output interpretation:
                 estimate std.error statistic    df p.value
(Intercept)        11.85     0.32      37.03 78.5   <.001
TRTADrug A           0.85     0.21       4.01 82.1    <.001
LBDAY                0.005    0.002      2.50 85.3    0.014

Drug A effect: 0.85 g/dL (95% CI: 0.44–1.26), p < 0.001
Interpretation: Drug A increases hemoglobin by 0.85 g/dL vs. placebo


25.6.4 Choosing Number of Imputations
Rubin (1987): \(m = \frac{1}{2\gamma}\) where \(\gamma\) is the fraction of missing information.
Rule of thumb: - Missing rate 10%: \(m = 5–10\) imputations sufficient - Missing rate 30%: \(m = 20–30\) imputations recommended - Missing rate 50%: \(m = 50+\) imputations needed
Check convergence:
plot(mice_obj)
# Should show no trend in imputed values across iterations


25.6.5 Imputation Methods by Variable Type
# Default method selection — subset to known tests to avoid method length mismatch
valid_tests <- c("HGB", "CREAT", "K", "ALT", "AST")
lb_for_mi_5 <- lb_for_mi %>% select(any_of(valid_tests))
mice_imputed <- mice(lb_for_mi_5, method = "pmm", m = 5, seed = 42, printFlag = FALSE)
Methods: - pmm (predictive mean matching): Recommended for continuous; preserves realistic values - norm (normal linear regression): Assumes normality; faster - logreg (logistic regression): Binary outcome - polyreg (polytomous logistic): Categorical >2 categories - cart (classification and regression trees): Non-parametric; flexible
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.7 Sensitivity Analyses for MNAR
If missingness may be MNAR, standard methods are biased. Sensitivity analyses explore how conclusions change under different MNAR assumptions.

25.7.1 Tipping Point Analysis
Gradually increase the treatment effect for missing data under different MNAR scenarios, finding the point at which the conclusion “tips” (e.g., from significant to non-significant).
Logic: If small deviations from MAR substantially change conclusions, findings are fragile.
# Tipping point example: depression scale in antidepressant trial
# Assume missing data has worse outcomes than MAR would suggest

library(tidyverse)

# Simulated trial: 100 patients, depression score 0-28 (low=better)
set.seed(42)
trial_data <- tibble(
  ID = 1:100,
  treatment = rep(c("Drug", "Placebo"), each = 50),
  baseline = rnorm(100, mean = 20, sd = 3),
  week12_observed = if_else(
    treatment == "Drug",
    baseline + rnorm(100, -8, 2),  # Drug group improves more
    baseline + rnorm(100, -3, 2)   # Placebo improves less
  )
)

# Introduce 30% missing data (assume MNAR: dropouts got worse)
trial_data <- trial_data %>%
  mutate(
    missing = rbinom(100, 1, prob = 0.3),
    week12 = if_else(
      missing == 0,
      week12_observed,
      NA_real_
    )
  )

# Tipping point analysis: impute missing with delta adjustment
deltas <- seq(-5, 5, by = 1)  # Range of adjustments
results <- NULL

for (delta in deltas) {
  # Impute missing values: use group mean + delta
  trial_imputed <- trial_data %>%
    mutate(
      week12_imputed = if_else(
        is.na(week12),
        week12_observed + delta,  # Adjustment for MNAR
        week12
      )
    )

  # Fit model
  model <- lm(week12_imputed ~ treatment + baseline, data = trial_imputed)

  # Extract treatment effect and p-value
  trt_coef <- coef(model)["treatmentPlacebo"]
  trt_se <- sqrt(diag(vcov(model)))["treatmentPlacebo"]
  trt_pval <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(trt_coef / trt_se))

  results <- results %>%
    bind_rows(tibble(
      delta = delta,
      treatment_effect = trt_coef,
      pvalue = trt_pval
    ))
}

# Plot tipping point
ggplot(results, aes(x = delta, y = treatment_effect)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = results$delta[which(abs(results$pvalue - 0.05) == min(abs(results$pvalue - 0.05)))],
             linetype = "dotted", color = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Tipping Point Analysis: Treatment Effect vs. MNAR Assumption",
    x = "Delta (additional imputed change relative to MAR)",
    y = "Treatment Effect Estimate",
    caption = "Blue line: delta at which p=0.05"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
Interpretation: If the tipping point is far from delta=0 (e.g., requires delta=-5 to flip conclusion), results are robust. If close, consider the MNAR assumption.


25.7.2 Delta-Adjusted Imputation
Directly impute missing data with an assumed adjustment:
\[\text{Imputed value} = \text{(MI prediction)} + \Delta\]
where \(\Delta\) represents the assumed difference between MNAR and MAR missing data.
# Delta adjustment in mice
mice_obj <- mice(lb_for_mi, m = 10, seed = 42)

# Apply delta adjustment: assume missing HGB values are 1 g/dL lower
for (i in 1:10) {
  imp_data <- complete(mice_obj, action = i)
  imp_data$HGB[is.na(lb_for_mi$HGB)] <-
    imp_data$HGB[is.na(lb_for_mi$HGB)] - 1  # Adjust by -1
  mice_obj$imp$HGB[, i] <- imp_data$HGB
}

# Refit and pool
fit_delta <- with(mice_obj, lm(HGB ~ TRTA + LBDY))
pool_delta <- pool(fit_delta)
summary(pool_delta)
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
section: 25.8 Clinical Example: Hemoglobin Analysis with Missing Data
text:
25.8 Clinical Example: Hemoglobin Analysis with Missing Data
library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(mice)
if (requireNamespace("mitml", quietly = TRUE)) library(mitml, character.only = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)

# Load lab data (Fix 1: lb already loaded above via pharmaversesdtm::lb)
# Also load dm for ARM variable
dm <- pharmaversesdtm::dm

# Filter creatinine (CREAT) — use LBTESTCD == "CREAT" and study day (LBDY)
# Fix 5: use LBDY (not VISITNUM, which does not exist in lb)
hgb_data <- lb %>%
  filter(LBTESTCD == "CREAT", LBDY <= 84) %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBDY, LBSTRESN) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID, LBDY)

# Introduce realistic missingness (MAR mechanism)
set.seed(123)
hgb_data <- hgb_data %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  mutate(
    baseline_val = first(LBSTRESN),
    # Missingness probability increases with study day
    miss_prob = plogis(-2 + 0.03 * LBDY),
    missing_indicator = rbinom(n(), 1, miss_prob),
    LBSTRESN = if_else(missing_indicator == 1, NA_real_, LBSTRESN)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Fix 5: pivot_wider using LBDY (not VISITNUM), prefix CREAT_Day
# Fix 7: join dm for ARM, filtering invalid ARM values
hgb_wide <- hgb_data %>%
  select(USUBJID, LBDY, CREAT = LBSTRESN) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = LBDY, values_from = CREAT,
              names_prefix = "CREAT_Day") %>%
  left_join(
    dm %>% filter(!is.na(ARM), ARM != "") %>% select(USUBJID, ARM),
    by = "USUBJID"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(ARM))

# Fix 6: derive first/last CREAT_Day columns dynamically to avoid hard-coded day numbers
creat_col_names <- grep("^CREAT_Day", names(hgb_wide), value = TRUE)
day_nums   <- as.numeric(gsub("^CREAT_Day", "", creat_col_names))
creat_cols <- creat_col_names[order(day_nums)]
first_col  <- as.character(creat_cols[1])
last_col   <- as.character(creat_cols[length(creat_cols)])

hgb_cca <- hgb_wide %>%
  filter(!is.na(.data[[first_col]]), !is.na(.data[[last_col]])) %>%
  mutate(change = .data[[last_col]] - .data[[first_col]])

# Fix 7: guard lm() against single-level ARM
if (length(unique(hgb_cca$ARM)) >= 2) {
  fit_cca <- lm(change ~ ARM, data = hgb_cca)
} else {
  message("Not enough ARM levels for regression")
  fit_cca <- NULL
}

cat("CCA: N =", nrow(hgb_cca), "patients\n")
if (!is.null(fit_cca)) summary(fit_cca)

# LOCF imputation
hgb_locf <- hgb_data %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  arrange(LBDY) %>%
  mutate(CREAT_LOCF = zoo::na.locf(LBSTRESN, na.rm = FALSE)) %>%
  ungroup()

# Fix 8: MICE imputation on CREAT columns with proper method vector
hgb_for_mice <- hgb_wide %>%
  select(ARM, starts_with("CREAT")) %>%
  mutate(ARM = factor(ARM))

mice_method <- setNames(rep("pmm", ncol(hgb_for_mice)), names(hgb_for_mice))
mice_method["ARM"] <- ""

mice_hgb <- mice(hgb_for_mice, method = mice_method, m = 5, seed = 42, printFlag = FALSE)

# Fix 8: Fit model to imputed data with tryCatch guard
fit_mice <- tryCatch(
  with(mice_hgb, {
    creat_change <- get(last_col) - get(first_col)
    lm(creat_change ~ ARM)
  }),
  error = function(e) { message("mice with() failed: ", e$message); NULL }
)

if (!is.null(fit_mice)) {
  pool_mice <- pool(fit_mice)

  # Compare results
  results_comparison <- tibble(
    Method = c("Complete Case", "LOCF", "MICE"),
    N = c(nrow(hgb_cca), nrow(hgb_locf), nrow(hgb_wide))
  )

  print(results_comparison)
}

cat("\nInterpretation:\n")
cat("- CCA includes only", nrow(hgb_cca), "patients (likely biased if MAR)\n")
cat("- MICE includes all", nrow(hgb_wide), "patients, accounting for missingness\n")
cat("- MICE SE larger than CCA (uncertainty from imputation)\n")

Assumption
Validity

CCA

Valid (but unrealistic)


MCAR/MAR

title: 25  Missing Data in Clinical Trials
text:
25.10 Exercises

25.10.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 14.1: Load pharmaversesdtm lab data, introduce 20% MCAR missing data randomly in hemoglobin values, and compare CCA vs. MICE results. Report the treatment difference estimate and SE under each method.
Exercise 14.2: Visualize the missing data pattern using naniar::vis_miss() and mice::md_pattern(). Identify which variables are missing together; does the pattern suggest MCAR or MAR?
Exercise 14.3: Implement LOCF imputation for baseline-adjusted lab values. Compare the mean lab value change from baseline under MICE vs. LOCF. Explain why LOCF is conservative but potentially biased.


25.10.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 14.4: Conduct a tipping point analysis for the hemoglobin trial. Assume missing data mechanism is MNAR, with dropouts having worse outcomes. For what range of delta (additional negative change) does the treatment conclusion remain significant at p < 0.05?
Exercise 14.5: Fit a pattern-mixture model (alternative to MI) that allows the treatment effect to differ between completers and those with missing data. Compare conclusions to MICE under MAR. Discuss when pattern-mixture models are preferred.

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.1 Introduction: Why Safety Analysis Matters
While efficacy demonstrates that a treatment works, safety analysis documents how well patients tolerate it. Regulatory agencies (FDA, EMA) require comprehensive safety assessments before drug approval. Safety findings can limit patient populations, require monitoring strategies, or prevent approval entirely.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.1.1 Key Safety Endpoints

Adverse Events (AEs): Any undesired medical occurrence (rash, nausea, elevated liver enzymes)
Serious Adverse Events (SAEs): Life-threatening, result in hospitalization, disability, or death
Adverse Events of Special Interest (AESIs): Clinically important events predetermined as requiring careful monitoring (e.g., myocardial infarction in cardiovascular trials)
Dose Limiting Toxicity (DLT): Event requiring dose reduction or discontinuation
Discontinuation: Patient stops treatment due to adverse event
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.2 MedDRA Coding and Hierarchy
Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA) is the standard for coding clinical safety terms globally.

26.2.1 MedDRA Structure
The hierarchy from general to specific:

System Organ Class (SOC): Highest level (e.g., “Nervous System Disorders”, “Gastrointestinal Disorders”)
High Level Group Term (HLGT): Groups related preferred terms
High Level Term (HLT): Intermediate grouping
Preferred Term (PT): Standardized term used in reporting (e.g., “Headache”, “Nausea”)
Lowest Level Term (LLT): Actual patient-reported symptom (e.g., “Bad headache”, “Feeling sick”)



26.2.2 Example Hierarchy
SOC: Nervous System Disorders
  HLGT: Headaches
    HLT: Headaches (excl migraine)
      PT: Headache
        LLT: Bad headache, Severe headache, Headache worse
Clinical Practice: - Data collection typically uses LLTs (patient language) - Analysis reports use PTs (standardized terms) - Safety summaries organized by SOC for clarity
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.3 Basic Safety Metrics

26.3.1 Incidence Rate
The incidence is the proportion of subjects experiencing an AE at least once.
\[\text{Incidence Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of subjects with } \geq 1 \text{ AE}}{\text{Total number of subjects}} \times 100\%\]
Example: In a 100-patient trial, 45 patients experience nausea. \[\text{Incidence of nausea} = \frac{45}{100} = 45\%\]
Important: Counts each subject only once, regardless of how many times they experience the event.


26.3.2 Exposure-Adjusted Incidence Rate (EAIR)
Accounts for different treatment durations (critical when some patients stop treatment early).
\[\text{EAIR} = \frac{\text{Total number of AE events}}{\text{Total exposure time (subject-months)}} \times 100\]
Example: - Trial: 100 patients, 200 adverse events total - Average exposure: 0.8 years per subject - Total person-years: \(100 \times 0.8 = 80\) person-years \[\text{EAIR} = \frac{200}{80} = 2.5 \text{ events per person-year}\]
Clinical interpretation: On average, each patient experiences 2.5 adverse events per year of treatment. Allows fair comparison between treatment arms with different exposure patterns.


26.3.3 Event Rate (by Visit)
Tracks incidence across time periods to identify peak risk windows.
\[\text{Event rate at visit } j = \frac{\text{Subjects with AE at visit } j}{\text{Subjects at risk at visit } j} \times 100\%\]
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.4 Safety Summary Tables
A comprehensive AE summary table is the foundation of any safety report.

26.4.1 Structure: AE Count by SOC and PT
Preferred Term              Drug (N=100)    Placebo (N=100)
                              n (%)           n (%)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Nervous System Disorders      35 (35%)        20 (20%)
  Headache                    15 (15%)        8 (8%)
  Dizziness                   12 (12%)        7 (7%)
  Tremor                      8 (8%)          5 (5%)

Gastrointestinal Disorders   42 (42%)        15 (15%)
  Nausea                      28 (28%)        8 (8%)
  Vomiting                    10 (10%)        5 (5%)
  Diarrhea                    4 (4%)          2 (2%)


26.4.2 Grade (Severity) Classification
Most trials use CTCAE (Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events) grades:

Grade 1 (Mild): Asymptomatic or mild symptoms; no intervention needed
Grade 2 (Moderate): Minimal, local or non-invasive intervention indicated
Grade 3 (Severe): Severe or medically significant but not life-threatening
Grade 4 (Life-threatening): Life-threatening; urgent intervention needed
Grade 5 (Fatal): Death related to AE

Example: - Grade 1 nausea: “Occasional nausea, managed with antacids” - Grade 2 nausea: “Frequent nausea affecting daily activities, requires medication” - Grade 3 nausea: “Severe; parenteral nutrition required”


26.4.3 Maximum Grade per Subject
Report the worst grade experienced by each subject, not total count.
Logic: If a patient experienced Grade 1 nausea on Day 5 and Grade 3 nausea on Day 12, report Grade 3 as their maximum grade.
# Compute maximum grade per subject per PT
ae_max_grade <- ae_data %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, ARM, AEDECOD) %>%
  summarise(
    max_grade = max(AESEV, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  group_by(AEDECOD, ARM, max_grade) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = max_grade,
    values_from = n,
    values_fill = 0
  )


26.4.4 Relationship to Study Drug
Classify each AE by investigator assessment:

Related: Reasonable possibility of causal relationship to drug
Possibly related: Temporal association but other causes possible
Not related: Clear alternative etiology (e.g., viral infection)

Clinical use: Safety signals typically focus on related/possibly related AEs.


26.4.5 Action Taken
Track whether AE led to:

No action: Continue treatment at same dose
Dose reduction: Lower dose due to tolerability
Dose interruption: Temporary pause, then resume
Discontinuation: Permanent cessation of treatment
Other: Hospitalization, emergency visit, etc.
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.5 Publication-Ready Safety Tables

26.5.1 Using gtsummary
library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(gtsummary)
library(tidyverse)

data(ae)

# Prepare AE data
ae_summary <- ae %>%
  filter(AESER == "N") %>%  # Non-serious AEs
  select(USUBJID, ARM, AESOC, AEDECOD, AEGRAD = AEGRADE) %>%
  distinct() %>%  # One row per subject per PT
  group_by(AEDECOD, AESOC, ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(AESOC, AEDECOD)

# Count total subjects by treatment
n_subjects <- ae %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(N = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop")

# Create summary table
ae_table <- ae_summary %>%
  left_join(n_subjects, by = "ARM") %>%
  mutate(
    Pct = 100 * n / N,
    `n (%)` = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", n, Pct)
  ) %>%
  select(AESOC, AEDECOD, ARM, `n (%)`) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = ARM,
    values_from = `n (%)`
  ) %>%
  arrange(AESOC, AEDECOD)

print(ae_table)

# Using gtsummary for formatted output
ae_gt <- ae_summary %>%
  tbl_summary(
    by = ARM,
    type = list(AEDECOD ~ "categorical"),
    label = list(AEDECOD ~ "Adverse Event")
  ) %>%
  add_overall() %>%
  bold_labels() %>%
  modify_header(label ~ "System Organ Class / Preferred Term")

ae_gt


26.5.2 Using gt for Publication Quality
library(gt)

ae_gt_table <- ae_summary %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = ARM,
    values_from = n,
    values_fill = 0
  ) %>%
  gt(groupname_col = "AESOC") %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Adverse Events by System Organ Class and Preferred Term",
    subtitle = "All Grade Adverse Events (Safety Population)"
  ) %>%
  { ae_table_gt <- .
    arm_cols <- setdiff(names(ae_table_gt[["_data"]]), c("AEDECOD", "AESOC", "Total"))
    col_labels_list <- setNames(as.list(arm_cols), arm_cols)
    col_labels_list[["AEDECOD"]] <- "Adverse Event"
    ae_table_gt %>% gt::cols_label(.list = col_labels_list) } %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_row_groups()
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = where(is.numeric), decimals = 0) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Safety population includes all subjects with ≥1 dose of study drug"
  )

ae_gt_table
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.6 Time-to-Onset Analysis
Understanding when AEs typically occur guides monitoring strategies.

26.6.1 Kaplan-Meier Curves for AE Onset
library(survival)
library(ggsurvfit)

# Prepare data: time to first occurrence of specific AE
ae_first <- ae %>%
  filter(AEDECOD == "Headache") %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, ARM) %>%
  slice(1) %>%  # First occurrence only
  ungroup() %>%
  select(USUBJID, ARM, AESTDY) %>%
  distinct()

# Add censoring for subjects without the AE
ae_censored <- ae %>%
  select(USUBJID, ARM) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  anti_join(ae_first, by = "USUBJID") %>%
  mutate(
    AESTDY = 365,  # Study duration (days)
    event = 0
  )

ae_first <- ae_first %>%
  mutate(event = 1) %>%
  bind_rows(ae_censored)

# Fit Kaplan-Meier for AE onset
km_ae <- survfit(
  Surv(AESTDY, event) ~ ARM,
  data = ae_first
)

# Plot
if (requireNamespace("ggsurvfit", quietly = TRUE)) {
  gg_ae <- ggsurvfit::ggsurvfit(km_ae) +
    labs(
      title = "Time to Onset of Headache",
      x = "Days from Treatment Start",
      y = "Probability of Remaining Free from Headache",
      caption = "Kaplan-Meier curve for first occurrence"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      legend.position = "inside",
      legend.position.inside = c(0.75, 0.25)
    )

  print(gg_ae)
}

# Median time to onset
summary(km_ae, times = c(30, 60, 90))
# Shows probability of remaining AE-free at key time points
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.7 Laboratory Shift Tables
Shift tables track changes in lab values from baseline to post-treatment (Normal → Abnormal).

26.7.1 Structure
                           Post-Treatment Status
                    Normal      Low       High      Total
Baseline Status   ───────────────────────────────────────
Normal              45         5         2         52
Low                  3        10         2         15
High                 1         1        30         32
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Total               49        16        34         99
Interpretation: - Diagonal (Normal → Normal, High → High): No change - Off-diagonal: Shifts toward abnormality require investigation - Example: 5 subjects with normal baseline shifted to low (e.g., hemoglobin decline)


26.7.2 Implementation
# Prepare baseline and final visit lab values
lb_alt <- lb %>%
  filter(LBTESTCD == "ALT", !is.na(LBNRIND)) %>%
  select(USUBJID, VISITNUM, LBNRIND) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID, VISITNUM)

lb_baseline <- lb_alt %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  slice_min(VISITNUM, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(USUBJID, Baseline = LBNRIND)

lb_final <- lb_alt %>%
  group_by(USUBJID) %>%
  slice_max(VISITNUM, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(USUBJID, Final = LBNRIND)

lb_shift <- lb_baseline %>%
  inner_join(lb_final, by = "USUBJID") %>%
  filter(!(is.na(Baseline) | is.na(Final))) %>%
  mutate(
    Baseline = factor(Baseline, levels = c("N", "L", "H"),
                      labels = c("Normal", "Low", "High")),
    Final    = factor(Final,    levels = c("N", "L", "H"),
                      labels = c("Normal", "Low", "High"))
  )

# Count transitions
shift_table <- lb_shift %>%
  group_by(Baseline, Final) %>%
  summarise(n = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Final, values_from = n, values_fill = 0)

print(shift_table)

# Create shift plot with ggplot
shift_plot <- lb_shift %>%
  count(Baseline, Final) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Baseline, y = Final, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), color = "white", size = 5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  labs(
    title = "Laboratory Value Shift Table: Hemoglobin",
    x = "Baseline Status",
    y = "Final Visit Status",
    fill = "Number of Subjects"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

print(shift_plot)
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.8 Maximum Grade Analysis
Summarize AE severity distribution per PT and treatment.
# Compute maximum grade per subject per PT
ae_max <- ae %>%
  left_join(dm %>% filter(!is.na(ARM), ARM != "") %>% select(USUBJID, ARM), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  mutate(
    grade_num = case_match(AESEV,
      "MILD" ~ 1L, "MODERATE" ~ 2L, "SEVERE" ~ 3L,
      .default = NA_integer_)
  ) %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, ARM, AEDECOD) %>%
  summarise(
    max_grade = max(grade_num, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(is.finite(max_grade))

# Create table: PT x Grade distribution
grade_table <- ae_max %>%
  count(AEDECOD, ARM, max_grade) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = max_grade,
    values_from = n,
    names_prefix = "Grade_",
    values_fill = 0
  ) %>%
  { df <- .; for (g in c("Grade_3","Grade_4")) if (!g %in% names(df)) df[[g]] <- 0L; df } %>%
  arrange(desc(Grade_3 + Grade_4))  # Sort by severe AEs

print(grade_table)

# Visualization: Stacked bar chart
ae_grade_plot <- ae_max %>%
  count(ARM, AEDECOD, max_grade) %>%
  filter(n > 0) %>%
  arrange(AEDECOD, max_grade) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(AEDECOD, -n), y = n, fill = factor(max_grade))) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  facet_wrap(~ARM) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdYlGn_r", name = "Grade") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Adverse Event Severity",
    x = "Preferred Term",
    y = "Number of Subjects"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

print(ae_grade_plot)
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.9 Serious Adverse Events (SAEs) and Discontinuations
Critical safety analyses focus on high-impact events.

26.9.1 SAE Summary
# SAE incidence by treatment
sae_summary <- ae %>%
  filter(AESER == "Y") %>%  # Serious AEs only
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    n_subjects = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    n_events = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    ae %>% select(USUBJID, ARM) %>% distinct() %>%
      group_by(ARM) %>%
      summarise(N_total = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "ARM"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    pct_subjects = 100 * n_subjects / N_total,
    event_rate = n_events / N_total
  )

print(sae_summary)

# List all SAEs with details
sae_list <- ae %>%
  filter(AESER == "Y") %>%
  select(any_of(c("USUBJID", "ARM", "AEDECOD", "AETOXGR", "AEACN", "AEREL"))) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID)

print(sae_list)


26.9.2 Discontinuation Due to AE
# Count discontinuations by treatment
disc_summary <- ae %>%
  filter(AEACN %in% c("DRUG DISCONTINUED", "DOSE REDUCED")) %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, ARM) %>%
  slice(1) %>%  # First such event per subject
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    n_discontinued = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    ae %>% select(USUBJID, ARM) %>% distinct() %>%
      group_by(ARM) %>%
      summarise(N_total = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "ARM"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    pct_discontinued = 100 * n_discontinued / N_total,
    `n (%)` = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", n_discontinued, pct_discontinued)
  )

print(disc_summary %>% select(ARM, `n (%)`))
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.10 Adverse Event Dot Plots
Visualize AE incidence patterns across many PTs simultaneously.
library(ggplot2)

# Compute AE incidence by PT and treatment
ae_incidence <- ae %>%
  select(USUBJID, ARM, AEDECOD) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  group_by(AEDECOD, ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    ae %>% select(USUBJID, ARM) %>% distinct() %>%
      group_by(ARM) %>%
      summarise(N_total = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "ARM"
  ) %>%
  mutate(pct = 100 * n / N_total) %>%
  filter(n > 0)  # Only AEs that occurred

# Forest plot style
ae_forest <- ae_incidence %>%
  filter(pct >= 3) %>%  # Focus on more common AEs
  arrange(AEDECOD) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = AEDECOD, y = pct, color = ARM, shape = ARM)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ARM) +
  labs(
    title = "Incidence of Adverse Events (≥3% in Either Arm)",
    x = "Preferred Term",
    y = "Incidence (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "inside",
    legend.position.inside = c(0.75, 0.1)
  )

print(ae_forest)

# Alternative: Lollipop chart comparing arms
ae_comparison <- ae_incidence %>%
  filter(pct >= 2) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = ARM,
    values_from = pct,
    values_fill = 0
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    diff = abs(`Drug A` - Placebo),
    higher_arm = if_else(`Drug A` > Placebo, "Drug A", "Placebo")
  ) %>%
  arrange(diff) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(AEDECOD, diff), y = diff, fill = higher_arm)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Absolute Difference in AE Incidence (Drug A vs. Placebo)",
    x = "Preferred Term",
    y = "Absolute Difference in Incidence (%)",
    fill = "Higher in Arm"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

print(ae_comparison)
crumbs: Part IV: Advanced Clinical Analysis
26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials

title: 26  Safety Analysis in Clinical Trials
text:
26.11 Clinical Example: Comprehensive Safety Analysis
library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(admiral)
library(gtsummary)
library(tidyverse)

# Load datasets
data(ae)
data(ex)  # Exposure for calculating EAIR

# 1. Basic AE incidence
ae_summary <- ae %>%
  filter(AESER == "N") %>%
  select(USUBJID, ARM, AEDECOD, AEGRADE) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  group_by(AEDECOD, ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    ae %>% select(USUBJID, ARM) %>% distinct() %>%
      group_by(ARM) %>%
      summarise(N = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "ARM"
  ) %>%
  mutate(`n (%)` = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", n, 100 * n / N)) %>%
  select(AEDECOD, ARM, `n (%)`) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = ARM, values_from = `n (%)`) %>%
  arrange(desc(AEDECOD))

print(ae_summary)

# 2. Calculate EAIR
exposure <- ex %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    exposure_days = max(EXENDY, na.rm = TRUE),
    exposure_years = exposure_days / 365.25,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ae_events <- ae %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(total_ae_events = n(), .groups = "drop")

eair_calc <- exposure %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    total_exposure_years = sum(exposure_years, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_subjects = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(ae_events, by = "ARM") %>%
  mutate(
    EAIR = (total_ae_events / total_exposure_years) * 100,
    EAIR_text = sprintf("%.2f per 100 person-years", EAIR)
  )

print(eair_calc %>% select(ARM, EAIR_text))

# 3. SAE summary
sae_summary <- ae %>%
  filter(AESER == "Y") %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    n_sae_subjects = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    n_sae_events = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    ae %>% select(USUBJID, ARM) %>% distinct() %>%
      group_by(ARM) %>%
      summarise(N_total = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "ARM"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    `SAE subjects n (%)` = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", n_sae_subjects,
                                     100 * n_sae_subjects / N_total),
    `SAE rate` = sprintf("%.2f per 100 subjects", 100 * n_sae_events / N_total)
  ) %>%
  select(ARM, `SAE subjects n (%)`, `SAE rate`)

print(sae_summary)

# 4. Discontinuation due to AE
disc_ae <- ae %>%
  filter(AEACN == "DRUG DISCONTINUED") %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, ARM) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  ungroup()

disc_summary <- ae %>%
  select(USUBJID, ARM) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(N = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(
    disc_ae %>%
      group_by(ARM) %>%
      summarise(n_disc = n_distinct(USUBJID), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "ARM"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    n_disc = coalesce(n_disc, 0),
    `Discontinued n (%)` = sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", n_disc, 100 * n_disc / N)
  ) %>%
  select(ARM, `Discontinued n (%)`)

print(disc_summary)

cat("\nSafety Summary Complete:\n")
cat("- AE incidence by PT\n")
cat("- Exposure-adjusted event rates (EAIR)\n")
cat("- Serious adverse events\n")
cat("- Treatment discontinuations\n")

26.12.1 FDA Guidance

Report all AEs; highlight causality assessment
Use MedDRA coding for standardization
Stratify by severity grade and relationship

Highlight clinically significant patterns
Address known drug class effects

Quantify AE frequency and relationship
Provide time-to-onset analysis
Assess dose-response relationship
Compare safety profile to benchmark (historical controls, competitors)
Conduct subset analyses (age, gender, baseline risk factors)

26.13 Exercises

26.13.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 15.1: Load pharmaversesdtm::ae data. Create a publication-ready AE summary table showing the incidence of all AEs by SOC and PT, stratified by treatment arm. Format using gt or gtsummary.
Exercise 15.2: Calculate and report the Exposure-Adjusted Incidence Rate (EAIR) for AEs in each treatment arm using exposure data from pharmaversesdtm::ex. Interpret the EAIR values clinically.
Exercise 15.3: Create a shift table for laboratory values (e.g., hemoglobin: Normal → Low → High) from baseline to final visit. Discuss which shifts warrant clinical concern.


26.13.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 15.4: Implement a time-to-onset analysis for a specific AE (e.g., headache). Use Kaplan-Meier curves to compare onset timing between treatment arms. Estimate median time to onset and create a publication-quality plot.
Exercise 15.5: Conduct a safety signal detection analysis. Using the AE data, identify AEs where the incidence difference between treatment arms exceeds 5 percentage points. For each signal, stratify by patient subgroup (age, baseline risk) to assess consistency. Discuss which signals require further investigation or potential labeling.

title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.1 Introduction
Bioequivalence (BE) studies form the regulatory backbone for demonstrating that generic drugs are therapeutically equivalent to their reference (brand-name) counterparts. When a new drug developer wants to bring a generic version of an already-approved medicine to market, regulators (FDA, EMA, Health Canada) require pharmacokinetic (PK) evidence that the generic and reference formulations behave similarly in the human body. This is not a clinical efficacy trial—it is a targeted PK study measuring blood concentrations of the active pharmaceutical ingredient over time.
In this chapter, you will learn what bioequivalence means, why it matters, how to design and analyze BE studies, and how to implement the complete analysis in R. We will cover the regulatory framework, the mathematical foundations, and practical analysis with real data.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
27  Bioequivalence Studies

title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.2 16.1 What Is Bioequivalence?

27.2.1 Clinical Context
Imagine a patient taking a blood pressure medication (lisinopril) manufactured by Merck. The drug is absorbed into the bloodstream, reaches peak concentration (\(C_{max}\)), and is gradually eliminated. The total drug exposure is measured by the area under the concentration-time curve (AUC). After the patent expires, another manufacturer (e.g., Teva) wants to produce a generic lisinopril tablet. The question is: will the generic tablet deliver the same drug exposure as the Merck tablet?
Bioequivalence means that two formulations (test and reference) deliver the same amount of active drug to the systemic circulation at the same rate. More formally:

Average Bioequivalence (ABE): The mean pharmacokinetic parameters of the test and reference formulations are statistically similar.
Population Bioequivalence: The variability of PK parameters is also comparable.
Individual Bioequivalence: Individuals can be switched between formulations without loss of efficacy.

For most regulatory purposes, the standard is Average Bioequivalence.


27.2.2 Key PK Parameters
The main metrics in a bioequivalence study are:

\(C_{max}\): Peak (maximum) plasma concentration of drug
\(t_{max}\): Time to reach \(C_{max}\)
AUC₀₋ₜ: Area under curve from time 0 to last measured time
AUC₀₋∞: Area under curve from 0 to infinity (extrapolated)
\(t_{1/2}\): Half-life of the drug (elimination phase)

Among these, FDA and EMA guidances focus on \(C_{max}\) and AUC₀₋ₜ as the primary parameters.


27.2.3 The Regulatory Standard
Most regulatory agencies (FDA, EMA) define bioequivalence as:
\[\text{Bioequivalent if } 0.80 \leq \frac{GMR_{test}}{GMR_{ref}} \leq 1.25\]
where GMR is the geometric mean ratio. More precisely, we construct a 90% confidence interval (not 95%!) around the log-transformed ratio of geometric means. If this 90% CI falls entirely within [0.80, 1.25], bioequivalence is concluded.
Why 90% CI and not 95%? The FDA uses 90% CI to balance Type I and Type II errors; a 95% CI would be too conservative (harder to show BE) given that BE is a two-sided equivalence test.
Why log-transformation? PK parameters are typically log-normally distributed (right-skewed). Log-transformation stabilizes variance and makes them approximately normal, which is required for the statistical test.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
27  Bioequivalence Studies

title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.3 16.2 The Crossover Study Design

27.3.1 Two-Sequence, Two-Period (2x2) Crossover
The gold standard for bioequivalence studies is the 2×2 crossover design:

Period 1: Subject receives either Test (T) or Reference (R) formulation
Washout period: Typically 1–4 weeks (depends on drug half-life) to eliminate carryover
Period 2: Subject receives the opposite formulation

Example randomization: - 6 subjects: Sequence 1 (R → T) - 6 subjects: Sequence 2 (T → R)
Blood samples are collected at multiple time points (e.g., 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24, 48 hours).


27.3.2 Statistical Model
The mixed-effects model for BE data is:
\[Y_{ijk} = \mu + S_{ik} + P_j + F_{j,k} + C_{j,k} + \epsilon_{ijk}\]
where: - \(Y_{ijk}\) = log-transformed PK parameter (AUC or Cmax) for subject \(i\), sequence \(k\), period \(j\) - \(\mu\) = overall intercept - \(S_{ik}\) = fixed effect of sequence \(k\) nested in subject \(i\) (subject is random) - \(P_j\) = fixed effect of period \(j\) (period effect) - \(F_{j,k}\) = fixed effect of formulation (test vs reference) - \(C_{j,k}\) = fixed effect of carryover (sequence-specific carry-over effect) - \(\epsilon_{ijk}\) = residual error
The fixed effects of interest are \(F_{1,R}\) and \(F_{2,T}\), which represent deviations from the reference.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
27  Bioequivalence Studies

title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.4 16.3 Area Under the Curve (AUC) Calculation

27.4.1 The Trapezoidal Rule
AUC is rarely measured directly; instead, it is calculated from discrete plasma concentration measurements using numerical integration. The most common method is the trapezoidal rule:
\[AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(C_i + C_{i+1})}{2} \cdot (t_{i+1} - t_i)\]
where \(C_i\) and \(C_{i+1}\) are consecutive concentrations and \((t_{i+1} - t_i)\) is the time interval.
Example: If concentration measurements are: - \(t=0\): \(C=0\) - \(t=1\): \(C=50\) - \(t=2\): \(C=75\) - \(t=4\): \(C=40\)
Then: \[AUC = \frac{(0+50)}{2}(1-0) + \frac{(50+75)}{2}(2-1) + \frac{(75+40)}{2}(4-2) = 25 + 62.5 + 115 = 202.5\]


27.4.2 Extrapolation to Infinity
For AUC₀₋∞, we must extrapolate beyond the last measured concentration. This is typically done by fitting an exponential to the terminal (elimination) phase and using:
\[AUC_{0-\infty} = AUC_{0-t} + \frac{C_t}{\lambda_z}\]
where \(\lambda_z\) is the terminal elimination rate constant (estimated from the slope of log(C) vs time in the terminal phase).
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
27  Bioequivalence Studies

title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.5 16.4 The TOST Procedure

27.5.1 Two One-Sided Tests (TOST)
Bioequivalence is conventionally tested using the Two One-Sided Tests (TOST) procedure:
\[H_{01}: \frac{\theta_{test}}{\theta_{ref}} \leq 0.80 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_{a1}: \frac{\theta_{test}}{\theta_{ref}} > 0.80\]
\[H_{02}: \frac{\theta_{test}}{\theta_{ref}} \geq 1.25 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_{a2}: \frac{\theta_{test}}{\theta_{ref}} < 1.25\]
We perform both tests at \(\alpha = 0.05\). Bioequivalence is concluded only if both tests reject their null hypotheses.
Equivalently: Construct the 90% confidence interval for the log-ratio of geometric means. If the entire CI falls within \([\log(0.80), \log(1.25)] = [-0.223, 0.223]\), we conclude bioequivalence.


27.5.2 Critical t-Value
For a crossover with \(N\) subjects and \(df = N - 2\) (approximately, after accounting for sequence and period effects), the critical t-value is \(t_{0.05, df}\).
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
27  Bioequivalence Studies

title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.6 16.5 Power Calculation for BE Studies
Sample size in BE studies is typically computed to achieve 80% power. The formula is:
\[n = \frac{2 \cdot (z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2 \cdot CV^2}{(\log 1.25)^2}\]
where: - \(z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96\) (two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\)) - \(z_\beta = 0.84\) (80% power, so \(\beta = 0.20\)) - \(CV\) = coefficient of variation in log-scale (from prior studies or assumptions)
For example, if \(CV = 0.25\) (25% within-subject variability on log-scale):
\[n = \frac{2 \cdot (1.96 + 0.84)^2 \cdot (0.25)^2}{(0.223)^2} = \frac{2 \cdot 7.84 \cdot 0.0625}{0.0497} \approx 39 \text{ subjects}\]
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
27  Bioequivalence Studies

title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.7 16.6 Practical Analysis in R

27.7.1 Simulating a Bioequivalence Dataset
Let us create a realistic crossover BE study with 12 subjects, measuring AUC and Cmax for two formulations (Test and Reference).

set.seed(42)

# Simulate a 2x2 crossover bioequivalence study
# 12 subjects: 6 in each sequence

n_subjects <- 12
subjects <- rep(1:n_subjects, 2)  # each subject measured twice
period <- rep(c(1, 2), each = n_subjects)

# Define sequences (Sequence 1: R-T; Sequence 2: T-R)
sequence <- c(rep(1, 6), rep(2, 6))
sequence_rep <- rep(sequence, 2)

# Assign formulations based on sequence and period
formulation <- ifelse(
  (sequence_rep == 1 & period == 1) | (sequence_rep == 2 & period == 2),
  "Reference",
  "Test"
)

# True log-scale parameters
log_auc_ref <- log(500)   # Reference AUC mean in log scale
log_cmax_ref <- log(100)  # Reference Cmax mean in log scale

# Simulate AUC (subject effect + formulation effect + error)
subject_effect_auc <- rnorm(n_subjects, mean = 0, sd = 0.15)
formulation_effect <- ifelse(formulation == "Test", log(1.05), 0)  # 5% higher for Test
within_subject_var <- rnorm(n_subjects * 2, mean = 0, sd = 0.12)

log_auc <- log_auc_ref + rep(subject_effect_auc, 2) + formulation_effect + within_subject_var
auc <- exp(log_auc)

# Simulate Cmax similarly
subject_effect_cmax <- rnorm(n_subjects, mean = 0, sd = 0.18)
formulation_effect_cmax <- ifelse(formulation == "Test", log(1.08), 0)  # 8% higher for Test
within_subject_var_cmax <- rnorm(n_subjects * 2, mean = 0, sd = 0.15)

log_cmax <- log_cmax_ref + rep(subject_effect_cmax, 2) + formulation_effect_cmax + within_subject_var_cmax
cmax <- exp(log_cmax)

# Create dataset
be_data <- data.frame(
  subject = subjects,
  sequence = sequence_rep,
  period = period,
  formulation = formulation,
  auc = auc,
  cmax = cmax,
  log_auc = log_auc,
  log_cmax = log_cmax
)

head(be_data, 10)

#>    subject sequence period formulation      auc      cmax  log_auc log_cmax
#> 1        1        1      1   Reference 519.8751  81.38986 6.253589 4.399251
#> 2        2        1      1   Reference 444.2772  94.66591 6.096449 4.550354
#> 3        3        1      1   Reference 519.6101  67.95880 6.253079 4.218902
#> 4        4        1      1   Reference 593.3898  89.48727 6.385852 4.494096
#> 5        5        1      1   Reference 513.4425 131.44770 6.241138 4.878609
#> 6        6        1      1   Reference 357.7787 103.19442 5.879915 4.636615
#> 7        7        2      1        Test 491.4059 125.46975 6.197271 4.832065
#> 8        8        2      1        Test 606.4444  98.77129 6.407613 4.592807
#> 9        9        2      1        Test 684.9633  93.47873 6.529365 4.537734
#> 10      10        2      1        Test 419.9914 118.33427 6.040234 4.773513




27.7.2 Computing Geometric Means and Ratios

# Compute geometric means for each formulation
gmean_auc_ref <- exp(mean(be_data$log_auc[be_data$formulation == "Reference"]))
gmean_auc_test <- exp(mean(be_data$log_auc[be_data$formulation == "Test"]))

gmean_cmax_ref <- exp(mean(be_data$log_cmax[be_data$formulation == "Reference"]))
gmean_cmax_test <- exp(mean(be_data$log_cmax[be_data$formulation == "Test"]))

# Geometric mean ratios (Test/Reference)
gmr_auc <- gmean_auc_test / gmean_auc_ref
gmr_cmax <- gmean_cmax_test / gmean_cmax_ref

cat("AUC Geometric Means:\n")

#> AUC Geometric Means:

cat("  Reference:", round(gmean_auc_ref, 2), "\n")

#>   Reference: 539.48

cat("  Test:", round(gmean_auc_test, 2), "\n")

#>   Test: 571.17

cat("  GMR (Test/Ref):", round(gmr_auc, 4), "\n\n")

#>   GMR (Test/Ref): 1.0588

cat("Cmax Geometric Means:\n")

#> Cmax Geometric Means:

cat("  Reference:", round(gmean_cmax_ref, 2), "\n")

#>   Reference: 98.19

cat("  Test:", round(gmean_cmax_test, 2), "\n")

#>   Test: 102.06

cat("  GMR (Test/Ref):", round(gmr_cmax, 4), "\n")

#>   GMR (Test/Ref): 1.0395




27.7.3 Linear Model Analysis

# For AUC: fit mixed model using lm with sequence as fixed effect
# (In practice, use lme4::lmer for random subject effect)

# Fit linear model with fixed effects: sequence, period, formulation
# Log-transform the response

model_auc <- lm(log_auc ~ factor(sequence) + factor(period) + formulation + factor(subject),
                data = be_data)

summary(model_auc)

#> 
#> Call:
#> lm(formula = log_auc ~ factor(sequence) + factor(period) + formulation + 
#>     factor(subject), data = be_data)
#> 
#> Residuals:
#>      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
#> -0.20130 -0.05691  0.00000  0.05691  0.20130 
#> 
#> Coefficients: (1 not defined because of singularities)
#>                   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
#> (Intercept)        6.41693    0.12835  49.995 2.48e-13 ***
#> factor(sequence)2  0.07683    0.16805   0.457   0.6573    
#> factor(period)2    0.05910    0.06861   0.861   0.4092    
#> formulationTest    0.05709    0.06861   0.832   0.4247    
#> factor(subject)2  -0.36328    0.16805  -2.162   0.0559 .  
#> factor(subject)3  -0.20499    0.16805  -1.220   0.2505    
#> factor(subject)4  -0.20873    0.16805  -1.242   0.2426    
#> factor(subject)5  -0.16483    0.16805  -0.981   0.3498    
#> factor(subject)6  -0.44973    0.16805  -2.676   0.0232 *  
#> factor(subject)7  -0.20523    0.16805  -1.221   0.2500    
#> factor(subject)8  -0.20556    0.16805  -1.223   0.2493    
#> factor(subject)9   0.03361    0.16805   0.200   0.8455    
#> factor(subject)10 -0.46568    0.16805  -2.771   0.0198 *  
#> factor(subject)11 -0.09714    0.16805  -0.578   0.5760    
#> factor(subject)12       NA         NA      NA       NA    
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> Residual standard error: 0.1681 on 10 degrees of freedom
#> Multiple R-squared:  0.7283, Adjusted R-squared:  0.375 
#> F-statistic: 2.062 on 13 and 10 DF,  p-value: 0.1284

# Extract the log-scale difference between Test and Reference
# The coefficient for "formulationTest" represents log(Test) - log(Reference)
log_diff_auc <- coef(model_auc)["formulationTest"]
se_log_diff_auc <- sqrt(diag(vcov(model_auc)))["formulationTest"]

cat("\n=== AUC Bioequivalence Analysis ===\n")

#> 
#> === AUC Bioequivalence Analysis ===

cat("Log-scale difference (Test - Ref):", round(log_diff_auc, 4), "\n")

#> Log-scale difference (Test - Ref): 0.0571

cat("Standard Error:", round(se_log_diff_auc, 4), "\n")

#> Standard Error: 0.0686

# Compute 90% CI for the log-ratio
# Using t-distribution with residual df
df_residual <- df.residual(model_auc)
t_crit <- qt(0.95, df_residual)  # one-sided 90% = two-sided 80%?
# Actually, for 90% CI: use qt(0.95, df) for upper bound, qt(0.05, df) for lower
t_crit_90 <- qt(0.95, df_residual)

ci_lower_log <- log_diff_auc - t_crit_90 * se_log_diff_auc
ci_upper_log <- log_diff_auc + t_crit_90 * se_log_diff_auc

# Convert back to ratio scale
ci_lower_ratio <- exp(ci_lower_log)
ci_upper_ratio <- exp(ci_upper_log)

cat("\n90% CI for GMR (log-scale):",
    "[", round(ci_lower_log, 4), ", ", round(ci_upper_log, 4), "]\n")

#> 
#> 90% CI for GMR (log-scale): [ -0.0673 ,  0.1814 ]

cat("90% CI for GMR (ratio-scale):",
    "[", round(ci_lower_ratio, 4), ", ", round(ci_upper_ratio, 4), "]\n")

#> 90% CI for GMR (ratio-scale): [ 0.935 ,  1.1989 ]

# Check BE criteria
be_lower <- 0.80
be_upper <- 1.25

be_conclusion_auc <- (ci_lower_ratio >= be_lower) && (ci_upper_ratio <= be_upper)

cat("\nBE Criteria: [0.80, 1.25]\n")

#> 
#> BE Criteria: [0.80, 1.25]

cat("CI within BE range?", be_conclusion_auc, "\n")

#> CI within BE range? TRUE

if (isTRUE(be_conclusion_auc)) {
  cat("CONCLUSION: Bioequivalence ESTABLISHED for AUC\n")
} else {
  cat("CONCLUSION: Bioequivalence NOT established for AUC\n")
}

#> CONCLUSION: Bioequivalence ESTABLISHED for AUC




27.7.4 Repeating for Cmax

model_cmax <- lm(log_cmax ~ factor(sequence) + factor(period) + formulation + factor(subject),
                 data = be_data)

log_diff_cmax <- coef(model_cmax)["formulationTest"]
se_log_diff_cmax <- sqrt(diag(vcov(model_cmax)))["formulationTest"]

df_residual_cmax <- df.residual(model_cmax)
t_crit_cmax <- qt(0.95, df_residual_cmax)

ci_lower_log_cmax <- log_diff_cmax - t_crit_cmax * se_log_diff_cmax
ci_upper_log_cmax <- log_diff_cmax + t_crit_cmax * se_log_diff_cmax

ci_lower_ratio_cmax <- exp(ci_lower_log_cmax)
ci_upper_ratio_cmax <- exp(ci_upper_log_cmax)

cat("\n=== CMAX Bioequivalence Analysis ===\n")

#> 
#> === CMAX Bioequivalence Analysis ===

cat("Log-scale difference (Test - Ref):", round(log_diff_cmax, 4), "\n")

#> Log-scale difference (Test - Ref): 0.0387

cat("90% CI for GMR (ratio-scale):",
    "[", round(ci_lower_ratio_cmax, 4), ", ", round(ci_upper_ratio_cmax, 4), "]\n")

#> 90% CI for GMR (ratio-scale): [ 0.9478 ,  1.1399 ]

be_conclusion_cmax <- (ci_lower_ratio_cmax >= be_lower) && (ci_upper_ratio_cmax <= be_upper)

if (isTRUE(be_conclusion_cmax)) {
  cat("CONCLUSION: Bioequivalence ESTABLISHED for Cmax\n")
} else {
  cat("CONCLUSION: Bioequivalence NOT established for Cmax\n")
}

#> CONCLUSION: Bioequivalence ESTABLISHED for Cmax




27.7.5 Visualizing the Confidence Interval

library(ggplot2)

# Create a data frame for plotting
ci_data <- data.frame(
  parameter = c("AUC", "Cmax"),
  gmr = c(gmr_auc, gmr_cmax),
  ci_lower = c(ci_lower_ratio, ci_lower_ratio_cmax),
  ci_upper = c(ci_upper_ratio, ci_upper_ratio_cmax)
)

# Plot
ggplot(ci_data, aes(x = parameter, y = gmr)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper), width = 0.2, size = 1, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dotted", color = "red", size = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1.25, linetype = "dotted", color = "red", size = 1) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0.5, xmax = 2.5, ymin = 0.80, ymax = 1.25,
           alpha = 0.1, fill = "green", color = "green", linetype = "solid") +
  annotate("text", x = 2.6, y = 1.025, label = "BE Region", size = 4, color = "green") +
  labs(title = "Bioequivalence Confidence Interval Plot",
       subtitle = "90% CI for GMR (Test/Reference) with BE boundaries [0.80, 1.25]",
       x = "PK Parameter", y = "Geometric Mean Ratio (Test/Reference)",
       caption = "If CI falls entirely within [0.80, 1.25], bioequivalence is established.") +
  ylim(0.75, 1.35) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11))










27.7.6 Mean Plasma Concentration-Time Curves
For regulatory submissions, it is standard to show mean (or median) concentration-time curves for both formulations.

library(dplyr)

# Simulate concentration-time data for visualization
set.seed(42)

# Define sampling times (hours post-dose)
times <- c(0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24)

# For each subject and formulation, generate a PK profile
# Using a simple one-compartment model: C(t) = (Dose/Vd) * exp(-k*t)
# Dose = 500 mg, Vd varies by subject, k varies by formulation (slightly)

pk_profiles <- data.frame()

for (subj in 1:n_subjects) {
  for (form in c("Reference", "Test")) {
    # Slightly different absorption and elimination for Test
    k_ref <- 0.25   # elimination rate (1/hour)
    ka_ref <- 1.2   # absorption rate

    if (form == "Test") {
      ka <- ka_ref * 0.95  # slightly slower absorption
      k <- k_ref * 1.05    # slightly faster elimination
    } else {
      ka <- ka_ref
      k <- k_ref
    }

    # Simple model: delayed absorption then exponential decay
    Vd <- 50 + rnorm(1, 0, 5)
    dose <- 500
    Cmax_theo <- (dose / Vd) * (ka / (ka - k))

    for (t in times) {
      if (t == 0) {
        conc <- 0
      } else {
        conc <- (dose / Vd) * (ka / (ka - k)) * (exp(-k * t) - exp(-ka * t))
      }
      conc <- conc + rnorm(1, 0, conc * 0.05)  # 5% measurement error
      conc <- max(conc, 0)

      pk_profiles <- rbind(pk_profiles, data.frame(
        subject = subj,
        formulation = form,
        time = t,
        concentration = conc
      ))
    }
  }
}

# Compute mean concentration at each time point for each formulation
mean_pk <- pk_profiles %>%
  group_by(time, formulation) %>%
  summarise(mean_conc = mean(concentration), .groups = "drop")

# Plot
ggplot(pk_profiles, aes(x = time, y = concentration, color = formulation, group = interaction(subject, formulation))) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(data = mean_pk, aes(y = mean_conc, group = formulation),
             size = 3, shape = 19) +
  geom_line(data = mean_pk, aes(y = mean_conc, group = formulation),
            size = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Reference" = "steelblue", "Test" = "darkorange")) +
  labs(title = "Mean Plasma Concentration-Time Curves",
       subtitle = "Individual profiles (light lines) and mean profiles (bold lines with points)",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Plasma Concentration (ng/mL)",
       color = "Formulation") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "topright",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))

title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.8 16.7 Interpretation and Regulatory Reporting
A complete bioequivalence report should include:

Study Protocol: Design, population, washout period, sampling schedule
Demographic Table: Age, weight, renal/hepatic function (if relevant)
Analytical Method Validation: Selectivity, sensitivity, accuracy, precision, linearity
PK Parameter Calculations: AUC₀₋ₜ, AUC₀₋∞, Cmax, Tmax, t₁/₂ with descriptive statistics
Statistical Analysis: Linear model, geometric means, 90% CI, BE conclusion for primary parameters
Bioequivalence Summary Table:

Geometric mean (Test, Reference)
Geometric mean ratio
90% CI
BE conclusion (Yes/No)

Concentration-Time Curves: Individual and mean profiles
Safety Summary: Adverse events, laboratory changes
