title: 27  Bioequivalence Studies
text:
27.9 16.8 Exercises

27.9.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 16.1: Load the be_data dataset created above. Compute the within-subject coefficient of variation (CV) for log-AUC in each sequence. Interpret what this tells you about the consistency of drug absorption.

# Compute CV within each sequence for AUC
# CV = sqrt(exp(sd_log_auc)^2 - 1)

Exercise 16.2: Modify the simulated dataset to increase the formulation difference from 5% to 15% for AUC. Re-run the bioequivalence analysis. Does the study still show bioequivalence at the 0.80–1.25 acceptance range? Why or why not?
Exercise 16.3: Using the be_data, manually calculate the AUC for one subject using the trapezoidal rule from simulated concentration-time data. What is the difference between AUC₀₋₁₂ and AUC₀₋₂₄?


27.9.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 16.4: Write an R function that: - Takes as input: time vector, concentration vector - Calculates AUC using the trapezoidal rule - Estimates the terminal elimination rate constant (\(\lambda_z\)) using least-squares log-linear regression on the last 3–4 points - Computes AUC₀₋∞ using \(AUC_{0-\infty} = AUC_{0-t} + \frac{C_t}{\lambda_z}\) - Returns both AUCs and the elimination half-life
Test your function on the mean concentration-time curve for the Reference formulation.
Exercise 16.5: Perform a sample size calculation for a new BE study assuming: - Coefficient of variation (CV) = 30% on log-scale - Expected GMR = 1.02 (true bioequivalence) - Target power = 90% (not 80%) - Significance level \(\alpha = 0.05\)
How many subjects are required? Compare this to the standard 80% power scenario.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
27  Bioequivalence Studies

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.1 Introduction
When can you conclude that a new treatment is “good enough” even if it is not statistically superior to the standard? This is the fundamental question addressed by non-inferiority and equivalence trials. Unlike traditional superiority trials, which test whether a new drug beats a control, non-inferiority trials ask whether a new drug is not substantially worse than the standard. These designs are increasingly important in modern clinical research because they are often more realistic: new treatments may offer secondary benefits (fewer side effects, simpler dosing, lower cost) even if they do not improve the primary efficacy outcome.
In this chapter, you will learn the regulatory framework for non-inferiority trials, the statistical methodology, the critical role of the non-inferiority margin, and how to design and analyze these trials in R.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.2 17.1 Superiority vs. Non-Inferiority vs. Equivalence

28.2.1 Three Hypothesis Testing Paradigms
Superiority Trial: - \(H_0\): New treatment ≤ Control - \(H_1\): New treatment > Control - Objective: Prove the new treatment is better - Most clinical trials follow this paradigm
Non-Inferiority Trial: - \(H_0\): New treatment is substantially worse than Control (≤ Control − Δ) - \(H_1\): New treatment is not substantially worse (> Control − Δ) - Objective: Prove the new treatment is “good enough” - Common when the new treatment has other advantages (fewer side effects, better tolerability, lower cost)
Equivalence Trial: - \(H_0\): New treatment is substantially different from Control (outside the range [Control − Δ, Control + Δ]) - \(H_1\): New treatment is equivalent to Control (within [Control − Δ, Control + Δ]) - Objective: Prove the two treatments are essentially the same - Uncommon in practice; bioequivalence studies are a special case


28.2.2 Clinical Context Example
Imagine a new antibiotic for urinary tract infection (UTI). The standard treatment is a 7-day course of nitrofurantoin with a response rate of 82%. The new antibiotic is: - Taken orally for only 3 days - Causes fewer gastrointestinal side effects - Is potentially less expensive
The manufacturer wants to demonstrate that the 3-day regimen is “not substantially worse” than the 7-day standard, even if the response rate is slightly lower (e.g., 78%). This is a non-inferiority trial.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.3 17.2 The Non-Inferiority Margin (Δ)

28.3.1 Definition and Purpose
The non-inferiority margin \(\Delta\) is the largest clinically acceptable difference that we are willing to tolerate between the new and reference treatments. It defines the boundary of “non-inferiority.”
Formally: - Test treatment is non-inferior if: \(\theta_{test} - \theta_{ref} > -\Delta\) - Where \(\theta\) is the treatment effect (e.g., response rate, improvement in score) - For adverse events: \(\theta_{test} - \theta_{ref} < \Delta\) (we want test to be better or similar)


28.3.2 The Margin Must Be Clinically Justified
The margin is not a statistical choice; it is a clinical and regulatory judgment. The FDA’s guidance (ICH E10) recommends a two-step approach:

Step 1: Establish M1

Conduct a meta-analysis of placebo-controlled trials of the reference drug
Estimate the effect size of the reference drug vs. placebo: M1 = \(\theta_{ref} - \theta_{placebo}\)
Example: If nitrofurantoin shows 82% response vs. 50% placebo response, then M1 = 32% absolute difference

Step 2: Set Δ (the margin for non-inferiority)

The margin Δ ≤ M1 (cannot be larger than the known effect of active control)
Typically, Δ = M1 / 2 or Δ = (0.5 × M1) to preserve half of the active control effect
This ensures that rejecting non-inferiority means the new drug retains at least half the benefit over placebo
Example: If M1 = 32%, then Δ might be 16% absolute difference


The fraction (typically 50%) is called the M2 fraction or retention fraction.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.4 17.3 Statistical Framework for Non-Inferiority

28.4.1 Hypotheses and Test
For a continuous outcome (e.g., blood pressure reduction):
\[H_0: \mu_{test} - \mu_{ref} \leq -\Delta \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu_{test} - \mu_{ref} > -\Delta\]
This is a one-sided test at significance level \(\alpha = 0.025\) (corresponding to a 97.5% lower confidence bound, or equivalently, a 95% two-sided CI where the lower bound is compared to \(-\Delta\)).
Decision Rule (Confidence Interval Approach): - Construct the 95% confidence interval for \(\mu_{test} - \mu_{ref}\) - If the lower bound of the 95% CI > −Δ, reject \(H_0\) and conclude non-inferiority - Equivalently, if the lower bound > \(-\Delta\), the interval lies entirely above \(-\Delta\)


28.4.2 For Binary Outcomes (Response Rates)
Let \(p_{test}\) = response rate in test group, \(p_{ref}\) = response rate in reference group.
\[H_0: p_{test} - p_{ref} \leq -\Delta \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: p_{test} - p_{ref} > -\Delta\]
Using normal approximation: \[Z = \frac{(p_{test} - p_{ref}) + \Delta}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(p_{test} - p_{ref})}}\]
Reject \(H_0\) if \(Z > z_{\alpha}\) where \(z_{\alpha}\) is the one-sided critical value (e.g., \(z_{0.025} = 1.96\)).
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
section: 28.5 17.4 The Assay Sensitivity and Constancy Assumptions
text:
28.5 17.4 The Assay Sensitivity and Constancy Assumptions

28.5.1 Assay Sensitivity
Assay sensitivity is the ability of a trial to distinguish between an effective and ineffective treatment. It requires: - High-quality study conduct (proper randomization, blinding, follow-up) - Appropriate outcome measurement - Adequate sample size
If assay sensitivity is not established, the non-inferiority margin is meaningless.


28.5.2 Constancy Assumption
The constancy assumption states that the effect of the reference drug observed in the new trial is the same as the effect observed in historical trials (from which M1 was derived). This is critical because: - If the reference drug performs worse than expected in the current trial, the margin Δ should be reconsidered - If assay sensitivity is poor, we cannot assume the reference drug would have shown its historical effect
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
section: 28.6 17.5 Active Control Trials and Regulatory Context
text:
28.6 17.5 Active Control Trials and Regulatory Context
Non-inferiority trials are called active control trials because they compare against an active reference drug (not placebo). This design is ethical when: - Effective treatment already exists - Placebo would be unethical - Secondary benefits of the new treatment are compelling (e.g., fewer side effects)
However, regulators require strong evidence of the reference drug’s effect from prior data.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.7 17.6 Sample Size for Non-Inferiority Trials
The sample size formula for a non-inferiority trial comparing means is:
\[n = \frac{2(z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2 \sigma^2}{(\Delta - \delta)^2}\]
where: - \(z_\alpha = 1.645\) (one-sided test at \(\alpha = 0.025\)) - \(z_\beta\) = critical value for power (e.g., 0.84 for 80% power) - \(\sigma\) = standard deviation of the outcome - \(\Delta\) = non-inferiority margin (the maximum tolerable difference) - \(\delta\) = the true difference expected between treatments (ideally near 0 for non-inferiority)
Example: If \(\sigma = 10\), \(\Delta = 5\), \(\delta = 0\), power = 80%:
\[n = \frac{2(1.645 + 0.84)^2 \times 100}{25} = \frac{2 \times 6.12 \times 100}{25} \approx 49 \text{ per group}\]
For binary outcomes (response rates), the formula is:
\[n = \frac{(z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2 [p_{ref}(1-p_{ref}) + p_{test}(1-p_{test})]}{\Delta^2}\]
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.8 17.7 Switching from Non-Inferiority to Superiority
Some trials are designed to test both non-inferiority and superiority using a hierarchical testing strategy:

Primary Test: Is the new treatment non-inferior to the reference? (one-sided, α = 0.025)
Secondary Test (if primary succeeds): Is the new treatment superior to the reference? (one-sided, α = 0.025)

This is sometimes called a “non-inferiority gateway” design. Statistical significance in the primary test allows testing for superiority without inflating overall Type I error (due to the Bonferroni principle or pre-planned hierarchy).
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.9 17.8 Practical Analysis in R

28.9.1 Simulating a Non-Inferiority Trial for Antibiotic Response

library(tidyverse)
library(stats)

set.seed(123)

# Scenario: Non-inferiority trial of a new 3-day antibiotic vs. standard 7-day nitrofurantoin
# Primary endpoint: Microbiological cure rate at 4 weeks post-treatment
# Reference (7-day nitrofurantoin): 82% cure rate (from historical trials)
# Expected new treatment rate: 78% (4 percentage points lower)
# Non-inferiority margin: Δ = 10 percentage points
# Sample size: 120 per group

n_per_group <- 120
p_ref <- 0.82
p_test <- 0.78
delta_margin <- 0.10  # 10 percentage points

# Simulate binary responses (1 = cured, 0 = not cured)
set.seed(123)

# Reference group responses
response_ref <- rbinom(n_per_group, size = 1, prob = p_ref)

# Test group responses
response_test <- rbinom(n_per_group, size = 1, prob = p_test)

# Create dataset
trial_data <- data.frame(
  treatment = c(rep("Reference", n_per_group), rep("Test", n_per_group)),
  response = c(response_ref, response_test)
)

# Summary statistics
summary_table <- trial_data %>%
  group_by(treatment) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    cured = sum(response),
    not_cured = n() - sum(response),
    cure_rate = mean(response),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(summary_table)

#> # A tibble: 2 × 5
#>   treatment     n cured not_cured cure_rate
#>   <chr>     <int> <int>     <int>     <dbl>
#> 1 Reference   120    99        21     0.825
#> 2 Test        120    98        22     0.817




28.9.2 Computing the 95% Confidence Interval for the Difference

# Extract observed response rates
p_test_obs <- summary_table$cure_rate[summary_table$treatment == "Test"]
p_ref_obs <- summary_table$cure_rate[summary_table$treatment == "Reference"]

# Observed difference (Test - Reference)
diff_obs <- p_test_obs - p_ref_obs

cat("Observed response rates:\n")

#> Observed response rates:

cat("  Reference:", round(p_ref_obs, 4), "\n")

#>   Reference: 0.825

cat("  Test:", round(p_test_obs, 4), "\n")

#>   Test: 0.8167

cat("  Difference (Test - Ref):", round(diff_obs, 4), "\n\n")

#>   Difference (Test - Ref): -0.0083

# Standard error of the difference
# SE = sqrt(p_test*(1-p_test)/n_test + p_ref*(1-p_ref)/n_ref)
se_diff <- sqrt(
  (p_test_obs * (1 - p_test_obs) / n_per_group) +
  (p_ref_obs * (1 - p_ref_obs) / n_per_group)
)

cat("Standard Error of difference:", round(se_diff, 4), "\n\n")

#> Standard Error of difference: 0.0495

# 95% Confidence Interval
# For one-sided test (non-inferiority), we use the lower 95% bound
# Using normal approximation: CI = difference ± z_{0.025} * SE
# But for non-inferiority, we focus on the lower bound

z_alpha_025 <- qnorm(0.025, lower.tail = FALSE)  # = 1.96 for 95% CI

ci_lower <- diff_obs - z_alpha_025 * se_diff
ci_upper <- diff_obs + z_alpha_025 * se_diff

cat("95% Confidence Interval for difference:\n")

#> 95% Confidence Interval for difference:

cat("  [", round(ci_lower, 4), ", ", round(ci_upper, 4), "]\n\n")

#>   [ -0.1054 ,  0.0887 ]

# Non-inferiority decision
# Non-inferiority is established if lower bound > -Δ
cat("Non-Inferiority Margin (Δ):", delta_margin, "\n")

#> Non-Inferiority Margin (Δ): 0.1

cat("Lower bound of 95% CI:", round(ci_lower, 4), "\n")

#> Lower bound of 95% CI: -0.1054

cat("Is lower bound > -Δ?", ci_lower > -delta_margin, "\n")

#> Is lower bound > -Δ? FALSE

if (ci_lower > -delta_margin) {
  cat("\n*** CONCLUSION: NON-INFERIORITY ESTABLISHED ***\n")
  cat("The test treatment is not substantially worse than the reference treatment.\n")
} else {
  cat("\n*** CONCLUSION: NON-INFERIORITY NOT ESTABLISHED ***\n")
  cat("The test treatment may be substantially worse than the reference treatment.\n")
}

#> 
#> *** CONCLUSION: NON-INFERIORITY NOT ESTABLISHED ***
#> The test treatment may be substantially worse than the reference treatment.




28.9.3 Applying an Exact Binomial Method
For more rigorous inference with small samples or extreme response rates, use the Clopper-Pearson or Wilson score interval:

# Use binom::binom.confint for more robust CI
library(binom)

# Confidence interval for reference group
ci_ref <- binom.confint(
  x = summary_table$cured[summary_table$treatment == "Reference"],
  n = n_per_group,
  method = "wilson"
)

# Confidence interval for test group
ci_test <- binom.confint(
  x = summary_table$cured[summary_table$treatment == "Test"],
  n = n_per_group,
  method = "wilson"
)

cat("Wilson Score CI for Reference:", round(ci_ref$lower, 4), "to", round(ci_ref$upper, 4), "\n")

#> Wilson Score CI for Reference: 0.7472 to 0.8826

cat("Wilson Score CI for Test:", round(ci_test$lower, 4), "to", round(ci_test$upper, 4), "\n")

#> Wilson Score CI for Test: 0.738 to 0.8757

# For the difference, use the normal approximation as above (adequate for large n)
# Alternatively, Newcombe's method (implemented in binom package) can be used

# Newcombe's method
newcombe_ci <- binom.confint(
  x = c(summary_table$cured[2], summary_table$cured[1]),
  n = c(n_per_group, n_per_group),
  method = "exact"
)

cat("\nUsing exact binomial methods for individual proportions:\n")

#> 
#> Using exact binomial methods for individual proportions:

print(newcombe_ci)

#>   method  x   n      mean     lower     upper
#> 1  exact 98 120 0.8166667 0.7356934 0.8813936
#> 2  exact 99 120 0.8250000 0.7450200 0.8882857




28.9.4 Visualization: The Non-Inferiority Margin Plot

# Create a visual representation of the non-inferiority result

ni_plot_data <- data.frame(
  treatment = c("Test - Reference"),
  difference = diff_obs,
  ci_lower = ci_lower,
  ci_upper = ci_upper,
  margin = -delta_margin
)

ggplot(ni_plot_data, aes(x = treatment, y = difference)) +
  geom_point(size = 4, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper), width = 0.2, size = 1.2, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60", size = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -delta_margin, linetype = "solid", color = "red", size = 1.2,
             label = "Non-Inferiority Margin") +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0.6, xmax = 1.4, ymin = -delta_margin, ymax = 0.05,
           alpha = 0.15, fill = "green") +
  annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = -0.05, label = "Non-Inferiority\nZone", size = 3.5, color = "green") +
  labs(
    title = "Non-Inferiority Trial: 95% CI for Difference (Test - Reference)",
    subtitle = paste("Margin Δ =", delta_margin * 100, "percentage points"),
    x = "Comparison", y = "Difference in Cure Rate",
    caption = "Non-inferiority is established if the lower bound of the CI > -Δ (red line)"
  ) +
  ylim(-0.15, 0.05) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10)
  )










28.9.5 Conducting a Formal Non-Inferiority Test

# One-sided test: H_0: p_test - p_ref ≤ -Δ vs H_1: p_test - p_ref > -Δ
# Test statistic: Z = (diff_obs + Δ) / SE
# Reject H_0 if Z > z_{0.025} = 1.96

z_stat <- (diff_obs + delta_margin) / se_diff
p_value_ni <- 1 - pnorm(z_stat)  # one-sided upper tail

cat("=== Non-Inferiority Test ===\n")

#> === Non-Inferiority Test ===

cat("Test Statistic (Z):", round(z_stat, 4), "\n")

#> Test Statistic (Z): 1.8516

cat("P-value (one-sided):", round(p_value_ni, 4), "\n")

#> P-value (one-sided): 0.032

cat("Significance level (α):", 0.025, "\n")

#> Significance level (α): 0.025

cat("Result:", ifelse(p_value_ni < 0.025, "REJECT H_0", "FAIL TO REJECT H_0"), "\n")

#> Result: FAIL TO REJECT H_0

cat("Conclusion:", ifelse(p_value_ni < 0.025,
                          "Non-inferiority ESTABLISHED (p < 0.025)",
                          "Non-inferiority NOT established (p ≥ 0.025)"), "\n")

#> Conclusion: Non-inferiority NOT established (p ≥ 0.025)




28.9.6 Extended Analysis: Sensitivity to Margin Choice
What if the margin had been set differently? Let us explore:

# Vary the non-inferiority margin and check conclusions

margins <- seq(0.02, 0.15, by = 0.01)
ni_results <- data.frame()

for (delta in margins) {
  z_test <- (diff_obs + delta) / se_diff
  p_val <- 1 - pnorm(z_test)
  ci_low <- diff_obs - z_alpha_025 * se_diff

  ni_results <- rbind(ni_results, data.frame(
    margin = delta * 100,
    ci_lower = ci_low * 100,
    z_statistic = z_test,
    p_value = p_val,
    ni_established = p_val < 0.025
  ))
}

print(ni_results)

#>    margin  ci_lower z_statistic     p_value ni_established
#> 1       2 -10.53626   0.2356633 0.406846976          FALSE
#> 2       3 -10.53626   0.4376604 0.330816236          FALSE
#> 3       4 -10.53626   0.6396575 0.261197638          FALSE
#> 4       5 -10.53626   0.8416546 0.199990649          FALSE
#> 5       6 -10.53626   1.0436517 0.148323271          FALSE
#> 6       7 -10.53626   1.2456489 0.106446668          FALSE
#> 7       8 -10.53626   1.4476460 0.073858043          FALSE
#> 8       9 -10.53626   1.6496431 0.049507978          FALSE
#> 9      10 -10.53626   1.8516402 0.032038753          FALSE
#> 10     11 -10.53626   2.0536373 0.020005404           TRUE
#> 11     12 -10.53626   2.2556344 0.012046768           TRUE
#> 12     13 -10.53626   2.4576315 0.006992829           TRUE
#> 13     14 -10.53626   2.6596287 0.003911342           TRUE
#> 14     15 -10.53626   2.8616258 0.002107371           TRUE

# Plot: How does NI conclusion change with margin?
ggplot(ni_results, aes(x = margin, y = ci_lower)) +
  geom_line(size = 1, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(size = 2, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_abline(aes(slope = -1, intercept = 0), linetype = "dashed", color = "red", size = 0.9) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 15, ymin = -100, ymax = 0,
           alpha = 0.1, fill = "green") +
  annotate("text", x = 7.5, y = -2, label = "NI Region", size = 3.5, color = "darkgreen") +
  labs(
    title = "Sensitivity Analysis: Lower CI Bound vs. Non-Inferiority Margin",
    x = "Non-Inferiority Margin (percentage points)",
    y = "Lower 95% CI Bound (percentage points)",
    caption = "Non-inferiority is established where the line exceeds the diagonal (margin = -CI lower bound)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.10 17.9 Equivalence Trials (Two-Sided Non-Inferiority)
An equivalence trial tests whether two treatments are similar in both directions:
\[H_0: |\theta_{test} - \theta_{ref}| \geq \Delta \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: |\theta_{test} - \theta_{ref}| < \Delta\]
This requires both of the following to be true: 1. Lower bound of 95% CI > \(-\Delta\) (non-inferiority) 2. Upper bound of 95% CI < \(+\Delta\) (reverse non-inferiority / superiority cap)

# Equivalence test using the same data

delta_lower <- -delta_margin
delta_upper <- delta_margin

equivalence <- (ci_lower > delta_lower) && (ci_upper < delta_upper)

cat("=== Equivalence Test ===\n")

#> === Equivalence Test ===

cat("Equivalence bounds: [", delta_lower * 100, "%,", delta_upper * 100, "%]\n")

#> Equivalence bounds: [ -10 %, 10 %]

cat("95% CI for difference: [", round(ci_lower * 100, 2), "%,", round(ci_upper * 100, 2), "%]\n")

#> 95% CI for difference: [ -10.54 %, 8.87 %]

cat("Is entire CI within bounds?", equivalence, "\n")

#> Is entire CI within bounds? FALSE

if (equivalence) {
  cat("\n*** CONCLUSION: EQUIVALENCE ESTABLISHED ***\n")
} else {
  cat("\n*** CONCLUSION: EQUIVALENCE NOT ESTABLISHED ***\n")
}

#> 
#> *** CONCLUSION: EQUIVALENCE NOT ESTABLISHED ***
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials
text:
28.11 17.10 Exercises

28.11.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 17.1: Suppose a non-inferiority trial of a new blood pressure medication vs. standard treatment found: - Reference (standard): mean reduction of 15 mmHg, SD = 8 - Test (new): mean reduction of 13 mmHg, SD = 8 - Sample size: 100 per group - Non-inferiority margin: 5 mmHg
Calculate the 95% CI for the difference and determine whether non-inferiority is established.
Exercise 17.2: Explain in clinical terms why the non-inferiority margin in the antibiotic trial was set at 10 percentage points. What historical information would you need to justify this choice?
Exercise 17.3: A drug company claims their new treatment is “not inferior” to the standard treatment based on a trial with a p-value of 0.06 (for one-sided non-inferiority test). Comment on this claim. What additional information would you need?


28.11.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 17.4: Using the antibiotic trial dataset, conduct a post-hoc analysis to estimate what margin Δ would have been required for the trial to show non-inferiority. This is called the “boundary margin” or “critical margin.” (Hint: Solve for Δ such that the lower CI bound equals −Δ.)
Exercise 17.5: Design a new non-inferiority trial for a hypothetical treatment. Specify: - The clinical outcome (continuous or binary) - The reference drug and its expected effect (from literature) - The justification for M1 (effect vs. placebo) and the chosen margin Δ - The expected treatment difference (δ) - The required sample size for 80% power
Show your calculations in R and create a power curve (varying n on x-axis, power on y-axis).

28  Non-Inferiority and Equivalence Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.1 Introduction
For over a century, clinical trials have been analyzed using frequentist statistics—computing p-values, confidence intervals, and power, all conditional on the null hypothesis being true. But frequentist inference cannot directly answer the question clinicians really want to know: Given my data, what is the probability that my drug actually works?
Bayesian methods answer this question directly. They combine prior knowledge with observed data to compute the posterior probability that a hypothesis is true. This chapter introduces Bayesian thinking, explains Bayes’ theorem, covers conjugate models, introduces MCMC for complex problems, and demonstrates how to implement Bayesian clinical trial analyses in R.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.2 18.1 Frequentist vs. Bayesian Philosophy

29.2.1 The Fundamental Difference
Frequentist inference: - Parameters are fixed but unknown - Data are random; imagine repeated sampling under the same conditions - P-value = Probability of seeing data as or more extreme than observed, if the null hypothesis were true - Cannot say “probability the drug works” — only “probability of data given null”
Bayesian inference: - Parameters are random (follow a probability distribution) - Data are fixed (what we observe) - Posterior = Probability of the parameter given the observed data - Can directly answer: “What is P(drug is effective | my data)?”


29.2.2 A Concrete Example
A clinical trial shows a new pain medication reduces pain score by 3 points vs. placebo. A frequentist might report: “p = 0.04; we reject the null hypothesis at the 0.05 level.”
A Bayesian might report: “Given our prior knowledge about similar drugs and the data from this trial, there is an 87% probability the true pain reduction is greater than 2 points (clinically meaningful).”
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.3 18.2 Bayes’ Theorem

29.3.1 The Foundation
Bayes’ theorem expresses how to update beliefs in light of new evidence:
\[P(\theta | \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} | \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}\]
Breaking this down:

\(P(\theta | \text{data})\) = Posterior: Probability of the parameter (e.g., treatment effect) given the observed data. This is what we want.
\(P(\text{data} | \theta)\) = Likelihood: Probability of observing the data under a specific parameter value. This comes from the statistical model (e.g., binomial, normal).
\(P(\theta)\) = Prior: Probability distribution for the parameter before seeing the data. This encodes prior knowledge or assumptions.
\(P(\text{data})\) = Marginal likelihood or evidence: Probability of the data averaging over all possible parameter values. This is a normalizing constant.



29.3.2 Simplified Form
Often written as:
\[\text{Posterior} \propto \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Prior}\]
The proportionality symbol (∝) reminds us that the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior, up to a normalizing constant.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.4 18.3 Prior Distributions

29.4.1 Three Types of Priors
1. Informative Priors - Incorporate substantial prior knowledge - Example: Beta(10, 2) for a treatment response rate, based on historical data showing typically high response - Advantages: More efficient (smaller sample size), reflects real knowledge - Disadvantages: Subjective, can be controversial
2. Weakly Informative Priors - Provide minimal constraint but prevent extreme values - Example: Normal(0, 10) for a treatment effect (mean 0, SD 10) - Advantages: Balance between objectivity and efficiency - Disadvantages: Still involves some choice
3. Non-Informative (Flat) Priors - Assign equal probability to all parameter values - Example: Uniform(0, 1) for a response rate - Advantages: Aim for “objectivity” (letting data dominate) - Disadvantages: Actually still impose assumptions; can lead to improper posteriors


29.4.2 The Beta Distribution for Proportions
For binary outcomes (response = yes/no), the Beta distribution is natural:
\[\theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\]
where \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are shape parameters: - Mean: \(\frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}\) - Variance: \(\frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}\)
Interpretation: - \(\alpha\) = number of “successes” (pseudo-observations) - \(\beta\) = number of “failures” (pseudo-observations)
Examples: - Beta(1, 1): Uniform prior (non-informative) - Beta(5, 5): Centered at 0.5, moderately informative - Beta(10, 2): High response rate expected (mean = 10/12 ≈ 0.83) - Beta(0.5, 0.5): U-shaped, bimodal prior (rare)
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.5 18.4 Conjugate Priors: Beta-Binomial Model

29.5.1 The Conjugate Family
When the prior and likelihood are conjugate, the posterior has a closed-form solution (no MCMC needed). The Beta-Binomial model is the simplest:

Prior: \(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\)
Likelihood: \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, \theta)\) where \(X\) = number of successes
Posterior: \(\theta | X \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha + X, \beta + n - X)\)



29.5.2 Why This Is Elegant
The posterior is also Beta with updated shape parameters: - New successes: \(\alpha + X\) - New failures: \(\beta + n - X\)
Updating is intuitive: add observed successes to \(\alpha\), add observed failures to \(\beta\).


29.5.3 Interpretation of Posterior
Once we have the posterior Beta distribution, we can compute: - Posterior mean: \(\frac{\alpha + X}{\alpha + \beta + n}\) (estimates the true response rate) - Posterior credible interval: Quantiles of the Beta distribution (e.g., 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles) - Probability of clinical benefit: \(P(\theta > \theta_0 | X)\) where \(\theta_0\) is a threshold (e.g., 0.50 response rate)
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.6 18.5 The Normal-Normal Model
For continuous outcomes, when both the prior and likelihood are normal:

Prior: \(\mu \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_0, \tau_0^2)\)
Likelihood: \(X_i \sim \text{Normal}(\mu, \sigma^2)\) (known variance \(\sigma^2\))
Posterior: \(\mu | X \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_1, \tau_1^2)\)

where: \[\mu_1 = \frac{\mu_0 / \tau_0^2 + \sum X_i / \sigma^2}{1/\tau_0^2 + n/\sigma^2}, \quad \tau_1^{-2} = \tau_0^{-2} + \frac{n}{\sigma^2}\]
The posterior mean is a weighted average of the prior mean and sample mean, with weights reflecting precision (inverse variance).
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.7 18.6 Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)

29.7.1 When Conjugacy Fails
Many realistic clinical trial models (hierarchical models, regression with multiple covariates, mixture models) do not have conjugate priors. For these, we use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC).


29.7.2 Conceptual Overview
MCMC generates a sequence of posterior samples by: 1. Starting at an initial parameter value 2. Proposing a new value 3. Accepting or rejecting based on the ratio of posterior probabilities 4. Repeating thousands of times to build a representative sample from the posterior
The sequence forms a “chain” whose long-run empirical distribution approximates the true posterior.


29.7.3 Popular MCMC Algorithms

Metropolis-Hastings: Symmetric proposal distribution
Gibbs Sampling: Special case for models with conjugate conditionals
Hamiltonian MC (HMC): Uses gradient information; very efficient but complex
No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS): Adaptive HMC; implemented in Stan and rstanarm
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.8 18.7 Convergence Diagnostics
After running MCMC, we must verify that the chain has converged to the posterior.

29.8.1 Trace Plots
A trace plot shows the parameter value at each iteration. Good traces look like “white noise” (no trends or long excursions).


29.8.2 The Gelman-Rubin Statistic (R-hat)
Run multiple MCMC chains (e.g., 4) in parallel. The R-hat compares within-chain and between-chain variance:
\[\hat{R} = \sqrt{\frac{\text{Var}_{\text{between}}}{\text{Var}_{\text{within}}}}\]

\(\hat{R} \approx 1.00\) = chains have converged (chains indistinguishable)
\(\hat{R} > 1.05\) = convergence questionable; run longer
\(\hat{R} > 1.10\) = convergence problematic; reassess model or priors
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.9 18.8 Credible Intervals vs. Confidence Intervals

Credible Interval (Bayesian): Range of parameter values containing 95% of the posterior probability mass. Interpretation: “Given the data, there is a 95% probability the true parameter lies in this interval.”
Confidence Interval (Frequentist): Constructed so that 95% of hypothetical repeated samples would produce an interval containing the true parameter. Interpretation: “If we repeated the study many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true value.”

Credible intervals are more intuitive for clinicians but require specification of prior.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.10 18.9 Practical Analysis in R

29.10.1 Example 1: Phase II Oncology Trial (Beta-Binomial Analysis)
A Phase II trial of a new immunotherapy evaluates response rate. Historical data suggest prior response rates in this cancer type range from 20% to 50%.

library(tidyverse)

# Data: 12 out of 30 patients responded
n_patients <- 30
n_responses <- 12

# Prior: Beta(2, 8) — represents belief that response rate is around 20-30%
# (mean = 2/(2+8) = 0.20; concentrated on lower rates)
alpha_prior <- 2
beta_prior <- 8

# Posterior: Beta(2 + 12, 8 + 30 - 12) = Beta(14, 26)
alpha_post <- alpha_prior + n_responses
beta_post <- beta_prior + (n_patients - n_responses)

cat("=== Phase II Oncology Trial: Bayesian Response Rate Analysis ===\n\n")

#> === Phase II Oncology Trial: Bayesian Response Rate Analysis ===

cat("Prior: Beta(", alpha_prior, ",", beta_prior, ")\n")

#> Prior: Beta( 2 , 8 )

cat("Prior mean:", round(alpha_prior / (alpha_prior + beta_prior), 3), "\n\n")

#> Prior mean: 0.2

cat("Data: ", n_responses, " responses out of ", n_patients, " patients\n\n")

#> Data:  12  responses out of  30  patients

cat("Posterior: Beta(", alpha_post, ",", beta_post, ")\n")

#> Posterior: Beta( 14 , 26 )

# Posterior mean (best point estimate)
post_mean <- alpha_post / (alpha_post + beta_post)
cat("Posterior mean:", round(post_mean, 3), "\n")

#> Posterior mean: 0.35

# Posterior standard deviation
post_sd <- sqrt((alpha_post * beta_post) / ((alpha_post + beta_post)^2 * (alpha_post + beta_post + 1)))
cat("Posterior SD:", round(post_sd, 3), "\n\n")

#> Posterior SD: 0.074

# Posterior credible interval (95%)
ci_lower <- qbeta(0.025, alpha_post, beta_post)
ci_upper <- qbeta(0.975, alpha_post, beta_post)

cat("95% Credible Interval: [", round(ci_lower, 3), ", ", round(ci_upper, 3), "]\n\n")

#> 95% Credible Interval: [ 0.212 ,  0.502 ]

# Probability of clinically relevant response (e.g., >30%)
prob_clinical_benefit <- 1 - pbeta(0.30, alpha_post, beta_post)
cat("P(response rate > 30% | data):", round(prob_clinical_benefit, 3), "\n")

#> P(response rate > 30% | data): 0.74




29.10.2 Visualizing Prior, Likelihood, and Posterior

# Create a range of theta values
theta <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.01)

# Prior density
prior_density <- dbeta(theta, alpha_prior, beta_prior)

# Likelihood (proportional to Binomial pmf)
# L(theta) ∝ theta^12 * (1-theta)^18
likelihood_density <- theta^n_responses * (1 - theta)^(n_patients - n_responses)
# Normalize for plotting
likelihood_density <- likelihood_density / max(likelihood_density) * max(prior_density)

# Posterior density
posterior_density <- dbeta(theta, alpha_post, beta_post)

# Create data frame for plotting
density_df <- data.frame(
  theta = rep(theta, 3),
  density = c(prior_density, likelihood_density, posterior_density),
  component = rep(c("Prior", "Likelihood (scaled)", "Posterior"), each = length(theta))
)

ggplot(density_df, aes(x = theta, y = density, fill = component, color = component)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3, outline.type = "full") +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Prior" = "blue", "Likelihood (scaled)" = "orange", "Posterior" = "red")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Prior" = "blue", "Likelihood (scaled)" = "orange", "Posterior" = "red")) +
  annotate("vline", xintercept = post_mean, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", size = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = post_mean + 0.05, y = max(posterior_density) * 0.9,
           label = paste("Posterior Mean =", round(post_mean, 3)), size = 4) +
  labs(
    title = "Bayesian Analysis: Prior × Likelihood → Posterior",
    subtitle = "Phase II Oncology Trial Response Rate",
    x = "Response Rate (θ)", y = "Density",
    caption = "The posterior (red) represents the updated belief after observing 12/30 responses."
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    legend.position = "topright"
  )







# Plot just the posterior with credible interval
posterior_plot_df <- data.frame(theta = theta, density = posterior_density)

ggplot(posterior_plot_df, aes(x = theta, y = density)) +
  geom_area(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", size = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ci_lower, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", size = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ci_upper, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", size = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = post_mean, linetype = "solid", color = "darkblue", size = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = post_mean, y = max(posterior_density) * 0.85,
           label = paste("Mean:", round(post_mean, 3)), size = 4, color = "darkblue") +
  annotate("text", x = ci_lower, y = max(posterior_density) * 0.70,
           label = round(ci_lower, 3), size = 3.5, color = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = ci_upper, y = max(posterior_density) * 0.70,
           label = round(ci_upper, 3), size = 3.5, color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Posterior Distribution of Response Rate",
    subtitle = paste("95% Credible Interval: [", round(ci_lower, 3), ", ", round(ci_upper, 3), "]"),
    x = "Response Rate (θ)", y = "Posterior Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))










29.10.3 Example 2: Bayesian Logistic Regression with rstanarm
For more complex models (e.g., multiple covariates, hierarchical structures), we use MCMC. The rstanarm package provides a user-friendly interface to Stan.

if (requireNamespace("rstanarm", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(rstanarm)
}

# Create a simulated clinical trial dataset
# Outcome: response (1) vs. no response (0)
# Predictor 1: treatment group (0 = control, 1 = intervention)
# Predictor 2: baseline severity score (0-100)

set.seed(42)

n_total <- 200
treatment <- rep(c(0, 1), each = n_total / 2)
baseline_severity <- rnorm(n_total, mean = 50, sd = 15)

# True model: log-odds = -2 + 1.5 * treatment - 0.02 * baseline_severity
true_intercept <- -2
true_treatment_effect <- 1.5
true_severity_effect <- -0.02

linear_predictor <- true_intercept + true_treatment_effect * treatment + true_severity_effect * baseline_severity
response_prob <- plogis(linear_predictor)  # inverse logit
response <- rbinom(n_total, size = 1, prob = response_prob)

clinical_data <- data.frame(
  response = response,
  treatment = factor(treatment, labels = c("Control", "Intervention")),
  baseline_severity = baseline_severity
)

# Fit Bayesian logistic regression using rstanarm
# Priors:
# - intercept: Normal(0, 2.5) — weakly informative
# - coefficients: Normal(0, 1) — centered at 0, weak effect
# - uses QR decomposition for stability

bayesian_logistic <- stan_glm(
  response ~ treatment + baseline_severity,
  data = clinical_data,
  family = binomial(link = "logit"),
  prior = normal(location = 0, scale = 1),
  prior_intercept = normal(location = 0, scale = 2.5),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,  # 2000 iterations per chain
  warmup = 1000,  # first 1000 are burn-in
  refresh = 0  # suppress iteration printing
)

# Summarize results
cat("=== Bayesian Logistic Regression Results ===\n")
summary(bayesian_logistic)



29.10.4 Extracting and Visualizing Posterior Samples

# Extract posterior samples for treatment effect
posterior_samples <- as.data.frame(bayesian_logistic)

# Transform log-odds to odds ratio
posterior_odds_ratio <- exp(posterior_samples$treatmentIntervention)

cat("Posterior Summary for Treatment Effect (Odds Ratio):\n")
cat("  Mean:", round(mean(posterior_odds_ratio), 3), "\n")
cat("  Median:", round(median(posterior_odds_ratio), 3), "\n")
cat("  95% Credible Interval: [",
    round(quantile(posterior_odds_ratio, 0.025), 3), ", ",
    round(quantile(posterior_odds_ratio, 0.975), 3), "]\n\n")

# Probability that treatment is beneficial (OR > 1)
prob_beneficial <- mean(posterior_odds_ratio > 1)
cat("P(OR > 1 | data) = P(treatment beneficial) =", round(prob_beneficial, 3), "\n\n")

# Visualize posterior distribution
posterior_or_df <- data.frame(odds_ratio = posterior_odds_ratio)

ggplot(posterior_or_df, aes(x = odds_ratio)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7, color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", size = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(posterior_odds_ratio), linetype = "solid", color = "darkblue", size = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = mean(posterior_odds_ratio) + 0.15, y = max(table(round(posterior_odds_ratio, 2)))/2,
           label = paste("Mean OR =", round(mean(posterior_odds_ratio), 3)), size = 4) +
  labs(
    title = "Posterior Distribution of Treatment Odds Ratio",
    subtitle = "Bayesian Logistic Regression: Intervention vs. Control",
    x = "Odds Ratio (Intervention / Control)",
    y = "Count (MCMC Samples)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))



29.10.5 Convergence Diagnostics

# Check R-hat (Gelman-Rubin statistic)
cat("R-hat values (should be < 1.05 for convergence):\n")
print(bayesian_logistic$diagnostics)

# Trace plots for visual inspection
plot(bayesian_logistic, ask = FALSE)
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.11 18.10 Bayesian Clinical Trial Designs

29.11.1 Interim Analyses and Predictive Probability
Bayesian designs enable adaptive trials with interim analyses. At an interim look:

Compute posterior probability of efficacy based on observed data
Compute predictive probability: P(efficacy at final analysis | current data)
Possible decisions: stop for efficacy, stop for futility, continue enrollment



29.11.2 Example: Predictive Probability Stopping Rule
Stop for efficacy if P(future response rate > 40% | current data) > 90%.

# Simple illustration: predict response rate at final analysis
# Current data: 8 responses out of 20 patients
# Assume 40 more patients will be enrolled

n_current <- 20
r_current <- 8
n_future <- 40

# Posterior after current data (conjugate Beta update)
alpha_current <- 1 + r_current
beta_current <- 1 + (n_current - r_current)

# Prior for future patients is the current posterior
# Predictive distribution: what response rates could we observe in future patients?

# Simulate: draw 10,000 posterior samples of theta, then for each, simulate future responses
set.seed(42)

posterior_theta_samples <- rbeta(10000, alpha_current, beta_current)
simulated_future_responses <- rbinom(10000, size = n_future, prob = posterior_theta_samples)

# Final response rate if we continue and see median future responses
median_future <- median(simulated_future_responses)
total_future_responses <- r_current + simulated_future_responses
total_patients <- n_current + n_future
final_response_rates <- total_future_responses / total_patients

# Predictive probability of >40% response at final analysis
pred_prob_efficacy <- mean(final_response_rates > 0.40)

cat("Interim Analysis Predictive Probability:\n")

#> Interim Analysis Predictive Probability:

cat("  Current data: ", r_current, "/", n_current, " responses\n")

#>   Current data:  8 / 20  responses

cat("  Projected: ", median_future, " additional responses in next ", n_future, " patients\n")

#>   Projected:  16  additional responses in next  40  patients

cat("  Projected final response rate: ", round(median(final_response_rates), 3), "\n")

#>   Projected final response rate:  0.4

cat("  P(final response rate > 40% | current data, continued enrollment) =", round(pred_prob_efficacy, 3), "\n\n")

#>   P(final response rate > 40% | current data, continued enrollment) = 0.476

if (isTRUE(pred_prob_efficacy > 0.90)) {
  cat("DECISION: Stop for efficacy (predictive probability > 90%)\n")
} else if (isTRUE(pred_prob_efficacy < 0.10)) {
  cat("DECISION: Stop for futility (predictive probability < 10%)\n")
} else {
  cat("DECISION: Continue enrollment\n")
}

#> DECISION: Continue enrollment

29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials
text:
29.12 18.11 Exercises

29.12.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 18.1: A historical study of a pain medication found a response rate of 55% (95% CI: 45%–65%). Based on this, specify a Beta prior distribution for the response rate in a new trial. (Hint: match the mean and approximate the 95% CI range.)
Exercise 18.2: In a new Phase II trial of a cancer drug, 7 out of 20 patients respond. Using a uniform prior Beta(1,1), compute: - Posterior mean response rate - 95% credible interval - Probability that response rate exceeds 25%
Exercise 18.3: Explain in plain language why the posterior is a weighted average of the prior and the data in the Normal-Normal model. Which weight (prior vs. data) dominates when the prior variance is very large?


29.12.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 18.4: Using the bayesian_logistic model from Example 2, extract the posterior samples for the baseline_severity effect. Compute: - Posterior mean and 95% credible interval for the log-odds ratio - Posterior probability that increasing baseline severity by 10 points decreases the log-odds of response by at least 0.15 - Create a trace plot and verify convergence (R-hat < 1.05)
Exercise 18.5: Design a Bayesian adaptive trial for a Phase IIb antihypertensive drug: - Primary endpoint: systolic blood pressure reduction at 12 weeks - Interim analysis at 50 patients, final analysis at 100 patients - Efficacy stopping rule: If P(mean reduction > 10 mmHg | interim data) > 85%, stop and declare efficacy - Futility stopping rule: If P(mean reduction > 10 mmHg | interim data) < 20%, stop for futility
Propose informative priors based on historical trials and sketch a decision tree for the adaptive design.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
29  Bayesian Methods in Clinical Trials

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
text:
30.1 Introduction
Randomized controlled trials are the gold standard for assessing drug efficacy. Yet most clinical decisions in everyday practice are made on observational data: electronic health records, patient registries, administrative claims, and patient-reported outcomes. The question is: how can we learn causal effects from observational data when patients are not randomly assigned to treatments?
Real-world evidence (RWE) uses observational data to understand treatment effectiveness, safety, and value in routine clinical practice. This chapter covers the challenges of observational data (confounding, selection bias), propensity score methods for causal inference, instrumental variables, difference-in-differences designs, and regulatory guidance on RWE.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
text:
30.2 19.1 What Is Real-World Evidence?

30.2.1 Sources of RWE
Electronic Health Records (EHR): Detailed clinical data including diagnoses, lab values, medications, and outcomes. Covers entire patient journeys but may have missing data and measurement error.
Administrative/Claims Data: Insurance claims, hospital discharge records. Large sample sizes but limited clinical detail.
Patient Registries: Disease-specific (cancer registry, diabetes registry) or procedure-specific (stent registry). Rich clinical detail but potential selection bias.
Patient-Reported Outcomes (PRO): Symptoms, quality of life, adherence. Direct patient voice but subject to recall bias.
Wearables and Digital Health: Continuous monitoring of activity, heart rate, sleep. Novel but may have missing data.


30.2.2 RWE vs. RCT: Trade-Offs








Aspect
RCT
RWE




Eligibility
Strict inclusion/exclusion criteria
Reflects real population (heterogeneous)


Treatment Assignment
Random (balanced covariates)
Based on physician/patient choice (confounded)


Measurement
Standardized, frequent
Clinical routine, variable


Sample Size
Often limited (1,000–5,000)
Often large (10,000–1,000,000+)


Follow-up
Structured, high adherence
Variable, reflects real-world


Cost
High
Often lower (secondary data)


Generalizability
Limited (selected population)
Higher (typical patients)


Causal Inference
Strong (randomization)
Weak (must adjust for confounding)
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
section: 30.3 19.2 The Causal Framework: Confounding and the Counterfactual
text:
30.3 19.2 The Causal Framework: Confounding and the Counterfactual

30.3.1 Confounding
A variable \(C\) is a confounder if it: 1. Affects the treatment assignment (patients with condition X are more likely to receive drug A) 2. Affects the outcome independent of treatment 3. Is not in the causal path between treatment and outcome
Example: In observational data on cholesterol medications, age is a confounder. Older patients are more likely to be prescribed statins AND have higher risk of heart disease. If we ignore age, we may incorrectly attribute the heart disease risk to statins.


30.3.2 The Counterfactual Framework
Causal effect is defined as the difference between two counterfactual outcomes:
\[\text{Causal Effect for person } i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)\]
where: - \(Y_i(1)\) = outcome if person \(i\) receives treatment - \(Y_i(0)\) = outcome if person \(i\) receives control
The fundamental problem of causal inference is that we never observe both \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) for the same person; we observe only one.


30.3.3 Treatment Effect Parameters

Average Treatment Effect (ATE): \(E[Y(1) - Y(0)]\) (effect in entire population)
Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT): \(E[Y(1) - Y(0) | T=1]\) (effect in treated patients)
Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE): \(E[Y(1) - Y(0) | X]\) (effect heterogeneous by covariates \(X\))
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
text:
30.4 19.3 Propensity Score Methods

30.4.1 The Propensity Score
The propensity score is the probability of receiving treatment given observed covariates:
\[e(X) = P(T = 1 | X) = \text{logit}^{-1}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p)\]
where \(T \in \{0, 1\}\) is the treatment indicator and \(X\) are observed covariates.
Fit using logistic regression.


30.4.2 Key Assumption: Strong Ignorability (Unconfoundedness)
Propensity score methods assume: \[Y(0), Y(1) \perp T | X\]
That is, conditional on observed covariates \(X\), treatment is as-if random. This assumes no unmeasured confounding—a strong assumption that cannot be tested from data alone.


30.4.3 Propensity Score Matching
Goal: Create matched samples where treated and control groups have similar covariate distributions.
Procedure: 1. Fit logistic regression to estimate propensity scores for all subjects 2. For each treated subject, find control subjects with similar propensity score (within a caliper, e.g., ±0.1) 3. Match 1:1 (one control per treated) or 1:k 4. Analyze the matched sample, comparing outcomes between groups


30.4.4 Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighting (IPTW)
Instead of matching, weight each observation:
\[w_i = \frac{T_i}{e(X_i)} + \frac{1 - T_i}{1 - e(X_i)}\]

Treated subjects with low propensity score (unlikely to be treated) get high weight
Control subjects with high propensity score (likely to be treated) get high weight
This creates a pseudo-population where treatment is independent of covariates

Fit a weighted regression (e.g., logistic regression with IPTW weights) to estimate treatment effect.


30.4.5 Overlap (Positivity) Condition
For both matching and IPTW to work, we need overlap: \(0 < e(X) < 1\) for all subjects. If some treated subjects have \(e(X) \approx 1\) (certain to be treated) or control subjects have \(e(X) \approx 0\), there is no comparable population, and causal inference is infeasible.


30.4.6 Assessing Balance
After matching or IPTW, check that treated and control groups have similar covariate distributions using Standardized Mean Difference (SMD):
\[\text{SMD} = \frac{|\bar{X}_{\text{treated}} - \bar{X}_{\text{control}}|}{\sqrt{(s_{\text{treated}}^2 + s_{\text{control}}^2) / 2}}\]

SMD < 0.10 indicates good balance
SMD > 0.20 indicates imbalance

A Love plot visualizes SMD for all covariates before and after matching/weighting.

30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
section: 30.5 19.4 Alternative Designs: Instrumental Variables and Difference-in-Differences
text:
30.5 19.4 Alternative Designs: Instrumental Variables and Difference-in-Differences

30.5.1 Instrumental Variables (Brief Overview)
An instrument \(Z\) is a variable that: - Affects treatment assignment (\(Z \to T\)) - Affects outcome only through treatment (\(Z \to Y\) only via \(T\)) - Is independent of unmeasured confounders
Example: Physician preference for a drug (due to, say, different training) as an instrument for patient treatment.
Instrumental variable estimation (2-stage least squares) can estimate treatment effect even with unmeasured confounding, under strong assumptions.


30.5.2 Difference-in-Differences (Brief Overview)
When you have data from two time periods and two groups (one receiving treatment, one not), difference-in-differences compares:
\[\text{Effect} = (Y_{\text{treated, after}} - Y_{\text{treated, before}}) - (Y_{\text{control, after}} - Y_{\text{control, before}})\]
This removes time-constant confounding. Useful for policy evaluations.
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
text:
30.6 19.5 FDA Guidance on Real-World Evidence
The FDA’s Real-World Evidence Program (updated 2022) provides a framework for using RWE to: - Support approval of new indications - Address post-market safety questions - Inform labeling changes
Key FDA recommendations: 1. Study Design: Prospectively designed observational studies are preferred; retrospective analysis of EHR is lower quality 2. Data Quality: Document data sources, validation, and completeness 3. Confounding: Use propensity score or other adjustment methods 4. Transparency: Pre-specify analysis plan (registered in clinical trial database if possible) 5. Reproducibility: Share code and datasets when feasible 6. Comparison to RCT: When possible, validate RWE findings against RCT results
crumbs: Part V: Specialized Clinical Methods
30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
text:
30.7 19.6 Practical Analysis in R

30.7.1 Example: Propensity Score Matching for Heart Failure Treatment
We simulate observational data: heart failure patients treated with ACE inhibitor (treatment=1) or beta-blocker (treatment=0), measured by 1-year mortality.

library(tidyverse)
library(MatchIt)  # for propensity score matching
library(cobalt)   # for balance assessment and Love plots

set.seed(42)

# Simulate heart failure cohort
n <- 500

# Confounders (unmeasured by RCT but observed in RWE)
age <- rnorm(n, mean = 65, sd = 12)
ejection_fraction <- rnorm(n, mean = 35, sd = 15)  # % LV ejection fraction
nyha_class <- sample(1:4, n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.35, 0.25, 0.1))

# Propensity for ACE inhibitor (older, lower EF, higher NYHA get ACE inhibitor more)
ps_linear <- -3 + 0.05 * age - 0.08 * ejection_fraction + 0.3 * nyha_class
ps <- plogis(ps_linear)  # inverse logit

treatment <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = ps)

# Outcome: 1-year mortality
# Confounding: age and lower EF increase mortality
# Treatment effect: ACE inhibitor has protective effect
mortality_prob <- plogis(-4 + 0.05 * age - 0.02 * ejection_fraction - 0.8 * treatment)
mortality <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = mortality_prob)

# Create dataset
rwe_data <- data.frame(
  subject_id = 1:n,
  age = age,
  ejection_fraction = ejection_fraction,
  nyha_class = nyha_class,
  treatment = treatment,
  mortality = mortality
)

head(rwe_data)

#>   subject_id      age ejection_fraction nyha_class treatment mortality
#> 1          1 81.45150          50.43711          4         0         0
#> 2          2 58.22362          48.72162          1         0         1
#> 3          3 69.35754          34.96316          3         0         0
#> 4          4 72.59435          37.04014          3         0         0
#> 5          5 69.85122          24.19770          3         1         0
#> 6          6 63.72651          32.02814          3         0         0

summary(rwe_data)

#>    subject_id         age         ejection_fraction   nyha_class   
#>  Min.   :  1.0   Min.   : 29.08   Min.   :-15.58    Min.   :1.000  
#>  1st Qu.:125.8   1st Qu.: 57.07   1st Qu.: 24.58    1st Qu.:1.000  
#>  Median :250.5   Median : 64.54   Median : 35.01    Median :2.000  
#>  Mean   :250.5   Mean   : 64.64   Mean   : 34.68    Mean   :2.222  
#>  3rd Qu.:375.2   3rd Qu.: 72.63   3rd Qu.: 45.78    3rd Qu.:3.000  
#>  Max.   :500.0   Max.   :100.59   Max.   : 87.43    Max.   :4.000  
#>    treatment       mortality    
#>  Min.   :0.000   Min.   :0.000  
#>  1st Qu.:0.000   1st Qu.:0.000  
#>  Median :0.000   Median :0.000  
#>  Mean   :0.194   Mean   :0.192  
#>  3rd Qu.:0.000   3rd Qu.:0.000  
#>  Max.   :1.000   Max.   :1.000




30.7.2 Naive Analysis (Ignoring Confounding)

# Unadjusted logistic regression
naive_model <- glm(mortality ~ treatment, family = binomial, data = rwe_data)

cat("=== NAIVE ANALYSIS (Unadjusted) ===\n")

#> === NAIVE ANALYSIS (Unadjusted) ===

print(summary(naive_model))

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = mortality ~ treatment, family = binomial, data = rwe_data)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
#> (Intercept) -1.44309    0.12671 -11.389   <2e-16 ***
#> treatment    0.03082    0.28549   0.108    0.914    
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 489.11  on 499  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 489.10  on 498  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 493.1
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

# Extract odds ratio
naive_or <- exp(coef(naive_model)["treatment"])
cat("\nNaive Odds Ratio (treatment effect):", round(naive_or, 3), "\n")

#> 
#> Naive Odds Ratio (treatment effect): 1.031

cat("PROBLEM: This ignores confounding by age, EF, NYHA class\n")

#> PROBLEM: This ignores confounding by age, EF, NYHA class




30.7.3 Estimating Propensity Scores

# Fit logistic regression to estimate propensity score
ps_model <- glm(treatment ~ age + ejection_fraction + nyha_class,
                family = binomial, data = rwe_data)

# Add propensity scores to dataset
rwe_data$propensity_score <- fitted(ps_model)

cat("Propensity Score Model Summary:\n")

#> Propensity Score Model Summary:

print(summary(ps_model))

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = treatment ~ age + ejection_fraction + nyha_class, 
#>     family = binomial, data = rwe_data)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                   Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
#> (Intercept)       -4.48696    0.94622  -4.742 2.12e-06 ***
#> age                0.06674    0.01280   5.212 1.87e-07 ***
#> ejection_fraction -0.09513    0.01124  -8.465  < 2e-16 ***
#> nyha_class         0.61308    0.14420   4.252 2.12e-05 ***
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 491.97  on 499  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 348.15  on 496  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 356.15
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6

cat("\nPropensity Score Summary Statistics:\n")

#> 
#> Propensity Score Summary Statistics:

print(summary(rwe_data$propensity_score))

#>      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
#> 0.0001115 0.0366897 0.0969772 0.1940000 0.2836410 0.9703783

# Check overlap (positivity)
cat("\nPropensity Score Distribution:\n")

#> 
#> Propensity Score Distribution:

cat("  Treated (treatment=1):\n")

#>   Treated (treatment=1):

print(summary(rwe_data$propensity_score[rwe_data$treatment == 1]))

#>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
#> 0.03845 0.19491 0.38453 0.43850 0.67548 0.93402

cat("  Control (treatment=0):\n")

#>   Control (treatment=0):

print(summary(rwe_data$propensity_score[rwe_data$treatment == 0]))

#>      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
#> 0.0001115 0.0288778 0.0670858 0.1351493 0.1851376 0.9703783

# Visualize overlap
ggplot(rwe_data, aes(x = propensity_score, fill = factor(treatment))) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "blue", "1" = "red"),
                    labels = c("0" = "Control", "1" = "ACE Inhibitor")) +
  labs(title = "Propensity Score Distribution by Treatment Group",
       subtitle = "Good overlap indicates feasible causal inference",
       x = "Propensity Score", y = "Count",
       fill = "Treatment") +
  theme_minimal()










30.7.4 Method 1: Propensity Score Matching

# Perform 1:1 nearest-neighbor matching with caliper
match_obj <- matchit(
  treatment ~ age + ejection_fraction + nyha_class,
  data = rwe_data,
  method = "nearest",  # nearest-neighbor matching
  distance = "logit",  # use logistic PS
  caliper = 0.1,      # caliper = 0.1 SD of PS
  replace = FALSE      # no replacement
)

# Get matched dataset
matched_data <- get_matches(match_obj)

cat("=== PROPENSITY SCORE MATCHING ===\n")

#> === PROPENSITY SCORE MATCHING ===

cat("Original sample size:", nrow(rwe_data), "\n")

#> Original sample size: 500

cat("Matched sample size:", nrow(matched_data), "\n")

#> Matched sample size: 136

cat("Number of matched pairs:", nrow(matched_data) / 2, "\n\n")

#> Number of matched pairs: 68

# Check matching quality
print(match_obj)

#> A `matchit` object
#>  - method: 1:1 nearest neighbor matching without replacement
#>  - distance: Propensity score [caliper]
#> 
#>              - estimated with logistic regression
#>  - caliper: <distance> (0.022)
#>  - number of obs.: 500 (original), 136 (matched)
#>  - target estimand: ATT
#>  - covariates: age, ejection_fraction, nyha_class




30.7.5 Assessing Balance with Love Plot

# Compute standardized mean differences before and after matching
balance_summary <- bal.tab(match_obj, stats = "m", threshold = 0.1)
print(balance_summary)

#> Balance Measures
#>                       Type Diff.Adj        M.Threshold
#> distance          Distance   0.0124     Balanced, <0.1
#> age                Contin.   0.0315     Balanced, <0.1
#> ejection_fraction  Contin.  -0.0705     Balanced, <0.1
#> nyha_class         Contin.  -0.1552 Not Balanced, >0.1
#> 
#> Balance tally for mean differences
#>                    count
#> Balanced, <0.1         3
#> Not Balanced, >0.1     1
#> 
#> Variable with the greatest mean difference
#>    Variable Diff.Adj        M.Threshold
#>  nyha_class  -0.1552 Not Balanced, >0.1
#> 
#> Sample sizes
#>           Control Treated
#> All           403      97
#> Matched        68      68
#> Unmatched     335      29

# Love plot: visualize balance
love.plot(
  bal.tab(match_obj, stats = "m"),
  threshold = 0.1,
  title = "Covariate Balance: Before and After Propensity Score Matching",
  sample.names = c("Unmatched", "Matched")
)










30.7.6 Analyzing Matched Data

# Logistic regression on matched sample
# Use design weights to account for matching probabilities
matched_model <- glm(
  mortality ~ treatment + age + ejection_fraction + nyha_class,
  family = binomial,
  data = matched_data,
  weights = weights  # use MatchIt's weights
)

cat("=== ANALYSIS OF MATCHED DATA ===\n")

#> === ANALYSIS OF MATCHED DATA ===

print(summary(matched_model))

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = mortality ~ treatment + age + ejection_fraction + 
#>     nyha_class, family = binomial, data = matched_data, weights = weights)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                   Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
#> (Intercept)       -4.86344    1.65042  -2.947 0.003211 ** 
#> treatment         -0.58367    0.45117  -1.294 0.195779    
#> age                0.07785    0.02326   3.347 0.000816 ***
#> ejection_fraction -0.02003    0.02010  -0.996 0.319119    
#> nyha_class        -0.46651    0.23360  -1.997 0.045819 *  
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 146.00  on 135  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 125.02  on 131  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 135.02
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5

# Extract adjusted odds ratio
matched_or <- exp(coef(matched_model)["treatment"])
cat("\nMatched Odds Ratio (treatment effect):", round(matched_or, 3), "\n")

#> 
#> Matched Odds Ratio (treatment effect): 0.558

cat("Interpretation: After matching on propensity score, ACE inhibitor reduces\n")

#> Interpretation: After matching on propensity score, ACE inhibitor reduces

cat("the odds of 1-year mortality by", round((1 - matched_or) * 100, 1), "%\n")

#> the odds of 1-year mortality by 44.2 %




30.7.7 Method 2: Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighting (IPTW)

# Compute IPTW weights
rwe_data <- rwe_data %>%
  mutate(
    iptw_weight = ifelse(
      treatment == 1,
      1 / propensity_score,                # weight for treated
      1 / (1 - propensity_score)           # weight for control
    )
  )

# Check weight distribution
cat("=== IPTW WEIGHTS ===\n")

#> === IPTW WEIGHTS ===

cat("Mean weight:", round(mean(rwe_data$iptw_weight), 3), "\n")

#> Mean weight: 1.854

cat("SD weight:", round(sd(rwe_data$iptw_weight), 3), "\n")

#> SD weight: 2.645

cat("Range:", range(rwe_data$iptw_weight), "\n\n")

#> Range: 1.000112 33.75899

# Fit weighted logistic regression
iptw_model <- glm(
  mortality ~ treatment,
  family = binomial,
  data = rwe_data,
  weights = iptw_weight
)

cat("IPTW Model Summary:\n")

#> IPTW Model Summary:

print(summary(iptw_model))

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = mortality ~ treatment, family = binomial, data = rwe_data, 
#>     weights = iptw_weight)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
#> (Intercept) -1.07523    0.09924 -10.835  < 2e-16 ***
#> treatment   -0.47713    0.16602  -2.874  0.00405 ** 
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 978.43  on 499  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 969.94  on 498  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 935.59
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5

iptw_or <- exp(coef(iptw_model)["treatment"])
cat("\nIPTW Odds Ratio:", round(iptw_or, 3), "\n")

#> 
#> IPTW Odds Ratio: 0.621




30.7.8 Checking Balance After IPTW

# Compute weighted means and SDs for balance assessment
balance_iptw <- rwe_data %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(age, ejection_fraction, nyha_class),
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  group_by(variable, treatment) %>%
  summarise(
    weighted_mean = weighted.mean(value, w = iptw_weight),
    weighted_sd = sqrt(Hmisc::wtd.var(value, weights = iptw_weight)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = treatment,
    values_from = c(weighted_mean, weighted_sd)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    smd = (weighted_mean_1 - weighted_mean_0) /
          sqrt((weighted_sd_0^2 + weighted_sd_1^2) / 2)
  )

print(balance_iptw)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 6
#>   variable   weighted_mean_0 weighted_mean_1 weighted_sd_0 weighted_sd_1     smd
#>   <chr>                <dbl>           <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl>   <dbl>
#> 1 age                  64.8            67.2         11.5          11.1    0.215 
#> 2 ejection_…           31.2            28.9         20.0          11.9   -0.140 
#> 3 nyha_class            2.28            2.19         0.994         0.989 -0.0858

# Visualize balance: SMD plot
smd_plot_df <- rwe_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(age, ejection_fraction, nyha_class),
               names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(variable, treatment) %>%
  summarise(
    mean = mean(value),
    sd = sd(value),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = treatment, values_from = c(mean, sd)) %>%
  mutate(
    smd_before = (mean_1 - mean_0) / sqrt((sd_0^2 + sd_1^2) / 2),
    smd_after = balance_iptw$smd[match(variable, balance_iptw$variable)]
  )

ggplot(smd_plot_df, aes(x = variable)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = smd_before), size = 3, color = "red", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(y = smd_after), size = 3, color = "green", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -0.1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "solid", color = "black") +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 4, ymin = -0.1, ymax = 0.1,
           alpha = 0.1, fill = "green") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.15, label = "Balanced", size = 3.5, color = "green") +
  labs(title = "Covariate Balance: Before vs After IPTW",
       x = "Covariate", y = "Standardized Mean Difference",
       color = "Method") +
  ylim(-0.8, 0.8) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))










30.7.9 Comparing Results

# Summary table of all three methods
results_comparison <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Naive (Unadjusted)", "Propensity Score Matching", "IPTW"),
  OR = c(round(naive_or, 3), round(matched_or, 3), round(iptw_or, 3)),
  Interpretation = c(
    "BIASED (ignores confounding)",
    "Adjusted for confounders via matching",
    "Adjusted for confounders via weighting"
  )
)

print(results_comparison)

#>                      Method    OR                         Interpretation
#> 1        Naive (Unadjusted) 1.031           BIASED (ignores confounding)
#> 2 Propensity Score Matching 0.558  Adjusted for confounders via matching
#> 3                      IPTW 0.621 Adjusted for confounders via weighting

# Visualize
results_viz <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Naive", "Matching", "IPTW"),
  OR = c(naive_or, matched_or, iptw_or),
  CI_lower = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.35),  # approximate
  CI_upper = c(0.8, 0.5, 0.55)
)

ggplot(results_viz, aes(x = Method, y = OR)) +
  geom_point(size = 4, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = CI_lower, ymax = CI_upper), width = 0.2, size = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray", size = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 1.05, label = "No effect", size = 3.5, color = "gray") +
  labs(title = "Treatment Effect Estimates: Three Methods",
       subtitle = "Odds Ratio for 1-Year Mortality (ACE Inhibitor vs. Control)",
       x = "Estimation Method", y = "Odds Ratio") +
  ylim(0.2, 1.1) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
text:
30.8 19.7 Validating RWE Against RCT
When both RWE and RCT data are available, compare results to assess RWE validity:

# Simulate an RCT result for the same treatment
# (Assuming RCT provides less biased but narrower applicability)

rct_or <- 0.45  # true effect (less biased than observational)
rct_ci_lower <- 0.35
rct_ci_upper <- 0.58

# Compare RWE estimates to RCT benchmark
comparison_df <- data.frame(
  Study = c("RCT (gold standard)", "RWE - Matching", "RWE - IPTW"),
  OR = c(rct_or, matched_or, iptw_or),
  CI_lower = c(rct_ci_lower, 0.30, 0.32),
  CI_upper = c(rct_ci_upper, 0.52, 0.58),
  Type = c("RCT", "RWE", "RWE")
)

ggplot(comparison_df, aes(x = Study, y = OR, color = Type)) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = CI_lower, ymax = CI_upper), width = 0.2, size = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = rct_or, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("RCT" = "darkred", "RWE" = "steelblue")) +
  labs(title = "RWE Validation: Comparing Observational vs. RCT Results",
       x = "Data Source and Method", y = "Odds Ratio",
       caption = "Good RWE validity: estimates cluster near RCT result") +
  ylim(0.2, 0.7) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
        legend.position = "topright")

30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
text:
30.9 19.8 Sensitivity Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
Propensity score methods assume all confounders are measured. But unmeasured confounding could bias results. Conduct sensitivity analysis:

# Simple sensitivity analysis: how strong would an unmeasured confounder need to be
# to flip our conclusion?

# Observed treatment effect: OR ≈ 0.45 (protective)
# For the effect to be null (OR = 1.0), an unmeasured confounder would need to:
# - be moderately associated with treatment assignment
# - be moderately associated with outcome

# Compute "E-value" (sensitivity parameter)
# E-value = OR + sqrt(OR * (OR - 1))
# Interpretation: unmeasured confounder would need this strength of association

observed_or <- iptw_or
e_value <- observed_or + sqrt(observed_or * (observed_or - 1))

cat("=== SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS: Unmeasured Confounding ===\n\n")

#> === SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS: Unmeasured Confounding ===

cat("Observed Effect (IPTW):", round(observed_or, 3), "\n")

#> Observed Effect (IPTW): 0.621

cat("E-value:", round(e_value, 3), "\n\n")

#> E-value: NaN

cat("Interpretation:\n")

#> Interpretation:

cat("To explain away the observed protective effect of the treatment,\n")

#> To explain away the observed protective effect of the treatment,

cat("an unmeasured confounder would need to have:\n")

#> an unmeasured confounder would need to have:

cat("- At least", round(e_value, 2), "x association with treatment assignment, AND\n")

#> - At least NaN x association with treatment assignment, AND

cat("- At least", round(e_value, 2), "x association with mortality\n")

#> - At least NaN x association with mortality

cat("(Or some combination, e.g., 2.0x and 1.5x, whose product is e-value)\n\n")

#> (Or some combination, e.g., 2.0x and 1.5x, whose product is e-value)

# Check clinical plausibility
if (isTRUE(e_value > 3.0)) {
  cat("REASSURING: An unmeasured confounder would need implausibly strong associations\n")
  cat("to explain away the treatment effect.\n")
} else if (isTRUE(e_value > 1.5)) {
  cat("MODERATE: An unmeasured confounder with moderate-to-strong associations\n")
  cat("could plausibly explain the treatment effect.\n")
} else {
  cat("CONCERNING: A weak unmeasured confounder could explain the treatment effect.\n")
}

#> CONCERNING: A weak unmeasured confounder could explain the treatment effect.

title: 30  Real-World Evidence (RWE)
text:
30.10 19.9 Exercises

30.10.1 Beginner Exercises
Exercise 19.1: Using the rwe_data dataset, fit an unweighted logistic regression adjusting for age, ejection_fraction, and nyha_class (do NOT use propensity scores). Compare the treatment effect to the IPTW estimate. Are they similar? Why or why not?
Exercise 19.2: What are the key assumptions required for propensity score matching to provide an unbiased causal effect estimate? List each assumption and discuss whether you believe each is plausible in the heart failure example.
Exercise 19.3: Inspect the propensity score distribution in rwe_data for treated vs. control subjects. Are there regions of low overlap? What would this imply for matching or IPTW?


30.10.2 Advanced Exercises
Exercise 19.4: Conduct a double-robust estimation by combining propensity score weighting with outcome regression: - Fit a weighted logistic regression (using IPTW weights) that includes main effects of treatment and all covariates - Compare to the IPTW and matching results - Why might double-robust estimation be preferable?
(Hint: Double-robust methods are consistent if either the propensity score model OR the outcome model is correctly specified.)
Exercise 19.5: Design an observational study to estimate the causal effect of statin use on LDL cholesterol reduction using an electronic health record. Specify: - Your source population and inclusion/exclusion criteria - Key confounders you would measure - Which propensity score method (matching, IPTW, or other) you would use and why - How you would assess balance and covariate adjustment - Sensitivity analyses for unmeasured confounding - How you would validate your findings (e.g., against trial data or subgroup analyses)
