title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
text:
31.1 20.1 Introduction to Tables, Listings, and Figures
Clinical study reports (CSRs) submitted to regulatory agencies such as the FDA, EMA, and PMDA are built upon three foundational deliverables: Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs). These documents represent the core evidence of a drug’s safety and efficacy in the submitted indication.
A Table is a concise, summarized presentation of analysed data—for example, the primary efficacy endpoint results by treatment arm. A Listing is a detailed row-level display of individual subject data, typically used to support tables or present specific safety signals. A Figure is a visual representation of data, ranging from plots and graphs to forest plots and swimmer plots.
The stakes are high: regulatory reviewers scrutinize TLFs to assess the quality, completeness, and integrity of the trial. Poorly formatted tables, missing footnotes, inconsistent numbering, or lack of traceability can trigger Requests for Additional Information (RFIs), delaying approval by months or years. This chapter equips you with practical, standards-based workflows to generate publication-quality TLFs using R.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
section: 31.2 20.2 Regulatory Context and ICH E3 Structure
text:
31.2 20.2 Regulatory Context and ICH E3 Structure
The ICH E3 guideline (“Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports”) defines where TLFs appear in a CSR. Understanding this structure ensures your outputs align with regulatory expectations.

31.2.1 20.2.1 The ICH E3 Framework
A typical CSR structure follows:

Module A.2: Overview and quality overall summary (no TLFs)
Module B.3.2: Quality overall summary (no TLFs)
Module B.3.3: Nonclinical overview and summaries (limited TLFs)
Module B.3.4: Nonclinical study reports (TLFs from animal data)
Modules B.3.5–B.3.7: Clinical overview and summaries (key TLFs)

Demographics and baseline characteristics (Table 14.1.1)
Efficacy tables (Tables 14.3.x)
Safety tables (Tables 14.3.x)
Pharmacokinetics tables (if applicable)

Module 5.3: Clinical study reports (full TLFs and detailed analyses)

Table and figure numbering typically follows the pattern 14.X.Y (Module 5.3) or 14.3.X (summary sections). Listings are usually presented in appendices.


31.2.2 20.2.2 Population Flags in ADaM
Before generating TLFs, you must define the Analysis Populations (populations flags in ADaM datasets):









Population
Abbreviation
Definition
Common Use




Intention-to-Treat
ITT
All randomized subjects
Primary efficacy analysis


Per-Protocol
PP
Randomized subjects with ≥80% protocol adherence
Sensitivity analysis


Safety
SAF
All dosed subjects
Safety analyses


Pharmacokinetic
PK
Subjects with evaluable PK data
PK/PD analyses



In ADaM, these are typically encoded as binary flags: ITT = "Y", PP = "Y", SAF = "Y", etc. When generating a Table 14.1.1 (Demographics), you filter to ITT = "Y" and group by treatment arm.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
text:
31.3 20.3 Table Standards and Formatting Rules

31.3.1 20.3.1 Core Formatting Principles
Clinical tables must adhere to several formatting rules:

Decimal Alignment: Numeric columns align on the decimal point (right-aligned in most cases, but decimal-aligned in publication format).
Header Rows: Include clear header rows with treatment arm names, N values, and percentage bases.
Spanning Headers: Group related columns under a single header (e.g., “Treatment Arm A” spanning three columns: N, Mean, SD).
Footnotes: Include superscript footnotes explaining special values, abbreviations, and analytical definitions.
Page Numbering: Tables spanning multiple pages must include continued headers and page numbers.
Abbreviations Table: A legend of all abbreviations used.



31.3.2 20.3.2 Example: Standard Demographics Table Format
Table 14.1.1: Demographics and Baseline Characteristics – Intent-to-Treat Population

Characteristic              Treatment A      Treatment B      Control           Total
                           (N = 120)        (N = 115)        (N = 118)         (N = 353)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Age (years)
  Mean (SD)                  45.3 (12.1)      46.8 (11.5)      44.9 (12.8)       45.7 (12.2)
  Median (Range)            45 (18–75)       46 (19–74)       44 (18–73)        45 (18–75)

Sex, n (%)
  Male                       72 (60.0)        66 (57.4)        70 (59.3)         208 (58.9)
  Female                     48 (40.0)        49 (42.6)        48 (40.7)         145 (41.1)

Race, n (%)
  White                      96 (80.0)        92 (80.0)        95 (80.5)         283 (80.2)
  Black                      18 (15.0)        16 (13.9)        16 (13.6)         50 (14.2)
  Other                      6 (5.0)          7 (6.1)          7 (5.9)           20 (5.7)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
footnote a: SD = standard deviation.
footnote b: N is the number of subjects; percentages are based on N.

31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
section: 31.4 20.4 Key R Packages for Clinical Tables
text:
31.4 20.4 Key R Packages for Clinical Tables

31.4.1 20.4.1 gtsummary: Quick Demographic Tables
The gtsummary package excels at producing publication-ready demographic and baseline tables with minimal code.
Key functions: - tbl_summary(): Creates a demographic table from a dataframe. - tbl_regression(): Produces regression tables. - add_stat(), add_p(): Add custom statistics and p-values. - as_gt(), as_flextable(): Convert to other table formats.


31.4.2 20.4.2 gt: Highly Customizable Tables
gt (grammar of tables) provides granular control over table styling, colors, fonts, and layout. It excels for complex, multi-level headers and custom cell formatting.


31.4.3 20.4.3 flextable: Word/RTF-Compatible Output
flextable is ideal when you need to export to Word (.docx) or RTF—common regulatory deliverable formats. It supports: - Merged cells and spanning headers - Conditional formatting (cell coloring) - Page breaks between rows - Native Word integration


31.4.4 20.4.4 rtables: Roche’s Framework (Brief Intro)
rtables is a specialized framework developed by Roche for clinical trial reporting. It enforces a row-column structure optimized for regulatory tables. While powerful, it has a steeper learning curve; we’ll focus on gtsummary, gt, and flextable in this chapter.

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
text:
31.6 20.6 Exporting to Regulatory Formats

31.6.1 20.6.1 Export to RTF
RTF (Rich Text Format) is widely used for regulatory submissions because it preserves formatting across platforms and systems.

library(flextable)

# Export demographics table to RTF (run in HTML environment)
save_as_rtf(ae_table, path = "table_14_3_1_ae_summary.rtf")



31.6.2 20.6.2 Export to Word (.docx)

# Export to Word (run in HTML environment)
save_as_docx(ae_table, path = "table_14_3_1_ae_summary.docx")



31.6.3 20.6.3 Export to PDF
For PDF export, use gt or convert via Quarto (Chapter 21):

# Using gt to PDF (requires webshot2 or similar)
if (requireNamespace("webshot2", quietly = TRUE)) {
  demographics_gt %>% gtsave("table_14_1_1_demographics.pdf")
} else {
  message("webshot2 not available; skipping PDF export of gt table.")
}



31.6.4 20.6.4 HTML and Interactive Tables
For internal review or CSR supplementary materials:

# Save as HTML
writeLines(as_raw_html(demographics_gt), "table_14_1_1_demographics.html")
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
text:
31.7 20.7 Figures for Regulatory Submission

31.7.1 20.7.1 Resolution and Quality Standards
Regulatory figures must meet strict technical requirements:

Resolution: ≥300 DPI (dots per inch) for print publication.
Font Size: Minimum 10 pt (Arial or Helvetica preferred).
Color: Use colorblind-friendly palettes (e.g., viridis); provide grayscale alternatives.
Labels: All axes, legends, and footnotes must be clearly labeled.
Format: PDF is preferred for regulatory submissions (preserves formatting).



31.7.2 20.7.2 Example: Kaplan-Meier Survival Curve

library(survival)
library(ggplot2)

# Simulate survival data
set.seed(42)
survival_data <- data.frame(
  SUBJID = 1:300,
  ARM = rep(c("Treatment A", "Treatment B", "Control"), each = 100),
  time = c(
    rexp(100, rate = 0.03),  # Treatment A: longer survival
    rexp(100, rate = 0.04),  # Treatment B: intermediate
    rexp(100, rate = 0.05)   # Control: shorter survival
  ),
  event = rep(c(rep(1, 80), rep(0, 20)), 3)  # 80% events, 20% censored
)

# Fit survival curves
survfit_obj <- survfit(Surv(time, event) ~ ARM, data = survival_data)

# KM plot using base survival + ggplot2 (no ggsurvfit dependency)
km_sum <- summary(survfit_obj)
km_df  <- data.frame(
  time   = km_sum$time,
  surv   = km_sum$surv,
  lower  = km_sum$lower,
  upper  = km_sum$upper,
  ARM    = sub("ARM=", "", as.character(km_sum$strata))
)

km_plot <- ggplot(km_df, aes(x = time, y = surv, colour = ARM, fill = ARM)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("#2C6E9E", "#E07B39", "#4CAF50")) +
  scale_fill_manual(  values = c("#2C6E9E", "#E07B39", "#4CAF50")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Figure 14.2.1: Kaplan-Meier Survival Curve",
    subtitle = "Intent-to-Treat Population",
    x        = "Time (days)",
    y        = "Probability of Survival",
    colour   = "Treatment",
    fill     = "Treatment"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

km_plot










31.7.3 20.7.3 Example: Forest Plot for Efficacy

library(ggplot2)

# Simulate efficacy data: odds ratios by subgroup
subgroup_efficacy <- data.frame(
  subgroup = c(
    "Overall",
    "Age ≤ 50 years",
    "Age > 50 years",
    "Male",
    "Female",
    "Baseline ECOG 0",
    "Baseline ECOG 1"
  ),
  or = c(1.55, 1.48, 1.68, 1.52, 1.59, 1.62, 1.41),
  lower_ci = c(1.22, 1.10, 1.25, 1.18, 1.15, 1.25, 0.95),
  upper_ci = c(1.97, 2.00, 2.25, 1.96, 2.20, 2.10, 2.10)
)

forest_plot <- ggplot(
  subgroup_efficacy,
  aes(x = reorder(subgroup, or), y = or, ymin = lower_ci, ymax = upper_ci)
) +
  geom_pointrange(size = 0.8, color = "darkblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", alpha = 0.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Figure 14.3.5: Forest Plot – Efficacy by Subgroup",
    subtitle = "Odds Ratio for Response (Treatment A vs Control)",
    x = "Subgroup",
    y = "Odds Ratio (95% CI)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans", size = 11),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13)
  )

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
text:
31.8 20.8 CDISC-Compliant Labelling
All TLF outputs should reference standard CDISC labels. For example:

Variable: AGE → Label: “Age (years)”
Variable: SEX → Label: “Sex”
Variable: AETERM → Label: “Adverse Event Preferred Term”

Ensure your code extracts these labels from ADaM attributes:

# Get label from variable
get_label <- function(df, var) {
  attr(df[[var]], "label") %||% var
}

# Example
label_age <- get_label(dm, "AGE")  # Returns "Age (years)" if set

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
text:
31.9 20.9 Troubleshooting Common TLF Issues








Issue
Cause
Solution




Spanning headers not aligned
Incorrect colwidths in add_header_row()
Ensure colwidths sum equals number of columns


Footnotes don’t display
Missing locations argument
Specify locations = cells_body() or cells_column_labels()


Page breaks mid-table
No pagination set
Use paginate_at_bkpt() in flextable


Percentages don’t sum to 100%
Rounding of individual percentages
Use raw counts for auditing; round only for display


Decimal alignment wrong
Text alignment instead of numeric
Ensure column is numeric; use fmt_number() in gt

31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
text:
31.10 20.10 Summary

Tables summarize analysed data and are central to regulatory submissions.
Listings provide detailed, row-level subject data for traceability.
Figures visualize trends and support primary conclusions.
Population flags (ITT, PP, SAF) are defined in ADaM and determine table populations.
gtsummary is ideal for quick demographic tables; gt for highly customizable publication tables; flextable for Word/RTF exports.
All TLFs must be formatted to regulatory standards (decimal alignment, footnotes, spanning headers, high-resolution figures).

title: 31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)
text:
31.11 20.11 Exercises
Exercise 20.1: Using pharmaversesdtm::dm, create a demographics table (Table 14.1.1) for the ITT population. Include mean age, age SD, and percentages for sex and race. Export to PDF and Word.
Exercise 20.2: Using pharmaversesdtm::ae, generate an AE summary table (Table 14.3.1) with counts and percentages by treatment arm. Include only AEs with incidence ≥5% in any arm.
Exercise 20.3: Create a listing of all subjects with serious adverse events (SAEs), sorted by treatment arm and onset date. Export to Word with page breaks after every 50 rows.
Exercise 20.4: Create a forest plot showing efficacy (e.g., response rate) by subgroup (age, sex, disease stage). Ensure the plot meets regulatory standards (300 DPI, clear labels, colorblind-friendly palette). Save as PDF.
Exercise 20.5: Write a function create_demographic_table(data, by_var) that accepts a dataset and a grouping variable (e.g., “ARM”), and returns a gtsummary table with demographics. Test with pharmaversesdtm::dm.

Key References: - ICH E3 Guideline: Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports (https://www.ich.org/) - FDA Guidance on Clinical Trial Reporting (FDA.gov) - gtsummary Package Documentation (https://www.danieldsjoberg.com/gtsummary/) - gt Package Documentation (https://gt.rstudio.com/) - flextable Package Documentation (https://ardata-fr.github.io/flextable-book/)

31  Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs)

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.1 21.1 Introduction to Reproducible Reporting
The replication crisis in biomedical research has exposed a fundamental vulnerability: many published findings cannot be reproduced. A 2016 survey in Nature found that 70% of researchers had tried and failed to reproduce published results; 50% failed to reproduce their own results. The causes are multifaceted—poor statistical reporting, undisclosed data manipulations, and opaque analytical workflows—but one cure is clear: reproducible reporting.
In clinical research, reproducibility carries regulatory weight. The FDA’s 21 CFR Part 11 rule (“Electronic Records; Electronic Signatures”) requires that computer systems used to generate clinical data and reports maintain an audit trail—a permanent record of who did what and when. Reproducible reports, version-controlled through git and executed via scripts, provide exactly this traceability.
Reproducible reporting means: 1. Raw data → Code → Results (no manual copy-pasting of numbers into tables). 2. All analytical choices are documented and version-controlled. 3. Any reviewer can re-run the entire pipeline and obtain identical results. 4. The report is a “living document” that updates when data or code changes.
This chapter focuses on two tools: R Markdown and Quarto. While R Markdown has dominated clinical reporting, Quarto is its modern successor—faster, more flexible, and increasingly the standard in biostatistics and regulatory circles.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
section: 32.2 21.2 R Markdown vs. Quarto: Key Differences
text:
32.2 21.2 R Markdown vs. Quarto: Key Differences

32.2.1 21.2.1 What They Have in Common
Both R Markdown and Quarto are literate programming environments that weave code and narrative text:

YAML header for metadata (title, author, date).
Markdown text for documentation.
Code chunks (R, Python, Julia, etc.) executed during rendering.
Automatic generation of tables, figures, and inline values.
Multiple output formats (HTML, PDF, Word, etc.).



32.2.2 21.2.2 Key Differences








Feature
R Markdown
Quarto




Underlying Engine
knitr (R only)
Pandoc + knitr/jupyter (R, Python, Julia)


Development
RStudio (stable)
Posit + community (active development)


Execution Speed
Slower (knitr parsing)
Faster (Pandoc architecture)


Cross-References
Manual numbering
Native @fig-, @tbl- syntax


Multi-file Projects
bookdown (add-on)
_quarto.yml (native)


Parameter Rendering
Limited (params:)
Robust parameterized reports


Code Reuse
Difficult
_quarto.yml include: directives


Future Roadmap
Limited updates
Active development (2024+)



Why Quarto is the future: Quarto is Posit’s (formerly RStudio) strategic direction. While R Markdown will remain supported, Quarto offers superior architecture for complex, multi-file regulatory reports. Major pharmaceutical companies (Roche, Novartis) are adopting Quarto for CSR generation.


32.2.3 21.2.3 Recommendation
For new projects (2024+), use Quarto. For legacy reports using R Markdown, migration is straightforward (minor YAML syntax adjustments).

32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.3 21.3 Quarto Document Anatomy
A Quarto document comprises three sections:

32.3.1 21.3.1 YAML Header
---
title: "Safety Report: Treatment Arm A"
subtitle: "Phase III Clinical Trial XYZ-201"
author:
  - "John Smith (Biostatistics)"
  - "Jane Doe (Data Management)"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
  docx:
    reference-doc: template.docx
params:
  treatment_arm: "A"
  analysis_date: !r Sys.Date()
---
Key YAML options: - title, subtitle, author, date: Document metadata. - format: Output format(s) and options. - params: Parameters for parameterized reports (discussed in 21.4). - execute: Global code execution options (see 21.3.2).


32.3.2 21.3.2 Quarto Markdown Text
Regular Markdown text with embedded cross-references:
## Safety Summary

As shown in @fig-ae-trend, adverse events increased over time in Treatment A.
Table @tbl-ae-summary presents a detailed breakdown.

### Serious Adverse Events

Six serious adverse events (SAEs) were reported: @ref_saes_count.


32.3.3 21.3.3 Code Chunks
Code chunks are delimited by triple backticks with language and options:
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.4 21.4 Code Chunk Options
Quarto chunk options are specified in YAML-like comments (using #|):








Option
Purpose
Example




label
Chunk identifier (for cross-references)
#\| label: create-ae-table


eval
Execute chunk? (Y/N)
#\| eval: true


echo
Display code in output? (Y/N)
#\| echo: false


warning
Display warnings? (Y/N)
#\| warning: false


message
Display messages? (Y/N)
#\| message: false


fig.width, fig.height
Figure dimensions (inches)
#\| fig.width: 8


fig.cap
Figure caption
#\| fig.cap: "Figure 1: AE Trends"


cache
Cache chunk results? (Y/N)
#\| cache: true


include
Include output in document? (Y/N)
#\| include: false


error
Continue on error? (Y/N)
#\| error: false




32.4.1 21.4.1 Global Execution Options
Set defaults in the YAML header:
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: false
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.5 21.5 Parameterized Reports
A parameterized report is a Quarto document that accepts input parameters, allowing you to generate multiple versions automatically without changing code.

32.5.1 21.5.1 Define Parameters in YAML
---
title: "Safety Report"
params:
  treatment_arm: "A"
  analysis_date: !r Sys.Date()
  include_serious_ae: true
---


32.5.2 21.5.2 Reference Parameters in Code
Use params$variable_name to access parameters:

library(dplyr)

# Filter to the selected treatment arm (join ARM from dm)
data_filtered <- ae %>%
  left_join(dm %>% select(USUBJID, ARM), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  filter(ARM == params$treatment_arm)

# Get analysis date
analysis_date <- params$analysis_date

# Report
cat("Analysis Date:", analysis_date, "\n")
cat("Treatment Arm:", params$treatment_arm, "\n")
cat("N subjects with AE:", n_distinct(data_filtered$USUBJID), "\n")



32.5.3 21.5.3 Render with Different Parameters
Render a report with parameters via command line or R:
# Bash: Render for Treatment A
quarto render safety_report.qmd \
  -P treatment_arm:"A" \
  -P include_serious_ae:true \
  -o "safety_report_arm_a.html"

# Render for Treatment B
quarto render safety_report.qmd \
  -P treatment_arm:"B" \
  -P include_serious_ae:true \
  -o "safety_report_arm_b.html"
Or in R:

library(quarto)

# Render for each treatment arm
for (arm in c("A", "B", "Control")) {
  quarto_render(
    input = "safety_report.qmd",
    execute_params = list(treatment_arm = arm),
    output_file = paste0("safety_report_arm_", arm, ".html")
  )
}



32.5.4 21.5.4 Example: Parameterized Safety Report Template
Create a file safety_report_template.qmd:

---
title: "Safety Report: Treatment {{params$treatment_arm}}"
params:
  treatment_arm: "A"
  data_cutoff: !r Sys.Date()
  min_aesev: "MODERATE"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
  docx:
    reference-doc: style_template.docx
---

## Executive Summary

This report presents a safety analysis for **`r params$treatment_arm`**
as of **`r format(params$data_cutoff, '%B %d, %Y')`**.

## Adverse Events by Severity

text:
32.9 21.7 Inline R Code
Insert computed values directly into narrative text using `r ` backticks:
A total of 306 subjects were randomized across three treatment arms.
The mean age was 75.1 years (SD = 8.5).
Renders as: “A total of 353 subjects were randomized across three treatment arms. The mean age was 45.7 years (SD = 12.2).”
This approach ensures that all reported numbers are programmatically generated from data, preventing transcription errors.

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.10 21.8 Quarto Books and Multi-File Projects
For large clinical study reports (hundreds of pages), organize content across multiple files using Quarto projects.

32.10.1 21.8.1 Project Structure
clinical_study_report/
├── _quarto.yml
├── index.qmd
├── chapters/
│   ├── 01_background.qmd
│   ├── 02_methods.qmd
│   ├── 03_results.qmd
│   ├── 04_safety.qmd
│   └── 05_conclusions.qmd
├── appendices/
│   ├── a_demographics_tables.qmd
│   └── b_ae_listings.qmd
└── data/
    ├── adsl.rds
    ├── adae.rds
    └── adlb.rds


32.10.2 21.8.2 Configuration with _quarto.yml
Create _quarto.yml to control project settings:
project:
  type: book
  output-dir: _book

book:
  title: "Clinical Study Report: Trial XYZ-201"
  author:
    - "Clinical Development Team"
    - "Biostatistics"
  date: today

  chapters:
    - index.qmd
    - chapters/01_background.qmd
    - chapters/02_methods.qmd
    - chapters/03_results.qmd
    - chapters/04_safety.qmd
    - chapters/05_conclusions.qmd
    - appendices/a_demographics_tables.qmd
    - appendices/b_ae_listings.qmd

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  docx:
    reference-doc: csr_template.docx
    number-sections: true

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false


32.10.3 21.8.3 Render the Entire Book
From the project root, run:
quarto render
This generates HTML, PDF, and Word versions of the entire CSR in the _book/ directory.

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.11 21.9 Automation Pipelines with targets
The targets package (by Will Landau) is an R-native pipeline tool that tracks dependencies and only re-executes code when inputs change—essential for large analyses.

32.11.1 21.9.1 Basic targets Workflow
Create _targets.R in your project root:

library(targets)
library(tarchetypes)

# Define pipeline
list(
  # Load raw data
  tar_target(
    name = raw_data,
    command = {
      # Download or load from server
      read.csv("data/raw_adsl.csv")
    }
  ),

  # Clean and preprocess
  tar_target(
    name = clean_data,
    command = {
      raw_data %>%
        filter(AVAL >= 0) %>%
        mutate(AVAL_log = log(AVAL + 1))
    },
    dependson = raw_data
  ),

  # Run analysis
  tar_target(
    name = efficacy_analysis,
    command = {
      fit <- lm(AVAL ~ ARM + ABASE + AGE, data = clean_data)
      broom::tidy(fit)
    },
    dependson = clean_data
  ),

  # Generate report
  tar_quarto(
    name = report,
    path = "clinical_report.qmd",
    execute_params = list(
      analysis_results = efficacy_analysis,
      data_timestamp = Sys.time()
    )
  )
)



32.11.2 21.9.2 Execute the Pipeline

# Run pipeline
targets::tar_make()

# Check status
targets::tar_visnetwork()

# Load results
results <- targets::tar_read(efficacy_analysis)

The targets package ensures that: - Raw data changes trigger all downstream reprocessing. - Reports only re-render if their inputs change. - A visual dependency graph tracks all relationships.

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.12 21.10 Version Control Integration: git + RStudio

32.12.1 21.10.1 Initialize a git Repository
cd ~/clinical_study_report
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit: CSR template"


32.12.2 21.10.2 .gitignore for Sensitive Data
Create .gitignore to exclude sensitive files:
# Data files
data/*.csv
data/*.rds
*.xlsx

# Rendered outputs
_book/
*.html
*.pdf
*.docx

# System files
.Rhistory
.Rdata
.DS_Store
.Rproj.user/

# Credentials (NEVER commit!)
.env
credentials.txt
api_keys.txt


32.12.3 21.10.3 Collaborative Workflow
# Feature branch for new safety analysis
git checkout -b feature/sae-analysis

# Make changes, commit
git add chapters/04_safety.qmd
git commit -m "Add serious AE subgroup analysis"

# Push to remote
git push -u origin feature/sae-analysis

# Create pull request for peer review
# (GitHub/GitLab web interface)


32.12.4 21.10.4 Audit Trail Benefits
Once merged to main, git history provides: - WHO made each change (author metadata). - WHEN the change was made (timestamp). - WHAT was changed (diff). - WHY it was changed (commit message).
This satisfies 21 CFR Part 11 audit trail requirements.

text:
32.13 21.11 Example: Complete Parameterized Safety Report
Here’s a comprehensive safety report template (safety_report_full.qmd) that demonstrates all concepts:

---
title: "Safety Report Template"
subtitle: "Phase III Clinical Trial XYZ-201"
author:
  - "Biostatistics Team"
date: today
params:
  treatment_arm: "Treatment A"
  data_cutoff: !r Sys.Date()
  include_serious_ae: true

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: flatly
  docx:
    reference-doc: csr_style_template.docx
    number-sections: true

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: false
---

## {#sec-intro}

This report summarizes safety data for **`r params$treatment_arm`**
as of **`r format(params$data_cutoff, '%B %d, %Y')`**.


library(dplyr)
library(gtsummary)
library(ggplot2)
library(flextable)
library(pharmaversesdtm)

# Load data
data("dm")
data("ae")
data("adsl")

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.14 Study Population

# Filter to treatment arm
dm_arm <- dm %>%
  filter(ARM == params$treatment_arm)

n_subjects <- nrow(dm_arm)
mean_age <- round(mean(dm_arm$AGE), 1)
sd_age <- round(sd(dm_arm$AGE), 1)
n_female <- sum(dm_arm$SEX == "F")
pct_female <- round(100 * n_female / n_subjects, 1)

A total of 120 subjects were enrolled in Treatment A. The population was 42.5% female with a mean age of 45.3 ± 12.1 years.

32.14.1 Demographics Table



Table 32.2: Baseline Demographics


dm %>%
  filter(ARM == params$treatment_arm) %>%
  select(AGE, SEX, RACE, ETHNIC) %>%
  tbl_summary(
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    label = list(
      AGE ~ "Age (years)",
      SEX ~ "Sex",
      RACE ~ "Race",
      ETHNIC ~ "Ethnicity"
    )
  ) %>%
  modify_caption("Table 1: Baseline Demographics")

32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.15 Adverse Events

ae_arm <- ae %>%
  filter(ARM == params$treatment_arm)

n_ae_subjects <- n_distinct(ae_arm$SUBJID)
total_ae_events <- nrow(ae_arm)
ae_incidence_pct <- round(100 * n_ae_subjects / n_subjects, 1)

# SAEs if requested
sae_count <- 0
if (isTRUE(params$include_serious_ae)) {
  sae_count <- ae_arm %>%
    filter(AESER == "Y") %>%
    distinct(SUBJID) %>%
    nrow()
}

A total of 150 adverse events were reported in 95 (79.2%) subjects.
if (isTRUE(params$include_serious_ae)) { - Serious AEs: 8 subjects had at least one serious adverse event. }

32.15.1 AE Table by SOC



Table 32.3: Adverse Events by System Organ Class


ae_summary <- ae_arm %>%
  distinct(SUBJID, AESOC, AETERM) %>%
  group_by(AESOC) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n_distinct(SUBJID),
    .groups = 'drop'
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(n)) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(100 * n / n_subjects, 1)) %>%
  rename(
    `System Organ Class` = AESOC,
    `N Subjects` = n,
    `%` = pct
  )

flextable(ae_summary) %>%
  theme_vanilla() %>%
  autofit() %>%
  add_header_row(
    values = c(
      paste("Adverse Events by SOC –", params$treatment_arm),
      "", ""
    )
  )





32.15.2 AE Trend Figure

ae_timeline <- ae_arm %>%
  arrange(AESTDTC) %>%
  mutate(
    cumulative_subjects = cumsum(!duplicated(SUBJID))
  ) %>%
  select(AESTDTC, cumulative_subjects) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  mutate(cumulative_pct = 100 * cumulative_subjects / n_subjects)

ggplot(ae_timeline, aes(x = AESTDTC, y = cumulative_pct)) +
  geom_step(color = "darkred", size = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "darkred", size = 2) +
  labs(
    x = "Date",
    y = "Cumulative AE Incidence (%)",
    title = paste("Cumulative Adverse Events –", params$treatment_arm)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylim(0, 100)

As shown in the cumulative AE incidence figure above, AE incidence accumulated over the study period, reaching 30% by the data cutoff.

Safety profile is consistent with previous observations.
No unexpected safety signals identified.
Adverse events were generally manageable and reversible.


Report Generated: 2026-04-20 16:51:31.876373 Analysis Date: 2026-04-20 Treatment Arm: Treatment A

## 21.12 Rendering and Output Options

### 21.12.1 Render from Command Line

```bash
# HTML output
quarto render safety_report_full.qmd -t html

# Word output
quarto render safety_report_full.qmd -t docx

# PDF output (requires TinyTeX or LaTeX)
quarto render safety_report_full.qmd -t pdf

# With parameters
quarto render safety_report_full.qmd \
  -P treatment_arm:"Treatment B" \
  -t html \
  -o "safety_report_arm_b.html"

32.17.1 21.12.2 Render from R
library(quarto)

quarto_render(
  input = "safety_report_full.qmd",
  execute_params = list(
    treatment_arm = "Treatment A",
    include_serious_ae = TRUE
  ),
  output_format = "all"
)


32.17.2 21.12.3 Batch Rendering for Multiple Sites/Arms
# Render report for each treatment arm
arms <- c("Treatment A", "Treatment B", "Control")

for (arm in arms) {
  quarto_render(
    input = "safety_report_full.qmd",
    execute_params = list(treatment_arm = arm),
    output_file = paste0("safety_report_", tolower(gsub(" ", "_", arm)), ".html")
  )
}

Issue

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.19 21.14 Summary

Reproducible reporting is essential for regulatory compliance (21 CFR Part 11) and scientific integrity.
Quarto is the modern successor to R Markdown, with superior architecture for clinical reports.
Parameterized reports allow automated generation of multiple report variants (by site, arm, data cutoff).
Cross-references (@fig-, @tbl-) enable automatic numbering and updates.
Inline R code ensures all reported numbers are programmatically generated.
Quarto books organize large reports across multiple files with _quarto.yml configuration.
targets pipelines track dependencies and enable efficient re-execution.
git integration provides audit trails and version control for regulatory compliance.

title: 32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto
text:
32.20 21.15 Exercises
Exercise 21.1: Convert an existing R Markdown report to Quarto. Update YAML syntax, code chunk options (from {r, echo=FALSE} to #| echo: false), and test all output formats.
Exercise 21.2: Create a parameterized safety report that accepts treatment_arm and data_cutoff parameters. Render it for three arms (A, B, Control) automatically using a for loop.
Exercise 21.3: Build a Quarto book project with three chapters: Background, Methods, and Results. Use _quarto.yml to configure multi-file rendering, and test HTML, PDF, and Word outputs.
Exercise 21.4: Implement a targets pipeline that: - Loads raw ADaM data (simulated or pharmaversesdtm) - Performs efficacy analysis - Generates a Quarto report with inline results - Visualizes the dependency graph with tar_visnetwork()
Exercise 21.5: Set up git version control for a clinical report project, create a feature branch for a safety analysis, and simulate a pull request review workflow.

Key References: - Quarto Official Documentation: https://quarto.org/ - R Markdown: The Definitive Guide: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/ - targets Package: https://books.ropensci.org/targets/ - 21 CFR Part 11 (FDA): https://www.ecfr.gov/ - PHUSE Guidelines on Reproducibility: https://www.phuse.global/

32  Reproducible Reporting with R Markdown and Quarto

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.1 22.1 Introduction to Validation in Clinical Programming
The stakes for data integrity in clinical trials are extraordinarily high. A single computational error in efficacy analysis could delay a drug approval by years; a missed safety signal could harm patients. Regulatory agencies like the FDA, EMA, and ICH recognize this, embedding validation requirements into guidance documents like 21 CFR Part 11 (“Electronic Records; Electronic Signatures”), ICH GCP (“Good Clinical Practice”), and GAMP 5 (“Guidance for the Use of Prototyping in the Pharmaceutical Industry”).
Validation in clinical programming means ensuring that: 1. Software and analytical code produce correct, reproducible results. 2. Data transformations are accurate and traceable. 3. Statistical analyses comply with the statistical analysis plan (SAP). 4. Regulatory submissions contain complete, auditable records of all computational steps.
This chapter presents practical workflows and R tools to validate ADaM datasets, statistical analyses, and clinical outputs. We focus on three core strategies: double programming, unit testing, and automated dataset comparison.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.2 22.2 Regulatory and Quality Frameworks

33.2.1 22.2.1 21 CFR Part 11: Electronic Records
21 CFR Part 11 is the FDA rule governing electronic records and signatures in regulated industries. Key requirements include:

Audit Trail: Permanent, computer-generated records of all data entries, deletions, and modifications.
Data Integrity: Systems must ensure that data remain accurate and complete.
User Authentication: Only authorized users can access and modify data.
Validation of Systems: Software used to process clinical data must be validated.

For R programmers, this means: - Version control (git) provides audit trails (who, when, what). - Reproducible scripts eliminate manual, untracked manipulations. - Unit tests provide evidence of correctness. - Change logs document modifications and rationale.


33.2.2 22.2.2 ICH GCP
ICH GCP (Good Clinical Practice) requires that: - All data be traceable to source documentation. - Statistical analyses are performed according to the SAP, which is finalized before database lock. - Any deviations from the SAP are documented and justified.


33.2.3 22.2.3 GAMP 5: Software Validation Phases
GAMP 5 defines a lifecycle for software validation:








Phase
Activity
Evidence




IQ (Installation Qualification)
Verify software is installed correctly and meets specifications
Installation logs, vendor documentation


OQ (Operational Qualification)
Verify software operates as specified under normal conditions
Test scripts, results


PQ (Performance Qualification)
Verify software meets user requirements in the actual operational environment
Test cases with real/realistic data



For R package validation, this translates to: - IQ: Confirm R version, package versions, and dependencies via renv.lock. - OQ: Run unit tests to verify functions work correctly. - PQ: Test with realistic clinical data (e.g., pharmaversesdtm) to verify end-to-end workflows.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.3 22.3 Double Programming and Reconciliation
Double programming is the gold standard in clinical data validation: two independent programmers perform the same analysis using independent code, and results are compared cell-by-cell.

33.3.1 22.3.1 Double Programming Workflow
Raw Data (SDTM)
    ↓
Programmer A writes ADaM code → ADaM_A
Programmer B writes ADaM code → ADaM_B
    ↓
Compare ADaM_A vs ADaM_B using diffdf
    ↓
Identify discrepancies → Root cause analysis
    ↓
Reconciliation meeting → Agree on correct approach
    ↓
Final ADaM dataset


33.3.2 22.3.2 Example: Double Programming for Change from Baseline
Programmer A’s approach (using dplyr):

# Programmer A: Calculate change from baseline
library(dplyr)

adlb_a <- lab_data %>%
  arrange(SUBJID, LBTESTCD, VISITNUM) %>%
  group_by(SUBJID, LBTESTCD) %>%
  mutate(
    AVAL = LBSTRESN,
    ABASE = first(LBSTRESN),  # First value = baseline
    CHG = AVAL - ABASE,
    PCHG = (CHG / ABASE) * 100
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

Programmer B’s approach (using data.table):

# Programmer B: Calculate change from baseline
library(data.table)

lab_dt <- as.data.table(lab_data)

adlb_b <- lab_dt[
  order(SUBJID, LBTESTCD, VISITNUM),
  c("AVAL", "ABASE", "CHG", "PCHG") := {
    aval <- LBSTRESN
    abase <- LBSTRESN[1]  # First value = baseline
    chg <- aval - abase
    pchg <- (chg / abase) * 100
    list(AVAL = aval, ABASE = abase, CHG = chg, PCHG = pchg)
  },
  by = .(SUBJID, LBTESTCD)
]



33.3.3 22.3.3 Comparing Outputs with diffdf
The diffdf package (from admiral) compares two datasets and flags all differences:

if (requireNamespace("diffdf", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(diffdf)

  # Compare Programmer A's and B's outputs
  comparison <- diffdf(
    base = adlb_a,
    compare = adlb_b,
    keys = c("SUBJID", "LBTESTCD", "VISITNUM")
  )

  # View summary of differences
  summary(comparison)

  # Extract detailed differences
  comparison$comparison_result
}

#> NULL


Output might show: - Structure differences: Extra/missing columns - Value differences: Cell-level discrepancies - Key differences: Records in one dataset but not the other
If differences exist, the team meets to identify the root cause: - Rounding rules different? - Baseline definition different (first visit vs. screening)? - Missing data handling different?
Once reconciled, the agreed-upon approach is coded and validated.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.4 22.4 Unit Testing with testthat
Unit testing is a software development practice in which small, isolated code units are tested in isolation. In clinical programming, unit tests verify that: - Data transformations work correctly. - Edge cases (missing data, extreme values) are handled properly. - Statistical calculations match known results.

33.4.1 22.4.1 testthat Package Basics
The testthat package provides a framework for writing tests:

if (requireNamespace("testthat", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(testthat)

  # Define a test file
  test_that("change from baseline calculated correctly", {
    # Setup
    test_data <- data.frame(
      SUBJID = c(1, 1, 2, 2),
      VISITNUM = c(1, 2, 1, 2),
      VALUE = c(100, 110, 50, 55)
    )

    # Define expected behavior
    expected <- data.frame(
      SUBJID = c(1, 1, 2, 2),
      VISITNUM = c(1, 2, 1, 2),
      VALUE = c(100, 110, 50, 55),
      CHG = c(0, 10, 0, 5),
      PCHG = c(0, 10, 0, 10)
    )

    # Run function
    result <- calculate_change(test_data)

    # Assert correctness
    expect_equal(result$CHG, expected$CHG)
    expect_equal(result$PCHG, expected$PCHG)
  })
}

── Error: change from baseline calculated correctly ───────────────────────────────────
Error in `calculate_change(test_data)`: could not find function "calculate_change"
Error:
! Test failed with 1 failure and 0 successes.
Backtrace:
▆
1. └─testthat::test_that(...)
2. └─testthat:::test_code(code, parent.frame())
3. ├─withr (local) `<fn>`()
4. └─reporter$end_test(context = reporter$.context, test = test)
5. └─cli::cli_abort(...)
6. └─rlang::abort(...)


33.4.2 22.4.2 Common testthat Assertions








Function
Purpose
Example




expect_equal()
Check exact equality
expect_equal(2 + 2, 4)


expect_identical()
Check exact identity (stricter)
expect_identical(x, y)


expect_true()
Check boolean TRUE
expect_true(all(x > 0))


expect_false()
Check boolean FALSE
expect_false(any(is.na(x)))


expect_error()
Verify error is thrown
expect_error(bad_func(), "error message")


expect_warning()
Verify warning is issued
expect_warning(func())


expect_length()
Check vector length
expect_length(x, 10)


expect_gt()
Greater than
expect_gt(mean(x), 100)


expect_lt()
Less than
expect_lt(p_value, 0.05)





33.4.3 22.4.3 Comprehensive Test Suite
Create a file tests/test-adae.R:

if (requireNamespace("testthat", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(testthat)
  library(dplyr)

  # Test: Serious AE flag assignment
  test_that("AESRDFN flag assigned correctly", {
    test_ae <- data.frame(
      AESER = c("Y", "N", "Y", NA),
      expected_aesrdfn = c("Serious", "Non-serious", "Serious", NA)
    )

    # Function to test
    assign_aesrdfn <- function(aeser) {
      ifelse(aeser == "Y", "Serious",
             ifelse(aeser == "N", "Non-serious", NA))
    }

    result <- assign_aesrdfn(test_ae$AESER)
    expect_equal(result, test_ae$expected_aesrdfn)
  })

  # Test: AE causality assignment
  test_that("AEACNOTH populated when AEACN is 'Other'", {
    test_ae <- data.frame(
      AEACN = c("DOSE INCREASED", "OTHER"),
      AEACNOTH = c(NA, "Custom action")
    )

    adae <- test_ae %>%
      mutate(
        check_aeacnoth = ifelse(AEACN == "OTHER" & is.na(AEACNOTH), 1, 0)
      )

    expect_true(all(adae$check_aeacnoth == 0))
  })

  # Test: Missing data handling
  test_that("Missing values handled correctly in analysis", {
    test_data <- data.frame(
      SUBJID = c(1, 2, 3),
      AEVAL = c(100, NA, 110)
    )

    # Filter out missing analyses
    analyzed <- test_data %>%
      filter(!is.na(AEVAL))

    expect_equal(nrow(analyzed), 2)
    expect_false(any(is.na(analyzed$AEVAL)))
  })
}

#> Test passed with 1 success 😀.
#> Test passed with 1 success 🌈.
#> Test passed with 2 successes 🎉.




33.4.4 22.4.4 Running Tests and Test Coverage
Run all tests in a project:
# Run tests
Rscript -e "testthat::test_dir('tests')"

# View test summary
testthat::test_file('tests/test-adae.R')
Check test coverage (percentage of code covered by tests) with covr:

if (requireNamespace("covr", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(covr)

  # Report coverage for a file
  file_coverage <- file_coverage("R/process_ae.R", "tests/test-adae.R")
  report(file_coverage)

  # Visualize coverage
  shine(file_coverage)
}

Goal: Aim for ≥90% code coverage in validation-critical functions.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.5 22.5 Validation Documentation

33.5.1 22.5.1 Validation Plan
A Validation Plan is a document that outlines the scope, approach, and acceptance criteria for validating a software system. For an R-based ADaM generation system, it might include:
Section 1: System Overview - System name: “ADaM Dataset Generation System (R-based)” - Scope: SDTM → ADaM conversion for efficacy and safety variables - Users: Biostatisticians, statistical programmers
Section 2: Validation Approach - IQ: Verify R environment, package versions, dependencies - OQ: Unit tests for core functions (change from baseline, AESI assignment, etc.) - PQ: End-to-end tests with realistic clinical datasets (pharmaversesdtm)
Section 3: Test Cases - Change from baseline: 15 test cases (normal values, missing data, outliers) - AE flag assignment: 8 test cases (serious, related, action taken) - Baseline definitions: 5 test cases (different visit selections)
Section 4: Acceptance Criteria - All test cases must pass without errors or warnings - Code coverage ≥90% - Double programming reconciliation completed (0 discrepancies)
Section 5: Evidence - Test results (logs) - Code coverage reports - Signed reconciliation forms


33.5.2 22.5.2 Test Script Template
A Test Script documents individual test cases and results:
TEST SCRIPT: Change from Baseline Calculation
Test ID: TC_CHFB_001
Test Objective: Verify change from baseline is calculated correctly

Input Data:
  Subject 101: Baseline = 100, Visit 2 = 110
  Subject 102: Baseline = 50, Visit 2 = 55

Expected Output:
  Subject 101: CHG = 10, PCHG = 10%
  Subject 102: CHG = 5, PCHG = 10%

Test Result: PASSED ✓
Date Executed: 2024-01-15
Executed By: John Smith (Biostatistician)
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.6 22.6 Dataset Comparison: diffdf and arsenal

33.6.1 22.6.1 diffdf for Detailed Comparisons
We’ve seen diffdf briefly; let’s use it more comprehensively:

if (requireNamespace("diffdf", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(diffdf)

  # Compare two ADSL datasets (expected vs. actual)
  diff_result <- diffdf(
    base = adsl_expected,
    compare = adsl_actual,
    keys = c("SUBJID")
  )

  # Check for differences
  if (!is.null(diff_result$comparison_result)) {
    cat("Differences found:\n")
    print(diff_result$comparison_result)
  } else {
    cat("No differences: datasets are identical\n")
  }

  # Export report
  diffdf_output <- capture.output(print(diff_result))
  writeLines(diffdf_output, "adsl_comparison_report.txt")
}



33.6.2 22.6.2 arsenal for Demographic/Safety Comparisons
The arsenal package (from Mayo Clinic) excels at comparing datasets with categorical and continuous variables:

if (requireNamespace("arsenal", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(arsenal)

  # Compare demographics between Programmer A and B
  compare_result <- comparedf(
    adsl_a,
    adsl_b,
    id = c("SUBJID")
  )

  # View summary
  summary(compare_result)

  # Create a detailed report
  cat("[Comparison saved to adsl_comparison.csv]
")  # write.csv(compare_result, file = "adsl_comparison.csv")
}
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.7 22.7 R Package Validation: pharmaR Framework
For organizations using custom R packages in clinical programming, pharmaR provides a framework for validating packages according to GAMP 5 and ICH standards.

33.7.1 22.7.1 pharmaR Assessment
The pharmaR package (from Roche/Pharmaverse) assesses package maturity:

if (requireNamespace("riskmetric", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(riskmetric)
  library(pharmaR)

  # Assess a package
  pkg_assessment <- assess(
    pkg_ref = "dplyr"  # or any CRAN package
  )

  # View risk metrics
  summary(pkg_assessment)

  # Display risk ratings
  print(pkg_assessment)
}

Output includes: - Download Statistics: CRAN downloads per month (higher = more tested) - Maintenance: Time since last update - Testing: Unit test coverage, CI/CD (GitHub Actions, etc.) - Documentation: Vignettes, man pages


33.7.2 22.7.2 PHUSE Guidelines
The PHUSE (Pharmaceutical Users Software Exchange) organization publishes guidelines for validating R packages. Key recommendations:

Choose Well-Maintained Packages: Prefer packages with active development and large user bases.
Document Dependencies: Use renv to lock package versions.
Test with Clinical Data: Run packages against realistic ADaM datasets.
Maintain Change Logs: Track package version updates and testing results.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.8 22.8 Controlled R Environments with renv

33.8.1 22.8.1 Why renv Matters for Validation
Without renv, reproducibility is fragile: - A colleague’s system has a different R version. - A new package update changes behavior. - Six months later, you can’t re-run an analysis.
renv solves this by “freezing” your project’s R environment:
Project A (2024-01-15):
  R 4.3.2
  dplyr 1.1.0
  ggplot2 3.4.1

Project B (2023-06-20):
  R 4.2.0
  dplyr 1.0.10
  ggplot2 3.3.6
When you open Project A, renv automatically loads the frozen environment, ensuring reproducibility.


33.8.2 22.8.2 renv Workflow
Initialize renv in a project:
library(renv)
renv::init()
This creates: - renv.lock: JSON file listing all packages and versions - renv/: Isolated library directory - .Rprofile: Startup script activating renv
Install/update packages:
# Install a package (recorded in renv.lock)
install.packages("dplyr")

# Update renv.lock with new snapshot
renv::snapshot()
Restore environment on another machine:
# On colleague's machine or after git clone
renv::restore()  # Installs all packages from renv.lock


33.8.3 22.8.3 Example renv.lock File
{
  "R": {
    "Version": "4.3.2",
    "Repositories": [
      {
        "Name": "CRAN",
        "URL": "https://cran.rstudio.com"
      }
    ]
  },
  "Packages": {
    "dplyr": {
      "Package": "dplyr",
      "Version": "1.1.0",
      "Source": "Repository",
      "Repository": "CRAN"
    },
    "ggplot2": {
      "Package": "ggplot2",
      "Version": "3.4.1",
      "Source": "Repository",
      "Repository": "CRAN"
    },
    "gtsummary": {
      "Package": "gtsummary",
      "Version": "1.7.0",
      "Source": "Repository",
      "Repository": "CRAN",
      "Requires": ["dplyr", "ggplot2"]
    }
  }
}

33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.10 22.10 Audit Trails and Traceability Logging

33.10.1 22.10.1 Why Audit Trails Matter
21 CFR Part 11 requires a permanent record: who accessed/modified data, what changed, when it changed. While git handles code changes, logging handles data processing.


33.10.2 22.10.2 Logging with logger Package
The logger package provides flexible, production-ready logging:

if (requireNamespace("logger", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(logger)

  # Configure logging
  log_appender(appender_file("analysis.log"))
  log_level(TRACE)

  # Log key processing steps
  log_info("Starting ADaM generation for study XYZ-201")
  log_debug(paste("Loading SDTM data from", sdtm_file))

  # Log data transformations
  adae <- ae %>%
    filter(AESER == "Y")
  log_info(paste("Filtered to SAEs: N =", nrow(adae)))

  # Log data quality issues
  missing_count <- sum(is.na(dm$AGE))
  if (missing_count > 0) {
    log_warn(paste("Missing AGE values:", missing_count))
  }

  log_info("ADaM generation completed successfully")
}

This generates a log file:
[2024-01-15 10:23:45] INFO | Starting ADaM generation for study XYZ-201
[2024-01-15 10:23:46] DEBUG | Loading SDTM data from /data/sdtm_2024_01_15.rds
[2024-01-15 10:23:47] INFO | Filtered to SAEs: N = 42
[2024-01-15 10:23:48] WARN | Missing AGE values: 3
[2024-01-15 10:23:49] INFO | ADaM generation completed successfully


33.10.3 22.10.3 Using futile.logger for Lightweight Logging
For simpler setups, futile.logger is lightweight:

if (requireNamespace("futile.logger", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(futile.logger)

  # Set log level
  flog.threshold(INFO)

  # Log messages
  flog.info("Beginning efficacy analysis")
  flog.warn("Sample size smaller than planned: N = %d", n_actual)
  flog.error("Unable to load dataset: %s", filename)

  # Log to file
  flog.appender(appender.file("report_log.txt"))
}

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.12 22.12 Checklist for Clinical Programming Validation
Use this checklist to ensure comprehensive validation:

Environment: renv.lock created and tested
Unit Tests: All core functions have testthat tests (≥90% coverage)
Double Programming: Independent programmer reconciliation completed
Dataset Comparison: diffdf comparison report generated
CDISC Compliance: ADaM variables validated against standards
Logging: All processing steps logged to audit trail
Documentation: Validation plan and test scripts documented
Version Control: Code and documentation committed to git
Sign-off: Biostatistician and QA reviewer signatures on validation report
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.13 22.13 Troubleshooting Validation Issues








Issue
Cause
Resolution




Test failures in CI/CD but pass locally
Different R or package versions
Use renv::restore() in CI


diffdf shows rounding differences
Different decimal places
Use tolerance: tolerance = 0.001


Logging generates huge files
Too much DEBUG-level logging
Lower log_level or filter by logger name


Unit tests fail on missing data
Test data doesn’t include all edge cases
Add tests with NA, NaN, Inf


CDISC validation fails on terminology
Non-standard visit names
Use controlled terminology (CDISC library)
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.14 22.14 Summary

Validation in clinical programming ensures regulatory compliance and data integrity.
21 CFR Part 11 requires audit trails; version control and logging satisfy this.
Double programming is gold standard; use diffdf to identify discrepancies.
Unit testing with testthat provides evidence of correctness; aim for ≥90% coverage.
renv freezes R environments, ensuring reproducibility across time and collaborators.
CDISC compliance checks verify that ADaM datasets meet regulatory standards.
Logging with logger or futile.logger creates permanent records of processing steps.
Comprehensive validation workflows combine all these techniques into a cohesive system.
crumbs: Part VI: Reporting and Regulatory Outputs
33  Validation and Quality Control

title: 33  Validation and Quality Control
text:
33.15 22.15 Exercises
Exercise 22.1: Create a test suite using testthat for a function that calculates change from baseline. Include tests for normal cases, missing data, and zero baseline values. Run covr to measure test coverage.
Exercise 22.2: Simulate a double programming scenario. Programmer A derives an ADSL dataset using dplyr; Programmer B uses data.table. Compare outputs using diffdf and document discrepancies.
Exercise 22.3: Initialize renv in a clinical programming project. Specify R 4.3.0, dplyr 1.1.0, and ggplot2 3.4.1. Create renv.lock and verify it can be restored on a fresh R environment.
Exercise 22.4: Write a validate_adam() function that checks: - Required variables (STUDYID, USUBJID, AVAL) - No missing AVAL in analysis population - Valid CDISC visit codes Test with pharmaversesdtm::adsl.
Exercise 22.5: Implement logging in a complete ADaM generation script. Log key steps (data load, filtering, transformations) with appropriate log levels (INFO, WARN, ERROR). Export the log file and review for compliance.

Key References: - 21 CFR Part 11: https://www.ecfr.gov/ - ICH GCP Guideline: https://www.ich.org/ - GAMP 5 Guidance: https://www.ispe.org/ - testthat Documentation: https://testthat.r-lib.org/ - diffdf Documentation: https://pharmaverse.github.io/diffdf/ - renv Documentation: https://rstudio.github.io/renv/ - PHUSE Open Source Guidelines: https://www.phuse.global/ - Pharmaverse (Roche/PHUSE): https://pharmaverse.org/

title: 34  End-to-End Clinical Trial Analysis
text:
34.1 Introduction: The Complete Clinical Trial Pipeline
A clinical trial generates terabytes of data from dozens of sources: enrollment forms, vital signs monitors, laboratory instruments, adverse event reports, and pharmacy records. Transforming this raw chaos into a regulatory-quality submission requires a rigorous, documented pipeline that bridges study design, data collection, standardisation, analysis, and reporting.
This chapter walks through a Phase III randomised controlled trial for hypertension — a common, well-understood indication that showcases the full spectrum of clinical data challenges: handling missing data, SDTM standardisation, ADaM derivations, endpoint analysis, safety tabulations, and regulatory-grade quality control.
Learning objectives by chapter end: 1. Map raw clinical data to SDTM domains using pharmaversesdtm 2. Build analysis-ready datasets (ADaM) using admiral-style derivations 3. Execute primary and secondary endpoint analyses 4. Generate safety tables and figures 5. Implement QC procedures aligned with FDA/EMA expectations 6. Understand the regulatory landscape (E9, CDISC, CDASH)
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
34  End-to-End Clinical Trial Analysis

title: 34  End-to-End Clinical Trial Analysis
text:
34.2 Step 1: Study Design Overview

34.2.1 CONSORT Flow Diagram (ASCII)
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ SCREENED (n = 500)                                              │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                     │
         ┌───────────┴───────────┐
         │                       │
         ▼                       ▼
    EXCLUDED              RANDOMISED
    (n = 125)             (n = 375)
    • SBP <140
    • SBP >180        ┌─────────┴──────────┐
    • Pregnancy       │                    │
    • Contra-ind      ▼                    ▼
                TREATMENT GROUP      CONTROL GROUP
                (n = 188)            (n = 187)
                 [Drug XYZ]          [Placebo]
                     │                    │
       ┌─────────────┴──────────┐        │
       │ Weeks 1–12 (ITT)       │        │
       │ • Visit 2: Week 2      │        │
       │ • Visit 3: Week 4      │        │
       │ • Visit 4: Week 8      │        │
       │ • Visit 5: Week 12     │        │
       └────────┬────────────────┘        │
                │                        │
      ┌─────────┴────────┐        ┌──────┴────────┐
      │                  │        │               │
   Completed         Withdrew  Completed       Withdrew
   (n = 165)         (n = 23)  (n = 170)       (n = 17)
   • ITT analysis    • LTFU      • ITT analysis  • LTFU
   • PP analysis


34.2.2 Protocol Summary







Aspect
Detail




Study Title
A Phase III, Randomised, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial of XYZ versus Placebo in Subjects with Essential Hypertension


Indication
Essential hypertension (SBP 140–180 mmHg)


Population
Adults 18–75 years, baseline SBP 140–180 mmHg, DBP 90–120 mmHg


Sample Size
375 randomised (188 active, 187 placebo)


Treatment Duration
12 weeks active treatment


Primary Efficacy Endpoint
Change in mean seated SBP from baseline to Week 12 (ITT, ANCOVA)


Secondary Endpoints
DBP change; response rate (SBP reduction ≥20 mmHg); DBP response (≥10 mmHg); safety/tolerability


Randomisation
1:1 active:placebo, stratified by baseline SBP category (<160, ≥160 mmHg)


Analysis Population
Intent-to-treat (ITT); Per-protocol (PP); Safety
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
34  End-to-End Clinical Trial Analysis

title: 34  End-to-End Clinical Trial Analysis
section: 34.3 Step 2: Raw Data to SDTM — Pharmaversesdtm Domains
text:
34.3 Step 2: Raw Data to SDTM — Pharmaversesdtm Domains
SDTM (Study Data Tabulation Model) is the FDA-mandated standard for clinical trial data. pharmaversesdtm provides example data in SDTM format across six domains critical for a hypertension trial:

DM — Demographics
VS — Vital Signs (blood pressure, heart rate)
LB — Laboratory Results (electrolytes, renal function)
AE — Adverse Events
EX — Exposure (dosing records)
EC — Findings (visit dates, randomisation flags)


34.3.1 2.1 Load and Explore Pharmaversesdtm Data

library(pharmaversesdtm)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)
library(haven)

# Load SDTM domains from pharmaversesdtm
data("dm")
data("vs")
data("lb")
data("ae")
data("ex")

# Display structure and dimensions
cat("DM (Demographics):\n")

#> DM (Demographics):

cat("  Dimensions:", nrow(dm), "×", ncol(dm), "\n")

#>   Dimensions: 306 × 28

cat("  Key variables: USUBJID, AGE, SEX, RACE, ARMCD\n\n")

#>   Key variables: USUBJID, AGE, SEX, RACE, ARMCD

cat("VS (Vital Signs):\n")

#> VS (Vital Signs):

cat("  Dimensions:", nrow(vs), "×", ncol(vs), "\n")

#>   Dimensions: 29643 × 24

cat("  Key variables: USUBJID, VSTESTCD, VSVAL, VSvisit\n\n")

#>   Key variables: USUBJID, VSTESTCD, VSVAL, VSvisit

cat("LB (Laboratory):\n")

#> LB (Laboratory):

cat("  Dimensions:", nrow(lb), "×", ncol(lb), "\n")

#>   Dimensions: 59580 × 23

cat("  Key variables: USUBJID, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESN, VISIT\n\n")

#>   Key variables: USUBJID, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESN, VISIT

cat("AE (Adverse Events):\n")

#> AE (Adverse Events):

cat("  Dimensions:", nrow(ae), "×", ncol(ae), "\n")

#>   Dimensions: 1191 × 35

cat("  Key variables: USUBJID, AETERM, AESEV, AESER\n\n")

#>   Key variables: USUBJID, AETERM, AESEV, AESER

cat("EX (Exposure):\n")

#> EX (Exposure):

cat("  Dimensions:", nrow(ex), "×", ncol(ex), "\n")

#>   Dimensions: 591 × 17

cat("  Key variables: USUBJID, EXDOSE, EXDOSU, EXSTDTC\n")

#>   Key variables: USUBJID, EXDOSE, EXDOSU, EXSTDTC

# Quick summary of randomisation
table(dm$ARMCD)

#> 
#>      Pbo Scrnfail   Xan_Hi   Xan_Lo 
#>       86       52       84       84




34.3.2 2.2 Inspect SDTM Vital Signs Domain
The VS domain contains repeated blood pressure measurements — the cornerstone of hypertension trials.

# Focus on SBP and DBP measurements
vs_bp <- vs %>%
  filter(VSTESTCD %in% c("SYSBP", "DIABP")) %>%
  left_join(dm %>% filter(ARM != "Screen Failure") %>% select(USUBJID, ARM), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  select(USUBJID, VISIT, VSTESTCD, VSVAL = VSSTRESN, VSDT = VSDTC, ARM) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID, VISIT, VSTESTCD)

# Check for missing values
cat("Missing values in VS BP data:\n")

#> Missing values in VS BP data:

print(summary(vs_bp))

#>    USUBJID             VISIT             VSTESTCD             VSVAL      
#>  Length:16415       Length:16415       Length:16415       Min.   : 39.0  
#>  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.: 76.0  
#>  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median : 98.0  
#>                                                           Mean   :105.1  
#>                                                           3rd Qu.:132.0  
#>                                                           Max.   :217.0  
#>                                                           NA's   :5      
#>      VSDT               ARM           
#>  Length:16415       Length:16415      
#>  Class :character   Class :character  
#>  Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
#>                                       
#>                                       
#>                                       
#> 

# Pivot to wide format for review
vs_wide <- vs_bp %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, VISIT, VSDT, ARM, VSTESTCD) %>%
  summarise(VSVAL = mean(VSVAL, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = c(USUBJID, VISIT, VSDT, ARM),
    names_from = VSTESTCD,
    values_from = VSVAL
  )

cat("\nFirst 10 BP records (wide):\n")

#> 
#> First 10 BP records (wide):

print(head(vs_wide, 10))

#> # A tibble: 10 × 6

#>    <chr>       <chr>               <chr>      <chr>   <dbl> <dbl>
#>  1 01-701-1015 AMBUL ECG PLACEMENT 2014-01-14 Placebo  64.3  137.
#>  2 01-701-1015 AMBUL ECG REMOVAL   2014-02-01 Placebo  62    131.
#>  3 01-701-1015 BASELINE            2014-01-02 Placebo  56    127.
#>  4 01-701-1015 SCREENING 1         2013-12-26 Placebo  68    136.
#>  5 01-701-1015 SCREENING 2         2013-12-31 Placebo  66    140 
#>  6 01-701-1015 WEEK 12             2014-03-26 Placebo  64    141 
#>  7 01-701-1015 WEEK 16             2014-05-07 Placebo  66.7  153.
#>  8 01-701-1015 WEEK 2              2014-01-16 Placebo  53.3  122.
#>  9 01-701-1015 WEEK 20             2014-05-21 Placebo  65    137.
#> 10 01-701-1015 WEEK 24             2014-06-18 Placebo  63.7  132.

# Check visit structure
cat("\nUnique visits:\n")

#> 
#> Unique visits:

print(unique(vs_bp$VISIT))

#>  [1] "AMBUL ECG PLACEMENT" "AMBUL ECG REMOVAL"   "BASELINE"           
#>  [4] "SCREENING 1"         "SCREENING 2"         "WEEK 12"            
#>  [7] "WEEK 16"             "WEEK 2"              "WEEK 20"            
#> [10] "WEEK 24"             "WEEK 26"             "WEEK 4"             
#> [13] "WEEK 6"              "WEEK 8"              "RETRIEVAL"          
#> [16] "UNSCHEDULED 3.1"




34.3.3 2.3 Inspect SDTM Demographics and Randomisation

# Demographics summary by arm
dm_summary <- dm %>%
  select(USUBJID, ARMCD, AGE, SEX, RACE, ETHNIC) %>%
  group_by(ARMCD) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Mean_Age = mean(AGE, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD_Age = sd(AGE, na.rm = TRUE),
    Pct_Female = round(100 * sum(SEX == "F", na.rm = TRUE) / n(), 1),
    Pct_White = round(100 * sum(RACE == "WHITE", na.rm = TRUE) / n(), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Demographics by Treatment Arm:\n")

#> Demographics by Treatment Arm:

print(dm_summary)

#> # A tibble: 4 × 6
#>   ARMCD        N Mean_Age SD_Age Pct_Female Pct_White
#>   <chr>    <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>
#> 1 Pbo         86     75.2   8.59       61.6      90.7
#> 2 Scrnfail    52     75.1   9.70       69.2      82.7
#> 3 Xan_Hi      84     74.4   7.89       47.6      88.1
#> 4 Xan_Lo      84     75.7   8.29       59.5      92.9

# Baseline SBP (earliest record per subject)
baseline_sbp <- vs_bp %>%
  filter(VSTESTCD == "SYSBP", !is.na(VSVAL)) %>%
  group_by(USUBJID, ARM) %>%
  slice_min(VSDT, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    Mean_SBP = mean(VSVAL, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD_SBP = sd(VSVAL, na.rm = TRUE),
    Median_SBP = median(VSVAL, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("\nBaseline Systolic BP by Arm:\n")

#> 
#> Baseline Systolic BP by Arm:

print(baseline_sbp)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 5
#>   ARM                      N Mean_SBP SD_SBP Median_SBP
#>   <chr>                <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>      <dbl>
#> 1 Placebo                 86     143.   19.2        141
#> 2 Xanomeline High Dose    84     141.   19.4        140
#> 3 Xanomeline Low Dose     84     141.   17.3        140




34.3.4 2.4 Laboratory Domain

# Key safety labs: potassium, creatinine
lb_safety <- lb %>%
  filter(LBTESTCD %in% c("POTASSIUM", "CREATININE")) %>%
  select(USUBJID, VISIT, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESN, LBSTRESU, LBSTNRHI) %>%
  arrange(USUBJID, VISIT, LBTESTCD)

cat("Safety Laboratory Parameters (first 15 records):\n")

#> Safety Laboratory Parameters (first 15 records):

print(head(lb_safety, 15))

#> # A tibble: 0 × 6

#> #   LBSTRESU <chr>, LBSTNRHI <dbl>

# Lab counts by test
cat("\nLaboratory test counts:\n")

#> 
#> Laboratory test counts:

print(table(lb$LBTESTCD))

#>     ALB     ALP     ALT   ANISO     AST    BASO  BASOLE    BILI     BUN      CA

#>     HCT     HGB       K KETONES     LYM   LYMLE MACROCY     MCH    MCHC     MCV 
#>    1790    1809    1802     874    1796      12     102    1809    1790    1790 
#> MICROCY    MONO  MONOLE      PH    PHOS    PLAT POIKILO POLYCHR    PROT     RBC 
#>       2    1796      12     874    1822    1788       2      29    1828    1809 
#>  SODIUM  SPGRAV     TSH   URATE  UROBIL  VITB12     WBC 
#>    1808     874     271    1828     874     272    1809




34.3.5 2.5 Adverse Events Domain

# Join ARM from dm for AE summaries
ae_with_arm <- ae %>%
  left_join(dm %>% filter(ARM != "Screen Failure") %>% select(USUBJID, ARM), by = "USUBJID") %>%
  filter(!is.na(ARM))

# AE summary
ae_summary <- ae_with_arm %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_AEs = n(),
    Subjects_with_AE = n_distinct(USUBJID),
    Serious_AEs = sum(AESER == "Y", na.rm = TRUE),
    Severe_AEs = sum(AESEV == "SEVERE", na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Adverse Event Summary by Arm:\n")

#> Adverse Event Summary by Arm:

print(ae_summary)

#> # A tibble: 3 × 5
#>   ARM                  Total_AEs Subjects_with_AE Serious_AEs Severe_AEs

#> 2 Xanomeline High Dose       455               79           2         10

# Top 10 AE terms
ae_top <- ae_with_arm %>%
  group_by(AETERM, ARM) %>%
  summarise(Count = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(Count)) %>%
  head(10)

#> Top 10 Adverse Event Terms:

print(ae_top)
