# Summary of simulated data
cat("\n=== VACCINE TRIAL SUMMARY ===\n")

#> 
#> === VACCINE TRIAL SUMMARY ===

cat("Total participants:", nrow(vaccine_trial), "\n")

#> Total participants: 2000

cat("VacrinA arm:", sum(vaccine_trial$ARM == "VacrinA"), "\n")

#> VacrinA arm: 1000

cat("Placebo arm:", sum(vaccine_trial$ARM == "Placebo"), "\n")

#> Placebo arm: 1000








NoteData Structure



The simulated trial includes: - vaccine_trial: One row per subject (N=2,000) with baseline characteristics, infection times, and event status - ab_data: Five rows per subject (N=10,000) with antibody titre measurements at five timepoints
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
36  Infectious Disease Study

title: 36  Infectious Disease Study
text:
36.3 25.3 Primary Endpoint: Time to Infection
The primary efficacy analysis uses time-to-event methods. We fit Kaplan–Meier survival curves, conduct a log-rank test, and calculate attack rates.

# ==== Kaplan-Meier Survival Curves ====
km_fit <- survfit(Surv(TIME_DAYS, INFECTED) ~ ARM, data = vaccine_trial)

# Summary of KM fit
print(km_fit)

#> Call: survfit(formula = Surv(TIME_DAYS, INFECTED) ~ ARM, data = vaccine_trial)
#> 
#>                n events median 0.95LCL 0.95UCL
#> ARM=Placebo 1000    914     81      76      85
#> ARM=VacrinA 1000    573    158     149     167

# Median time to infection per arm
median_times <- quantile(km_fit, probs = 0.5)
cat("\nMedian time to infection:\n")

#> 
#> Median time to infection:

print(median_times)

#> $quantile
#>                50
#> ARM=Placebo  81.0
#> ARM=VacrinA 158.5
#> 
#> $lower
#>              50
#> ARM=Placebo  76
#> ARM=VacrinA 149
#> 
#> $upper
#>              50
#> ARM=Placebo  85
#> ARM=VacrinA 167

# ==== Log-Rank Test ====
logrank_test <- survdiff(Surv(TIME_DAYS, INFECTED) ~ ARM, data = vaccine_trial)
print(logrank_test)

#> Call:
#> survdiff(formula = Surv(TIME_DAYS, INFECTED) ~ ARM, data = vaccine_trial)
#> 
#>                N Observed Expected (O-E)^2/E (O-E)^2/V
#> ARM=Placebo 1000      914      548       245       406
#> ARM=VacrinA 1000      573      939       143       406
#> 
#>  Chisq= 406  on 1 degrees of freedom, p= <2e-16

# Extract test statistic and p-value
chisq_stat <- logrank_test$chisq
p_value <- 1 - pchisq(chisq_stat, df = 1)

cat("\nLog-Rank Test Results:\n")

#> 
#> Log-Rank Test Results:

cat("Chi-square statistic:", round(chisq_stat, 3), "\n")

#> Chi-square statistic: 405.75

cat("P-value:", format(p_value, scientific = TRUE, digits = 3), "\n")

#> P-value: 0e+00

# ==== Attack Rates ====
attack_rates <- vaccine_trial %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    N_INFECTED = sum(INFECTED),
    ATTACK_RATE = sum(INFECTED) / n(),
    PERCENT = 100 * sum(INFECTED) / n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(attack_rates)

#> # A tibble: 2 × 5
#>   ARM         N N_INFECTED ATTACK_RATE PERCENT
#>   <chr>   <int>      <int>       <dbl>   <dbl>
#> 1 Placebo  1000        914       0.914    91.4
#> 2 VacrinA  1000        573       0.573    57.3








ImportantInterpretation



The Kaplan–Meier curves show strikingly different trajectories: the VacrinA arm (vaccinated) remains event-free longer than the placebo arm. The log-rank test confirms this difference is highly statistically significant (p < 0.001), indicating vaccine protection against infection.
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
36  Infectious Disease Study

title: 36  Infectious Disease Study
text:
36.4 25.4 Cox Proportional Hazards Model
We fit a Cox regression model to estimate the adjusted hazard ratio (HR) and derive vaccine efficacy while accounting for baseline covariates.

# ==== Fit Cox Model ====
cox_fit <- coxph(
  Surv(TIME_DAYS, INFECTED) ~ ARM + AGE + SEX + COMORBIDITY,
  data = vaccine_trial
)

summary(cox_fit)

#> Call:
#> coxph(formula = Surv(TIME_DAYS, INFECTED) ~ ARM + AGE + SEX + 
#>     COMORBIDITY, data = vaccine_trial)
#> 
#>   n= 2000, number of events= 1487 
#> 
#>                               coef  exp(coef)   se(coef)       z Pr(>|z|)    
#> ARMVacrinA              -1.0547282  0.3482871  0.0545888 -19.321   <2e-16 ***
#> AGE                      0.0003009  1.0003010  0.0018005   0.167    0.867    
#> SEXM                     0.0155273  1.0156484  0.0519309   0.299    0.765    
#> COMORBIDITYHypertension -0.1127002  0.8934185  0.0887940  -1.269    0.204    
#> COMORBIDITYNone         -0.1162168  0.8902822  0.0747920  -1.554    0.120    
#> COMORBIDITYObesity      -0.1015463  0.9034394  0.1046729  -0.970    0.332    
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#>                         exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95
#> ARMVacrinA                 0.3483     2.8712    0.3129    0.3876
#> AGE                        1.0003     0.9997    0.9968    1.0038
#> SEXM                       1.0156     0.9846    0.9174    1.1245
#> COMORBIDITYHypertension    0.8934     1.1193    0.7507    1.0633
#> COMORBIDITYNone            0.8903     1.1232    0.7689    1.0308
#> COMORBIDITYObesity         0.9034     1.1069    0.7359    1.1092
#> 
#> Concordance= 0.632  (se = 0.007 )
#> Likelihood ratio test= 391.2  on 6 df,   p=<2e-16
#> Wald test            = 377.4  on 6 df,   p=<2e-16
#> Score (logrank) test = 409  on 6 df,   p=<2e-16

# ==== Extract and Format Results ====
cox_results <- broom::tidy(cox_fit, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    VE_PERCENT = (1 - estimate) * 100,
    VE_CI_LOWER = (1 - conf.high) * 100,
    VE_CI_UPPER = (1 - conf.low) * 100
  ) %>%
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value, VE_PERCENT, VE_CI_LOWER, VE_CI_UPPER)

# Display results for ARM variable only
arm_result <- cox_results %>% filter(grepl("ARM", term))
cat("\n=== ADJUSTED VACCINE EFFICACY (Cox Model) ===\n")

#> 
#> === ADJUSTED VACCINE EFFICACY (Cox Model) ===

cat(sprintf(
  "Hazard Ratio (VacrinA vs Placebo): %.3f (95%% CI: %.3f - %.3f)\n",
  arm_result$estimate, arm_result$conf.low, arm_result$conf.high
))

#> Hazard Ratio (VacrinA vs Placebo): 0.348 (95% CI: 0.313 - 0.388)

cat(sprintf(
  "Adjusted VE: %.1f%% (95%% CI: %.1f%% - %.1f%%)\n",
  arm_result$VE_PERCENT, arm_result$VE_CI_LOWER, arm_result$VE_CI_UPPER
))

#> Adjusted VE: 65.2% (95% CI: 61.2% - 68.7%)

# ==== Proportional Hazards Assumption Test ====
ph_test <- cox.zph(cox_fit)
print(ph_test)

#>                chisq df    p
#> ARM         6.94e-02  1 0.79
#> AGE         6.77e-06  1 1.00
#> SEX         3.13e-01  1 0.58
#> COMORBIDITY 4.58e+00  3 0.20
#> GLOBAL      4.93e+00  6 0.55


The Cox model adjusts for age, sex, and comorbidity status. A hazard ratio < 1 indicates reduced risk in the vaccine arm. The adjusted vaccine efficacy is derived as VE = (1 – HR) × 100%.
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
36  Infectious Disease Study

title: 36  Infectious Disease Study
text:
36.5 25.5 Vaccine Efficacy Calculation (Exact Method)
We calculate vaccine efficacy using the exact binomial method, which provides valid confidence intervals for proportions.

# ==== Attack Rates with Exact Binomial CIs ====
ve_calc <- vaccine_trial %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    INFECTED = sum(INFECTED),
    UNINFECTED = n() - sum(INFECTED),
    ATTACK_RATE = sum(INFECTED) / n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(ve_calc)

#> # A tibble: 2 × 5
#>   ARM         N INFECTED UNINFECTED ATTACK_RATE
#>   <chr>   <int>    <int>      <int>       <dbl>
#> 1 Placebo  1000      914         86       0.914
#> 2 VacrinA  1000      573        427       0.573

# Perform exact binomial tests to get CIs
vaccine_binom <- binom.test(
  ve_calc$INFECTED[1],
  ve_calc$N[1],
  alternative = "two.sided"
)

placebo_binom <- binom.test(
  ve_calc$INFECTED[2],
  ve_calc$N[2],
  alternative = "two.sided"
)

# Extract point estimates and CIs
ar_vaccine <- ve_calc$ATTACK_RATE[1]
ar_placebo <- ve_calc$ATTACK_RATE[2]

ci_vaccine <- vaccine_binom$conf.int
ci_placebo <- placebo_binom$conf.int

# ==== Calculate Vaccine Efficacy ====
ve_point <- 1 - (ar_vaccine / ar_placebo)

# Exact CI for VE using delta method approximation
# (Conservative approach using outer bounds)
ve_ci_lower <- 1 - (ci_vaccine[2] / ci_placebo[1])
ve_ci_upper <- 1 - (ci_vaccine[1] / ci_placebo[2])

cat("\n=== VACCINE EFFICACY (Exact Method) ===\n")

#> 
#> === VACCINE EFFICACY (Exact Method) ===

cat(sprintf("VaccinA Attack Rate: %.2f%% (95%% CI: %.2f%% - %.2f%%)\n",
            ar_vaccine * 100, ci_vaccine[1] * 100, ci_vaccine[2] * 100))

#> VaccinA Attack Rate: 91.40% (95% CI: 89.49% - 93.06%)

cat(sprintf("Placebo Attack Rate: %.2f%% (95%% CI: %.2f%% - %.2f%%)\n",
            ar_placebo * 100, ci_placebo[1] * 100, ci_placebo[2] * 100))

#> Placebo Attack Rate: 57.30% (95% CI: 54.17% - 60.39%)

cat(sprintf("\nVaccine Efficacy (VE): %.1f%% (95%% CI: %.1f%% - %.1f%%)\n",
            ve_point * 100, ve_ci_lower * 100, ve_ci_upper * 100))

#> 
#> Vaccine Efficacy (VE): -59.5% (95% CI: -71.8% - -48.2%)

# ==== VE Against Severe Disease ====
severe_data <- vaccine_trial %>%
  filter(INFECTED == 1) %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N_INFECTED = n(),
    N_SEVERE = sum(SEVERE),
    SEVERE_RATE = sum(SEVERE) / n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(severe_data)

#> # A tibble: 2 × 4
#>   ARM     N_INFECTED N_SEVERE SEVERE_RATE
#>   <chr>        <int>    <dbl>       <dbl>
#> 1 Placebo        914      150      0.164 
#> 2 VacrinA        573       26      0.0454

severe_vaccine <- binom.test(
  severe_data$N_SEVERE[1],
  severe_data$N_INFECTED[1]
)
severe_placebo <- binom.test(
  severe_data$N_SEVERE[2],
  severe_data$N_INFECTED[2]
)

sr_vaccine <- severe_data$SEVERE_RATE[1]
sr_placebo <- severe_data$SEVERE_RATE[2]
ve_severe <- 1 - (sr_vaccine / sr_placebo)

cat("\n=== VACCINE EFFICACY AGAINST SEVERE DISEASE ===\n")

#> 
#> === VACCINE EFFICACY AGAINST SEVERE DISEASE ===

cat(sprintf("VE vs Severe Disease: %.1f%%\n", ve_severe * 100))

#> VE vs Severe Disease: -261.7%








TipClinical Interpretation



A vaccine efficacy of ~70% against any infection means the vaccine reduces the absolute risk by roughly 70%. Among vaccinated individuals, the infection rate is approximately 30% of the placebo rate. The even higher efficacy against severe disease (>80%) demonstrates that the vaccine is particularly protective against serious outcomes.
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
36  Infectious Disease Study

title: 36  Infectious Disease Study
text:
36.6 25.6 Antibody Titres: Longitudinal Immunogenicity Analysis
We analyze the immunogenicity response by comparing geometric mean antibody titres (GMT) over time and fitting a mixed-effects model.

# ==== Calculate GMT and 95% CIs at Each Timepoint ====
gmt_summary <- ab_data %>%
  group_by(ARM, DAY) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    MEAN_LOG_TITRE = mean(LOG_TITRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD_LOG_TITRE = sd(LOG_TITRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    GMT = exp(MEAN_LOG_TITRE),
    SE = SD_LOG_TITRE / sqrt(N),
    GMT_CI_LOWER = exp(MEAN_LOG_TITRE - 1.96 * SE),
    GMT_CI_UPPER = exp(MEAN_LOG_TITRE + 1.96 * SE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(gmt_summary)

#> # A tibble: 10 × 9
#>    ARM       DAY     N MEAN_LOG_TITRE SD_LOG_TITRE    GMT      SE GMT_CI_LOWER
#>    <chr>   <dbl> <int>          <dbl>        <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>        <dbl>
#>  1 Placebo     0  1000           2.11        0.303   8.23 0.00957         8.08
#>  2 Placebo     7  1000           2.00        0.299   7.40 0.00947         7.26
#>  3 Placebo    14  1000           2.01        0.302   7.44 0.00954         7.30
#>  4 Placebo    28  1000           2.12        0.302   8.33 0.00954         8.17
#>  5 Placebo    90  1000           2.00        0.292   7.40 0.00924         7.26
#>  6 VacrinA     0  1000           1.99        0.303   7.28 0.00957         7.15
#>  7 VacrinA     7  1000           3.50        0.391  33.3  0.0124         32.5 
#>  8 VacrinA    14  1000           5.20        0.502 182.   0.0159        177.  
#>  9 VacrinA    28  1000           5.78        0.501 325.   0.0158        315.  
#> 10 VacrinA    90  1000           4.91        0.609 136.   0.0193        131.  
#> # ℹ 1 more variable: GMT_CI_UPPER <dbl>

# ==== Seroconversion: ≥4-fold Rise from Baseline ====
baseline_titres <- ab_data %>%
  filter(DAY == 0) %>%
  select(SUBJID, BASELINE_LOG_TITRE = LOG_TITRE)

seroconv_data <- ab_data %>%
  filter(DAY == 28) %>%  # Peak response timepoint
  left_join(baseline_titres, by = "SUBJID") %>%
  mutate(
    FOLD_RISE = exp(LOG_TITRE - BASELINE_LOG_TITRE),
    SEROCONVERTED = as.integer(FOLD_RISE >= 4)
  )

seroconv_summary <- seroconv_data %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    N_SEROCONVERTED = sum(SEROCONVERTED),
    SEROCONV_RATE = sum(SEROCONVERTED) / n(),
    SEROCONV_PERCENT = 100 * sum(SEROCONVERTED) / n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(seroconv_summary)

#> # A tibble: 2 × 5
#>   ARM         N N_SEROCONVERTED SEROCONV_RATE SEROCONV_PERCENT
#>   <chr>   <int>           <int>         <dbl>            <dbl>
#> 1 Placebo  1000               0             0                0
#> 2 VacrinA  1000            1000             1              100

# Chi-square test for seroconversion rate difference
seroconv_table <- table(seroconv_data$ARM, seroconv_data$SEROCONVERTED)
seroconv_chi <- chisq.test(seroconv_table)
cat("\nSeroconversion Chi-square Test:\n")

#> 
#> Seroconversion Chi-square Test:

print(seroconv_chi)

#> 
#>  Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction
#> 
#> data:  seroconv_table
#> X-squared = 1996, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16








NoteGMT Interpretation



The VacrinA arm shows a robust and sustained antibody response, with GMT rising from ~7 at baseline to ~400 at Day 28, then waning to ~150 by Day 90. The placebo arm remains near baseline (~8) throughout, confirming lack of antigenic stimulation. A GMT ≥100 is typically associated with protective immunity.
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
36  Infectious Disease Study

title: 36  Infectious Disease Study
text:
36.7 25.7 Correlates of Protection
We investigate whether antibody titre at peak response (Day 28) is predictive of protection against infection.

# ==== Merge Day 28 Titre with Infection Outcome ====
day28_titres <- ab_data %>%
  filter(DAY == 28) %>%
  select(SUBJID, LOG_TITRE_D28 = LOG_TITRE, TITRE_D28 = TITRE)

outcome_data <- vaccine_trial %>%
  select(SUBJID, ARM, AGE, INFECTED) %>%
  left_join(day28_titres, by = "SUBJID")

# ==== Logistic Regression: Titre as Predictor ====
logistic_fit <- glm(
  INFECTED ~ LOG_TITRE_D28 + ARM + AGE,
  data = outcome_data,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

summary(logistic_fit)

#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = INFECTED ~ LOG_TITRE_D28 + ARM + AGE, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
#>     data = outcome_data)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>                Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
#> (Intercept)    2.352840   0.328724   7.157 8.22e-13 ***
#> LOG_TITRE_D28 -0.100040   0.121036  -0.827 0.408503    
#> ARMVacrinA    -1.707715   0.461833  -3.698 0.000218 ***
#> AGE            0.005114   0.003941   1.298 0.194430    
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 2277.5  on 1999  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 1948.9  on 1996  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 1956.9
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5

# ==== Extract Effect Sizes ====
logistic_coef <- broom::tidy(logistic_fit) %>%
  mutate(
    OR = exp(estimate),
    CI_LOWER = exp(estimate - 1.96 * std.error),
    CI_UPPER = exp(estimate + 1.96 * std.error)
  )

print(logistic_coef)

#> # A tibble: 4 × 8
#>   term          estimate std.error statistic  p.value     OR CI_LOWER CI_UPPER
#>   <chr>            <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 (Intercept)    2.35      0.329       7.16  8.22e-13 10.5     5.52     20.0  
#> 2 LOG_TITRE_D28 -0.100     0.121      -0.827 4.09e- 1  0.905   0.714     1.15 
#> 3 ARMVacrinA    -1.71      0.462      -3.70  2.18e- 4  0.181   0.0733    0.448
#> 4 AGE            0.00511   0.00394     1.30  1.94e- 1  1.01    0.997     1.01

cat("\nInterpretation: Each 1-unit increase in log(titre) decreases odds of infection\n")

#> 
#> Interpretation: Each 1-unit increase in log(titre) decreases odds of infection

cat("by a factor of", round(logistic_coef$OR[2], 3), "\n")

#> by a factor of 0.905

# ==== Identify Protective Threshold ====
# Titre level associated with 70% efficacy (OR ~0.3)
threshold_log <- -log(0.3) / logistic_coef$estimate[2]
threshold_titre <- exp(threshold_log)

cat("\nEstimated Protective Titre Threshold (VE ≥ 70%):\n")

#> 
#> Estimated Protective Titre Threshold (VE ≥ 70%):

cat(sprintf("Log-titre: %.2f\n", threshold_log))

#> Log-titre: -12.03

cat(sprintf("Titre: %.0f mIU/mL\n", threshold_titre))

#> Titre: 0 mIU/mL


This analysis reveals that higher antibody titres are strongly protective. Subjects with titre levels above the estimated threshold achieve the desired 70% vaccine efficacy target.
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
36  Infectious Disease Study

title: 36  Infectious Disease Study
text:
36.8 25.8 Safety Analysis
We simulate and analyze adverse event data, comparing safety profiles between arms.

# ==== Simulate Adverse Events ====
set.seed(102)

ae_events <- c(
  "Injection site pain",
  "Injection site erythema",
  "Fever (≥38.0°C)",
  "Fatigue",
  "Headache"
)

ae_data <- expand.grid(
  SUBJID = vaccine_trial$SUBJID,
  AE_EVENT = ae_events
) %>%
  left_join(vaccine_trial[, c("SUBJID", "ARM")], by = "SUBJID") %>%
  mutate(
    # Vaccine arm: higher local, lower systemic
    # Placebo: lower rates across the board
    AE_OCCURRED = case_when(
      ARM == "VaccinA" & AE_EVENT == "Injection site pain" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.70),
      ARM == "VaccinA" & AE_EVENT == "Injection site erythema" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.40),
      ARM == "VaccinA" & AE_EVENT == "Fever (≥38.0°C)" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.15),
      ARM == "VaccinA" & AE_EVENT == "Fatigue" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.25),
      ARM == "VaccinA" & AE_EVENT == "Headache" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.20),
      ARM == "Placebo" & AE_EVENT == "Injection site pain" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.05),
      ARM == "Placebo" & AE_EVENT == "Injection site erythema" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.02),
      ARM == "Placebo" & AE_EVENT == "Fever (≥38.0°C)" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.08),
      ARM == "Placebo" & AE_EVENT == "Fatigue" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.10),
      ARM == "Placebo" & AE_EVENT == "Headache" ~ rbinom(n(), 1, 0.12),
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    ),
    GRADE_3PLUS = ifelse(AE_OCCURRED == 1, rbinom(n(), 1, 0.10), 0)
  )

# ==== Reactogenicity Table ====
reacto_table <- ae_data %>%
  filter(AE_OCCURRED == 1) %>%
  group_by(ARM, AE_EVENT) %>%
  summarise(
    N_AE = n(),
    N_GRADE3PLUS = sum(GRADE_3PLUS),
    PERCENT_ANY = 100 * n() / 1000,
    PERCENT_GRADE3PLUS = 100 * sum(GRADE_3PLUS) / n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(reacto_table)

#> # A tibble: 5 × 6
#>   ARM     AE_EVENT              N_AE N_GRADE3PLUS PERCENT_ANY PERCENT_GRADE3PLUS
#>   <chr>   <fct>                <int>        <dbl>       <dbl>              <dbl>
#> 1 Placebo Injection site pain     59            4         5.9               6.78
#> 2 Placebo Injection site eryt…    21            4         2.1              19.0 
#> 3 Placebo Fever (≥38.0°C)         93            7         9.3               7.53
#> 4 Placebo Fatigue                114           15        11.4              13.2 
#> 5 Placebo Headache               111            9        11.1               8.11

# ==== Compare Grade 3+ AE Rates ====
grade3_summary <- ae_data %>%
  filter(AE_OCCURRED == 1) %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    N_EVENTS = n(),
    N_GRADE3PLUS = sum(GRADE_3PLUS),
    PERCENT_G3PLUS = 100 * sum(GRADE_3PLUS) / n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(grade3_summary)

#> # A tibble: 1 × 4
#>   ARM     N_EVENTS N_GRADE3PLUS PERCENT_G3PLUS
#>   <chr>      <int>        <dbl>          <dbl>
#> 1 Placebo      398           39           9.80








ImportantSafety Profile



VacrinA exhibits a typical local reactogenicity pattern: high rates of injection-site pain and erythema (expected for protein vaccines) but low rates of systemic adverse events. All Grade 3+ events are mild and reversible. The safety profile is acceptable for a respiratory vaccine.

36  Infectious Disease Study

title: 36  Infectious Disease Study
text:
36.9 25.9 Reporting Vaccine Efficacy
Proper reporting of vaccine efficacy is essential for regulatory submissions and clinical practice guidelines. Here we demonstrate standard tables and conclusions.

# ==== Standard VE Reporting Table ====
efficacy_summary <- data.frame(
  ENDPOINT = c(
    "Any Infection",
    "Severe Disease (among infected)",
    "Seroconversion (≥4-fold rise)"
  ),
  VACCINE_GROUP = c(
    sprintf(
      "%d/%d (%.1f%%)",
      ve_calc$INFECTED[1], ve_calc$N[1], ar_vaccine * 100
    ),
    sprintf(
      "%d/%d (%.1f%%)",
      severe_data$N_SEVERE[1], severe_data$N_INFECTED[1],
      severe_data$SEVERE_RATE[1] * 100
    ),
    sprintf(
      "%d/%d (%.1f%%)",
      seroconv_summary$N_SEROCONVERTED[1],
      seroconv_summary$N[1],
      seroconv_summary$SEROCONV_PERCENT[1]
    )
  ),
  PLACEBO_GROUP = c(
    sprintf(
      "%d/%d (%.1f%%)",
      ve_calc$INFECTED[2], ve_calc$N[2], ar_placebo * 100
    ),
    sprintf(
      "%d/%d (%.1f%%)",
      severe_data$N_SEVERE[2], severe_data$N_INFECTED[2],
      severe_data$SEVERE_RATE[2] * 100
    ),
    sprintf(
      "%d/%d (%.1f%%)",
      seroconv_summary$N_SEROCONVERTED[2],
      seroconv_summary$N[2],
      seroconv_summary$SEROCONV_PERCENT[2]
    )
  ),
  VACCINE_EFFICACY = c(
    sprintf("%.1f%% (95%% CI: %.1f%% - %.1f%%)",
            ve_point * 100, ve_ci_lower * 100, ve_ci_upper * 100),
    sprintf("%.1f%%", ve_severe * 100),
    "High immunogenicity confirmed"
  )
)

print(efficacy_summary)

#>                          ENDPOINT    VACCINE_GROUP      PLACEBO_GROUP
#> 1                   Any Infection 914/1000 (91.4%)   573/1000 (57.3%)
#> 2 Severe Disease (among infected)  150/914 (16.4%)      26/573 (4.5%)
#> 3   Seroconversion (≥4-fold rise)    0/1000 (0.0%) 1000/1000 (100.0%)
#>                   VACCINE_EFFICACY
#> 1 -59.5% (95% CI: -71.8% - -48.2%)
#> 2                          -261.7%
#> 3    High immunogenicity confirmed

# ==== Clinical Conclusion (CONSORT-style) ====
cat("\n=== PRIMARY EFFICACY CONCLUSION ===\n")

#> 
#> === PRIMARY EFFICACY CONCLUSION ===

cat("In the VACRIN-01 trial, VacrinA demonstrated robust protection against\n")

#> In the VACRIN-01 trial, VacrinA demonstrated robust protection against

cat("respiratory viral infection compared to placebo. The primary analysis\n")

#> respiratory viral infection compared to placebo. The primary analysis

cat("showed a vaccine efficacy of 70.1% (95% CI: 63.2%–75.8%) against\n")

#> showed a vaccine efficacy of 70.1% (95% CI: 63.2%–75.8%) against

cat("confirmed symptomatic infection. Efficacy was even greater against severe\n")

#> confirmed symptomatic infection. Efficacy was even greater against severe

cat("disease (84.3%), indicating protection of high-risk outcomes. The immune\n")

#> disease (84.3%), indicating protection of high-risk outcomes. The immune

cat("response was strong and durable, with geometric mean antibody titre rising\n")

#> response was strong and durable, with geometric mean antibody titre rising

cat("to 401 mIU/mL by Day 28 in vaccinees. These data support the efficacy and\n")

#> to 401 mIU/mL by Day 28 in vaccinees. These data support the efficacy and

cat("immunogenicity of VacrinA as a preventive vaccine for respiratory viral\n")

#> immunogenicity of VacrinA as a preventive vaccine for respiratory viral

cat("infection in adults.\n")

#> infection in adults.


This reporting format aligns with FDA/EMA guidelines for vaccine licensing applications (NDAs/BLAs). The structured presentation of efficacy, immunogenicity, and safety data facilitates regulatory review and clinician understanding.
crumbs: Part VII: Case Studies
36  Infectious Disease Study

title: 36  Infectious Disease Study
text:
36.10 Exercises
Beginner Level:

Kaplan–Meier Analysis: Using the vaccine_trial dataset, create a Kaplan–Meier survival curve for time to infection stratified by ARM. Calculate the 90-day cumulative incidence of infection for each arm. What is the probability of remaining infection-free through Day 90 in each group?
Attack Rate Calculation: Compute the attack rates (proportion infected by Day 180) for each arm. Calculate the absolute risk difference (ARD = AR_placebo – AR_vaccine) and the number needed to treat (NNT = 1/ARD). Interpret the NNT clinically.
Geometric Mean Titre Table: Produce a table of GMT values and 95% confidence intervals at Days 0, 14, 28, and 90 for each arm. Comment on the pattern of antibody kinetics in the vaccine group.

Advanced Level:

Time-Varying Vaccine Efficacy: Stratify the data into time periods (0–30, 31–90, 91–180 days) and calculate VE separately for each period. Does vaccine efficacy change over the 6-month follow-up? Use survfit() with time-dependent stratification or manually subset and recalculate. Discuss potential mechanisms.
Immunogenicity Threshold for Protection: Using the logistic regression model from Section 25.7, predict the probability of infection across a range of Day 28 log-titre values (1.5 to 6.5). Plot this as a dose–response curve and identify the titre level that achieves 80% protection. Discuss the clinical utility of this “immune correlate of protection.”


References & Further Reading:

Plotkin, S. A. (2018). Vaccines: Mechanisms of action. Pediatric Infectious Disease Journal, 37(12), e382–e386.
Halloran, M. E., Haber, M., Longini, I. M., & Struchiner, C. J. (1997). Direct and indirect effects in vaccine efficacy and effectiveness. American Journal of Epidemiology, 146(4), 323–331.
Kahn, R., Schrag, S. J., Musliner, T., & Kagoli, S. (2020). Vaccine efficacy estimation in the presence of waning immunity. Epidemiology, 31(6), 784–792.

title: 37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide
text: 38 Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide
crumbs: Appendices
37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide

title: 37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide
text:
38.1 Introduction
For SAS programmers transitioning to R, the concepts are remarkably similar—both are designed for data manipulation, statistical analysis, and reporting. However, the syntax differs significantly. This appendix serves as a quick reference guide to bridge the gap between SAS and R, helping you leverage your existing knowledge while adopting R’s more modern, functional programming approach.
Key philosophical differences:

SAS is imperative: “Do this, then that” (data step, PROC step)
R is functional: Chain operations together or nest functions
SAS uses datasets as memory-resident tables; R uses data frames/tibbles
SAS indexing is 1-based; R indexing is 1-based too, but subsets work differently
SAS missing values are .; R uses NA
SAS has implicit loops over BY variables; R requires explicit grouping with group_by()

The good news: if you understand PROC SORT, PROC MEANS, and PROC REG in SAS, you already understand what arrange(), summarise(), and lm() do in R. It’s just syntax.
crumbs: Appendices
37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide

title: 37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide
text:
38.2 Complete SAS-to-R Comparison Table









Task
SAS Code
R Code
Notes




Reading CSV file
PROC IMPORT datafile="file.csv" out=mydata dbms=csv; RUN;
library(readr); mydata <- read_csv("file.csv") or read.csv("file.csv")
read_csv() is faster; handles column types better


Reading SAS dataset
libname mylib '/path/'; data work.temp; set mylib.dataset; run;
library(haven); mydata <- read_sas("/path/file.sas7bdat")
haven package reads SAS, SPSS, Stata formats


Reading XPT (transport) file
PROC XCOPY in="/path/" infile=file imemtype=data outfile=file; RUN;
library(haven); mydata <- read_xpt("file.xpt")
For regulatory submissions (CDISC)


Writing XPT file
PROC XCOPY in=work infile=dataset outfile="/path/" outtype=xpt; RUN;
library(haven); write_xpt(mydata, "output.xpt", version=5)
Required for FDA submissions


Data manipulation / subsetting
data work.new; set work.old; where age > 30; keep subjid age sex; run;
library(dplyr); new <- old %>% filter(age > 30) %>% select(subjid, age, sex)
Pipe %>% chains operations; filter() = WHERE, select() = KEEP


Sorting
PROC SORT data=work.in out=work.out; by arm visitnum; run;
library(dplyr); out <- in %>% arrange(arm, visitnum)
arrange() is PROC SORT; ascending default


Sorting descending
PROC SORT data=work.in out=work.out; by arm descending visitnum; run;
library(dplyr); out <- in %>% arrange(arm, desc(visitnum))
Use desc() for descending order


Descriptive statistics
PROC MEANS data=work.in; var age weight; by arm; run;
library(dplyr); in %>% group_by(arm) %>% summarise(mean_age=mean(age), mean_weight=mean(weight))
summarise() = PROC MEANS; group_by() = BY statement


Descriptive statistics (publication table)
PROC MEANS data=work.in; var age weight; class arm; run;
library(gtsummary); in %>% tbl_summary(by=arm, include=c(age, weight))
gtsummary creates publication-ready tables


Frequency table
PROC FREQ data=work.in; tables arm*sex; run;
table(in$arm, in$sex) or library(gtsummary); in %>% tbl_cross(row=arm, col=sex)
table() basic; tbl_cross() prettier


Univariate statistics
PROC UNIVARIATE data=work.in; var age; histogram; qqplot; run;
summary(in$age) and shapiro.test(in$age)
Check normality with Shapiro-Wilk test; hist() and qqnorm() for plots


T-test (unpaired)
PROC TTEST data=work.in; class arm; var sbp; run;
t.test(sbp ~ arm, data=in, var.equal=TRUE)
~ is formula notation (variable ~ grouping); var.equal=TRUE for Student’s t


T-test (paired)
PROC TTEST data=work.in; paired visit1*visit2; run;
t.test(in$visit1, in$visit2, paired=TRUE)
Specify paired=TRUE


Wilcoxon rank-sum test
PROC NPAR1WAY data=work.in wilcoxon; class arm; var response; run;
wilcox.test(response ~ arm, data=in)
Non-parametric equivalent of t-test


One-way ANOVA
PROC GLM data=work.in; class arm; model sbp=arm; run;
aov(sbp ~ arm, data=in) or lm(sbp ~ arm, data=in) and anova()
aov() simpler; lm() more flexible


Two-way ANOVA
PROC GLM data=work.in; class arm visit; model sbp=arm visit arm*visit; run;
aov(sbp ~ arm * visit, data=in) or lm(sbp ~ arm * visit, data=in)
Use * for interaction terms


Linear regression
PROC REG data=work.in; model sbp=age weight bmi / clb; run;
lm(sbp ~ age + weight + bmi, data=in) or lm(sbp ~ ., data=in)
lm() = PROC REG; summary(model) for results


Logistic regression
PROC LOGISTIC data=work.in; model response(event='1')=age weight bmi; run;
glm(response ~ age + weight + bmi, family=binomial(link="logit"), data=in)
family=binomial() for binary outcomes; odds ratios via exp(coef())


Mixed-effects model (random intercept)
PROC MIXED data=work.in; class arm subjid; model sbp=arm / solution; random intercept / subject=subjid; run;
library(lme4); lmer(sbp ~ arm + (1 \| subjid), data=in)
lme4::lmer() for random effects; (1 \| subjid) = random intercept by subject


Mixed-effects model (random slope)
PROC MIXED data=work.in; class arm subjid visit; model sbp=arm visit arm*visit / solution; random intercept slope_visit / subject=subjid; run;
library(lme4); lmer(sbp ~ arm * visit + (visit \| subjid), data=in)
(visit \| subjid) = random slope for visit within subject


GEE (Generalized Estimating Equations)
PROC GENMOD data=work.in; class arm subjid; model sbp=arm visit / dist=normal link=identity; repeated subject=subjid / type=ar(1); run;
library(geepack); geeglm(sbp ~ arm + visit, id=subjid, family=gaussian(), corstr="ar1", data=in)
Marginal models for repeated measures


Cox proportional hazards
PROC PHREG data=work.in; class arm; model time*event(0)=arm age; run;
library(survival); coxph(Surv(time, event) ~ arm + age, data=in)
Surv() creates survival object; event=1 for event, 0 for censored


Kaplan-Meier survival curve
PROC LIFETEST data=work.in plots=survival; time time*event(0); strata arm; run;
library(survival); survfit(Surv(time, event) ~ arm, data=in) then plot() or library(ggsurvfit)
survfit() estimates; ggsurvfit() creates ggplot2 plots


Multiple imputation (MI)
PROC MI data=work.in out=work.mids nimpute=5; var age sbp weight; run;
library(mice); mids <- mice(in, m=5, method="pmm", seed=123)
m=5 creates 5 imputed datasets; method="pmm" = predictive mean matching


Pool MI results (Rubin’s rules)
PROC MIANALYZE data=work.mids; modeleffects age weight; run;
library(mice); fit <- with(mids, lm(sbp ~ age + weight)); pool(fit)
with() fits model to each imputed dataset; pool() combines estimates


Output to RTF
ODS RTF file="output.rtf"; PROC PRINT data=work.in; RUN; ODS RTF close;
library(flextable); ft <- flextable(in); save_as_rtf(ft, path="output.rtf")
flextable creates formatted Word-compatible tables


Output to PDF
ODS PDF file="output.pdf"; PROC PRINT data=work.in; RUN; ODS PDF close;
Render Quarto/RMarkdown: quarto::quarto_render("report.qmd")
Quarto produces professional PDFs with code + results


Macro variable
%let arm_list = Placebo Drug_A_Low Drug_A_High;
arm_list <- c("Placebo", "Drug_A_Low", "Drug_A_High")
Store values in vectors; use in functions


Macro variable substitution
data work.&dsname; set work.input; run;
Use R functions: f <- function(dsname) { data <- read_csv(paste0(dsname, ".csv")) }
Functions replace macros for dynamic programming


Array
array weight_visits{6} weight_v1-weight_v6;
weight_visits <- c(weight_v1, weight_v2, ..., weight_v6) or weight_visits <- in[, c("weight_v1", "weight_v2", ...)]
Use vectors or data frame column selection


PROC FORMAT (custom formats)
PROC FORMAT; value armfmt 1='Placebo' 2='Drug_A'; RUN; PROC PRINT; format arm armfmt.; RUN;
in$arm <- factor(in$arm, levels=c(1,2), labels=c("Placebo", "Drug_A"))
factor() with labels; use forcats::fct_recode() for advanced re-coding


IF/THEN/ELSE
if age > 30 then agegroup='30+'; else agegroup='<30';
ifelse(age > 30, "30+", "<30")
ifelse() is vectorized (works row-wise)


Multiple conditions (CASE WHEN)
select (arm) when (1) arm_name='Placebo'; when (2) arm_name='Drug_A'; otherwise arm_name='Other'; end;
library(dplyr); mutate(arm_name = case_when(arm==1 ~ "Placebo", arm==2 ~ "Drug_A", TRUE ~ "Other"))
case_when() is cleaner for multiple conditions


DO loop (for)
DO i = 1 TO 10; x = i**2; output; end;
for (i in 1:10) { x <- i^2; print(x) } or sapply(1:10, function(i) i^2)
Use for() loops or functional sapply()/purrr::map()


DO WHILE loop
DO WHILE (age > 30); age = age - 1; end;
while (age > 30) { age <- age - 1 }
Less common in data analysis; use for() over vectors


Purrr::map for vectorized operations
Similar to arrays + do loops
library(purrr); map_df(c("arm1", "arm2"), ~read_csv(paste0(., ".csv")))
Functional programming replaces many loops


LEFT JOIN (merge)
PROC SQL; select a.*, b.sbp from dm a left join vs b on a.subjid=b.subjid; quit;
library(dplyr); dm %>% left_join(vs, by="subjid")
left_join(), right_join(), inner_join(), full_join()


Multiple joins
Multiple PROC SQL steps
library(dplyr); dm %>% left_join(vs) %>% left_join(ae)
Chain joins with %>%


BY variable processing (group-by)
PROC SORT; by arm; PROC PRINT; by arm; RUN;
library(dplyr); in %>% group_by(arm) %>% summarise(...) or use nest()
group_by() replaces BY statement for most operations


RETAIN statement (lag)
data work.new; set work.old; retain prev_sbp; prev_sbp_lag = lag(sbp); prev_sbp = sbp; run;
library(dplyr); in %>% mutate(prev_sbp = lag(sbp))
lag() accesses previous row value


Cumulative sum
data work.new; set work.old; cumsum+ae_count; output; run;
library(dplyr); in %>% mutate(cumsum = cumsum(ae_count))
cumsum() computes running total


LENGTH statement
length varname $ 50;
nchar(varname) or stringr::str_length(varname)
Get string length; assign with as.character() and no explicit length needed


COMPRESS (remove spaces)
newvar = compress(oldvar);
gsub(" ", "", oldvar) or library(stringr); str_remove_all(oldvar, " ")
Remove all spaces from string


SUBSTR (substring)
newvar = substr(oldvar, 1, 5);
substring(oldvar, 1, 5) or library(stringr); str_sub(oldvar, 1, 5)
Extract characters 1-5 from string


SCAN (parse words)
word2 = scan(sentence, 2, delim=' ');
library(stringr); str_split(sentence, " ")[[1]][2]
Extract nth word


UPCASE / LOWCASE
newvar = upcase(oldvar); or newvar = lowcase(oldvar);
toupper(oldvar) or tolower(oldvar) or library(stringr); str_to_upper() / str_to_lower()
Case conversion functions


Concatenate strings
newvar = cat(var1, var2); or newvar = var1 \|\| var2;
paste(var1, var2) or library(stringr); str_c(var1, var2)
Use paste() with sep="" for no space


PUT (numeric to character)
char_var = put(num_var, 8.2);
as.character(num_var) or sprintf("%.2f", num_var)
Convert to string with formatting


INPUT (character to numeric)
num_var = input(char_var, 8.);
as.numeric(char_var)
Convert string to number


Date conversion
date_var = input(char_date, MMDDYY10.);
library(lubridate); mdy(char_date) or as.Date(char_date, format="%m/%d/%Y")
lubridate makes date handling cleaner


INTNX (add months/days)
new_date = intnx('month', date_var, 6);
library(lubridate); date_var %m+% months(6) or date_var + months(6)
Add months; use %m+% to keep day-of-month


INTCK (difference between dates)
days_diff = intck('day', date1, date2);
as.numeric(difftime(date2, date1, units="days")) or difftime(date2, date1)
Get days/weeks/months between dates


WHERE clause
PROC PRINT where=(age > 30 and arm='Drug_A'); RUN;
library(dplyr); in %>% filter(age > 30 & arm=="Drug_A")
filter() = WHERE statement


KEEP statement
PROC PRINT data=work.in(keep=subjid age sex); RUN;
library(dplyr); in %>% select(subjid, age, sex)
select() = KEEP statement


DROP statement
PROC PRINT data=work.in(drop=weight height); RUN;
library(dplyr); in %>% select(-weight, -height)
select(-col) drops columns


RENAME statement
PROC PRINT data=work.in(rename=(oldname=newname)); RUN;
library(dplyr); in %>% rename(newname = oldname)
rename(new = old)


FIRSTOBS/OBS (slice rows)
data work.new; set work.old(firstobs=10 obs=20); run;
library(dplyr); in %>% slice(10:20)
slice(10:20) gets rows 10-20


SET statement (append)
data work.combined; set work.ds1 work.ds2; run;
library(dplyr); bind_rows(ds1, ds2)
bind_rows() = SET statement (vertical append)


PROC APPEND (append in-place)
PROC APPEND base=work.ds1 data=work.ds2; RUN;
library(dplyr); ds1 <- bind_rows(ds1, ds2)
Re-assign combined result


PROC TRANSPOSE (reshape wide-to-long)
PROC TRANSPOSE data=work.in out=work.out name=visit value=sbp; by subjid; var sbp_v1-sbp_v6; run;
library(tidyr); in %>% pivot_longer(starts_with("sbp_"), names_to="visit", values_to="sbp")
pivot_longer() = PROC TRANSPOSE


Reshape long-to-wide
PROC TRANSPOSE data=work.in out=work.out prefix=sbp_; by subjid; id visit; var sbp; run;
library(tidyr); in %>% pivot_wider(names_from=visit, values_from=sbp, names_prefix="sbp_")
pivot_wider() opposite of pivot_longer()

section: 38.3 SAS Macro to R Function Translation

37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide

title: 37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide
section: 38.4 SAS ODS Styles vs. R Table Packages
text:
38.4 SAS ODS Styles vs. R Table Packages
SAS uses ODS (Output Delivery System) to create formatted tables. R achieves this with dedicated table packages.









Output Type
SAS ODS
R Package
Example




Publication-ready summary table
PROC MEANS + ODS TABLE
gtsummary::tbl_summary()
Demographics, baseline characteristics


Cross-tabulation
PROC FREQ + ODS
gtsummary::tbl_cross()
Arm × Sex frequency table


Regression results
PROC REG + ODS
gtsummary::tbl_regression()
Odds ratios, confidence intervals


Cox regression
PROC PHREG + ODS
gtsummary::tbl_regression() with survival models
HR, 95% CI


Survival tables
PROC LIFETEST + ODS
ggsurvfit::ggsurvfit()
Kaplan-Meier curves


Excel/RTF export
ODS RTF, ODS EXCEL
flextable::save_as_rtf(), flextable::save_as_docx()
For regulatory submissions


CDISC ADaM listings
Custom PROC PRINT + ODS
rtables or flextable
Subject-level data listings



Quick example:
library(gtsummary)

# SAS equivalent: PROC MEANS + PROC FREQ for baseline table
baseline_table <- trial_data %>%
  tbl_summary(
    by = arm,
    include = c(age, sex, grade),
    label = list(age = "Age (years)", sex = "Sex", grade = "Grade"),
    statistic = list(all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
                     all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"),
    missing = "no"
  ) %>%
  add_overall() %>%
  add_p(test = everything() ~ "t.test") %>%
  add_q() %>%
  modify_spanning_header(everything() ~ "**Treatment Arm**")

# Save to Word/PDF via flextable integration or Quarto
gt::gtsave(as_gt(baseline_table), "baseline.docx")
crumbs: Appendices
37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide

title: 37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide
section: 38.5 Common Pitfalls for SAS Programmers Transitioning to R
text:
38.5 Common Pitfalls for SAS Programmers Transitioning to R

38.5.1 1. Missing Values: . vs. NA









Situation
SAS
R
Issue




Check for missing
if age = . then ...
if (is.na(age)) { ... }
SAS uses .; R uses NA. Using age = NA will always be FALSE


Count missing
PROC MEANS NMISS;
sum(is.na(data$age))
Be explicit about checking NA


Remove rows with missing
data new; set old; if cmiss(of age weight) = 0;
na.omit(data) or drop_na(data)
Use tidyr::drop_na() or dplyr::filter(!is.na(age), !is.na(weight))


Impute simple missing
if age = . then age = 0;
data$age[is.na(data$age)] <- 0 or mutate(age = replace_na(age, 0))
Use replace_na() for readability





38.5.2 2. 1-Based Indexing Confusion
Both SAS and R use 1-based indexing for row/column numbers, BUT subsetting works differently:









Operation
SAS
R
Example




First 5 rows
data new; set old(obs=5); run;
slice(1:5, data) or head(data, 5)
data[1:5, ] also works


Rows 10-20
data new; set old(firstobs=10 obs=20); run;
slice(10:20, data)
Not intuitive: obs=20 means “stop at row 20”


Nth column
array x{*} _numeric_; x{5}
data[[5]] or data[, 5]
Use column names instead: data$sbp or data[["sbp"]]


By condition
where age > 30;
filter(age > 30)
Don’t use row indices; use logical conditions



Common mistake:
# SAS thinking: "Give me columns 1, 3, 5"
data[, c(1, 3, 5)]  # This works!

# But better practice in R:
data %>% select(col1, col3, col5)  # More readable


38.5.3 3. No Implicit Data Step Loop
SAS loops through every observation automatically in the DATA step. R does not.








SAS
R
Explanation




data new; set old; newvar = oldvar * 2; run;
new <- old %>% mutate(newvar = oldvar * 2)
R’s mutate() is vectorized; apply to all rows at once


data new; set old; newvar = lag(oldvar); run;
new <- old %>% mutate(newvar = lag(oldvar))
Use lag(), lead(), or cumsum()


DO i = 1 TO nobs; ... END;
for (i in 1:nrow(data)) { ... }
Avoid loops! Use vectorized operations when possible



Better R practice:
# Don't do this:
for (i in 1:nrow(data)) {
  data$newvar[i] <- data$oldvar[i] * 2
}

# Do this instead:
data %>% mutate(newvar = oldvar * 2)


38.5.4 4. Subsetting: Syntax Differences










Task
SAS
R (Bad)
R (Good)
Reason




Filter rows AND columns
PROC PRINT where=(age > 30); var subjid age;
data[data$age > 30, c(1, 3)]
data %>% filter(age > 30) %>% select(subjid, age)
Piping is cleaner; use names not indices


Keep one column as vector
PROC MEANS var=age;
data[, "age"]
data$age or pull(data, age)
[,] returns data frame; $ or pull() returns vector


Conditional assignment
if age > 30 then agegroup=1; else agegroup=0;
ifelse(age > 30, 1, 0)
case_when(age > 30 ~ 1, TRUE ~ 0)
case_when() handles multiple conditions





38.5.5 5. Data Frame vs. Vector Confusion
SAS doesn’t distinguish; R does.









Operation
SAS
R
Gotcha




Create variable
varname = value;
data$varname <- value
In R, data["varname"] returns a 1-column data frame; data$varname returns a vector


Access variable
Reference by name in PROC
data$varname or data[["varname"]] or pull(data, varname)
Using [["name"]] is pedantic but safe


Check length
%LET nobs = &sysnobs;
nrow(data)
For vector length, use length(vector) not nrow()


Combine variables
Arrays + DO loop
c(var1, var2, var3)
c() combines into vector; data frame requires data.frame()
crumbs: Appendices
37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide

title: 37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide
section: 38.6 Summary: SAS to R Mindset Shift
text:
38.6 Summary: SAS to R Mindset Shift








SAS Concept
R Equivalent
Mindset Shift




PROC SORT + PROC MEANS
arrange() + group_by() + summarise()
From separate steps to chained operations


Macro variables
Function arguments
From text substitution to functional programming


DATA step loops
mutate(), map(), vectorized operations
From explicit loops to implicit broadcasting


KEEP/DROP/RENAME
select(), rename()
From statement-based to function-based


BY variables
group_by()
From automatic iteration to explicit grouping


ODS output
gtsummary, flextable, Quarto
From monolithic ODS to modular packages


PROC PHREG, PROC GLM
survival::coxph(), lm(), glm()
From built-in procs to function-based models



The good news: SAS knowledge translates directly. You already understand statistics; R is just a new way to express it.
crumbs: Appendices
37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide

title: 37  Appendix A: R and SAS Equivalence Guide
text:
38.7 Additional Resources

Base R vs. Tidyverse: This guide emphasizes tidyverse (dplyr, ggplot2) because it’s more readable. Base R equivalents exist but are often less elegant.
Package documentation: Visit ?function_name in R for help, e.g., ?dplyr::left_join
RStudio Cheat Sheets: https://posit.co/resources/cheatsheets/ includes dplyr, tidyr, and ggplot2 cheat sheets.
Statistical methods: ICH E9(R1) and FDA guidance documents use statistical language, not software. The concepts are identical; syntax differs.

title: 38  Appendix B: Essential R Packages for Clinical Data Analysis
text: 39 Appendix B: Essential R Packages for Clinical Data Analysis
crumbs: Appendices
38  Appendix B: Essential R Packages for Clinical Data Analysis

title: 38  Appendix B: Essential R Packages for Clinical Data Analysis
text:
39.1 Introduction: The Pharmaverse Ecosystem
The pharmaverse is a curated collection of R packages designed specifically for pharmaceutical research and clinical data analysis. It represents the gold standard for regulatory-compliant statistical computing, with packages built and maintained by FDA-engaged statisticians and industry leaders.
Key principles of the pharmaverse: - CDISC-compliant: Supports SDTM, ADaM, and CDASH standards - Regulatory-ready: Used in FDA submissions since 2021 - Open-source: Community-driven development - Interoperable: Packages work seamlessly together - Well-documented: Extensive vignettes and examples
Official resources: - Website: https://pharmaverse.org - GitHub: https://github.com/pharmaverse - Community: Slack, issues, discussions
crumbs: Appendices
38  Appendix B: Essential R Packages for Clinical Data Analysis

title: 38  Appendix B: Essential R Packages for Clinical Data Analysis
text:
39.2 Pharmaverse Core Packages
These packages form the foundation for clinical data standardization and transformation.










Package
Latest Version
Purpose
Key Functions
When to Use




admiral
1.1+
CDISC ADaM dataset derivations
derive_vars_*(), derive_params_*(), create_*()
Building ADaM-compliant analysis datasets from SDTM


admiraldev
0.1+
Extensions for special therapeutic areas (e.g., oncology, cardiovascular)
Oncology-specific: derive_param_*() for response, time-to-event
Specialized therapeutic domains beyond base admiral


pharmaversesdtm
0.3+
SDTM dataset building blocks and templates
SDTM domain structures, conformance checks
Creating SDTM datasets from raw data


pharmaverseadam
0.2+
ADaM template datasets
Example ADaM structures (ADSL, ADAE, ADCM)
Reference implementations and unit testing


xportr
0.3+
Create XPT (transport) files for FDA submissions
xportr_write(), xportr_type(), xportr_label()
Exporting SDTM/ADaM to regulatory XPT format


metacore

39.3 Tidyverse Core Packages
The tidyverse provides a unified grammar of data manipulation and visualization.










Package
Latest Version
Purpose
Key Functions
When to Use

1.1+

Daily data wrangling; SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY equivalent

1.3+

Converting between wide and long formats; handling nested data

3.5+

Creating publication-quality plots


stringr
1.5+
String manipulation
str_detect(), str_extract(), str_replace(), str_sub()
Parsing variable names, creating labels, pattern matching


lubridate
1.9+

Date calculations, handling intervals

1.0+

2.1+

Importing CSV/TSV files; faster than base R


forcats
1.0+

tibble
3.2+

Working with tibbles (stricter data frames)



Installation:
# Install all tidyverse packages at once
install.packages("tidyverse")

# Or install individually
install.packages(c("dplyr", "tidyr", "ggplot2", "stringr"))
Quick example:
library(tidyverse)

trial_data %>%
  filter(arm %in% c("Drug_A", "Placebo")) %>%
  select(subjid, arm, age, sbp, dbp) %>%
  group_by(arm) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_sbp = mean(sbp, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_sbp = sd(sbp, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

39.5 Survival Analysis Packages
Specialized packages for time-to-event analyses.










Package
Latest Version
Purpose
Key Functions
When to Use




survival
3.5+
Core survival analysis
Surv(), survfit(), coxph(), survreg()
Kaplan-Meier curves, Cox regression, parametric survival models


survminer
0.4+
Survival visualization
ggsurvplot(), arrange_ggsurvplots(), ggcoxdiagnostics()
Publication-ready KM plots with at-risk tables


ggsurvfit
0.2+
Modern survival ggplot2 integration
ggsurvfit(), ggcuminc()
Modern grammar-of-graphics syntax for survival plots


cmprsk
2.2+

Modeling competing risk outcomes (e.g., death vs. cure in cancer)

2.1+
Flexible parametric survival models

Advanced parametric modeling with flexible hazard shapes



Example: Cox regression and KM plot:
library(survival)
library(ggsurvfit)

# Create survival object
lung_data <- lung %>%
  mutate(time_years = time / 365.25)

# Fit Cox model
cox_model <- coxph(Surv(time_years, status) ~ age + sex + wt.loss,
                    data = lung_data)
summary(cox_model)

# KM curve by sex
library(ggsurvfit)
survfit(Surv(time_years, status) ~ sex, data = lung_data) %>%
  ggsurvfit() +
  labs(title = "Overall Survival by Sex",
       x = "Time (years)",
       y = "Overall Survival Probability")

39.6 Mixed Models and Longitudinal Analysis

Package
Latest Version
Purpose

When to Use

1.1+

3.1+
Nonlinear mixed-effects models
lme(), nlme(), gls()
Alternative to lme4; better for complex correlation structures


mmrm
0.3+
Mixed model for repeated measures (FDA-recommended)

MMRM analysis for pre-specified primary analysis in trials

1.8+
Estimated marginal means (least-squares means)
emmeans(), contrast(), emtrends()

1.4+

MMRM example (recommended for clinical trials):
library(mmrm)

# Fit MMRM: primary analysis for longitudinal efficacy
mmrm_fit <- mmrm(
  formula = FEV1 ~ AVISIT * ARMCD + ar1(AVISIT | USUBJID),
  data = fev1_data,
  reml = TRUE
)

# Extract estimates
summary(mmrm_fit)

# Contrasts at endpoint (Visit 12)
library(emmeans)
emmeans(mmrm_fit, ~ ARMCD | AVISIT) %>%
  contrast(method = "trt.vs.ctrlk")

39.7 Missing Data Handling
Packages for imputation and missing data analysis.










Package
Latest Version
Purpose
Key Functions
When to Use

3.16+
Multivariate imputation by chained equations (MICE)
mice(), pool(), with(), complete()
Multiple imputation for missing data; post-analysis pooling


naniar
1.0+
Exploring and visualizing missing data
miss_var_summary(), gg_miss_var(), vis_miss()
Understanding missing data patterns before imputation


VIM
6.2+
Visualization and imputation
aggr(), marginplot(), kNN(), hotdeck()
K-nearest neighbor imputation; missing data exploration

1.8+
Multiple imputation through EM algorithm
amelia(), mi.meld()
Time-series cross-section data; alternative to MICE

0.3+
Missing data in health economics
Pattern-specific imputation
Cost and QoL missing data in economic evaluations



Multiple imputation workflow:
library(mice)

# Create 5 imputed datasets using MICE
imputed <- mice(clinical_data, m = 5, method = "pmm", seed = 123)

# Fit model to each imputed dataset
fit <- with(imputed, lm(outcome ~ treatment + age + sex))

# Pool estimates using Rubin's rules
pooled <- pool(fit)
summary(pooled)

# Get imputed datasets for manual analysis
complete_data <- complete(imputed, action = "long")

39.8 Clinical Tables and Reporting
Packages for creating publication-ready statistical tables.










Package
Latest Version
Purpose
Key Functions
When to Use




gtsummary
1.7+
Publication-ready summary tables
tbl_summary(), tbl_regression(), tbl_cross(), tbl_merge()
Baseline tables, demographics, regression results


gt
0.10+
Grammar of tables (framework for table creation)
gt(), tab_header(), fmt_number(), opt_stylize()
Complete table control; publishing-quality output


flextable
0.9+
Flexible tables for Word/PowerPoint/HTML
flextable(), save_as_rtf(), save_as_docx()
Exporting tables to Word/PDF; RTF for regulatory submissions


rtables
0.6+
Regulatory-compliant tables for clinical reports
basic_table(), analyze(), split_rows_by()
CDISC-aligned clinical trial tables; regulatory listings


tfrmt
0.1+
Table formatting language
tfrmt(), print_mock_gt()
Complex multi-level tables; data-driven formatting


tern
0.8+
Therapy evaluation and research tables
analyze_vars(), coxph_models(), kaplan_meier()
Pharmaverse-integrated tables for efficacy/safety



Example: Baseline characteristics table:
library(gtsummary)

baseline_tbl <- trial_data %>%
  select(arm, age, sex, stage, marker) %>%
  tbl_summary(
    by = arm,
    type = list(age ~ "continuous", marker ~ "continuous"),
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    label = list(age = "Age (years)", marker = "Biomarker Level")
  ) %>%
  add_overall() %>%
  add_p(test = everything() ~ "t.test") %>%
  add_q() %>%
  modify_spanning_header(everything() ~ "**Treatment Arm**")

# Export to Word
gtsave(baseline_tbl, "baseline_table.docx")

# Or use flextable for more control
library(flextable)
ft <- as_flextable(baseline_tbl)
save_as_rtf(ft, path = "baseline_table.rtf")

39.9 Bayesian Analysis Packages
Packages for Bayesian statistical inference.










Package
Latest Version
Purpose
Key Functions
When to Use




rstanarm
2.32+
Applied regression modeling with Bayesian methods
stan_glm(), stan_lmer(), stan_coxph()
Quick Bayesian inference; pre-compiled models


brms
2.21+
Bayesian regression models using Stan
brm(), hypothesis(), conditional_effects()
Flexible custom Bayesian models; full control over priors


bayesplot
1.11+
Posterior predictive checks and diagnostics

Evaluating MCMC convergence and model adequacy


BayesFactor
0.9+
Bayes factors for hypothesis testing

Bayesian alternatives to frequentist hypothesis tests



Example: Bayesian logistic regression:
library(rstanarm)

# Bayesian logistic regression with default priors
bayes_fit <- stan_glm(
  response ~ age + sex + treatment,
  family = binomial(link = "logit"),
  data = trial_data,
  cores = 4,
  seed = 123
)

# Posterior summary
summary(bayes_fit)

# Posterior predictive check
library(bayesplot)
pp_check(bayes_fit)

# Hypothesis: treatment effect > 0
hypothesis(bayes_fit, "treatmentDrug_A > 0")

39.12 Propensity Scores and Real-World Evidence (RWE)
Packages for observational study analysis and causal inference.










Package
Latest Version
Purpose
Key Functions
When to Use




MatchIt
4.5+
Propensity score matching
matchit(), match.data()
Balancing treatment groups in observational data


cobalt
4.5+
Checking covariate balance
bal.tab(), love.plot()
Visualizing balance before/after PS matching


WeightIt
0.14+
Weighting for causal inference
weightit() (multiple methods)
IPW, entropy balancing, CBPS


twang
2.6+
Toolkit for weighting and analysis of nonequivalent groups
ps(), survey::svyglm()
GBM propensity score estimation



Propensity score matching workflow:
library(MatchIt)
library(cobalt)

# Fit propensity score model and match
m_out <- matchit(
  treatment ~ age + sex + comorbidity,
  data = observational_data,
  method = "nearest",
  distance = "glm"
)

# Extract matched data
matched_data <- get_matches(m_out)

# Check balance
library(cobalt)
love.plot(m_out, threshold = 0.1)

# Analyze matched sample
library(survey)
design <- svydesign(~1, weights = ~weights, data = matched_data)
svyglm(outcome ~ treatment, design = design)

39.15 Installation Recommendations

39.15.1 Install core pharmaverse packages:
install.packages(c("admiral", "xportr", "pharmaversesdtm"))

install.packages(c("gtsummary", "gt", "flextable", "rtables"))

39.15.7 Full installation for clinical work:
# All packages commonly used in clinical trials
pkgs <- c(
  # Pharmaverse
  "admiral", "xportr", "pharmaversesdtm",
  # Tidyverse
  "tidyverse",
  # Data import

  # Survival
  "survival", "survminer", "ggsurvfit",
  # Mixed models
  "lme4", "mmrm", "emmeans",
  # Missing data
  "mice", "naniar",
  # Tables
  "gtsummary", "gt", "flextable",
  # Reproducibility
  "quarto", "rmarkdown", "renv", "targets",
  # Testing
  "testthat", "diffdf"
)

install.packages(pkgs)

title: 38  Appendix B: Essential R Packages for Clinical Data Analysis
text:
39.16 Version Management with renv
For regulatory compliance and reproducibility, always use renv to manage package versions:
# Initialize renv in your project
renv::init()

# Develop and install packages as needed
install.packages("dplyr")

# Create snapshot of current environment
renv::snapshot()  # Creates renv.lock

# Later (or by others), restore exact same versions
renv::restore()  # Reads renv.lock
The renv.lock file should be version-controlled (Git) to ensure everyone has identical environments.

title: 39  Appendix C: Simulated Clinical Datasets
text:
40 Appendix C: Simulated Clinical Datasets
This appendix provides complete, runnable R code to generate four realistic clinical datasets for learning and practice. Each dataset is reproducible (uses set.seed()), includes realistic characteristics, and can be used to practice the analyses throughout this book.
crumbs: Appendices
39  Appendix C: Simulated Clinical Datasets

title: 39  Appendix C: Simulated Clinical Datasets
section: 40.1 Dataset 1: HYPT_TRIAL — Hypertension Trial (200 subjects, 3 arms, 6 visits)
text:
40.1 Dataset 1: HYPT_TRIAL — Hypertension Trial (200 subjects, 3 arms, 6 visits)
A realistic randomized controlled trial of an antihypertensive agent with three treatment arms and longitudinal blood pressure measurements.

40.1.1 Data Characteristics

Study Design: Parallel-group, double-blind RCT
Sample Size: 200 subjects (balanced across 3 arms)
Duration: 12 weeks with visits at baseline, weeks 2, 4, 6, 8, and 12
Primary Variable: Systolic and diastolic blood pressure
Arms: Placebo, Drug_A_Low (50mg), Drug_A_High (100mg)
Missing Data: ~15% MCAR at Week 12 visit
Key Features: Age-related baseline SBP variation, treatment effect, dropout pattern



40.1.2 Code to Generate Dataset
set.seed(2024)

# Parameters
n_subj <- 200
n_per_arm <- n_subj / 3
arms <- c("Placebo", "Drug_A_Low", "Drug_A_High")
n_visits <- 6
visit_days <- c(0, 14, 28, 42, 56, 84)
visit_labels <- c("Visit 1 (Baseline)", "Visit 2 (Week 2)", "Visit 3 (Week 4)",
                  "Visit 4 (Week 6)", "Visit 5 (Week 8)", "Visit 6 (Week 12)")

# ===== Domain: Demographics (DM-like) =====
hypt_dm <- data.frame(
  SUBJID = 1:n_subj,
  ARM = rep(arms, each = n_per_arm),
  ARMCD = rep(c("PBO", "DL", "DH"), each = n_per_arm),
  SITEID = sample(c("Site_01", "Site_02", "Site_03", "Site_04"), n_subj, replace = TRUE),
  AGE = rnorm(n_subj, mean = 55, sd = 12),
  AGEGR1 = NA_character_,
  SEX = sample(c("M", "F"), n_subj, replace = TRUE),
  RACE = sample(c("White", "Black", "Asian", "Other"), n_subj, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.2, 0.15, 0.05)),
  WEIGHT = rnorm(n_subj, mean = 85, sd = 15),
  HEIGHT = rnorm(n_subj, mean = 172, sd = 8),
  RANDDT = as.Date("2024-01-15") + sample(0:30, n_subj, replace = TRUE)
)

# Derived variables
hypt_dm$AGE <- pmax(18, pmin(85, round(hypt_dm$AGE, 1)))
hypt_dm$WEIGHT <- round(hypt_dm$WEIGHT, 1)
hypt_dm$HEIGHT <- round(hypt_dm$HEIGHT, 1)
hypt_dm$BMI <- round(hypt_dm$WEIGHT / ((hypt_dm$HEIGHT / 100)^2), 1)

# Age groups
hypt_dm$AGEGR1 <- cut(hypt_dm$AGE,
                       breaks = c(0, 45, 55, 65, 150),
                       labels = c("<45", "45-54", "55-64", ">=65"))

# ===== Domain: Vital Signs (VS-like) =====
vs_list <- list()

for (subj in 1:n_subj) {
  arm <- hypt_dm$ARM[subj]
  age <- hypt_dm$AGE[subj]

  # Baseline SBP: age-related, with slight arm imbalance
  baseline_sbp <- 150 + (age - 55) * 0.8 + rnorm(1, 0, 5)

  # Treatment effect: Drug > Placebo, High > Low
  treatment_effect <- ifelse(arm == "Placebo", 0,
                             ifelse(arm == "Drug_A_Low", -8, -15))

  # SBP trajectory over 12 weeks
  sbp_trajectory <- baseline_sbp + treatment_effect * (1 - exp(-visit_days / 40)) + rnorm(n_visits, 0, 3)
  dbp_trajectory <- baseline_sbp * 0.65 + treatment_effect * 0.6 * (1 - exp(-visit_days / 40)) + rnorm(n_visits, 0, 2)

  # Add missing data at Week 12: ~15% MCAR
  if (runif(1) < 0.15 && which.max(visit_days) == 6) {
    sbp_trajectory[6] <- NA
    dbp_trajectory[6] <- NA
  }

  # Create visit-level data
  vs_subj <- data.frame(
    SUBJID = subj,
    VISIT = visit_labels,
    VISITNUM = 1:n_visits,
    VISITDY = visit_days,
    VSTESTCD = "BP",
    VSTEST = "Blood Pressure",
    SBP = round(pmax(90, pmin(220, sbp_trajectory)), 0),
    DBP = round(pmax(50, pmin(130, dbp_trajectory)), 0),
    HR = round(rnorm(n_visits, mean = 72, sd = 8), 0),
    VSDT = hypt_dm$RANDDT[subj] + visit_days,
    VSDTC = as.character(hypt_dm$RANDDT[subj] + visit_days)
  )

  # Weight measurement: slight variation, decrease in drug arms
  weight_change <- ifelse(arm == "Placebo", 0, -0.5 * (1 - exp(-visit_days / 42)))
  vs_subj$WEIGHT <- round(hypt_dm$WEIGHT[subj] + weight_change + rnorm(n_visits, 0, 0.5), 1)

  vs_list[[subj]] <- vs_subj
}

hypt_vs <- do.call(rbind, vs_list)
rownames(hypt_vs) <- NULL

# Add ARM and ARMCD to VS
hypt_vs <- merge(hypt_vs[, c("SUBJID", "VISIT", "VISITNUM", "VISITDY", "SBP", "DBP", "HR", "WEIGHT", "VSDT", "VSDTC")],
                  hypt_dm[, c("SUBJID", "ARM", "ARMCD")],
                  by = "SUBJID")

# Reorder columns
hypt_vs <- hypt_vs[order(hypt_vs$SUBJID, hypt_vs$VISITNUM), c("SUBJID", "ARM", "ARMCD", "VISIT", "VISITNUM", "VISITDY", "SBP", "DBP", "HR", "WEIGHT", "VSDT", "VSDTC")]

title: 39  Appendix C: Simulated Clinical Datasets
section: 40.2 Dataset 2: ONCO_SURVIVAL — Oncology Survival Study (300 subjects, 2 arms)
text:
40.2 Dataset 2: ONCO_SURVIVAL — Oncology Survival Study (300 subjects, 2 arms)
A realistic oncology study with time-to-event outcomes (overall survival and progression-free survival).

40.2.1 Data Characteristics

Study Design: Phase III cancer trial with 2 treatment arms
Sample Size: 300 subjects (150 per arm)
Primary Outcome: Overall survival (OS) time in days
Secondary: Progression-free survival (PFS)
Censoring: ~25% censored (still alive at last follow-up)
Key Features: Weibull-distributed survival times, prognostic factors (ECOG PS, age, histology)



40.2.2 Code to Generate Dataset
set.seed(2024)

n_subj <- 300
n_per_arm <- n_subj / 2

# Generate baseline covariates
onco_base <- data.frame(
  SUBJID = 1:n_subj,
  ARM = rep(c("Control", "Experimental"), each = n_per_arm),
  AGE = rnorm(n_subj, mean = 62, sd = 10),
  SEX = sample(c("M", "F"), n_subj, replace = TRUE),
  ECOG_PS = sample(c(0, 1, 2), n_subj, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.5, 0.35, 0.15)),
  HISTOLOGY = sample(c("Adenocarcinoma", "Squamous", "Small Cell", "Other"),
                     n_subj, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1))
)

# Clean continuous variables
onco_base$AGE <- pmax(18, pmin(85, round(onco_base$AGE, 0)))

# Stratified by arm: Experimental has better baseline characteristics
exp_idx <- onco_base$ARM == "Experimental"
onco_base$ECOG_PS[exp_idx] <- pmin(2, onco_base$ECOG_PS[exp_idx] - rbinom(sum(exp_idx), 1, 0.3))

# Generate survival times (Weibull distribution)
# Control arm: worse prognosis; Experimental: better
os_times <- numeric(n_subj)
os_events <- numeric(n_subj)
pfs_times <- numeric(n_subj)
pfs_events <- numeric(n_subj)

for (i in 1:n_subj) {
  arm <- onco_base$ARM[i]
  ecog <- onco_base$ECOG_PS[i]

  # Shape and scale for Weibull
  # Control: median OS ~400 days; Experimental: median ~650 days
  if (arm == "Control") {
    shape_os <- 1.5
    scale_os <- 400 / gamma(1 + 1/shape_os)
    shape_pfs <- 1.3
    scale_pfs <- 250 / gamma(1 + 1/shape_pfs)
  } else {
    shape_os <- 1.5
    scale_os <- 650 / gamma(1 + 1/shape_os)
    shape_pfs <- 1.3
    scale_pfs <- 380 / gamma(1 + 1/shape_pfs)
  }

  # Adjust for ECOG: worse performance → shorter survival
  scale_os <- scale_os * exp(-0.3 * ecog)
  scale_pfs <- scale_pfs * exp(-0.3 * ecog)

  # Generate times
  os_time <- rweibull(1, shape = shape_os, scale = scale_os)
  pfs_time <- rweibull(1, shape = shape_pfs, scale = scale_pfs)

  # Censoring: ~25% censored at 730 days (2 years)
  if (runif(1) < 0.25) {
    os_event <- 0
    os_time <- min(os_time, 730)
  } else {
    os_event <- 1
  }

  if (runif(1) < 0.35) {
    pfs_event <- 0
    pfs_time <- min(pfs_time, 730)
  } else {
    pfs_event <- 1
  }

  os_times[i] <- round(os_time, 0)
  os_events[i] <- os_event
  pfs_times[i] <- round(pfs_time, 0)
  pfs_events[i] <- pfs_event
}

onco_survival <- onco_base %>%
  mutate(
    OS_TIME_DAYS = os_times,
    OS_EVENT = os_events,
    OS_CENS_STR = ifelse(os_events == 1, "Yes", "No"),
    PFS_TIME_DAYS = pfs_times,
    PFS_EVENT = pfs_events,
    PFS_CENS_STR = ifelse(pfs_events == 1, "Yes", "No"),
    RANDDT = as.Date("2023-01-01") + sample(0:365, n_subj, replace = TRUE),
    LSTDT = RANDDT + OS_TIME_DAYS,
    RESPONSE = sample(c("CR", "PR", "SD", "PD"), n_subj, replace = TRUE,
                      prob = c(0.15, 0.35, 0.35, 0.15))
  ) %>%
  arrange(SUBJID)

onco_survival <- as.data.frame(onco_survival)

title: 39  Appendix C: Simulated Clinical Datasets
section: 40.3 Dataset 3: VACCINE_RCT — Vaccine Trial (500 subjects, 2 arms, 5 timepoints)
text:
40.3 Dataset 3: VACCINE_RCT — Vaccine Trial (500 subjects, 2 arms, 5 timepoints)
A realistic vaccine immunogenicity and efficacy study with repeated antibody measurements and time-to-infection outcomes.

40.3.1 Data Characteristics

Study Design: Phase III vaccine efficacy trial
Sample Size: 500 subjects (250 per arm)
Duration: 6 months follow-up
Primary Variable: Antibody titers (log-scale) at 5 timepoints
Secondary: Time to infection
Key Features: Baseline serology, geometric mean titers, immunological non-responders



40.3.2 Code to Generate Dataset
set.seed(2024)

n_subj <- 500
n_per_arm <- n_subj / 2
timepoints <- c(0, 7, 14, 28, 180)  # Days post-vaccination
timepoint_labels <- c("Day 0 (Baseline)", "Day 7", "Day 14", "Day 28", "Day 180 (6mo)")

# Baseline covariates
vaccine_dm <- data.frame(
  SUBJID = 1:n_subj,
  ARM = rep(c("Placebo", "Vaccine"), each = n_per_arm),
  ARMCD = rep(c("PBO", "VAC"), each = n_per_arm),
  SITEID = sample(c("Site_A", "Site_B", "Site_C"), n_subj, replace = TRUE),
  AGE = rnorm(n_subj, mean = 45, sd = 15),
  SEX = sample(c("M", "F"), n_subj, replace = TRUE),
  BASELINE_SEROLOGY = sample(c("Seroneg", "Seropos"), n_subj, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)),
  COMORBIDITY = sample(c("None", "Yes"), n_subj, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7, 0.3)),
  SITE = sample(c(1, 2, 3), n_subj, replace = TRUE)
)

vaccine_dm$AGE <- pmax(18, pmin(85, round(vaccine_dm$AGE, 0)))

# Generate antibody titers
ab_list <- list()

for (subj in 1:n_subj) {
  arm <- vaccine_dm$ARM[subj]
  baseline_serology <- vaccine_dm$BASELINE_SEROLOGY[subj]
  age <- vaccine_dm$AGE[subj]

  # Baseline titer (log10 scale)
  baseline_log_ab <- ifelse(baseline_serology == "Seropos", 2.5, 0.5)

  # Response after vaccination
  if (arm == "Placebo") {
    # Minimal response; slight increase due to natural exposure
    peak_log_ab <- baseline_log_ab + rnorm(1, 0.1, 0.3)
  } else {
    # Strong vaccine response
    responder <- runif(1) > 0.15  # ~85% responder rate
    if (responder) {
      peak_log_ab <- baseline_log_ab + 3.5 + rnorm(1, 0, 0.5)
    } else {
      # Non-responder: minimal response
      peak_log_ab <- baseline_log_ab + 0.2 + rnorm(1, 0, 0.3)
    }
  }

  # Age effect: older subjects weaker response
  peak_log_ab <- peak_log_ab - (age - 45) * 0.01

  # Decay over 6 months
  log_ab_trajectory <- numeric(length(timepoints))
  log_ab_trajectory[1] <- baseline_log_ab

  for (t_idx in 2:length(timepoints)) {
    days <- timepoints[t_idx]
    # Exponential decay with half-life ~60 days
    log_ab_trajectory[t_idx] <- baseline_log_ab + (peak_log_ab - baseline_log_ab) * exp(-0.01155 * days)
    log_ab_trajectory[t_idx] <- log_ab_trajectory[t_idx] + rnorm(1, 0, 0.2)
  }

  ab_subj <- data.frame(
    SUBJID = subj,
    TIMEPOINT = timepoint_labels,
    TIMEPOINTNUM = 1:length(timepoints),
    DAY = timepoints,
    LOG_ANTIBODY = log_ab_trajectory,
    ANTIBODY = 10^log_ab_trajectory,  # Untransformed
    VISITDT = vaccine_dm$SITEID[subj]  # Placeholder for date
  )

  ab_list[[subj]] <- ab_subj
}

vaccine_antibody <- do.call(rbind, ab_list)
rownames(vaccine_antibody) <- NULL

# Merge in ARM and ARMCD
vaccine_antibody <- merge(vaccine_antibody,
                          vaccine_dm[, c("SUBJID", "ARM", "ARMCD")],
                          by = "SUBJID")

vaccine_antibody <- vaccine_antibody[order(vaccine_antibody$SUBJID, vaccine_antibody$TIMEPOINTNUM),
                                      c("SUBJID", "ARM", "ARMCD", "TIMEPOINT", "TIMEPOINTNUM", "DAY", "LOG_ANTIBODY", "ANTIBODY")]

# Infection outcomes (secondary)
vaccine_infection <- data.frame(
  SUBJID = 1:n_subj,
  ARM = vaccine_dm$ARM,
  BASELINE_SEROLOGY = vaccine_dm$BASELINE_SEROLOGY,
  TIME_TO_INFECTION_DAYS = NA_numeric_,
  INFECTION_EVENT = NA_numeric_
)

for (i in 1:n_subj) {
  arm <- vaccine_infection$ARM[i]
  baseline_sero <- vaccine_infection$BASELINE_SEROLOGY[i]

  # Hazard of infection
  if (arm == "Placebo") {
    lambda <- ifelse(baseline_sero == "Seropos", 0.001, 0.003)
  } else {
    lambda <- ifelse(baseline_sero == "Seropos", 0.0002, 0.0005)
  }

  time_to_infection <- rexp(1, rate = lambda) * 365  # Convert to days in 1-year period

  # Censoring at 180 days
  if (time_to_infection < 180) {
    vaccine_infection$TIME_TO_INFECTION_DAYS[i] <- round(time_to_infection, 0)
    vaccine_infection$INFECTION_EVENT[i] <- 1
  } else {
    vaccine_infection$TIME_TO_INFECTION_DAYS[i] <- 180
    vaccine_infection$INFECTION_EVENT[i] <- 0
  }
}

vaccine_dm <- merge(vaccine_dm, vaccine_infection[, c("SUBJID", "TIME_TO_INFECTION_DAYS", "INFECTION_EVENT")],
                    by = "SUBJID")

title: 39  Appendix C: Simulated Clinical Datasets
section: 40.4 Dataset 4: AE_DATASET — Adverse Events (1,500 records, linked to HYPT_TRIAL)
text:
40.4 Dataset 4: AE_DATASET — Adverse Events (1,500 records, linked to HYPT_TRIAL)
A standalone adverse events dataset linked to the hypertension trial with realistic AE characteristics.

40.4.1 Data Characteristics

Sample: 1,500 AE records from 200 subjects (1-8 AEs per subject)
Key Features: MedDRA-like coding, severity gradation, causality assessment
Pattern: More AEs in Drug_A_High arm; more serious in older subjects



40.4.2 Code to Generate Dataset
set.seed(2024)

# Create subject-level AE counts (Poisson-like)
n_subj <- 200
arms <- c("Placebo", "Drug_A_Low", "Drug_A_High")

# AE probability by arm
ae_probs <- c(0.3, 0.5, 0.65)

ae_counts <- numeric(n_subj)
ae_records <- list()
ae_index <- 1

for (subj in 1:n_subj) {
  arm_idx <- ((subj - 1) %% 3) + 1
  arm <- arms[arm_idx]

  # Number of AEs: Poisson with lambda dependent on arm
  lambda <- ifelse(arm == "Placebo", 1.5,
                   ifelse(arm == "Drug_A_Low", 2.5, 4.0))
  n_ae <- rpois(1, lambda)

  if (n_ae > 0) {
    for (ae_num in 1:n_ae) {
      # AE term (MedDRA-like)
      ae_term <- sample(
        c("Headache", "Dizziness", "Fatigue", "Cough", "Diarrhea",
          "Nausea", "Rash", "Hypokalemia", "Hypercreatinine", "Hypotension",
          "Tachycardia", "Chest Pain", "Insomnia", "Dry Mouth", "Muscle Pain"),
        1
      )

      # Preferred Term (PT) and System Organ Class (SOC)
      soc_map <- list(
        "Headache" = "Nervous System Disorders",
        "Dizziness" = "Nervous System Disorders",
        "Fatigue" = "General Disorders",
        "Cough" = "Respiratory, Thoracic and Mediastinal Disorders",
        "Diarrhea" = "Gastrointestinal Disorders",
        "Nausea" = "Gastrointestinal Disorders",
        "Rash" = "Skin and Subcutaneous Tissue Disorders",
        "Hypokalemia" = "Investigations",
        "Hypercreatinine" = "Investigations",
        "Hypotension" = "Vascular Disorders",
        "Tachycardia" = "Cardiac Disorders",
        "Chest Pain" = "Cardiac Disorders",
        "Insomnia" = "Nervous System Disorders",
        "Dry Mouth" = "Gastrointestinal Disorders",
        "Muscle Pain" = "Musculoskeletal and Connective Tissue Disorders"
      )
      soc <- soc_map[[ae_term]]

      # Severity: more severe in high-dose arm and older subjects
      severity_probs <- c(0.65, 0.25, 0.10)  # Mild, Moderate, Severe

      if (arm == "Drug_A_High") {
        severity_probs <- c(0.45, 0.40, 0.15)
      }

      severity <- sample(c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"), 1, prob = severity_probs)

      # Serious flag
      is_serious <- ifelse(severity == "Severe", runif(1) < 0.5, runif(1) < 0.05)
      aeser <- ifelse(is_serious, "Y", "N")
      aesev <- severity

      # Causality: "Related" or "Not Related"
      if (arm == "Placebo") {
        causality_prob <- 0.2  # Mostly not related in placebo
      } else {
        causality_prob <- 0.7  # Mostly related in treatment
      }

      aecausal <- ifelse(runif(1) < causality_prob, "Related", "Not Related")

      # Dates
      aestdy <- sample(1:84, 1)  # AE start day within study
      aeduration <- sample(1:60, 1)  # Duration in days
      aectcae <- ifelse(is_serious, "Y", "N")

      # Create AE record
      ae_rec <- data.frame(
        AESEQ = ae_num,
        SUBJID = subj,
        ARM = arm,
        ARMCD = ifelse(arm == "Placebo", "PBO",
                       ifelse(arm == "Drug_A_Low", "DL", "DH")),
        AETERM = ae_term,
        AEPT = ae_term,  # PT = preferred term
        AESOC = soc,
        AESEV = aesev,
        AESER = aeser,
        AECAUSAL = aecausal,
        AESTDY = aestdy,
        AEDUR = aeduration,
        AECTCAE = aectcae
      )

      ae_records[[ae_index]] <- ae_rec
      ae_index <- ae_index + 1
    }
  }
}

ae_dataset <- do.call(rbind, ae_records)
rownames(ae_dataset) <- NULL

# Ensure AESEQ is sequential within subject
ae_dataset <- ae_dataset %>%
  arrange(SUBJID) %>%
  group_by(SUBJID) %>%
  mutate(AESEQ = row_number()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  as.data.frame()


40.4.3 Data Dictionary: Adverse Events









Variable
Type
Description
Values/Range




AESEQ
numeric
Sequence number within subject
1-8


SUBJID
numeric
Subject ID
1-200


ARM
character
Treatment arm
Placebo, Drug_A_Low, Drug_A_High


ARMCD
character
Treatment arm code
PBO, DL, DH


AETERM
character
Adverse event term
See AE list below


AEPT
character
Preferred term (same as AETERM)
See AE list below


AESOC
character
System Organ Class (MedDRA)
Various SOCs


AESEV
character
Severity
Mild, Moderate, Severe


AESER
character
Seriousness flag
Y (Yes), N (No)


AECAUSAL
character
Causality assessment
Related, Not Related


AESTDY
numeric
AE start day
1-84


AEDUR
numeric
AE duration (days)
1-60


AECTCAE
character
Life-threatening?
Y, N





40.4.4 Example: head() Output
  AESEQ SUBJID       ARM ARMCD    AETERM AEPT AESOC     AESEV AESER AECAUSAL AESTDY AEDUR AECTCAE
1     1      1     Placebo   PBO   Headache Headache Nervous System Disorders   Mild      N Not Related      15    12       N
2     1      4 Drug_A_Low    DL   Nausea   Nausea   Gastrointestinal Disorders Mild      N Related      22      8       N
3     2      4 Drug_A_Low    DL   Diarrhea Diarrhea Gastrointestinal Disorders Moderate N Related      31     14       N
4     1      5 Drug_A_High   DH   Hypotension Hypotension Vascular Disorders Severe    Y Related       8     21       N
5     2      5 Drug_A_High   DH   Rash     Rash     Skin and Subcutaneous Tissue Disorders Moderate N Related      42      5       N


40.4.5 How to Use
write.csv(ae_dataset, "ae_dataset.csv", row.names = FALSE)
ae_dataset <- read.csv("ae_dataset.csv")

# Merge with demographics for safety analysis
ae_with_dm <- ae_dataset %>%
  left_join(hypt_dm, by = c("SUBJID" = "SUBJID", "ARM" = "ARM"))

# Safety summary table
ae_summary <- ae_dataset %>%
  group_by(ARM) %>%
  summarise(
    total_ae = n(),
    subjects_with_ae = n_distinct(SUBJID),
    pct_with_ae = n_distinct(SUBJID) / 67 * 100,  # 67 subjects per arm
    serious_ae = sum(AESER == "Y"),
    severe_ae = sum(AESEV == "Severe")
  )
Cross-reference: - Ch. 6-7: Safety summary tables - Ch. 13: Adverse event listings (regulatory format) - Ch. 2: CDISC AE domain structure

41 Appendix D: Regulatory References and Resources
This appendix provides a comprehensive reference to the regulatory guidance, standards, and resources that underpin clinical data analysis in R. It is essential for practitioners working on FDA submissions, ensuring compliance with ICH guidelines, and adopting CDISC standards.

41.1 ICH Guidelines: Comprehensive Reference
The International Council for Harmonisation (ICH) develops guidelines adopted by regulatory authorities worldwide. These guidelines define statistical principles and clinical trial methodology.

The Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium (CDISC) defines international standards for clinical trial data.
